> **Memory Analogy:** A finite automaton has **no memory** other than its current state. It cannot "remember" past inputs in a general sense — all relevant history must be encoded into the state. This is what makes its memory *finite*.

---

## 1.5 Why is this Topic Important for KTU?

This topic forms the **bedrock** of Module 1 and the entire course. Every later concept — regular expressions, pumping lemma, Myhill-Nerode theorem, context-free grammars — relies on this formal 5-tuple definition. KTU examiners **frequently** ask direct definition questions (3 marks) and 14-mark constructive design questions from this topic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Anatomy of the 5-Tuple in Detail

### 2.1.1 State Set (Q)

A *state* captures everything the machine needs to remember about the input read so far. The set Q is:
- **Finite** — this is the defining constraint of FA.
- **Non-empty** — by definition, $Q \neq \emptyset$.
- The **size** $|Q|$ is called the **number of states**, denoted **n**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently tests whether a student can correctly identify the **size of the state set** and the **cardinality of the alphabet** from a given transition diagram.

### 2.1.2 Input Alphabet (Σ)

- The alphabet is the set of all allowed input symbols.
- Σ must be finite and non-empty.
- A **string** over Σ is a finite sequence $w = w_1 w_2 \dots w_k$ where each $w_i \in \Sigma$.
- The **length** of string w is denoted $|w|$.
- The **empty string** is denoted $\varepsilon$, with $|\varepsilon| = 0$.
- The set of **all strings** over Σ is denoted $\Sigma^*$.

### 2.1.3 Transition Function (δ)

The transition function is the **engine** of the automaton. For a DFA:

$$\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$$

This function is **total** — it must be defined for every (state, symbol) pair. A DFA missing a transition is considered **incomplete** and is sometimes called a **Partial DFA (PDFA)**.

For an NFA:

$$\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$$

The result is a **subset** of Q (possibly empty $\emptyset$).

### 2.1.4 Initial State (q₀)

A single distinguished element of Q where the machine starts reading the input. Mathematically, $q_0 \in Q$.

### 2.1.5 Set of Final States (F)

- $F \subseteq Q$ — final states are a subset of all states.
- A string is **accepted** if after reading the entire string, the machine is in some state $q_f \in F$.
- The **language accepted** by a DFA M is:

$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid \delta^*(q_0, w) \in F \,\}$$

where $\delta^*$ is the **extended transition function** applied to the whole string w.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Notation | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| DFA definition | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | 5-tuple formal structure |
| NFA transition | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^{Q}$ | Maps to power set of Q |
| DFA transition | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | Maps to single state |
| Extended transition | $\delta^* : Q \times \Sigma^* \rightarrow Q$ | Applied to whole string |
| Recursive defn of $\delta^*$ | $\delta^*(q, \varepsilon) = q$ | Base case (empty string) |
| Recursive defn of $\delta^*$ | $\delta^*(q, wa) = \delta(\delta^*(q, w), a)$ | Induction step |
| Accepted language | $L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid \delta^*(q_0, w) \in F \,\}$ | Strings ending in final state |
| Rejected language | $\overline{L(M)} = \Sigma^* - L(M)$ | Complement of accepted set |
| Power set size | $\vert 2^{Q} \vert = 2^{\vert Q \vert}$ | For NFA conversion to DFA |
| Number of transitions (DFA) | $\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert$ | Total edges in complete DFA |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Finite Automata are not just abstract toys — they are the **backbone of real engineering systems**:

> [!NOTE]
> **Lexical Analyzers in Compilers:** Tools like Lex/Yacc use DFAs to tokenize source code.
> **Digital Circuit Design:** Sequential circuits (flip-flops) are physical implementations of finite state machines.
> **Network Protocol Verification:** TCP state machines, communication protocols are modeled as FA.
> **Pattern Matching:** Tools like `grep` use automaton-based matching engines.
> **Vending Machines & Traffic Lights:** Classic embedded control systems.
> **Spell Checkers & Regular Expression Engines:** Underlying engine is an NFA/DFA.

The transition function δ is essentially a **lookup table** or a **read-only memory (ROM)** in hardware terms — for every (state, input) combination, a next state is hard-coded. This is why FA-based systems are extremely fast and predictable in real engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Extended Transition Function — Exhaustive Derivation

The transition function δ only handles **one symbol** at a time. To process an entire string, we need to **extend** it to operate on strings. This extension is denoted $\delta^*$.

### Step 1 — Define the Domain

The extended transition function takes a state and a string (a sequence of symbols):

$$\delta^* : Q \times \Sigma^* \rightarrow Q$$

### Step 2 — Base Case (Empty String)

When the string is empty, the machine does nothing and remains in its current state:

$$\delta^*(q, \varepsilon) = q, \quad \forall q \in Q$$

This is the **axiom** — it is the starting point of all string processing.

### Step 3 — Inductive Case (Recursive Step)

Suppose we have a string $w$ of length $k$ and a single extra symbol $a \in \Sigma$. The machine first processes $w$ to reach a new state, then reads $a$:

$$\delta^*(q, wa) = \delta(\delta^*(q, w), a)$$

**Logic:** Process the prefix $w$ first, then feed the resulting state along with the new symbol $a$ to the original δ.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

Consider a DFA with:
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$
- $F = \{q_2\}$
- Transition table:

| State | a | b |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| → q₀ | q₀ | q₁ |
| q₁ | q₂ | q₀ |
| *q₂ | q₂ | q₂ |

Let the input string be $w = \text{"ab"}$.

**Compute $\delta^*(q_0, \text{"ab"})$:**

$$\delta^*(q_0, \text{"ab"}) = \delta(\delta^*(q_0, \text{"a"}), \text{"b"})$$

Inner computation: $\delta^*(q_0, \text{"a"}) = \delta(q_0, a) = q_0$.

Substituting:

$$\delta(\delta^*(q_0, \text{"a"}), \text{"b"}) = \delta(q_0, b) = q_1$$

Therefore:

$$\delta^*(q_0, \text{"ab"}) = q_1$$

Since $q_1 \notin F$, the string **"ab"** is **rejected**.

### Step 5 — Acceptance Condition for a String

A string $w$ is accepted by DFA M if and only if:

$$\delta^*(q_0, w) \in F$$

Equivalently, the language accepted by M is:

$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid \delta^*(q_0, w) \in F \,\}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing DFA, NFA, and ε-NFA

| Feature | DFA | NFA | ε-NFA |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tuple form | $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ | $(Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ |
| Transition target | Single state in Q | Subset of Q (in $2^Q$) | Subset of Q |
| $\delta$ definition | $Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | $Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^Q$ | $Q \times (\Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}) \rightarrow 2^Q$ |
| ε-transitions | No | No | Yes |
| Number of transitions | $\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert$ | $\leq \vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Sigma \vert$ | $\leq \vert Q \vert \cdot (\vert \Sigma \vert + 1)$ |
| Equivalent DFA size | $\vert Q \vert$ | up to $2^{\vert Q \vert}$ | up to $2^{\vert Q \vert}$ |
| Acceptance | Unique path reaches F | Some path reaches F | Some path reaches F |
| Language class | Regular | Regular | Regular |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Theorem (Equivalence):** Every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA. Every ε-NFA can be converted to an equivalent NFA (and hence to a DFA). All three models accept exactly the **regular languages**. This theorem is a frequent KTU question.

---

## 3.3 Constructive Derivation — Acceptance of "aab" on a Sample DFA

Given DFA M with $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$, $q_0 = q_0$, $F = \{q_2\}$ and transitions:

$$\delta(q_0, a) = q_1, \quad \delta(q_0, b) = q_0$$
$$\delta(q_1, a) = q_1, \quad \delta(q_1, b) = q_2$$
$$\delta(q_2, a) = q_2, \quad \delta(q_2, b) = q_2$$

**Step 1:** Read the first 'a':
$$\delta^*(q_0, a) = \delta(q_0, a) = q_1$$

**Step 2:** Read the second 'a':
$$\delta^*(q_0, aa) = \delta(\delta^*(q_0, a), a) = \delta(q_1, a) = q_1$$

**Step 3:** Read the third symbol 'b':
$$\delta^*(q_0, aab) = \delta(\delta^*(q_0, aa), b) = \delta(q_1, b) = q_2$$

**Step 4:** Check acceptance:
$$q_2 \in F \implies \text{String "aab" is ACCEPTED.}$$

**Inferred Language:** This DFA accepts all strings of the form $a^n b$ where $n \geq 1$, i.e., $L(M) = \{ab, aab, aaab, aaaab, \dots\}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of a Finite Automaton

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **abstract architecture** of a Finite Automaton as a block-level functional system — showing how the 5-tuple components interact during computation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    InputStr["INPUT STRING W over Sigma"] --> ReadSym["Read Next Symbol a in Sigma"]
    ReadSym --> Lookup["Consult Transition Function Delta"]
    CurrState["Current State Q"] --> Lookup
    Lookup --> NextState["Compute Next State delta of Q comma a"]
    NextState --> CheckEmpty{"More Symbols in W?"}
    CheckEmpty -- Yes --> ReadSym
    CheckEmpty -- No --> CheckFinal{"NextState in F?"}
    CheckFinal -- Yes --> Accept["ACCEPT"]
    CheckFinal -- No --> Reject["REJECT"]
    InitState["Initial State q0"] -.assigns.-> CurrState
    FinalSet["Set of Final States F"] -.defines.-> CheckFinal
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **Input String Block** supplies symbols one by one.
- The **Transition Function Block** (δ) acts as the lookup engine, taking the current state and the current input symbol.
- The **Current State Register** holds the state — this is the only memory of the machine.
- The **Decision Diamond** checks whether all symbols are consumed; if yes, the final state is checked against F.
- The dotted lines (`.assigns.`, `.defines.`) represent the **static configuration** of the machine — the initial state and final state set, which are set once at design time.

---

## 4.2 Component Interaction Flow — DFA Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Static["STATIC CONFIGURATION (Defined at Design Time)"]
        Qset["Q = Finite Set of States"]
        Sigma["Sigma = Input Alphabet"]
        Delta["delta: Transition Function"]
        Q0["q0 = Initial State"]
        Fset["F = Final States"]
    end
    subgraph Dynamic["DYNAMIC EXECUTION (During String Processing)"]
        Buffer["Input Buffer holding w"]
        Ctrl["Control Unit reads symbol a"]
        Trans["Apply delta of Q cross Sigma"]
        StateReg["State Register stores current Q"]
    end
    Static --> Dynamic
    Buffer --> Ctrl
    Ctrl --> Trans
    StateReg --> Trans
    Trans --> StateReg
    Trans --> Out["Output: ACCEPT or REJECT"]
```

This diagram distinguishes between the **static mathematical definition** of the FA (the 5-tuple) and the **dynamic execution behaviour** during string processing — a distinction KTU examiners often test in 14-mark design questions.

---

## 4.3 State Transition Topology — Generic DFA Visualization

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sQ0
    sQ0 --> sQ0 : a
    sQ0 --> sQ1 : b
    sQ1 --> sQ2 : a
    sQ1 --> sQ0 : b
    sQ2 --> sQ2 : a
    sQ2 --> sQ2 : b
    sQ2 --> [*]
    note right of sQ0 : Initial State q0
    note right of sQ2 : Final State in F
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note:** Node IDs are kept alphanumeric (sQ0, sQ1, sQ2) to comply with Mermaid parser rules. Labels are kept as raw uppercase text without markdown formatting.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**Define a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA). Write down its formal 5-tuple representation and explain each component briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> A **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** is a 5-tuple mathematical structure defined as:
>
> $$M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$$
>
> where:
> 1. **Q** is a finite, non-empty set of states. (0.5 mark)
> 2. **Σ (Sigma)** is a finite, non-empty set of input symbols called the alphabet. (0.5 mark)
> 3. **δ (Delta)** is the transition function, $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$, which maps each (state, input) pair to exactly one next state. (1 mark)
> 4. **q₀** is the initial state, $q_0 \in Q$, from which the machine begins computation. (0.5 mark)
> 5. **F** is the set of final (accepting) states, $F \subseteq Q$. A string is accepted if processing ends in a state belonging to F. (0.5 mark)

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary]`

**Differentiate between a DFA and an NFA. How does the transition function differ in both?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> | Aspect | DFA | NFA |
> | :--- | :--- | :--- |
> | Transition function | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^Q$ |
> | Next state on input | Exactly one unique state | Zero, one, or many states |
> | Computation | Single deterministic path | Multiple parallel paths (non-deterministic choice) |
> | ε-transitions | Not allowed | Allowed (in ε-NFA) |
>
> The key difference lies in the **range** of the transition function: DFA returns a single state, whereas NFA returns a **subset of Q** (an element of the power set $2^Q$). (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1 Full Question]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Define a DFA formally. Construct a DFA that accepts all strings over the alphabet $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$ that contain the substring **"aba"**.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of the extended transition function $\delta^*$. Using your DFA from part (a), show step-by-step whether the string **"baba"** is accepted or rejected.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — Formal Definition and DFA Construction [7 marks]

**Formal Definition (2 marks):**

A DFA is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where:
- Q = finite set of states
- Σ = finite input alphabet
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ = transition function
- $q_0 \in Q$ = initial state
- $F \subseteq Q$ = set of final states

**DFA Construction for strings containing "aba" (5 marks):**

We use the standard **pattern-matching DFA** technique. The states track the longest prefix of "aba" matched so far.

- **Q** = $\{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\}$
- **Σ** = $\{a, b\}$
- **q₀** = $q_0$ (no characters of pattern matched)
- **F** = $\{q_3\}$ (pattern "aba" found)

**Transition Table:**

| State | a | b |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| → q₀ | q₁ | q₀ |
| q₁ | q₁ | q₂ |
| q₂ | q₃ | q₀ |
| *q₃ | q₃ | q₃ |

**State Meaning:**
- $q_0$ = last symbol did not match prefix of "aba"
- $q_1$ = "a" matched
- $q_2$ = "ab" matched
- $q_3$ = "aba" matched (accepting)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sQ0
    sQ0 --> sQ1 : a
    sQ0 --> sQ0 : b
    sQ1 --> sQ1 : a
    sQ1 --> sQ2 : b
    sQ2 --> sQ3 : a
    sQ2 --> sQ0 : b
    sQ3 --> sQ3 : a
    sQ3 --> sQ3 : b
    note right of sQ3 : Final State
```

---

### Part (b) — Extended Transition Function and String Evaluation [7 marks]

**Definition of $\delta^*$ (3 marks):**

The extended transition function handles **entire strings** instead of single symbols:

$$\delta^* : Q \times \Sigma^* \rightarrow Q$$

It is defined recursively:

$$\delta^*(q, \varepsilon) = q$$

$$\delta^*(q, wa) = \delta(\delta^*(q, w), a)$$

**Step-by-Step Evaluation of "baba" (4 marks):**

We compute $\delta^*(q_0, \text{"baba"})$ symbol by symbol.

**Step 1:** Read 'b' from $q_0$:
$$\delta^*(q_0, b) = \delta(q_0, b) = q_0$$

**Step 2:** Read 'a' from $q_0$:
$$\delta^*(q_0, ba) = \delta(\delta^*(q_0, b), a) = \delta(q_0, a) = q_1$$

**Step 3:** Read 'b' from $q_1$:
$$\delta^*(q_0, bab) = \delta(\delta^*(q_0, ba), b) = \delta(q_1, b) = q_2$$

**Step 4:** Read 'a' from $q_2$:
$$\delta^*(q_0, baba) = \delta(\delta^*(q_0, bab), a) = \delta(q_2, a) = q_3$$

**Step 5 — Acceptance Check:**

The machine ends in state $q_3$. Since $q_3 \in F = \{q_3\}$, the string **"baba" is ACCEPTED.** ✓

This is consistent with the language — "baba" contains "aba" as a substring (positions 2–4).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Alternative]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Define a Non-Deterministic Finite Automaton (NFA) with a formal 5-tuple. Construct an NFA over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$ that accepts all strings **ending with "01"**. Show that this NFA is smaller than its equivalent DFA.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the NFA from part (a), write down the **language accepted** $L(M)$ formally. Explain what happens when the NFA processes the string **"00101"** by tracing all possible computation paths.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — NFA Definition and Construction [7 marks]

**Formal Definition (2 marks):**

An NFA is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where:
- Q, Σ, $q_0$, F are defined as in a DFA
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^Q$ is the transition function, returning a **subset of Q** (an element of the power set $2^Q$)

**NFA Construction (5 marks):**

- **Q** = $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- **Σ** = $\{0, 1\}$
- **q₀** = $q_0$
- **F** = $\{q_2\}$

**Transition Table:**

| State | 0 | 1 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| → q₀ | $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | $\{q_0\}$ |
| q₁ | $\emptyset$ | $\{q_2\}$ |
| *q₂ | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ |

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> sQ0
    sQ0 --> sQ0 : 0, 1
    sQ0 --> sQ1 : 0
    sQ1 --> sQ2 : 1
    note right of sQ2 : Final State
```

**Comparison with Equivalent DFA:**

The equivalent DFA for this language requires **4 states** — the states are $\{q_0\}, \{q_0, q_1\}, \{q_0, q_2\}, \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ (reachable subsets). The NFA has only 3 states. Thus, the NFA is **structurally smaller and simpler to design** for this language.

---

### Part (b) — Language and Trace of "00101" [7 marks]

**Language Definition (2 marks):**

$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid \delta^*(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset \,\}$$

Equivalently, a string is accepted by an NFA if **at least one** computation path ends in a final state.

For this NFA:

$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \{0, 1\}^* \mid w \text{ ends with the substring "01"} \,\}$$

**Trace of "00101" (5 marks):**

The NFA may be in **multiple states simultaneously** after each input. We maintain the set of reachable states.

**Step 1:** Read '0' from $\{q_0\}$:
$$\delta(\{q_0\}, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

**Step 2:** Read '0' from $\{q_0, q_1\}$:
$$\delta(\{q_0, q_1\}, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \emptyset = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

**Step 3:** Read '1' from $\{q_0, q_1\}$:
$$\delta(\{q_0, q_1\}, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}$$

**Step 4:** Read '0' from $\{q_0, q_2\}$:
$$\delta(\{q_0, q_2\}, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_2, 0) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \emptyset = \{q_0, q_1\}$$

**Step 5:** Read '1' from $\{q_0, q_1\}$:
$$\delta(\{q_0, q_1\}, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}$$

**Acceptance Check:**

Final reachable set: $\{q_0, q_2\}$.

$$\{q_0, q_2\} \cap F = \{q_0, q_2\} \cap \{q_2\} = \{q_2\} \neq \emptyset$$

Therefore, the string **"00101" is ACCEPTED** by the NFA. ✓

(Verification: "00101" indeed ends in "01".)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 5-tuple notation:** Many students write the definition in plain words but forget the formal $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ notation. KTU examiners deduct up to **1 mark** for this omission in 3-mark questions and **2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
> 2. **Confusing δ and δ\*:** δ handles only **one symbol**, while $\delta^*$ handles a **whole string**. Mixing these up leads to incorrect step-by-step evaluations in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Missing transitions in transition tables:** Every DFA row for every symbol must have a defined transition. Leaving a blank cell is a guaranteed **0.5 mark deduction** per missing entry.
> 4. **Final state confusion:** In NFA acceptance, the machine accepts if the final reachable set **intersects** F — not if the entire set is contained in F. This is a frequent conceptual error.
> 5. **Not labelling the initial state with an arrow** (→) or the final state with a double circle in diagrams — KTU strictly expects standard FA diagram conventions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Finite Automaton** is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ — remember all five components.
- **Q** is finite and non-empty; **Σ** is finite and non-empty; **q₀ ∈ Q**; **F ⊆ Q**.
- The **DFA transition function** is $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q$ (single next state).
- The **NFA transition function** is $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow 2^Q$ (subset of Q).
- The **extended transition function** $\delta^*$ processes entire strings, defined recursively: $\delta^*(q, \varepsilon) = q$ and $\delta^*(q, wa) = \delta(\delta^*(q, w), a)$.
- A string $w$ is **accepted** by a DFA iff $\delta^*(q_0, w) \in F$.
- A string $w$ is **accepted** by an NFA iff $\delta^*(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset$.
- The **language accepted** by M is $L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid \text{w is accepted by M} \,\}$.
- **DFA, NFA, and ε-NFA** all accept exactly the **regular languages** — they are equivalent in power.
- The **maximum number of states** in the DFA equivalent to an NFA with n states is $2^n$.
- A complete DFA has exactly $|Q| \cdot |\Sigma|$ transitions — no missing entries.
- The **empty string** $\varepsilon$ is accepted by a DFA iff $q_0 \in F$.
- FA has **no memory** beyond its current state — all past input history must be encoded into the state.
- Standard diagram conventions: **arrow** for initial state, **double circle** for final states, **labelled arrows** for transitions.
- **Real-world applications**: lexical analyzers, digital circuits, network protocols, pattern matching, vending machines, traffic controllers.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Mnemonic for the 5-tuple:** **"Q-S-D-I-F"** → **Q**ueens, **S**igma, **D**elta, **I**nitial, **F**inal. (Or: *Quis custodiet ipsos custodes?* — watch who watches the states!)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a DFA

A **Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)** is a mathematical model of computation that reads a finite string of symbols from an input alphabet, transitions between a finite set of internal states in a *completely deterministic* manner, and either **accepts** or **rejects** the string.

Formally, a DFA is defined as a **5-tuple (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F)**, where each component is rigidly specified:

| Component | Symbol | Description |
|-----------|--------|-------------|
| Finite set of states | **Q** | A non-empty, finite collection of internal configurations the machine can occupy |
| Input alphabet | **Σ** | A finite, non-empty set of input symbols (e.g., {0, 1}, {a, b}) |
| Transition function | **δ** | A *total* function: δ: Q × Σ → Q (maps a state and a symbol to exactly **one** next state) |
| Initial state | **q₀** | The single starting state; must satisfy q₀ ∈ Q |
| Set of final states | **F** | The set of *accepting* states; F ⊆ Q |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Determinism Property:** For **every** state q ∈ Q and **every** input symbol a ∈ Σ, the transition function δ(q, a) is defined and yields **exactly one** state. No ambiguity, no "stuck" states, no choice — this is what makes the automaton *deterministic*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Σ\*** (Kleene Star) is the set of *all finite strings* (including the empty string ε) that can be formed from Σ. The language of a DFA, denoted **L(M)**, is the subset of Σ* consisting of all strings that drive the machine from q₀ into a state in F.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Vending Machine"

Imagine a **vending machine** as a perfect real-world analogy for a DFA:

- The machine has a **fixed number of internal states** (Ready, Coin-Inserted, Selection-Made, Dispensing, Out-Of-Stock) → these are the elements of **Q**.
- It accepts only specific inputs: ₹5 coin, ₹10 coin, "press A1", "press A2" → these form the alphabet **Σ**.
- The machine's behavior is **completely predictable**: insert ₹5 → state changes to "Coin-Inserted" → press A1 → state changes to "Dispensing". There is never a case where the same action produces two different outcomes → this represents the deterministic transition function **δ**.
- The machine starts in a known state ("Ready") → this is **q₀**.
- The machine "accepts" (dispenses the snack) only when it lands in a specific final state like "Dispensing" → this is **F**.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Summary:** A DFA is a tiny "decision-engine" with a **limited memory** (the states), a **strict set of allowed inputs** (the alphabet), and a **totally predictable reaction** to every possible input at every possible moment. It reads a string one symbol at a time, and at the end, it simply reports "ACCEPT" or "REJECT."

## 1.3 String Acceptance & Language of a DFA

**Extended Transition Function (δ̂):**
The transition function δ can be extended recursively to operate on entire strings:

$$\hat{\delta}(q, \epsilon) = q$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q, wa) = \delta(\hat{\delta}(q, w), a)$$

where w ∈ Σ* is a string and a ∈ Σ is a single symbol.

**A string w is accepted by DFA M if and only if:**

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in F$$

**The language recognized by M is:**

$$L(M) = \{ w \in \Sigma^* \mid \hat{\delta}(q_0, w) \in F \}$$

---

> **Quick Self-Check Concept Map:**
> - **DFA** = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F)
> - **δ** is a *function*, not a relation → unique next state
> - **δ** is *total* → defined for all (state, symbol) pairs
> - **L(M)** is the *language* of M
> - M is said to *recognize* L(M)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Transition Function δ

The transition function is the *brain* of the DFA. For a DFA with |Q| = n states and |Σ| = k symbols, the transition table contains exactly:

$$|Q \times \Sigma| = n \times k \text{ entries}$$

This is a critical KTU board fact — examiners love to ask it.

**Properties of δ:**
1. **Total:** Defined for every (q, a) ∈ Q × Σ. There are no "holes" in the transition table.
2. **Functional:** Each (q, a) maps to exactly one next state. This is the "deterministic" property.
3. **Deterministic in State:** Given the current state and next input symbol, the next state is uniquely determined.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contrast with NFA:** In a *Non-deterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)*, the transition function is replaced by a *transition relation* Δ: Q × Σ → 2^Q (power set of Q). For the same (q, a), there may be *zero, one, or many* possible next states. This is the single most important distinction students must internalize.

## 2.2 DFA Acceptance — Step-by-Step Logic

To determine if a DFA accepts a string w = a₁a₂...aₙ:

1. Start at the initial state q₀.
2. Read the first symbol a₁. Move to state q₁ = δ(q₀, a₁).
3. Read the second symbol a₂. Move to state q₂ = δ(q₁, a₂).
4. Continue this process for every symbol.
5. After consuming the entire string, you land in some state qₙ.
6. If qₙ ∈ F → **ACCEPT**. Else → **REJECT**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | DFA Definition | M = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F) | 5-tuple |
| 2 | Transition function | δ: Q × Σ → Q | Total, deterministic |
| 3 | # of transitions | \vert Q \vert × \vert Σ \vert | Full transition table size |
| 4 | Extended δ on empty string | δ̂(q, ε) = q | Base case |
| 5 | Extended δ on wa | δ̂(q, wa) = δ(δ̂(q, w), a) | Recursive |
| 6 | String accepted iff | δ̂(q₀, w) ∈ F | Acceptance condition |
| 7 | Language of DFA | L(M) = {w \in Σ* \mid δ̂(q₀, w) ∈ F} | Set notation |
| 8 | NFA transition relation | Δ: Q × Σ → 2^Q | Multiple next states |
| 9 | Complement language | L(Mᶜ) = Σ* \ L(M) | Swap F and Q \ F |
| 10 | DFA minimization states | Lower bound: \log_2(\vert L \vert + 1) | Theoretical minimum |
| 11 | Max length of test strings for distinguishing | 2^{\vert Q \vert} − 1 | Distinguishability bound |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DFAs are not merely academic toys. They are the computational backbone of:

- **Lexical Analyzers** in compilers (e.g., the `lex`/`flex` tool): tokens like identifiers, keywords, operators are recognized by DFAs.
- **Network Protocol Validators**: TCP state machines, HTTP request parsers.
- **Digital Circuit Design**: Hardware controllers, traffic light sequencers, elevator controllers.
- **Pattern Matching Engines**: `grep` with finite-state acceleration (Thompson's NFA → DFA construction).
- **Regular Expression Engines**: Every regex is compiled internally to a DFA (or DFA-like NFA) for fast matching.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A DFA can be implemented in hardware as a **Read-Only Memory (ROM)** indexed by (current_state, input_symbol). The ROM's output is the next state. This is exactly how many real-world finite-state controllers are built.

## 2.5 DFA Construction Methodology — Structured Logic Steps

When designing a DFA to accept a language L, follow this rigorous 5-step methodology (high-yield for KTU board problems):

1. **Identify the language L precisely** — write down what strings are accepted.
2. **Identify the "memory" requirements** — what must the DFA remember about the string read so far?
3. **Define states** — each state represents a specific piece of remembered information.
4. **Define transitions** — for every (state, symbol) pair, decide the next state.
5. **Mark initial and final states** — q₀ is the start; F is the set of states that correspond to "string accepted."

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — DFA Accepting Strings Ending in "01"

**Problem:** Construct a DFA that accepts the language L = {w ∈ {0,1}* | w ends in the substring "01"}.

**Step 1 — Identify L:** All binary strings whose last two symbols form "01". Empty string ε is *rejected* (does not end in "01").

**Step 2 — Identify memory needed:** We need to remember the *suffix* of the string read so far, specifically the last 0, 1, or 01.

**Step 3 — Define states:**

$$Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$$

- **q₀**: "Initial state / No relevant suffix yet"
- **q₁**: "Last symbol read was 0 (a potential start of '01')"
- **q₂**: "Last two symbols read are '01' (ACCEPT state)"

**Step 4 — Define transitions:**

| Current State | Input 0 | Input 1 |
|---------------|---------|---------|
| →q₀ (start) | q₁ | q₀ |
| q₁ | q₁ | q₂ |
| *q₂ (accept) | q₁ | q₀ |

**Step 5 — Mark initial and final states:**

$$q_0 = q_0, \quad F = \{q_2\}$$

**Verification — trace string w = "00101":**

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, 0) = q_1$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, 00) = \hat{\delta}(q_1, 0) = q_1$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, 001) = \hat{\delta}(q_1, 1) = q_2$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, 0010) = \hat{\delta}(q_2, 0) = q_1$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, 00101) = \hat{\delta}(q_1, 1) = q_2 \in F \;\; \checkmark \text{ ACCEPTED}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — DFA Accepting Strings with Even Number of 0s and Odd Number of 1s

**Problem:** Construct a DFA over Σ = {0, 1} that accepts all strings with an **even** number of 0s and an **odd** number of 1s.

**Step 1 — Identify L:** {w ∈ {0,1}* | #(0) is even AND #(1) is odd}.

**Step 2 — Identify memory needed:** Two independent parities — one for 0s, one for 1s. Each parity has 2 values → 2 × 2 = 4 combinations.

**Step 3 — Define states:**

$$Q = \{q_{00}, q_{01}, q_{10}, q_{11}\}$$

Naming convention: state name = (parity of 0s, parity of 1s) so:
- **q₀₀**: even 0s, even 1s (start)
- **q₀₁**: even 0s, odd 1s ← **this is our accept state**
- **q₁₀**: odd 0s, even 1s
- **q₁₁**: odd 0s, odd 1s

**Step 4 — Define transitions:**

When we read a **0**, we flip the 0-parity but keep the 1-parity.
When we read a **1**, we flip the 1-parity but keep the 0-parity.

This gives us the transition table:

| State | Input 0 | Input 1 |
|-------|---------|---------|
| →q₀₀ | q₁₀ | q₀₁ |
| *q₀₁ | q₁₁ | q₀₀ |
| q₁₀ | q₀₀ | q₁₁ |
| q₁₁ | q₀₁ | q₁₀ |

**Step 5 — Mark final states:**

$$F = \{q_{01}\}$$

**Verification — string w = "011" (two 0s = even ✓, two 1s = even ✗ should reject):**

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{00}, 0) = q_{10}$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{00}, 01) = \hat{\delta}(q_{10}, 1) = q_{11}$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{00}, 011) = \hat{\delta}(q_{11}, 1) = q_{10} \notin F \;\; \text{REJECTED} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Verification — string w = "101" (one 0 = odd ✗, two 1s = even ✗ should reject):**

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{00}, 1) = q_{01}$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{00}, 10) = \hat{\delta}(q_{01}, 0) = q_{11}$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{00}, 101) = \hat{\delta}(q_{11}, 1) = q_{10} \notin F \;\; \text{REJECTED} \;\; \checkmark$$

**Verification — string w = "11" (zero 0s = even ✓, two 1s = even ✗ should reject):**

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{00}, 1) = q_{01}$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_{00}, 11) = \hat{\delta}(q_{01}, 1) = q_{00} \notin F \;\; \text{REJECTED} \;\; \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Number of Distinct DFAs over a Given Alphabet

A KTU-favorite derivation. For |Σ| = k and |Q| = n states with a fixed initial state q₀ and a designated set of final states F, the number of *distinct* transition functions δ is:

$$N(\delta) = n^{(n \times k)}$$

**Derivation logic:**
- There are n × k cells in the transition table (one for each state-symbol pair).
- Each cell can be filled with any of the n states in Q.
- By the rule of product, total number of distinct transition tables is n^(n × k).

The number of *subsets* of Q that can serve as F is 2ⁿ (each state is independently in or out of F). So the total number of distinct DFAs with exactly n states and alphabet of size k is:

$$N(\text{DFA}) = n^{(n \cdot k)} \times 2^n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** This formula appears in KTU Module 1 questions frequently. Memorize: n^(nk) · 2ⁿ.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common DFA Design Patterns

| # | Language Pattern | # of States | Memory Required | State Count Formula |
|---|------------------|-------------|-----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Ends in fixed string s of length m | m + 1 | Last m−1 symbols | m + 1 |
| 2 | Contains substring s of length m | m + 1 | Longest suffix matching prefix of s | m + 1 |
| 3 | # of 0s divisible by n | n | 0-parity mod n | n |
| 4 | # of 0s even AND # of 1s odd | 4 | Two independent parities | 2 × 2 = 4 |
| 5 | Decimal number divisible by 3 | 3 | Remainder mod 3 | 3 |
| 6 | No two consecutive 1s | 3 | "Last symbol was 1?" | 3 |
| 7 | aⁿbⁿ for n ≥ 0 | Not regular → no DFA | Needs unbounded memory | ∞ (use PDA) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — DFA for Strings Ending in "01"

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> start
    start: q0
    start --> q1: 0
    start --> start: 1
    q1: q1
    q1 --> q1: 0
    q1 --> q2: 1
    q2: q2
    q2 --> q1: 0
    q2 --> start: 1
    q2 --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Double circle around **q2** indicates the accepting (final) state.
- Arrow from "nothing" to **q0** marks the initial state.
- Every state has **exactly one outgoing arrow per alphabet symbol** — the defining property of a DFA.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — DFA for Even 0s AND Odd 1s

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q00
    q00: q00
    q00 --> q10: 0
    q00 --> q01: 1
    q10: q10
    q10 --> q00: 0
    q10 --> q11: 1
    q01: q01
    q01 --> q11: 0
    q01 --> q00: 1
    q11: q11
    q11 --> q01: 0
    q11 --> q10: 1
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **q00** = start state (empty string: 0 zeros, 0 ones — both even).
- **q01** = accept state (the parity we want).
- Note the symmetric "flip" structure: every '0' arrow flips the first letter, every '1' arrow flips the second.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — DFA as a Hardware System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input String Buffer] --> B[Current State Register]
    B --> C[Combinational Logic: Transition Function Delta]
    D[Input Symbol Selector] --> C
    C --> E[Next State Value]
    E --> F[State Update Clock]
    F --> B
    B --> G{Is Current State in F?}
    G -- Yes --> H[ACCEPT Signal = 1]
    G -- No --> I[REJECT Signal = 1]
```

**Architecture Interpretation:**
- **Input String Buffer**: Holds the string symbol-by-symbol.
- **Current State Register**: A flip-flop bank storing the binary encoding of the current state.
- **Combinational Logic (δ)**: A ROM or PLA implementing the transition function. Given (state, symbol), outputs next state.
- **State Update Clock**: Synchronizes the transition (DFA advances one step per clock).
- **Acceptance Checker**: A simple comparator — if current state ∈ F, ACCEPT = 1, else REJECT = 1.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Connection:** This is precisely how a hardware-implemented DFA works. The combinational logic block IS the δ function. With |Q| = n and |Σ| = k, the ROM has n × k rows and outputs log₂(n) bits.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA). List the components of a DFA formally.**

**Model Answer:**

A DFA is a 5-tuple M = (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F) where:
- **Q** is a finite, non-empty set of states.
- **Σ** is a finite, non-empty input alphabet.
- **δ: Q × Σ → Q** is the transition function (total, deterministic).
- **q₀ ∈ Q** is the initial state.
- **F ⊆ Q** is the set of final (accepting) states.

A string w is accepted if δ̂(q₀, w) ∈ F.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for components, 1 for acceptance condition)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a DFA and an NFA. List any two differences.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | DFA | NFA |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Transition function | δ: Q × Σ → Q (single next state) | Δ: Q × Σ → 2^Q (set of next states) |
| Determinism | Always exactly one transition per (state, symbol) | Zero, one, or many transitions allowed |
| ε-transitions | Not allowed (in standard DFA) | Allowed (in ε-NFA) |
| Hardware realization | Direct (1-to-1) | Requires subset construction |
| Power | Both accept exactly the regular languages | Both accept exactly the regular languages |

*(3 marks: any two valid differences with explanation)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Construct a DFA that accepts the language L = {w ∈ {a, b}* | w contains "aba" as a substring}. Draw the transition diagram and provide the transition table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify L:** Strings over {a, b} that contain "aba" somewhere as a contiguous substring.

**Step 2 — Memory needed:** Track the longest suffix of the input that is also a prefix of "aba".

**Step 3 — States:**

$$Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3\}$$

- **q₀**: Initial state — no prefix of "aba" matched yet.
- **q₁**: Last symbol read was "a" (matched "a").
- **q₂**: Last two symbols were "ab" (matched "ab").
- **q₃**: Matched full "aba" — **ACCEPT state** (and stay here).

**Step 4 — Transition Table:**

| State | Input a | Input b |
|-------|---------|---------|
| → q₀ | q₁ | q₀ |
| q₁ | q₁ | q₂ |
| q₂ | q₃ | q₀ |
| * q₃ | q₃ | q₃ |

**Step 5 — Final State:** F = {q₃}

**Transition Diagram (described in text form):**
- q₀ --a--> q₁; q₀ --b--> q₀
- q₁ --a--> q₁; q₁ --b--> q₂
- q₂ --a--> q₃; q₂ --b--> q₀
- q₃ --a--> q₃; q₃ --b--> q₃

**Verification — w = "aaba":**

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, a) = q_1, \quad \hat{\delta}(q_0, aa) = q_1$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, aab) = \hat{\delta}(q_1, b) = q_2$$

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, aaba) = \hat{\delta}(q_2, a) = q_3 \in F \;\; \checkmark \text{ ACCEPTED}$$

**(7 marks breakdown:** 2 for state design, 2 for transition table, 2 for transition diagram, 1 for verification.)

---

**(b)** For a DFA with 4 states and alphabet Σ = {0, 1}, how many distinct DFAs are possible? Show the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** |Q| = 4, |Σ| = 2.

**Step 1 — Number of transition tables:**
The transition table has |Q| × |Σ| = 4 × 2 = 8 cells.
Each cell can take any of |Q| = 4 values.

$$N(\delta) = |Q|^{|Q| \times |\Sigma|} = 4^{4 \times 2} = 4^8$$

**Step 2 — Compute 4⁸:**

$$4^8 = (2^2)^8 = 2^{16} = 65536$$

**Step 3 — Number of possible final-state sets F:**
Each of the 4 states may be in F or not → 2⁴ = 16 subsets.

$$N(F) = 2^{|Q|} = 2^4 = 16$$

**Step 4 — Total number of distinct DFAs:**

$$N(\text{DFA}) = 4^8 \times 2^4 = 65536 \times 16 = 1048576 = 2^{20}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{N(\text{DFA}) = 4^{(4 \times 2)} \times 2^4 = 4^8 \times 16 = 2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576}$$

**(7 marks breakdown:** 2 for setup, 2 for transition table count, 2 for final-state count, 1 for final answer.)

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Construct a DFA over Σ = {0, 1} that accepts all strings with exactly **two 1s** (and any number of 0s). Provide the transition table and state diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify L:** {w ∈ {0, 1}* | #(1) = 2 exactly}.

**Step 2 — Memory needed:** Count of 1s seen so far, capped at 2 (0, 1, 2, or "more than 2" trap).

**Step 3 — States:**

$$Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_{dead}\}$$

- **q₀**: Zero 1s seen.
- **q₁**: Exactly one 1 seen.
- **q₂**: Exactly two 1s seen → **ACCEPT state**.
- **q_{dead}**: Three or more 1s seen (trap state, always rejected).

**Step 4 — Transition Table:**

| State | Input 0 | Input 1 |
|-------|---------|---------|
| → q₀ | q₀ | q₁ |
| q₁ | q₁ | q₂ |
| * q₂ | q₂ | q_{dead} |
| q_{dead} | q_{dead} | q_{dead} |

**Step 5 — Final State:** F = {q₂}

**Verification — w = "01100"** (two 1s ✓):

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, 0) = q_0 \to q_0 \to q_1 \to q_1 \to q_2 \to q_2 \in F \;\; \checkmark$$

**Verification — w = "01110"** (three 1s ✗):

$$\hat{\delta}(q_0, 0111) = \hat{\delta}(q_1, 11) = q_2 \to q_{dead} \notin F \;\; \text{REJECTED} \;\; \checkmark$$

**(7 marks breakdown:** 2 for states, 2 for table, 2 for diagram, 1 for verification.)

---

**(b)** Explain with an example why a DFA requires the transition function δ to be *total* (defined for every state-symbol pair). What happens if some transition is missing? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**The Total Transition Property:**

A DFA's transition function δ: Q × Σ → Q must be defined for **every** pair (q, a) where q ∈ Q and a ∈ Σ. This is the *totality* property.

**Why totality is essential:**

1. **Predictability:** A DFA must be able to process *any* input string. If δ(q, a) is undefined, then any string reaching state q and reading symbol a would cause the machine to "crash" — it has no defined behavior.

2. **Determinism Contract:** The "deterministic" guarantee to the outside world is: "for any input, I will always tell you the next state." A partial function violates this contract.

3. **Mathematical well-definedness:** The recursive definition of the extended transition function δ̂ requires that δ be total, otherwise δ̂(q, wa) is undefined whenever δ̂(q, w) = some state q' with δ(q', a) undefined.

**Example illustrating the problem:**

Consider a DFA over {a, b} with states {q₀, q₁}, q₀ initial, F = {q₁}, intended to accept strings starting with "a". Suppose we define:

- δ(q₀, a) = q₁
- δ(q₀, b) = **undefined** ← violation!

If the input string is "ba", the machine starts at q₀, reads 'b', and has no defined transition. The acceptance condition δ̂(q₀, "ba") cannot be evaluated. The language of the DFA is *not well-defined*.

**Resolution:** Add a "dead" or "trap" state q_d to Q, set F ∩ {q_d} = ∅, and define δ(q_d, a) = δ(q_d, b) = q_d. Then redirect all missing transitions to q_d. This restores totality.

**The Corrected DFA:**

| State | Input a | Input b |
|-------|---------|---------|
| → q₀ | q₁ | q_d |
| * q₁ | q₁ | q₁ |
| q_d | q_d | q_d |

Now δ is total, the DFA is well-defined, and L(M) = {w ∈ {a,b}* | w starts with 'a'} is correctly recognized.

**(7 marks breakdown:** 2 for explaining totality, 2 for the example, 2 for the resolution, 1 for final corrected DFA.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS in DFA questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the trap/dead state:** When constructing a DFA, students often leave transitions undefined. *Every* state must have *every* input symbol mapped. If a state would otherwise be "missing" a transition, add a non-accepting dead state. **(Lose 2-3 marks)**
> 
> 2. **Confusing δ and δ̂:** δ takes a state and a *single symbol*; δ̂ takes a state and a *string*. Examiners deduct marks if you write δ(q, "01") instead of δ̂(q, "01"). **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 
> 3. **Not justifying state design:** Simply listing states without explaining *what each state "remembers"* loses method marks. Always state: "State qᵢ represents..." **(Lose 1-2 marks)**
> 
> 4. **Missing the verification step:** A common KTU requirement is to trace 2-3 sample strings (one accepted, one rejected) to demonstrate correctness. Skipping this loses easy marks. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 
> 5. **Formula misapplication for counting DFAs:** The formula is n^(n·k) · 2ⁿ, not n^k · 2^n. Mixing these up is a frequent error. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 
> 6. **Wrong final state marking:** In diagrams, the accept state MUST be indicated (double circle or asterisk). In tables, F must be explicitly listed. **(Lose 1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — DFA (Module 1):**
> 
> - **DFA = 5-tuple (Q, Σ, δ, q₀, F)** — know every component cold.
> - **δ is total and deterministic:** δ: Q × Σ → Q (single state output, no holes).
> - **Acceptance:** w ∈ L(M) ⇔ δ̂(q₀, w) ∈ F.
> - **Extended δ recursion:** δ̂(q, ε) = q; δ̂(q, wa) = δ(δ̂(q, w), a).
> - **Transition table size:** |Q| × |Σ| entries.
> - **Counting distinct DFAs:** n^(n·k) · 2ⁿ, where n = |Q|, k = |Σ|.
> - **Trap state rule:** Always include a dead state for undefined transitions to make δ total.
> - **Verification is mandatory:** Trace at least one accepted and one rejected string in the answer.
> - **State naming convention:** Name states by what they "remember" (e.g., "last symbol", "parity", "count mod n").
> - **DFA vs NFA:** DFA → unique next state; NFA → set of next states. *Both* accept the regular languages.
> - **Complement trick:** To accept Lᶜ, keep the same DFA but swap F with Q \ F.
> - **Common design patterns:** Even-count DFAs need n states for divisibility by n; substring DFAs need (m+1) states for substring of length m.
> - **Engineered systems** using DFAs: lexical analyzers, hardware controllers, regex engines, network protocol state machines.

---

**End of Module 1 Topic: Deterministic Finite Automata (DFA) — KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regular Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Regular Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Regular Language

A language **L** is called a **regular language** if and only if it can be described by a **regular expression**, recognized by a **deterministic finite automaton (DFA)** or a **nondeterministic finite automaton (NFA)**, or generated by a **regular grammar**.

Formally, the class of regular languages over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is denoted as **REG**, and is defined inductively as the smallest class containing:
- The empty set $\emptyset$
- The empty string $\{\varepsilon\}$
- All singleton sets $\{a\}$ where $a \in \Sigma$

and closed under the operations of **union**, **concatenation**, and **Kleene star**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Paraphrase):** A language is *regular* if some finite automaton recognizes it. Equivalently, it can be expressed using a finite number of algebraic operations (union, concatenation, star) starting from atomic symbols.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Vending Machine"

Imagine a coin-operated **vending machine** with a finite number of states:
- `S0` = Idle (waiting for ₹5 coin)
- `S1` = One coin inserted
- `S2` = Two coins inserted
- `ACCEPT` = Item dispensed (accepting state)

The machine has **finite memory** (it does not count how many people came before). When you feed it a sequence of coins (your *input string*), it transitions between states according to a fixed rule book. If it ends in the `ACCEPT` state, the string of coins you inserted is considered a *valid* string.

This is precisely how a **DFA** works. The set of all coin-sequences that lead to `ACCEPT` is a **regular language**. The vending machine does not "remember history" beyond its current state — it is *finite* in memory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** Regular languages are exactly those languages that can be recognized by a machine with *no scratchpad, no counter, and no stack* — just a fixed number of internal states. This is the most restricted class in the Chomsky hierarchy.

---

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Regular Languages

A language is regular if and only if it has a representation in **any** of the following three equivalent forms:

| # | Representation | Tool/Object | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Descriptive** | Regular Expression (RE) | Algebraic notation for describing patterns |
| 2 | **Recognition (Deterministic)** | DFA | Reads input once, one symbol at a time, deterministically |
| 3 | **Recognition (Non-deterministic)** | NFA | May branch into multiple states simultaneously; accepts if *some* branch accepts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kleene's Theorem (S. C. Kleene, 1956):** A language **L** is regular **if and only if** there exists a **finite automaton** (DFA or NFA) that recognizes it. This is the foundational equivalence theorem in the theory of computation.

The standard metric: the **state count** $|\mathcal{Q}|$ of a DFA serves as the principal measure of complexity for the regular language it recognizes.

---

### 1.4 Quick Taxonomy of Languages in TOC

$$
\{\text{Regular Languages}\} \subsetneq \{\text{Context-Free Languages}\} \subsetneq \{\text{Context-Sensitive}\} \subsetneq \{\text{Recursively Enumerable}\}
$$

Regular languages occupy the **bottom-most rung** of the Chomsky hierarchy — they are the simplest, but also the most practically useful (lexical analysis in compilers, pattern matching in `grep`, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Formal Definition of a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA)

A **DFA** is a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where:
- $Q$ is a **finite** set of states
- $\Sigma$ is a **finite input alphabet** (input symbols)
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ is the **transition function** (deterministic, total)
- $q_0 \in Q$ is the **start state**
- $F \subseteq Q$ is the set of **accepting (final) states**

The **language accepted by M** is:
$$
L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid \delta^*(q_0, w) \in F \,\}
$$
where $\delta^* : Q \times \Sigma^* \to Q$ is the *extended* transition function defined inductively on string length.

---

### 2.2 Formal Definition of a Nondeterministic Finite Automaton (NFA)

An **NFA** is also a 5-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ but with one crucial difference:
- $\delta : Q \times \Sigma_\varepsilon \to 2^Q$ (transition maps to a *set* of states; $\varepsilon$-transitions allowed)

Formally, $\Sigma_\varepsilon = \Sigma \cup \{\varepsilon\}$, and the language accepted is:
$$
L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^* \mid \delta^*(q_0, w) \cap F \neq \emptyset \,\}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bother with NFAs?** NFAs are often exponentially more *concise* than their equivalent DFAs. For example, a pattern over $n$ alternatives can be described by an NFA with $n$ parallel branches, while the minimal DFA may require $2^n$ states. Algorithms like Thompson's construction for regex-to-NFA exploit this compactness.

---

### 2.3 Formal Definition of a Regular Expression (RE)

A regular expression **R** over alphabet $\Sigma$ is defined by the **inductive (recursive) rules**:

| Rule | Expression | Represents |
|---|---|---|
| **Base 1** | $\emptyset$ | $\emptyset$ (empty language) |
| **Base 2** | $\varepsilon$ | $\{\varepsilon\}$ |
| **Base 3** | $a$ (where $a \in \Sigma$) | $\{a\}$ |
| **Induction 1** | $R_1 \cup R_2$ | $L(R_1) \cup L(R_2)$ |
| **Induction 2** | $R_1 \circ R_2$ | $L(R_1) \cdot L(R_2)$ |
| **Induction 3** | $R^*$ | $L(R)^*$ (Kleene closure) |

Precedence (high → low): Kleene star $(*)$ → Concatenation $(\cdot)$ → Union $(\cup \mid \mid)$.

---

### 2.4 The Three Constructive Equivalences (Kleene's Theorem Proof Roadmap)

The standard textbook proof of Kleene's Theorem shows three constructive conversions:

1. **RE → NFA** via **Thompson's Construction**: $O(n)$ states, where $n$ is the length of the RE.
2. **NFA → DFA** via **Subset Construction**: Produces a DFA with at most $2^n$ states (for an NFA with $n$ states).
3. **DFA → RE** via **State Elimination** or **GNFA Conversion**: yields a single RE.

These three together establish:
$$
L \text{ is regular} \iff L = L(R) \text{ for some RE } R \iff L = L(M) \text{ for some DFA/NFA } M
$$

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Key Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Kleene Star** | $A^* = \bigcup_{i=0}^{\infty} A^i$ | Generating zero or more concatenations |
| 2 | **Plus Operator** | $A^+ = A \cdot A^* = \bigcup_{i=1}^{\infty} A^i$ | One or more concatenations |
| 3 | **NFA → DFA size** | $\vert Q_{DFA} \vert \le 2^{\vert Q_{NFA} \vert}$ | Worst-case state blow-up bound |
| 4 | **Subset Construction** | $D$-state $=$ subset of $N$-states | Convert NFA to equivalent DFA |
| 5 | **DFA to RE (State Elim.)** | $R_{ij}^{(k)}$ generalized state-remove formula | $R_{ij}^{(k)} = R_{ij}^{(k-1)} \mid R_{ik}^{(k-1)}(R_{kk}^{(k-1)})^* R_{kj}^{(k-1)}$ |
| 6 | **Pumping Lemma (Negation)** | If $\forall n, \exists w \in L, \vert w \ge n, w = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \le n, \vert y \ge 1, xy^kz \notin L$ for some $k \ge 0$, then $L$ is **not** regular | **Proves non-regularity** |
| 7 | **Myhill-Nerode** | # of equivalence classes of $\Sigma^*$ modulo $L$ = # of DFA states | Gives minimal DFA size |
| 8 | **Closure under $\cup$** | $L_1, L_2$ regular $\Rightarrow L_1 \cup L_2$ regular | Construct DFA via product or new start with $\varepsilon$ |
| 9 | **Closure under $\cap$** | $L_1, L_2$ regular $\Rightarrow L_1 \cap L_2$ regular | Product construction $\delta'(q_1, q_2, a) = (\delta_1(q_1, a), \delta_2(q_2, a))$ |
| 10 | **Closure under complement** | $L$ regular $\Rightarrow \overline{L} = \Sigma^* \setminus L$ regular | Swap accepting/non-accepting states |
| 11 | **Closure under reversal** | $L$ regular $\Rightarrow L^R$ regular | Reverse the automaton / reverse RE |
| 12 | **Closure under $\cdot$** | $L_1, L_2$ regular $\Rightarrow L_1 L_2$ regular | NFA concatenation by $\varepsilon$-transition |
| 13 | **Closure under $*$** | $L$ regular $\Rightarrow L^*$ regular | Add $\varepsilon$-loop on new start/accept |

---

### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Lexical Analyzers (Compilers):** Tools like Lex/Flex use regular expressions to tokenize source code (identifiers, keywords, numbers).
- **Network Protocol Design:** Finite automata are used to validate packet headers (TCP state machine).
- **Pattern Matching Utilities:** `grep`, `awk`, and search engines use DFA-based regex engines.
- **Hardware Design:** Sequential circuits (flip-flops + combinational logic) are formally modeled as finite state machines.
- **Model Checking:** Tools like SPIN verify finite-state concurrent systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Walkthrough: NFA → DFA via Subset Construction

Consider the following **NFA** $N$ over $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$:

- States: $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- Start: $q_0$
- Accept: $F = \{q_2\}$
- Transitions:
  - $\delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0\}$
  - $\delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\}$
  - $\delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_2\}$
  - $\delta(q_2, 0) = \{q_2\}$
  - $\delta(q_2, 1) = \{q_2\}$

This NFA accepts strings ending in `1` followed by an even-length run of `0`s (or equivalent). We will convert it to a DFA.

#### Step 1: Identify all subsets of $Q$

The set of all subsets (the power set) of $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ is:
$$
2^Q = \{\emptyset, \{q_0\}, \{q_1\}, \{q_2\}, \{q_0, q_1\}, \{q_0, q_2\}, \{q_1, q_2\}, \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}\}
$$

#### Step 2: Compute $\varepsilon$-closure (here, all are $\emptyset$ since no $\varepsilon$-transitions)

Since $N$ has no $\varepsilon$-moves, $\varepsilon$-closure of any set is the set itself.

#### Step 3: Determine the DFA start state

$$
q_{D,0} = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{q_0\}) = \{q_0\}
$$

#### Step 4: Compute transitions from $\{q_0\}$

For each input symbol $a \in \Sigma$, compute:
$$
\delta_{D}(\{q_0\}, a) = \bigcup_{q \in \{q_0\}} \delta(q, a)
$$

For $a = 0$:
$$
\delta_{D}(\{q_0\}, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) = \{q_0\}
$$

For $a = 1$:
$$
\delta_{D}(\{q_0\}, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\}
$$

#### Step 5: Compute transitions from the new state $\{q_0, q_1\}$

For $a = 0$:
$$
\delta_{D}(\{q_0, q_1\}, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0) = \{q_0\} \cup \emptyset = \{q_0\}
$$

For $a = 1$:
$$
\delta_{D}(\{q_0, q_1\}, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}
$$

#### Step 6: Compute transitions from $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$

For $a = 0$:
$$
\delta_{D}(\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_1, 0) \cup \delta(q_2, 0) = \{q_0\} \cup \emptyset \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}
$$

For $a = 1$:
$$
\delta_{D}(\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_1, 1) \cup \delta(q_2, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}
$$

#### Step 7: Compute transitions from $\{q_0, q_2\}$

For $a = 0$:
$$
\delta_{D}(\{q_0, q_2\}, 0) = \delta(q_0, 0) \cup \delta(q_2, 0) = \{q_0\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_2\}
$$

For $a = 1$:
$$
\delta_{D}(\{q_0, q_2\}, 1) = \delta(q_0, 1) \cup \delta(q_2, 1) = \{q_0, q_1\} \cup \{q_2\} = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}
$$

#### Step 8: Identify accepting DFA states

A DFA state is an accepting state iff it contains at least one accepting state of the NFA. Since $F_N = \{q_2\}$, the accepting DFA states are:
$$
F_D = \{\{q_2\}, \{q_0, q_2\}, \{q_1, q_2\}, \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}\}
$$

States $\{q_2\}$ and $\{q_1, q_2\}$ are unreachable from the start state $\{q_0\}$, so the **minimal reachable DFA** has:
- States: $\{q_0\}$, $\{q_0, q_1\}$, $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$, $\{q_0, q_2\}$
- Start: $\{q_0\}$
- Accept: $\{q_0, q_2\}$, $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$

---

### 3.2 Detailed Walkthrough: Pumping Lemma Proof That $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \ge 0\}$ Is **Not** Regular

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pumping Lemma for Regular Languages:** If $L$ is regular, then there exists a constant $p$ (the *pumping length*) such that every string $w \in L$ with $\vert w \vert \ge p$ can be decomposed as $w = xyz$ satisfying:
> 1. $\vert xy \vert \le p$
> 2. $\vert y \vert \ge 1$
> 3. $xy^k z \in L$ for all $k \ge 0$

**Strategy for proving non-regularity:** Assume $L$ is regular, derive a contradiction by *pumping* a specific witness string to produce a string that *cannot* be in $L$.

#### Step 1: Assume the contrary

Assume $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \ge 0\}$ is regular. Then by the Pumping Lemma, there exists a pumping length $p \in \mathbb{N}$.

#### Step 2: Choose a witness string

Choose the string $w = a^p b^p$. Clearly $w \in L$ and $\vert w \vert = 2p \ge p$.

#### Step 3: Apply the decomposition

By the Pumping Lemma, $w = xyz$ with $\vert xy \vert \le p$ and $\vert y \ge 1 \vert$.

Since $\vert xy \vert \le p$ and $w$ begins with $p$ a's, the substring $xy$ lies entirely within the leading $a$-block. Thus $y$ consists of only $a$'s:
$$
x = a^i, \quad y = a^j, \quad z = a^{p-i-j} b^p
$$
where $i \ge 0$, $j \ge 1$, and $i + j \le p$.

#### Step 4: Pump down (set $k = 0$)

Consider $xy^0 z = xz = a^{p-j} b^p$.

The number of $a$'s is now $p - j < p$ (since $j \ge 1$), but the number of $b$'s is still $p$. Hence:
$$
xy^0 z = a^{p-j} b^p \notin L
$$
because $a$-count $\neq$ $b$-count.

#### Step 5: Reach the contradiction

This contradicts condition (3) of the Pumping Lemma, which requires $xy^k z \in L$ for *all* $k \ge 0$.

#### Step 6: Conclude

Therefore, our assumption is false, and $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \ge 0\}$ is **not** a regular language. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DFA Minimization (Table-Filling Algorithm)

For the DFA constructed in §3.1, the equivalence classes (under indistinguishability) are:

| DFA State | Contains $q_2$? | Class |
|---|---|---|
| $\{q_0\}$ | No | Non-accepting |
| $\{q_0, q_1\}$ | No | Non-accepting |
| $\{q_0, q_2\}$ | Yes | Accepting |
| $\{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$ | Yes | Accepting |

This DFA is already minimal (4 states). The Myhill-Nerode theorem guarantees that this is the smallest possible DFA for $L$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** The *size* of the minimal DFA equals the number of equivalence classes of strings under the indistinguishability relation $\sim_L$ (Myhill-Nerode), providing a precise measure of the language's intrinsic complexity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture: The Three Equivalent Representations of Regular Languages

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Regular Expression RE] -->|Thompson Construction| B[NFA]
    B -->|Subset Construction| C[DFA]
    C -->|State Elimination| A
    
    D[Regular Grammar] -->|Left Linear to DFA| C
    D -->|Right Linear to NFA| B
    
    E[Language L] -->|Is Regular?| F{Pumping Lemma Test}
    F -->|Passes| C
    F -->|Fails| G[Context Free or Higher]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5e1,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style B fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#ef6c00,color:#000
    style C fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000
    style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    style E fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457,color:#000
    style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style G fill:#ffebee,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
```

---

### 4.2 DFA Diagram: Accepting Strings Ending in `01`

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts a minimal DFA with 3 states that accepts all binary strings ending in `01`:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s0((q0)) -->|0| s0
    s0 -->|1| s1((q1))
    s1 -->|0| s2(((q2)))
    s1 -->|1| s1
    s2 -->|0| s2
    s2 -->|1| s1
    
    style s0 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style s1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style s2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `q0` (start) loops on `0`; on reading a `1` it moves to `q1` (just saw the start of a candidate).
- `q1` on reading `0` moves to the accept state `q2`; on reading `1` it stays in `q1` (still "just saw a 1").
- `q2` (accept) on reading `0` stays in `q2` (string still ends in `0`); on `1` goes back to `q1`.

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Regex-to-Minimal-DFA Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Regular Expression R] --> B[Phase 1: Parse RE to AST]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Thompson Construction to NFA]
    C --> D[Phase 3: Subset Construction to DFA]
    D --> E[Phase 4: Remove Unreachable States]
    E --> F[Phase 5: Hopcroft DFA Minimization]
    F --> G[Phase 6: Final Minimal DFA]
    G --> H[Use: Lexical Analysis / Pattern Matching]
    
    style A fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style B fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    style C fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000
    style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#6a1b9a,color:#000
    style E fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#ad1457,color:#000
    style F fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
    style H fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pipeline Notes:** Each phase is a *polynomial-time* transformation. Thompson's construction produces an NFA with $O(n)$ states for an RE of length $n$. Subset construction yields a DFA with up to $2^n$ states. Hopcroft's minimization runs in $O(n \log n)$ for an $n$-state DFA — guaranteeing the smallest equivalent DFA.

---

### 4.4 Chomsky Hierarchy Position

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0["L0: Recursively Enumerable (TM)"]
    L1["L1: Context-Sensitive (LBA)"]
    L2["L2: Context-Free (PDA)"]
    L3["L3: Regular (DFA / NFA / RE)"]
    
    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    
    style L0 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000
    style L1 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000
    style L2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style L3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a regular language. State Kleene's Theorem.*

**Model Answer:**

A language $L$ over an alphabet $\Sigma$ is called a **regular language** if it can be recognized by some finite automaton (DFA or NFA), equivalently described by a regular expression, or generated by a regular grammar.

**Kleene's Theorem:** A language $L$ is regular if and only if there exists a finite automaton $M$ such that $L = L(M)$.

The theorem establishes the equivalence of three formalisms: **regular expressions, finite automata, and regular grammars**. **[3 Marks]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between a DFA and an NFA. Can every NFA be converted to an equivalent DFA? Justify.*

**Model Answer:**

| Property | DFA | NFA |
|---|---|---|
| Transition function | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ (single state) | $\delta : Q \times \Sigma_\varepsilon \to 2^Q$ (set of states) |
| $\varepsilon$-transitions | Not allowed | Allowed |
| Determinism | Total, deterministic | Non-deterministic |
| Backtracking | Not needed | Required (or subset simulation) |

**Yes**, every NFA can be converted to an equivalent DFA using the **Subset Construction** algorithm. The resulting DFA has at most $2^{|Q|}$ states, where $|Q|$ is the number of states in the NFA. The DFA and NFA accept exactly the same language. **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Dec 2023 Pattern]`

**(a)** Construct a DFA that accepts all binary strings that contain the substring `01` at least once. Show the state diagram and the transition table. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Prove using the Pumping Lemma that the language $L = \{ww \mid w \in \{a, b\}^*\}$ (the set of even-length palindromes formed by repetition) is not regular. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

We construct a DFA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \delta, q_0, F)$ where:
- $\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2\}$
- $q_0$ = start state
- $F = \{q_2\}$ (we have seen `01`)

**Transition Table:**

| State | Input `0` | Input `1` |
|---|---|---|
| $\to q_0$ | $q_0$ | $q_1$ |
| $q_1$ | $q_2$ | $q_1$ |
| $*q_2$ | $q_2$ | $q_2$ |

**Intuition of each state:**
- $q_0$: Start state — we have not yet seen a `1` (or have only seen `0`s).
- $q_1$: We have just seen a `1` — possibly the start of a `01` substring.
- $q_2$ (accept): We have already observed the substring `01` somewhere.

**State Diagram (text representation):**

```
       0              0,1
  ┌──────┐         ┌──────┐
  ▼      │         ▼      │
→(q0)──1─→(q1)──0─→((q2))─┐
  ▲                          │
  └──────────────────────────┘
```

From $q_1$: on input `1`, stay in $q_1$ (a new `1` could start a fresh candidate). On input `0`, go to $q_2$ (we have just observed `01`).

Once in $q_2$, stay there for any further input — the substring `01` has already been observed.

**Test Cases:**
- `01` → $q_0 \to q_1 \to q_2$ ✓ Accept
- `1101` → $q_0 \to q_1 \to q_1 \to q_1 \to q_2$ ✓ Accept
- `111` → $q_0 \to q_1 \to q_1 \to q_1$ ✗ Reject
- `ε` → $q_0$ ✗ Reject

**DFA States = 3, which is the minimum by Myhill-Nerode.**  **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

We want to prove that $L = \{ww \mid w \in \{a, b\}^*\}$ is **not regular** using the Pumping Lemma.

> [!NOTE]
> *Note:* The language $L$ is the set of all strings that can be written as some string concatenated with itself — these are the "doubled" strings. Examples: `aa`, `abab`, `babbab`, `ε`.

**Step 1: Assume for contradiction** that $L$ is regular. By the Pumping Lemma, there exists a pumping length $p \ge 1$.

**Step 2: Choose the witness string.** Let
$$
w = a^p b \, a^p b
$$
This is in $L$ since it is $(a^p b)$ concatenated with itself. The length is $|w| = 2(p+1) \ge p$.

**Step 3: Apply decomposition.** By the Pumping Lemma, write $w = xyz$ with:
- $|xy| \le p$
- $|y| \ge 1$
- $xy^k z \in L$ for all $k \ge 0$

Since $|xy| \le p$ and $w$ begins with $p$ a's, the substring $y$ consists entirely of $a$'s. Specifically:
$$
x = a^i, \quad y = a^j, \quad z = a^{p-i-j} b \, a^p b
$$
with $i \ge 0$, $j \ge 1$, and $i + j \le p$.

**Step 4: Pump with $k = 2$.** Consider
$$
xy^2 z = a^{i} a^{2j} a^{p-i-j} b \, a^p b = a^{p+j} b \, a^p b
$$
This string has a prefix of $p + j$ a's (where $j \ge 1$) followed by `b`. The first half of any string in $L$ has the same length as the second half. Here the total length is $2p + 2j + 2$, so each "half" should be $p + j + 1$ characters long.

The first $p + j + 1$ characters of $xy^2 z$ are $a^{p+j} b$. For $xy^2 z$ to be in $L = \{u u \mid u \in \Sigma^*\}$, the first $p + j + 1$ characters must equal the last $p + j + 1$ characters.

The last $p + j + 1$ characters are $b \, a^{p} b$ (only the final $p + j + 1$ characters). For these to be identical to $a^{p+j} b$, we would need:
- Position 1 of left half = `a`, Position 1 of right half = `b`. **Contradiction.**

Hence $xy^2 z \notin L$, contradicting the Pumping Lemma.

**Step 5: Conclusion.** $L$ is not regular. $\blacksquare$ **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[Module Internal Choice — KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Convert the following NFA to an equivalent DFA using the subset construction algorithm. Show all intermediate steps.

**NFA Specification:**
- States: $\{p, q, r, s\}$
- Start: $p$
- Accept: $\{s\}$
- Transitions:
  - $\delta(p, 0) = \{p, q\}$
  - $\delta(p, 1) = \{p\}$
  - $\delta(q, 1) = \{r\}$
  - $\delta(r, 0) = \{s\}$
  - $\delta(s, 0) = \{s\}$
  - $\delta(s, 1) = \{s\}$

**\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** State and prove the **closure properties** of regular languages under **union, concatenation, and Kleene star**. Provide a constructive proof using NFAs. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1: List the start state.**
$$
A_0 = \varepsilon\text{-closure}(\{p\}) = \{p\} \quad \text{(no $\varepsilon$-moves)}
$$

**Step 2: Compute $\delta_{D}(A_0, 0)$ and $\delta_{D}(A_0, 1)$.**

- $\delta_{D}(\{p\}, 0) = \delta(p, 0) = \{p, q\}$ → call this $A_1$
- $\delta_{D}(\{p\}, 1) = \delta(p, 1) = \{p\}$ → $A_0$ (already known)

**Step 3: Compute transitions from $A_1 = \{p, q\}$.**

- $\delta_{D}(\{p, q\}, 0) = \delta(p, 0) \cup \delta(q, 0) = \{p, q\} \cup \emptyset = \{p, q\}$ → $A_1$
- $\delta_{D}(\{p, q\}, 1) = \delta(p, 1) \cup \delta(q, 1) = \{p\} \cup \{r\} = \{p, r\}$ → call this $A_2$

**Step 4: Compute transitions from $A_2 = \{p, r\}$.**

- $\delta_{D}(\{p, r\}, 0) = \delta(p, 0) \cup \delta(r, 0) = \{p, q\} \cup \{s\} = \{p, q, s\}$ → call this $A_3$
- $\delta_{D}(\{p, r\}, 1) = \delta(p, 1) \cup \delta(r, 1) = \{p\} \cup \emptyset = \{p\}$ → $A_0$

**Step 5: Compute transitions from $A_3 = \{p, q, s\}$.**

- $\delta_{D}(\{p, q, s\}, 0) = \{p, q\} \cup \emptyset \cup \{s\} = \{p, q, s\}$ → $A_3$
- $\delta_{D}(\{p, q, s\}, 1) = \{p\} \cup \{r\} \cup \{s\} = \{p, r, s\}$ → call this $A_4$

**Step 6: Compute transitions from $A_4 = \{p, r, s\}$.**

- $\delta_{D}(\{p, r, s\}, 0) = \{p, q\} \cup \{s\} \cup \{s\} = \{p, q, s\}$ → $A_3$
- $\delta_{D}(\{p, r, s\}, 1) = \{p\} \cup \emptyset \cup \{s\} = \{p, s\}$ → call this $A_5$

**Step 7: Compute transitions from $A_5 = \{p, s\}$.**

- $\delta_{D}(\{p, s\}, 0) = \{p, q\} \cup \{s\} = \{p, q, s\}$ → $A_3$
- $\delta_{D}(\{p, s\}, 1) = \{p\} \cup \{s\} = \{p, s\}$ → $A_5$

**Step 8: Identify accept states.** Since the NFA's accept set is $\{s\}$, any DFA state containing $s$ is accepting:
$$
F_{D} = \{A_3, A_4, A_5\}
$$

**Final DFA Transition Table:**

| DFA State | `0` | `1` | Accept? |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\to A_0 = \{p\}$ | $A_1$ | $A_0$ | No |
| $A_1 = \{p, q\}$ | $A_1$ | $A_2$ | No |
| $A_2 = \{p, r\}$ | $A_3$ | $A_0$ | No |
| $A_3 = \{p, q, s\}$ | $A_3$ | $A_4$ | **Yes** |
| $A_4 = \{p, r, s\}$ | $A_3$ | $A_5$ | **Yes** |
| $A_5 = \{p, s\}$ | $A_3$ | $A_5$ | **Yes** |

**Number of reachable states: 6.** (The full power set would have $2^4 = 16$ states, but only 6 are reachable.) **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Theorem:** If $L_1$ and $L_2$ are regular languages, then $L_1 \cup L_2$, $L_1 L_2$, and $L_1^*$ are also regular.

**Proof by construction using NFAs:**

Let $N_1 = (Q_1, \Sigma, \delta_1, q_1, F_1)$ recognize $L_1$ and $N_2 = (Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_2, q_2, F_2)$ recognize $L_2$. Assume $Q_1 \cap Q_2 = \emptyset$ (rename states if needed).

---

**Closure under Union:** Construct $N_{\cup} = (Q_1 \cup Q_2 \cup \{q_0\}, \Sigma, \delta_{\cup}, q_0, F_1 \cup F_2)$ where:
$$
\delta_{\cup}(q, a) = \begin{cases} \delta_1(q, a) & q \in Q_1 \\ \delta_2(q, a) & q \in Q_2 \\ \{q_1, q_2\} & q = q_0, a = \varepsilon \\ \emptyset & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

A string $w$ is accepted by $N_{\cup}$ iff $N_{\cup}$ can nondeterministically branch via $\varepsilon$ to either $N_1$ or $N_2$ and be accepted there. So $L(N_{\cup}) = L_1 \cup L_2$. $\square$

---

**Closure under Concatenation:** Construct $N_{\cdot} = (Q_1 \cup Q_2, \Sigma, \delta_{\cdot}, q_1, F_2)$ where:
$$
\delta_{\cdot}(q, a) = \begin{cases} \delta_1(q, a) & q \in Q_1 \setminus F_1 \\ \delta_1(q, a) & q \in F_1, a \neq \varepsilon \\ \delta_1(q, a) \cup \{q_2\} & q \in F_1, a = \varepsilon \\ \delta_2(q, a) & q \in Q_2 \end{cases}
$$

The idea: on reaching any accept state of $N_1$, the automaton may nondeterministically *jump* via $\varepsilon$ to the start of $N_2$. Thus $w = w_1 w_2 \in L_1 L_2$ iff $w_1$ is accepted by $N_1$ and $w_2$ by $N_2$. $\square$

---

**Closure under Kleene Star:** Construct $N_{*} = (Q_1 \cup \{q_0\}, \Sigma, \delta_{*}, q_0, F_1)$ where:
$$
\delta_{*}(q, a) = \begin{cases} \delta_1(q, a) & q \in Q_1 \setminus F_1 \\ \delta_1(q, a) & q \in F_1, a \neq \varepsilon \\ \delta_1(q, a) \cup \{q_0\} & q \in F_1, a = \varepsilon \\ \{q_0\} & q = q_0, a = \varepsilon \\ \emptyset & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The new start state $q_0$ is accepting (accepts the empty string) and has an $\varepsilon$-transition to the original start of $N_1$. From any final state, $\varepsilon$-transitions loop back to $q_0$, allowing arbitrarily many repetitions. Hence $L(N_{*}) = L_1^*$. $\square$

This completes the proof of closure of regular languages under union, concatenation, and Kleene star. **[7 Marks]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the Pumping Lemma is a *one-way* test** — It proves non-regularity, never regularity. If a string *can* be pumped, you have not yet shown the language is regular.
> 2. **Wrong decomposition in Pumping Lemma** — The condition $|xy| \le p$ (NOT $|y| \le p$) is the most often-violated constraint. Students frequently let $y$ straddle the first and second halves of the witness string, which is incorrect.
> 3. **Confusing DFA and NFA transitions** — In a DFA, every state must have *exactly one* transition per input symbol. Missing transitions are a common deduction point.
> 4. **Subset construction incomplete** — Always check whether all reachable subsets have been enumerated. A "missing state" can cost up to 2 marks.
> 5. **Closure proof missing $\varepsilon$-jumps** — When proving closure under concatenation, students often forget to add the $\varepsilon$-transition from $F_1$ to the start of $N_2$.
> 6. **State minimization using wrong algorithm** — Use the table-filling method (Hopcroft's) properly: mark all pairs (accept, non-accept) as distinguishable *first*, then iterate.
> 7. **DFA to RE skipping the GNFA intermediate** — Always introduce a Generalized NFA (GNFA) with single start/accept and numbered states $q_{start}, q_{accept}, 1, 2, \ldots$ before eliminating states.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A language is **regular** iff recognized by a DFA/NFA, describable by a regular expression, or generable by a regular grammar (Kleene's Theorem).
- **DFA vs NFA:** DFAs are deterministic; NFAs allow $\varepsilon$-moves and branching. Every NFA is convertible to an equivalent DFA via **subset construction** (size: $\le 2^{|Q|}$).
- **RE Operations:** Union ($\cup$), Concatenation ($\cdot$), Kleene Star ($*$) — precedence: $* > \cdot > \cup$.
- **Thompson's Construction:** Converts an RE of length $n$ into an NFA with $O(n)$ states in linear time.
- **State Elimination:** Converts a DFA to a single Regular Expression by sequentially removing states.
- **Pumping Lemma:** If $L$ is regular, *long* strings in $L$ have a "pumpable" middle. **Use it to prove non-regularity.**
- **Myhill-Nerode Theorem:** The number of DFA states in the *minimal* DFA equals the number of equivalence classes of strings under $L$-indistinguishability.
- **Closure Properties:** Regular languages are closed under $\cup, \cap, \cdot, *, \text{complement}, \text{reversal}$. They are **NOT** closed under arbitrary subset or under "shuffle" in general.
- **Decision Problems Solvable for Regular Languages:** Emptiness, Finiteness, Equivalence, Membership — all decidable in polynomial time.
- **Canonical Examples of Non-Regular Languages:** $L = \{a^n b^n\}$, $L = \{ww\}$, $L = \{a^{n^2}\}$, $L = \{w \mid w = w^R\}$ (palindromes), $L = \{a^p \mid p \text{ prime}\}$.
- **Engineering Applications:** Lexical analyzers (Lex), pattern matching (grep, awk), hardware design (sequential circuits), network protocol verification, model checking.
- **Common KTU Exam Traps:** Watch for $|xy| \le p$ (not $|y|$), distinguish "pump up" vs "pump down" strategies, and always check that the witness string is in $L$.
- **State Minimization Algorithms:** Table-filling (Hopcroft) and Partition Refinement — both run in $O(|Q| \log |Q|)$ or $O(|Q|^2 |\Sigma|)$ time.
- **DFA Acceptance:** A string $w$ is accepted if $\delta^*(q_0, w) \in F$ — i.e., the computation *halts in* an accepting state after consuming all of $w$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
