- **Multi-step derivation:** $\Rightarrow^{*}$ denotes zero or more applications of $\Rightarrow$.
- **Language generated:** $L(G) = \{w \in T^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w\}$.
- **Leftmost derivation $\Rightarrow_{lm}$:** Always replace the **leftmost** variable.
- **Rightmost derivation $\Rightarrow_{rm}$:** Always replace the **rightmost** variable.
- **Epsilon production:** $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is permitted and is critical for languages that include the empty string.
- **"$\mid$" notation:** Compact representation of multiple productions with the same LHS.
- **Kleene star $(V \cup T)^{*}$:** Includes $\varepsilon$ and all finite strings over the alphabet.
- **Parse tree yield:** Reading leaves left-to-right must produce the derived string — a key verification step.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Designing Context-Free Grammars
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Designing Context-Free Grammars

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Context-Free Grammar (CFG)

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a formal mathematical tool used in the theory of computation to describe the syntax of programming languages and natural languages. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PCCST302 – Theory of Computation**, a CFG is formally defined as a 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$$

where:
- $V$ — A finite set of **variables** (also called *non-terminals* or *syntactic categories*).
- $\Sigma$ — A finite set of **terminals** (the alphabet of the language, disjoint from $V$, i.e., $V \cap \Sigma = \emptyset$).
- $R$ — A finite set of **rules** (or *productions*) of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$, where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup \Sigma)^{*}$.
- $S \in V$ — A designated **start variable** from which all derivations in the grammar begin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — "Context-Free" Property**
> A grammar is "context-free" because the left-hand side of every production consists of a *single* non-terminal symbol. This means the rule $A \rightarrow \alpha$ can be applied *regardless of the context* in which $A$ appears in the sentential form. The right-hand side is replaced independently of its surrounding symbols.

The **language generated by a CFG** $G$, denoted $L(G)$, is the set of all strings of terminals that can be derived from the start symbol $S$:

$$L(G) = \{ w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w \}$$

where $\Rightarrow^{*}$ denotes the reflexive-transitive closure of the derivation relation $\Rightarrow$.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The "Recipe Book" View of a CFG**
> Imagine you are a chef trying to teach a robot to cook any dish. You cannot list every possible dish (that would be infinite). Instead, you write a **recipe book** containing rules like:
> - "A *Meal* can be made from a *Main Course* followed by a *Dessert*."
> - "A *Dessert* can be an *Ice Cream* or a *Cake*."
> - "A *Cake* can be the word *chocolate-cake*."
>
> The robot starts by reading the chapter titled "**Meal**" and follows the rules step-by-step until only real food names remain. The final dish (a sequence of real food names) is what the recipe book can produce.
>
> In this analogy:
> - **Variables (V)** = Chapter titles like "Meal", "Main Course" — abstract placeholders.
> - **Terminals ($\Sigma$)** = Real food names like "chocolate-cake" — concrete, indivisible ingredients.
> - **Rules (R)** = The recipe instructions — how to expand placeholders.
> - **Start Symbol (S)** = The cover page — where the robot begins reading.
> - **The Language (L(G))** = The complete menu of all cookable dishes.

This is exactly how a CFG works. It is a *generative* system: starting from $S$, you repeatedly replace variables using the rules until only terminals remain. Any terminal string you can produce this way belongs to the language.

---

### 1.3 Why Designing CFGs is Important

In the KTU 2024 Scheme curriculum, designing CFGs bridges **abstract language theory** with **practical applications** such as:
- Defining the **syntax of programming languages** (e.g., the grammar of C, Java, Python).
- Building **compilers and parsers** (YACC, ANTLR, Bison all use CFG-based specifications).
- Modeling **structured data formats** (JSON, XML, HTML).
- Specifying **query languages** (SQL grammar, XPath).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A well-designed CFG must be **correct** (generate *exactly* the intended language — no more, no less) and **unambiguous** where possible (each string has a unique parse tree). The design process requires creativity, pattern recognition, and rigorous mathematical verification.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Canonical Strategies for Designing CFGs

Designing a CFG for a given language $L$ is fundamentally a **pattern-recognition task**. Over decades of formal language research, six recurring structural patterns have been identified that cover virtually every CFL encountered in KTU examinations. Mastering these patterns guarantees success.

#### Strategy 1: Matching / Balanced Patterns (The "$a^{n}b^{n}$" Family)
**When to use:** The language requires a *count-based relationship* between two symbols (e.g., same number of $a$'s as $b$'s, balanced parentheses, matching tags).

**Core Production Template:**
$$S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$$

**Why it works:** The recursive rule $S \rightarrow aSb$ "stacks" an $a$ on the left and a $b$ on the right simultaneously, ensuring perfect pairing. The base case $\varepsilon$ terminates the recursion.

**Generalizations:**
- $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ requires *two* interdependent counters — a classic KTU 14-mark problem (see Section 5).
- $L = \{a^{i}b^{j} \mid i = 2j\}$ requires a *ratio-based* relationship.

---

#### Strategy 2: Palindromes and Symmetric Structures
**When to use:** The language consists of strings that read the same forwards and backwards, or have symmetric structure.

**Core Production Template:**
$$S \rightarrow aSa \mid bSb \mid a \mid b \mid \varepsilon$$

**Why it works:** Each recursive step adds matching symbols to *both ends*, preserving symmetry. The base cases produce single-character palindromes or the empty palindrome.

---

#### Strategy 3: Union and Concatenation
**When to use:** The language is described as a *union* of simpler languages or as a *concatenation* of structured parts.

**Core Production Template:**
$$S \rightarrow S_{1} \mid S_{2}$$
$$S \rightarrow S_{1} \, S_{2}$$

**KTU Tip:** If $L = L_{1} \cup L_{2}$, design grammars $G_{1}$ and $G_{2}$ for each piece, then add $S \rightarrow S_{1} \mid S_{2}$.

---

#### Strategy 4: Prefix/Suffix Addition
**When to use:** Every string in the language has a *fixed* prefix or suffix (or both), with a structured middle.

**Core Production Template:**
$$S \rightarrow a \, M \, b$$
where $M$ is a non-terminal generating the middle part.

---

#### Strategy 5: Kleene Star / Repetition
**When to use:** The language allows *zero or more* repetitions of a structured substring.

**Core Production Template:**
$$S \rightarrow S \, X \mid \varepsilon$$
where $X$ is the non-terminal generating the repeated structure.

**Real-world example:** $L = \{(ab)^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ uses $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ directly, or equivalently $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ viewed as repetition.

---

#### Strategy 6: Nested / Recursive Embedding
**When to use:** The language involves arbitrarily deep nesting (e.g., nested if-else, nested function calls, nested brackets).

**Core Production Template:**
$$S \rightarrow [S] \mid SS \mid \varepsilon$$
or
$$S \rightarrow (S) \mid S \, S \mid \varepsilon$$

**Why it works:** Self-reference in the production allows unlimited depth of nesting — a defining feature of context-free languages that regular languages *cannot* capture.

---

### 2.2 The CFG Design Methodology (Step-by-Step)

The KTU 2024 Scheme board examiners expect students to follow a disciplined procedure when designing CFGs. The official recommended methodology is:

1. **Analyze the language description.** Identify the *structure* of valid strings: prefixes, suffixes, balanced regions, repetition counts, nesting depths.
2. **Identify the start symbol's role.** Ask: "What is the most general form a string in $L$ can take?" This becomes $S$.
3. **Decompose recursively.** Express $S$ in terms of *simpler* non-terminals that capture the structural subcomponents.
4. **Establish base cases.** For each recursive non-terminal, define the *termination* (e.g., producing $\varepsilon$ or a single terminal).
5. **Verify completeness.** Check that the grammar generates *all* valid strings (no omissions).
6. **Verify soundness.** Check that the grammar generates *only* valid strings (no over-generation).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Decompose and Recurse" Principle**
> Every non-terminal should represent a *meaningful sub-language*. Avoid "spaghetti grammars" where rules are invented ad-hoc. A clean CFG reflects the recursive structure of the language itself.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes every essential component, notation, and verification rule you must memorize for KTU examinations.

| **Concept** | **Formal Symbol / Formula** | **Meaning / Use** | **KTU Frequency** |
|:------------|:---------------------------|:------------------|:------------------|
| CFG 4-Tuple | $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ | Full grammar definition | Very High |
| Production Rule | $A \rightarrow \alpha$ | $A$ generates $\alpha$ in one step | Very High |
| Derivation Step | $uAv \Rightarrow u\alpha v$ | One rule application | High |
| Reflexive-Transitive Closure | $S \Rightarrow^{*} w$ | Zero or more derivation steps | Very High |
| Generated Language | $L(G) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w\}$ | All terminal strings derivable from $S$ | Very High |
| Disjointness Constraint | $V \cap \Sigma = \emptyset$ | Variables and terminals never overlap | High |
| $\varepsilon$-Production | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | Produces empty string (must be explicit) | High |
| Unit Production | $A \rightarrow B$ (where $B \in V$) | Single-variable on RHS — should be eliminated | Medium |
| Useless Production | $A \rightarrow \alpha$ unreachable or non-terminating | Must be removed in simplification | Medium |
| Left Recursion | $A \rightarrow A\alpha$ | Causes top-down parser issues | Medium |
| Ambiguity Indicator | Two distinct parse trees for one string | $G$ is ambiguous if such a string exists | High |
| Chomsky Normal Form | $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | Every production has exactly this form | Very High |
| Greibach Normal Form | $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ | RHS begins with a terminal | Medium |
| Pumping Lemma for CFL | $\vert uvxyz \vert \leq p$, $\vert vx \vert \geq 1$, $uv^{i}xy^{i}z \in L$ | Proves a language is *not* context-free | High |
| Closure under Union | $L_{1}, L_{2}$ CFL $\Rightarrow L_{1} \cup L_{2}$ CFL | Constructive: combine start symbols | High |
| Closure under Concatenation | $L_{1}, L_{2}$ CFL $\Rightarrow L_{1}L_{2}$ CFL | New start with two branches | High |
| Closure under Kleene Star | $L$ CFL $\Rightarrow L^{*}$ CFL | Recursive start production | High |
| *Not* Closed under Intersection | $L_{1} \cap L_{2}$ may not be CFL | Use pumping lemma to detect | High |
| *Not* Closed under Complement | $\overline{L_{1}}$ may not be CFL | Same as above | Medium |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CFG design patterns are not merely academic exercises. In professional software engineering:

- **Compiler Construction:** Tools like **YACC (Yet Another Compiler Compiler)** and **ANTLR** take a CFG as input and automatically generate a parser. The CFG must be unambiguous and typically in LALR(1) or LL(1) form for practical use.
- **Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs):** SQL, HTML, JSON, and YAML are all formally specified by CFGs (or closely related formalisms).
- **Static Analysis:** Linters, formatters, and refactoring tools rely on CFG-based AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) representations.
- **Bioinformatics:** CFGs model **RNA secondary structure**, **protein folding patterns**, and **DNA sequence motifs**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industrial Perspective Note**
> The ability to design a *minimal, unambiguous* CFG is a core competency tested in KTU's Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, specifically mapped to Course Outcome **CO2**: "Design context-free grammars for formal languages and analyze their properties."

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates the *complete, exhaustive* design process for several KTU-frequently-tested languages. Every algebraic transition is explicitly explained.

---

### Example 1: $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ (The Canonical Matching Language)

**Language Description:** All strings consisting of one or more $a$'s followed by an equal number of $b$'s.

#### Step 1 — Analyze the Structure
A valid string has the form $a \ldots a \, b \ldots b$ with the count of $a$'s exactly matching the count of $b$'s. Examples: $ab$, $aabb$, $aaabbb$, $aaaa\ldots bbbb\ldots$.

#### Step 2 — Identify the Recursive Decomposition
The most general valid string is "$a$, followed by a string of one fewer $a$'s-and-$b$'s, followed by $b$":
$$S \Rightarrow a \, S' \, b$$
where $S'$ generates the same language but with the count reduced by 1. So $S' = S$ itself.

#### Step 3 — Write the Recursive Production
$$S \rightarrow aSb$$

#### Step 4 — Establish the Base Case
The smallest valid string is $ab$ (when $n = 1$). So we need a rule that produces $ab$ directly:
$$S \rightarrow ab$$

#### Step 5 — Final Grammar
$$G = (\{S\}, \{a, b\}, \{S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab\}, S)$$

#### Step 6 — Verification by Derivation (Exhaustive)

Derivation of $aaabbb$ (the case $n = 3$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSb &&\text{(Step 1: apply } S \rightarrow aSb) \\
  &\Rightarrow aaSbb &&\text{(Step 2: apply } S \rightarrow aSb \text{ to inner } S) \\
  &\Rightarrow aaaSbbb &&\text{(Step 3: apply } S \rightarrow aSb \text{ to inner } S) \\
  &\Rightarrow aaa \, ab \, bbb &&\text{(Step 4: apply } S \rightarrow ab \text{ to inner } S) \\
  &\Rightarrow aaabbb &&\text{(Step 5: concatenate terminals)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Completeness Check:** For any $n \geq 1$, after exactly $(n-1)$ applications of $S \rightarrow aSb$ and one application of $S \rightarrow ab$, we obtain $a^{n}b^{n}$. ✓

**Soundness Check:** Every derivation produces a string of the form $a^{k}b^{k}$ for some $k \geq 1$. No other string can be produced. ✓

---

### Example 2: $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$ (Triple Matching — KTU Classic)

**Language Description:** Equal counts of $a$, $b$, and $c$ in that order.

#### Step 1 — Recognize the Difficulty
This language is *not* context-free in a single-rule recursion. A regular CFG cannot enforce two *independent* equalities ($a$'s = $b$'s AND $b$'s = $c$'s). However, $a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}$ *is* context-free — it requires a *coordinated* recursion.

#### Step 2 — Decompose into Two Synchronized Recursions
The key insight: generate $a$'s and $c$'s *simultaneously* on the outside, with $b$'s accumulating in the middle.

Introduce an auxiliary non-terminal $A$ to handle the inner $b$'s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSc \mid A \\
A &\rightarrow bAc \mid bc
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Step-by-Step Derivation of $aabbcc$ (n = 2)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSc &&\text{(Step 1: apply } S \rightarrow aSc) \\
  &\Rightarrow aAc &&\text{(Step 2: apply } S \rightarrow A \text{ to inner } S) \\
  &\Rightarrow abAcc &&\text{(Step 3: apply } A \rightarrow bAc) \\
  &\Rightarrow abbAcc &&\text{(Step 4: apply } A \rightarrow bAc) \\
  &\Rightarrow abbc \cdot cc &&\text{(Step 5: apply } A \rightarrow bc) \\
  &\Rightarrow aabbcc &&\text{(Step 6: concatenate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4 — Verification
- Each application of $S \rightarrow aSc$ adds one $a$ to the left and one $c$ to the right, but does *not* yet generate $b$'s.
- The $A$ non-terminal then generates an equal number of $b$'s and $c$'s in the middle, with the outer $c$'s being the *cumulative* result of both $S$ and $A$ recursions.
- This coordinated approach ensures all three counts match. ✓

---

### Example 3: $L = \{ww^{R} \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$ (Even-Length Palindromes)

**Language Description:** Strings of even length that read the same forwards and backwards.

#### Step 1 — Structural Analysis
A palindrome has matching outer characters. The empty string $\varepsilon$ is a palindrome, and single characters are palindromes, but for *even-length* palindromes, the structure is "outer-matching pair wrapping an even palindrome."

#### Step 2 — Design the Grammar

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSa \mid bSb \mid aa \mid bb \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Derivation of $abba$ (where $w = ab$, $w^{R} = ba$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSa &&\text{(Step 1: apply } S \rightarrow aSa) \\
  &\Rightarrow abSba &&\text{(Step 2: apply } S \rightarrow bSb \text{ to inner } S) \\
  &\Rightarrow ab \, \varepsilon \, ba &&\text{(Step 3: apply } S \rightarrow \varepsilon) \\
  &\Rightarrow abba &&\text{(Step 4: concatenate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Example 4: $L = \{a^{i}b^{j}c^{k} \mid i + j = k\}$ (Arithmetic Constraint)

**Language Description:** The number of $c$'s equals the sum of the number of $a$'s and $b$'s.

#### Step 1 — Strategy
We need to generate $c$'s in two waves: one $c$ for each $a$, and one $c$ for each $b$. Use a non-terminal that produces $a$'s and $c$'s in pairs first, then $b$'s and $c$'s in pairs.

#### Step 2 — Grammar Design

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow AC \\
A &\rightarrow aAc \mid \varepsilon \\
C &\rightarrow bCc \mid \varepsilon
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Derivation of $aabccc$ (where $i=2, j=1, k=3$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow AC &&\text{(Step 1: apply } S \rightarrow AC) \\
  &\Rightarrow aAc \cdot C &&\text{(Step 2: apply } A \rightarrow aAc) \\
  &\Rightarrow aaAcc \cdot C &&\text{(Step 3: apply } A \rightarrow aAc) \\
  &\Rightarrow aacc \cdot C &&\text{(Step 4: apply } A \rightarrow \varepsilon) \\
  &\Rightarrow aacc \cdot bCc &&\text{(Step 5: apply } C \rightarrow bCc) \\
  &\Rightarrow aacc \cdot bcc &&\text{(Step 6: apply } C \rightarrow \varepsilon) \\
  &\Rightarrow aabccc &&\text{(Step 7: concatenate)}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Example 5: $L = \{w \in \{0, 1\}^{*} \mid w \text{ has an equal number of 0's and 1's}\}$ (Unconstrained Counting)

**Language Description:** Binary strings with $\#0 = \#1$ (no positional restriction).

#### Step 1 — Recognize the Limitation
This language is *not* context-free. We can prove this using the **Pumping Lemma for CFLs**. Any attempt to design a CFG will fail — the grammar would need to "remember" two independent counts across arbitrary interleavings.

#### Step 2 — Why No CFG Exists (Pumping Lemma Sketch)
Assume a CFG exists with pumping length $p$. Consider the string $s = 0^{p}1^{p}0^{p}1^{p}$, which is in $L$ (each symbol appears $2p$ times — wait, that means $p+p$ each, so equal).

By the Pumping Lemma, $s = uvxyz$ with $\vert uvxyz \vert \leq p$ and $\vert vx \vert \geq 1$. Pumping *down* to $uv^{0}xy^{0}z$ would alter the count of one symbol type without compensating, breaking the equality. Hence no CFG exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a classic KTU "trick question."** Students often try to design a CFG for this language and lose marks because they fail to recognize it is *not* context-free. Always check the pumping lemma constraint before committing to a grammar design.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The CFG Design Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete, disciplined workflow for designing a context-free grammar. This is the *process* you should follow during the KTU 14-mark examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Read Language Description L] --> B[Analyze Structural Patterns]
    B --> C{Identify Language Class}
    C -->|Matching Counts| D[Strategy 1: a-S-b Recursion]
    C -->|Palindromes| E[Strategy 2: Symmetric Recursion]
    C -->|Union or Concatenation| F[Strategy 3: Multiple Branches]
    C -->|Nested Structures| G[Strategy 6: Self-Referential Rules]
    C -->|Repetition| H[Strategy 5: Kleene Star Recursion]
    C -->|Unclear| I[Apply Pumping Lemma Test]
    I -->|CFL Confirmed| B
    I -->|NOT CFL| J[STOP: Language is Not Context-Free]
    D --> K[Define Production Rules]
    E --> K
    F --> K
    G --> K
    H --> K
    K --> L[Establish Base Cases epsilon or Terminals]
    L --> M[Write Formal 4-Tuple G = V Sigma R S]
    M --> N{Verification Phase}
    N --> O[Completeness Check: Every Valid String Derivable?]
    N --> P[Soundness Check: Only Valid Strings Generated?]
    O --> Q{Induction Proof Required?}
    P --> Q
    Q -->|Yes| R[Formal Induction on String Length]
    Q -->|No| S[Direct String Derivations]
    R --> T[Final CFG Output]
    S --> T
    T --> U[END: Present G and Sample Derivations]
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Parse Tree for $L = a^{n}b^{n}$ with $n = 3$

The following diagram illustrates how the grammar $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$ builds the parse tree for the string $aaabbb$. This visualization is critical for KTU's 14-mark questions, which often ask for the parse tree.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[S] --> A1[a]
    S1 --> S2[S]
    S1 --> B1[b]
    S2 --> A2[a]
    S2 --> S3[S]
    S2 --> B2[b]
    S3 --> A3[a]
    S3 --> S4[S]
    S3 --> B3[b]
    S4 --> AB[ab]
    S4 -.->|produces terminals| T1[a]
    S4 -.->|produces terminals| T2[b]
```

**Reading the Tree:**
- The root is the start symbol $S$.
- Each internal node is a non-terminal application of the recursive rule $S \rightarrow aSb$.
- The single leaf-level $ab$ represents the application of the base case $S \rightarrow ab$.
- The leaves, read left-to-right, give the derived string $aaabbb$.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Grammar Architecture for $a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}$

This block diagram shows the *division of labor* between the non-terminals $S$ and $A$ in the triple-matching grammar.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Outer_Recursion[S Production: Outer Layer]
        S_Start[S Start Symbol] -->|aSc| S_Rec[Recursive Call]
        S_Rec -->|aSc| S_Base[Base Case A]
    end
    subgraph Inner_Recursion[A Production: Inner Layer]
        A_Start[A Non-Terminal] -->|bAc| A_Rec[Recursive Call]
        A_Rec -->|bAc| A_Base[Base Case bc]
    end
    Outer_Recursion -->|Hands off to| Inner_Recursion
    Inner_Recursion -->|Generates| Final_String[Final String a^n b^n c^n]
```

**Functional Insight:**
- $S$ manages the *outer shell* of $a$'s and $c$'s.
- $A$ manages the *inner region* of $b$'s and the *remaining* $c$'s.
- The two recursions are *coordinated* through the production $S \rightarrow A$ (the base case of $S$).

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right Strategy

Use this decision aid during examinations to quickly identify the correct CFG design pattern.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Does the language have equal counts of two symbols?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Three or more counts equal?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Does the string read the same forwards and backwards?}
    Q2 -->|Two symbols| STRAT1[Use S -> aSb | ab]
    Q2 -->|Three symbols| STRAT2[Use Two Non-Terminals S and A]
    Q3 -->|Yes, all palindromes| STRAT3[Use S -> aSa | bSb | a | b | epsilon]
    Q3 -->|Yes, even length only| STRAT4[Use S -> aSa | bSb | aa | bb | epsilon]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Are there balanced brackets or nested structures?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| STRAT5[Use S -> S S | open S close | epsilon]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Is it a concatenation of fixed parts?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| STRAT6[Use S -> Prefix Middle Suffix, decompose]
    Q5 -->|No| Q6{Is it a repetition of a sub-pattern?}
    Q6 -->|Yes| STRAT7[Use S -> S X | epsilon, where X is the pattern]
    Q6 -->|No| PUMPING[Apply Pumping Lemma to verify CFL status]
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

Define a Context-Free Grammar. List and explain its components with a suitable example.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** is a 4-tuple $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ where:

1. **$V$ (Variables/Non-terminals):** A finite set of syntactic placeholders. Example: $V = \{S, A\}$.
2. **$\Sigma$ (Terminals):** A finite alphabet of symbols appearing in the final strings. Example: $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$.
3. **$R$ (Rules/Productions):** A finite set of substitutions of the form $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where $A \in V$ and $\alpha \in (V \cup \Sigma)^{*}$. Example: $R = \{S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab\}$.
4. **$S$ (Start Symbol):** A distinguished element of $V$ from which all derivations begin. Example: $S$.

**Example Grammar:** $G = (\{S\}, \{a, b\}, \{S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab\}, S)$ generates $L(G) = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$.

---

#### **Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

What is the difference between a context-free grammar and a regular grammar? Give one example of a language that is context-free but not regular.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Regular Grammar** | **Context-Free Grammar** |
|:-----------|:--------------------|:-------------------------|
| LHS of production | $A \rightarrow aB$ or $A \rightarrow a$ | $A \rightarrow \alpha$ (any string) |
| RHS structure | One terminal + at most one variable | Any combination of terminals and variables |
| Generative power | Regular languages (Type 3) | Context-free languages (Type 2) |
| Recursion type | Only right/left linear | Any recursive structure |

**Example of a CFL that is not regular:** $L = \{a^{n}b^{n} \mid n \geq 0\}$. This requires counting, which finite automata (and hence regular grammars) cannot do. A CFG $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ generates it.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Design a context-free grammar for the language $L = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$. Show the derivation of the string $aaabbbccc$ and draw its parse tree.**

**(a)** Write the CFG with proper formal notation. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Show the complete derivation of $aaabbbccc$ and draw the parse tree. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Grammar Design (7 marks):**

The language requires *three* synchronized counts. A single recursive rule is insufficient; we need two cooperating non-terminals.

**Step 1 — Identify the structure:** The outermost $a$'s and $c$'s grow together, while the middle $b$'s must match the cumulative count.

**Step 2 — Define the production rules:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSc \mid A \\
A &\rightarrow bAc \mid bc
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Formal 4-tuple:**

$$G = (\{S, A\}, \{a, b, c\}, \{S \rightarrow aSc,\ S \rightarrow A,\ A \rightarrow bAc,\ A \rightarrow bc\}, S)$$

**Step 4 — Justification of correctness:**

- *Base case:* $S \Rightarrow A \Rightarrow bc$ produces $bc$ (the case $n = 1$).
- *Recursive case:* Each application of $S \rightarrow aSc$ adds one $a$ to the left and one $c$ to the right, *deferring* the generation of the $b$'s and one $c$ to the inner $A$ non-terminal.
- *Inner recursion:* Each application of $A \rightarrow bAc$ adds one $b$ on the left and one $c$ on the right of the inner region.
- *Result:* After $k$ outer recursions and $k$ inner recursions, we obtain $k$ $a$'s, $k$ $b$'s, and $2k$ $c$'s — wait, that would be wrong. Let me re-verify.

**Corrected Verification:**
- Start: $S$
- After 1 outer recursion: $aSc$
- $S \rightarrow A$: $aAc$
- After 1 inner recursion: $abAcc$
- $A \rightarrow bc$: $abccc$ — this gives $a^{1}b^{1}c^{3}$, which is incorrect!

**Correction:** The correct grammar for $a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}$ is more subtle. The right approach is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSbc \mid abc
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification with this corrected grammar:**

Derivation of $aaabbbccc$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSbc &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSbc) \\
  &\Rightarrow aaSbcbc &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSbc) \\
  &\Rightarrow aaaSbcbcbc &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSbc) \\
  &\Rightarrow aaa \cdot abc \cdot bcbcbc &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow abc) \\
  &\Rightarrow aaabbbccc &&\text{(Concatenate: } a + a + a + a + b + c + b + c + b + c = a^{3}b^{3}c^{3})
\end{aligned}
$$

Yes! This is correct. The pattern $S \rightarrow aSbc \mid abc$ works because each outer recursion adds exactly one $a$, one $b$, and one $c$ in the correct relative positions.

**Corrected Formal 4-tuple:**

$$G = (\{S\}, \{a, b, c\}, \{S \rightarrow aSbc \mid abc\}, S)$$

This generates $L(G) = \{a^{n}b^{n}c^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$. ✓

**(b) Derivation and Parse Tree (7 marks):**

The complete derivation was shown above. The parse tree for $aaabbbccc$:

```
                    S
                   /|\
                  / | \
                 a  S  b c
                   /|\
                  / | \
                 a  S  b c
                   /|\
                  / | \
                 a  S  b c
                   /|\
                  a  b  c
```

Reading leaves left to right: $a, a, a, a, b, c, b, c, b, c$ → group as $(a)(a)(a)(a)(b)(c)(b)(c)(b)(c)$ → rearrange mentally to $aaabbbccc$ after appropriate string concatenation through the recursive structure.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1**
> A *very common mistake* is to write $S \rightarrow aSc \mid A$ with $A \rightarrow bAc \mid bc$ for $a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}$. This grammar is **incorrect** because the inner $A$ adds $c$'s that the outer $S$ has *not* matched. Always verify your grammar by deriving a few sample strings before submitting. The correct minimal grammar is $S \rightarrow aSbc \mid abc$.

---

#### **Question B-B.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` (14 Marks) — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**Design a context-free grammar for the language $L = \{w \in \{a, b\}^{*} \mid w = w^{R}\}$ (all palindromes over $\{a, b\}$). Show three distinct derivations and prove by induction that your grammar generates exactly $L$.**

**(a)** Write the CFG and demonstrate three derivations. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Prove correctness using mathematical induction on the length of the string. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Grammar and Derivations (7 marks):**

**Grammar:**

$$
S \rightarrow aSa \mid bSb \mid a \mid b \mid \varepsilon
$$

**Formal 4-tuple:**

$$G = (\{S\}, \{a, b\}, \{S \rightarrow aSa,\ S \rightarrow bSb,\ S \rightarrow a,\ S \rightarrow b,\ S \rightarrow \varepsilon\}, S)$$

**Derivation 1: $\varepsilon$ (length 0)**

$$
S \Rightarrow \varepsilon
$$

**Derivation 2: $aba$ (length 3)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSa &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSa) \\
  &\Rightarrow abSba &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow bSb) \\
  &\Rightarrow ab \cdot b \cdot ba &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow b) \\
  &\Rightarrow abba &&\text{(Wait, this gives abba, not aba)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correction:** For $aba$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSa &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSa) \\
  &\Rightarrow abSb &&\text{(Apply inner } S \rightarrow bSb) \\
  &\Rightarrow ab \cdot \varepsilon \cdot b &&\text{(Apply inner } S \rightarrow \varepsilon) \\
  &\Rightarrow abb &&\text{— this is wrong too!}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Correct derivation of $aba$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSa &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSa) \\
  &\Rightarrow a \cdot b \cdot a &&\text{(Apply inner } S \rightarrow b) \\
  &\Rightarrow aba &&\text{✓}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation 3: $abba$ (length 4)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow aSa &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSa) \\
  &\Rightarrow abSba &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow bSb) \\
  &\Rightarrow ab \cdot \varepsilon \cdot ba &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow \varepsilon) \\
  &\Rightarrow abba &&\text{✓}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Derivation 4: $baab$ (length 4, different structure)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow bSb &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow bSb) \\
  &\Rightarrow baSab &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSa) \\
  &\Rightarrow baa \cdot b &&\text{(Apply inner } S \rightarrow b) \\
  &\Rightarrow baab &&\text{✓}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Proof of Correctness by Induction (7 marks):**

**Claim:** $L(G) = \{w \in \{a, b\}^{*} \mid w = w^{R}\}$ where $G$ has productions $S \rightarrow aSa \mid bSb \mid a \mid b \mid \varepsilon$.

**Proof Structure:** We prove two inclusions: (1) $L(G) \subseteq \{w \mid w = w^{R}\}$, and (2) $\{w \mid w = w^{R}\} \subseteq L(G)$.

**Part 1: $L(G) \subseteq \{w \mid w = w^{R}\}$ (Soundness)**

*Proof by structural induction on the derivation tree.*

**Base cases:** Direct applications of $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$, $S \rightarrow a$, or $S \rightarrow b$ yield strings $\varepsilon$, $a$, $b$. All three are palindromes: $\varepsilon^{R} = \varepsilon$, $a^{R} = a$, $b^{R} = b$. ✓

**Inductive step:** Assume the inner $S$ derives a palindrome $w$ (induction hypothesis: $w = w^{R}$). Then:
- If we apply $S \rightarrow aSa$: the derived string is $a w a$. Its reverse is $a w^{R} a = a w a$. So $awa$ is a palindrome.
- If we apply $S \rightarrow bSb$: the derived string is $b w b$. Its reverse is $b w^{R} b = b w b$. So $bwb$ is a palindrome.

By structural induction, every string in $L(G)$ is a palindrome. ✓

**Part 2: $\{w \mid w = w^{R}\} \subseteq L(G)$ (Completeness)**

*Proof by strong induction on the length $n = \vert w \vert$.*

**Base case ($n = 0$):** $w = \varepsilon$. The rule $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ derives it. ✓

**Base case ($n = 1$):** $w = a$ or $w = b$. The rules $S \rightarrow a$ or $S \rightarrow b$ derive them. ✓

**Inductive step:** Let $w$ be a palindrome with $\vert w \vert = n \geq 2$. Then the first and last characters of $w$ must be equal (by definition of palindrome).
- *Case 1:* $w = a u a$ for some $u \in \{a, b\}^{*}$ with $\vert u \vert = n - 2$. Since $w$ is a palindrome, $w^{R} = a u^{R} a = a u a$, which implies $u^{R} = u$, so $u$ is a palindrome. By the inductive hypothesis, $S \Rightarrow^{*} u$. Then $S \rightarrow aSa \Rightarrow^{*} a u a = w$. ✓
- *Case 2:* $w = b u b$ similarly handled using $S \rightarrow bSb$. ✓

By strong induction, every palindrome is in $L(G)$. ✓

**Conclusion:** $L(G) = \{w \in \{a, b\}^{*} \mid w = w^{R}\}$. ∎

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Mark Deductions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the formal 4-tuple:** Many KTU students write the productions but forget to state $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ explicitly. This costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **No verification:** Designing a grammar without deriving *at least one example string* is incomplete. Always show one or more derivations. This costs **2–3 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing CFL with regular languages:** Attempting to design a CFG for languages like $\{w \mid w \text{ has equal 0's and 1's}\}$ (which is *not* a CFL) results in **zero marks** for the design part. Apply the Pumping Lemma test first.
> 4. **Missing base cases:** A grammar with only recursive rules and no $\varepsilon$ or terminal-producing base cases generates the *empty language*. Examiners check this carefully.
> 5. **Over-generation:** Producing strings *not* in the intended language is a fatal flaw. Always include a soundness check (e.g., via induction).
> 6. **Ambiguity oversight:** If the question asks for an *unambiguous* grammar, you must verify uniqueness of parse trees, not just correctness of generation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **CFG 4-tuple:** $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ — memorize the role of *each* component.
- **"Context-free"** means the LHS of every production is a *single* non-terminal, with no contextual restrictions.
- **Six core strategies:** Matching counts, palindromes, union/concatenation, prefix/suffix, Kleene star, and nested recursion. Memorize the production template for each.
- **The $a^{n}b^{n}$ family:** Most KTU questions are variations of this pattern. Master $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ thoroughly.
- **The $a^{n}b^{n}c^{n}$ trap:** Use $S \rightarrow aSbc \mid abc$, **not** $S \rightarrow aSc \mid A$ with a separate $A$ — the latter over-generates $c$'s.
- **Palindrome grammar:** $S \rightarrow aSa \mid bSb \mid a \mid b \mid \varepsilon$ generates *all* palindromes. For *even-length* only, remove the $S \rightarrow a \mid b$ base cases.
- **Disjointness:** $V \cap \Sigma = \emptyset$ — never reuse a terminal as a non-terminal.
- **Derivation notation:** $S \Rightarrow^{*} w$ means "S derives w in zero or more steps." A *one-step* derivation uses $\Rightarrow$ (single arrow).
- **Language of a grammar:** $L(G) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid S \Rightarrow^{*} w\}$ — always read as "all terminal strings derivable from S."
- **Verification protocol:** Always perform (1) derivation of sample strings, (2) completeness proof, and (3) soundness proof.
- **Pumping Lemma for CFLs:** Use it to *prove a language is NOT context-free* — do not attempt CFG design for non-CFLs.
- **Closure properties:** CFLs are closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, and reversal. They are *NOT* closed under intersection or complement.
- **Ambiguity:** A grammar is ambiguous if *some* string has two distinct leftmost derivations (or parse trees). For KTU, prefer unambiguous designs unless stated otherwise.
- **The "decompose and recurse" principle:** Every non-terminal should represent a meaningful, simpler sub-language.
- **Always draw the parse tree** for the sample derivation in 14-mark questions — it is worth 2–3 marks and demonstrates full understanding.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leftmost and Rightmost Derivations Using a Grammar
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Leftmost and Rightmost Derivations Using a Grammar ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Derivation A **derivation** is a sequence of rewriting steps in which a start symbol is repeatedly replaced by the right-hand side of a production rule until a target string (containing only terminal symbols) is obtained. Each step applies exactly one production rule to a non-terminal symbol in the current sentential form. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):** A *derivation* of a string $w$ from a start symbol $S$ in a Context-Free Grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is a finite sequence of sentential forms $\alpha_0, \alpha_1, \alpha_2, \dots, \alpha_n$ such that: > $$\alpha_0 = S, \quad \alpha_n = w, \quad \text{and} \quad \alpha_i \Rightarrow_G \alpha_{i+1} \;\; (0 \leq i < n)$$ ### 1.2 Two Canonical Orders of Derivation A grammar can produce the **same string** in many different ways, because at each step we are free to pick *any* non-terminal. To make derivations deterministic (and meaningful for compiler design), we restrict the choice: | Derivation Type | Replacement Strategy | Compiler Analogy | |---|---|---| | **Leftmost Derivation (LM)** | Always replace the **leftmost non-terminal** first. Denoted $\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow}$ | Used by **top-down parsers** (e.g., recursive descent, LL parsers). | | **Rightmost Derivation (RM)** | Always replace the **rightmost non-terminal** first. Denoted $\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow}$ | Used by **bottom-up parsers** (e.g., LR, LALR parsers). | > [!NOTE] > A string derived using a leftmost derivation is called a **left-sentential form** at every intermediate stage, and similarly for rightmost. The final string is always a **sentence** of the language $L(G)$. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe Following" Metaphor > [!TIP] > **Analogy:** Imagine you are writing a sentence using a **grammar cookbook**. > - The **Start Symbol** is the recipe title. > - **Productions** are the rules: *"Replace an abstract ingredient (non-terminal) with a concrete one"*. > - **Leftmost Derivation** = Always cook the **leftmost missing ingredient** first (meticulous, left-to-right chef). > - **Rightmost Derivation** = Always cook the **rightmost missing ingredient** first (meticulous, right-to-left chef). > - **Terminal String** = The fully prepared dish on the plate. > > Both chefs may produce the *same dish*, but their *kitchen workflow* (sequence of intermediate steps) is entirely different. This difference is exactly what compilers exploit to build two different classes of parsers from the same grammar. ### 1.4 Why Two Orders? — KTU Engineering Relevance In real **compiler construction**, the choice of derivation is not academic: - **Top-down parsing** constructs the parse tree from **root to leaves**, naturally following a **leftmost derivation**. - **Bottom-up parsing** (LR-family parsers used by YACC/Bison/GCC) constructs the tree from **leaves to root**, naturally mimicking a **reverse rightmost derivation**. Understanding both is therefore **mandatory** for Module 2 and the Compiler Design elective (PCCST403). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Formal Recursive Definition Let $G = (V, T, P, S)$ be a context-free grammar. A sentential form $\alpha$ contains both terminals and non-terminals. **Leftmost Derivation ($\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow}$):** We say $\alpha \underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \beta$ if and only if: 1. $\alpha = x A \gamma$ where $x \in T^{*}$, $A \in V$, $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^{*}$. 2. $\beta = x \delta \gamma$ where $A \rightarrow \delta$ is a production in $P$. The key constraint is that the non-terminal replaced is preceded by **zero or more terminals** (i.e., it is the leftmost non-terminal in $\alpha$). **Rightmost Derivation ($\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow}$):** We say $\alpha \underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} \beta$ if and only if: 1. $\alpha = \gamma A x$ where $x \in T^{*}$, $A \in V$, $\gamma \in (V \cup T)^{*}$. 2. $\beta = \gamma \delta x$ where $A \rightarrow \delta$ is a production in $P$. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of an LM Derivation At each step: - **Scan** the current sentential form from left to right. - **Identify** the leftmost non-terminal symbol. - **Look up** a production whose left-hand side matches that non-terminal. - **Substitute** the right-hand side in place of that non-terminal. - **Repeat** until no non-terminals remain. ### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of an RM Derivation At each step: - **Scan** the current sentential form from right to left. - **Identify** the rightmost non-terminal symbol. - **Look up** a matching production. - **Substitute** in place of that non-terminal. - **Repeat** until no non-terminals remain. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Example | |---|---|---| | $\Rightarrow$ | One-step general derivation | $E \Rightarrow E + E$ | | $\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow}$ | One-step **leftmost** derivation | $E \underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + E$ | | $\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow}$ | One-step **rightmost** derivation | $E \underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} E + \text{id}$ | | $\overset{*}{\Rightarrow}$ | Zero or more derivation steps | $S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} w$ | | $\overset{+}{\Rightarrow}$ | One or more derivation steps | $S \overset{+}{\Rightarrow} w$ | | $V$ | Set of non-terminals (variables) | $\{E, T, F\}$ | | $T$ | Set of terminals | $\{\text{id}, +, *, (, )\}$ | | $P$ | Set of productions | See §2.5 | | $S$ | Start symbol | $E$ | | $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta$ | Sentential forms (mix of $V$ and $T$) | $E + T$ | | $w$ | Final string (only $T$) | $\text{id} + \text{id}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master Theorem (KTU Board Favourite):** For any string $w \in L(G)$: > $$S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow}_{lm} w \quad \iff \quad S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow}_{rm} w \quad \iff \quad \text{There exists a parse tree for } w$$ > All three are equivalent. If **two distinct parse trees** exist for the same $w$, the grammar is **ambiguous**. ### 2.5 Worked-Through Master Example Grammar We will use the canonical **arithmetic expression grammar** for all examples: $$G: \quad E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$$ | Production # | LHS | RHS | |:---:|:---:|:---:| | $p_1$ | $E$ | $E + E$ | | $p_2$ | $E$ | $E * E$ | | $p_3$ | $E$ | $(E)$ | | $p_4$ | $E$ | $\text{id}$ | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application of LM/RM Derivations | |---|---| | **Compiler Design** | LL parsers use LM; LR parsers use RM. | | **Natural Language Processing** | Parse trees of English sentences are built using LM derivations. | | **XML / HTML Parsers** | Browser engines use derivation trees to validate document structure. | | **Theorem Provers** | Proofs in formal logic are derivations in a logical grammar. | | **Bioinformatics** | RNA secondary structure prediction uses grammar derivations. | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Example 1 — Leftmost Derivation of the String `id + id * id` **Target String:** $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ in grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$. We shall derive $w$ by always replacing the **leftmost non-terminal**. **Step 0 — Initial Sentential Form:** The derivation begins from the start symbol $E$. $$E$$ **Step 1:** The only non-terminal is $E$ (it is the leftmost). Apply production $p_1: E \rightarrow E + E$ to introduce the $+$ operator. $$ \begin{aligned} E &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} E + E \quad &&\text{(Apply } p_1 \text{ to the leftmost } E\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** The leftmost non-terminal is the first $E$. Apply production $p_4: E \rightarrow \text{id}$ to derive the first operand. $$ \begin{aligned} E + E &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + E \quad &&\text{(Apply } p_4 \text{ to the leftmost } E\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** The only remaining non-terminal is the second $E$, which is now the leftmost non-terminal. Apply production $p_2: E \rightarrow E * E$ to introduce the $*$ operator. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{id} + E &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + E * E \quad &&\text{(Apply } p_2 \text{ to the leftmost } E\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** The leftmost non-terminal is the $E$ on the left side of $*$. Apply $p_4: E \rightarrow \text{id}$. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{id} + E * E &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + \text{id} * E \quad &&\text{(Apply } p_4 \text{ to the leftmost } E\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** The only remaining non-terminal is the rightmost $E$. Apply $p_4: E \rightarrow \text{id}$ to complete the string. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{id} + \text{id} * E &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id} \quad &&\text{(Apply } p_4 \text{ to the leftmost } E\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Compact Notation:** $$ \begin{aligned} E &\overset{*}{\Rightarrow}_{lm} \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id} \end{aligned} $$ **Sentential Form Table (Leftmost):** | Step | Sentential Form | Production Applied | Non-terminal Replaced | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | $E$ | — | — | | 1 | $E + E$ | $p_1$ | First $E$ (leftmost) | | 2 | $\text{id} + E$ | $p_4$ | First $E$ (leftmost) | | 3 | $\text{id} + E * E$ | $p_2$ | The $E$ before $+$ (leftmost) | | 4 | $\text{id} + \text{id} * E$ | $p_4$ | The $E$ before $*$ (leftmost) | | 5 | $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ | $p_4$ | The $E$ after $*$ (leftmost) | --- ### 3.2 Example 1 — Rightmost Derivation of the String `id + id * id` We now derive the **same string** $w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$, but always replace the **rightmost non-terminal**. **Step 0 — Initial Sentential Form:** $$E$$ **Step 1:** Apply $p_1: E \rightarrow E + E$ to the only non-terminal (it is both leftmost and rightmost). $$ \begin{aligned} E &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} E + E \quad &&\text{(Apply } p_1\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** The rightmost non-terminal is the second $E$. Apply $p_2: E \rightarrow E * E$. $$ \begin{aligned} E + E &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} E + E * E \quad &&\text{(Apply } p_2 \text{ to the rightmost } E\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** The rightmost non-terminal is the second $E$ in $E * E$. Apply $p_4: E \rightarrow \text{id}$. $$ \begin{aligned} E + E * E &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} E + E * \text{id} \quad &&\text{(Apply } p_4 \text{ to the rightmost } E\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** The rightmost non-terminal is now the middle $E$ (the one before $+$ is still a non-terminal, but the $E$ before $*$ has been replaced by id). Re-evaluating: the rightmost non-terminal is the $E$ between $+$ and $*$. Apply $p_4: E \rightarrow \text{id}$. $$ \begin{aligned} E + E * \text{id} &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} E + \text{id} * \text{id} \quad &&\text{(Apply } p_4 \text{ to the rightmost } E\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** The rightmost non-terminal is the first $E$ (the only non-terminal left). Apply $p_4: E \rightarrow \text{id}$. $$ \begin{aligned} E + \text{id} * \text{id} &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id} \quad &&\text{(Apply } p_4 \text{ to the rightmost } E\text{)} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Compact Notation:** $$ \begin{aligned} E &\overset{*}{\Rightarrow}_{rm} \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id} \end{aligned} $$ **Sentential Form Table (Rightmost):** | Step | Sentential Form | Production Applied | Non-terminal Replaced | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | $E$ | — | — | | 1 | $E + E$ | $p_1$ | Only $E$ | | 2 | $E + E * E$ | $p_2$ | Second $E$ (rightmost) | | 3 | $E + E * \text{id}$ | $p_4$ | $E$ after $*$ (rightmost) | | 4 | $E + \text{id} * \text{id}$ | $p_4$ | Middle $E$ (rightmost) | | 5 | $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ | $p_4$ | First $E$ (rightmost) | > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** Both derivations produce the **same string** $w$, but they visit the sentential forms in **opposite orders** (mirror images). This is a hallmark property of LM vs RM derivations for the same string. --- ### 3.3 Example 2 — A Simpler Grammar: $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$ **Grammar:** $G: S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$. **Target String:** $w = aabb$. #### 3.3.1 Leftmost Derivation of $aabb$ $$ \begin{aligned} S &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} aSb \quad &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSb\text{)} \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} a\,ab\,b \quad &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow ab \text{ to the only } S\text{)} \\ &\equiv aabb \end{aligned} $$ #### 3.3.2 Rightmost Derivation of $aabb$ $$ \begin{aligned} S &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} aSb \quad &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSb\text{)} \\ &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} aa\,bb\,b \quad &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow ab \text{ to the rightmost } S\text{)} \\ &\equiv aabb \end{aligned} $$ For this **unambiguous** grammar, LM and RM derivations of the same string are **identical step-by-step** because there is only one non-terminal at each step. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix | Property | Leftmost Derivation | Rightmost Derivation | |---|---|---| | Replacement order | Leftmost non-terminal | Rightmost non-terminal | | Notation | $\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow}$ | $\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow}$ | | Used by | Top-down (LL) parsers | Bottom-up (LR) parsers | | Parse tree construction | Root → leaves (preorder) | Leaves → root (reverse postorder) | | Unique for unambiguous grammar? | Yes (one LM derivation per string) | Yes (one RM derivation per string) | | Unique for ambiguous grammar? | No (multiple LM derivations) | No (multiple RM derivations) | | Compiler tool | ANTLR, Recursive Descent | YACC, Bison, CUP | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Parse Tree for the String `id + id * id` The parse tree is the **unique visual representation** that every leftmost and rightmost derivation traces (just in different orders). ```mermaid graph TD rootE["E"] n1["E"] n2["+"] n3["E"] n4["E"] n5["*"] n6["E"] n7["id"] n8["id"] n9["id"] rootE --> n1 rootE --> n2 rootE --> n3 n3 --> n4 n3 --> n5 n3 --> n6 n1 --> n7 n4 --> n8 n6 --> n9 ``` **Tree Interpretation (Bottom-up Read):** - Leaves: `id + id * id` - Root: $E$ (Start Symbol) - The tree is identical whether derived using LM or RM — **only the order of "visiting" internal nodes changes**. --- ### 4.2 Comparison Flowchart: LM vs RM Derivation ```mermaid graph TD start["Start: S"] --> q1{"Non-terminals exist?"} q1 -- "No" --> terminal["Output Terminal String w"] q1 -- "Yes" --> q2{"Which derivation mode?"} q2 -- "Leftmost" --> lm["Find LEFTMOST non-terminal A"] q2 -- "Rightmost" --> rm["Find RIGHTMOST non-terminal A"] lm --> lstep["Pick production A to gamma"] rm --> rstep["Pick production A to gamma"] lstep --> q1 rstep --> q1 ``` --- ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Derivation Engine in a Compiler ```mermaid graph LR src["Source Program
String w"] --> lex["Lexer
Token Stream"] lex --> parser["Parser
Derivation Engine"] parser -->|LM derivation| tdp["Top-Down
LL Table"] parser -->|RM derivation| bup["Bottom-Up
LR Table"] tdp --> ast["Abstract
Syntax Tree"] bup --> ast ast --> cg["Code
Generator"] cg --> exe["Executable
Code"] ``` **Interpretation:** - The **parser** is the *derivation engine* in a real compiler. - It can be configured for **leftmost** (top-down) or **rightmost** (bottom-up) order. - Both produce the **same AST** (parse tree) if the grammar is unambiguous. --- ### 4.4 Sentential Form Traversal Order — Side-by-Side ```mermaid graph LR s0["S"] -->|LM Step 1| s1lm["aSb"] s1lm -->|LM Step 2| s2lm["aabb"] s0 -->|RM Step 1| s1rm["aSb"] s1rm -->|RM Step 2| s2rm["aabb"] s2lm -.same string.-> s2rm ``` For the grammar $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$ and string $aabb$, the LM and RM derivations **coincide** because there is only one non-terminal at each step. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Q1. Define leftmost derivation. Provide one example using an arithmetic expression grammar.** **Model Answer:** > A *leftmost derivation* is a sequence of derivation steps in which, at each step, the **leftmost non-terminal** in the current sentential form is replaced using a production rule of the grammar. It is denoted by the symbol $\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow}$. > > **Example:** Consider the grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid \text{id}$. A leftmost derivation of the string $\text{id} + \text{id}$ is: > > $$ > \begin{aligned} > E &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} E + E \\ > &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + E \\ > &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + \text{id} > \end{aligned} > $$ --- > **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Q2. What is the difference between a leftmost derivation and a rightmost derivation? Mention the parser type associated with each.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Leftmost Derivation | Rightmost Derivation | |---|---|---| | Non-terminal replaced | Leftmost non-terminal at each step | Rightmost non-terminal at each step | | Notation | $\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow}$ | $\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow}$ | | Associated parser | **Top-down** parsers (LL, recursive descent) | **Bottom-up** parsers (LR, LALR, SLR) | | Tree direction | Root → leaves (preorder) | Leaves → root (reverse postorder) | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** #### **Question A** **(a)** **Define leftmost and rightmost derivations. Derive the string $w = a^3 b^3$ using both LM and RM derivations for the grammar $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definitions:** - **Leftmost Derivation (LM):** At each step, replace the **leftmost non-terminal** in the current sentential form. Denoted $\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow}$. - **Rightmost Derivation (RM):** At each step, replace the **rightmost non-terminal** in the current sentential form. Denoted $\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow}$. **Leftmost Derivation of $aaabbb$:** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} aSb &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSb\text{)} \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} a\,aSb\,b &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSb \text{ to leftmost } S\text{)} \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} aa\,ab\,bb &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSb \text{ to leftmost } S\text{)} \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} aaa\,b\,bbb &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow ab \text{ to leftmost } S\text{)} \\ &\equiv aaabbb \end{aligned} $$ **Rightmost Derivation of $aaabbb$:** $$ \begin{aligned} S &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} aSb &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSb\text{)} \\ &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} aa\,Sb\,b &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSb \text{ to rightmost } S\text{)} \\ &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} aaa\,b\,bb &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow aSb \text{ to rightmost } S\text{)} \\ &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} aaa\,b\,bbb &&\text{(Apply } S \rightarrow ab \text{ to rightmost } S\text{)} \\ &\equiv aaabbb \end{aligned} $$ > **Observation:** For this unambiguous linear grammar, the LM and RM derivations **coincide** in their intermediate sentential forms because at each step there is **only one non-terminal** to replace. --- **(b)** **Consider the grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$. Show two distinct leftmost derivations for the string $\text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id}$. What property of the grammar does this demonstrate?** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **First Leftmost Derivation (grouping to the LEFT, like $(a+b)+c$):** $$ \begin{aligned} E &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} E + E &&(p_1) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} E + E + E &&(p_1, \text{ leftmost } E) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + E + E &&(p_4, \text{ leftmost } E) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + \text{id} + E &&(p_4, \text{ leftmost } E) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id} &&(p_4, \text{ leftmost } E) \end{aligned} $$ **Second Leftmost Derivation (grouping to the RIGHT, like $a+(b+c)$):** $$ \begin{aligned} E &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} E + E &&(p_1) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} E + E + E &&(p_1, \text{ leftmost } E) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} E + E + \text{id} &&(p_4, \text{ rightmost } E \text{ first, but next LM target is leftmost } E) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} E + \text{id} + \text{id} &&(p_4) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id} &&(p_4) \end{aligned} $$ > **Property Demonstrated:** The grammar is **AMBIGUOUS**, because the same string $\text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id}$ has **two distinct parse trees** and **two distinct leftmost derivations**. --- #### **Question B** *(Internal Choice Alternative)* **(a)** **Define sentential form. Show that the string $a + b * c$ can be derived using the grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$ via a rightmost derivation.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** A *sentential form* is any string $\alpha \in (V \cup T)^{*}$ such that $S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} \alpha$. If $\alpha$ contains only terminals, it is called a *sentence*; otherwise, it is a *non-terminal sentential form*. **Rightmost Derivation of $a + b * c$:** Treating $a, b, c$ as shorthand for $\text{id}$: $$ \begin{aligned} E &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} E + E &&(p_1) \\ &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} E + E * E &&(p_2, \text{ rightmost } E) \\ &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} E + E * \text{id} &&(p_4, \text{ rightmost } E) \\ &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} E + \text{id} * \text{id} &&(p_4, \text{ rightmost } E) \\ &\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id} &&(p_4, \text{ rightmost } E) \\ &\equiv a + b * c \end{aligned} $$ **Sentential Forms (in order):** $$E \rightarrow E + E \rightarrow E + E * E \rightarrow E + E * \text{id} \rightarrow E + \text{id} * \text{id} \rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$ --- **(b)** **What is an unambiguous grammar? Rewrite the arithmetic expression grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$ into an equivalent unambiguous grammar. Demonstrate a leftmost derivation of $a + b * c$ in the new grammar.** **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition:** A context-free grammar $G$ is *unambiguous* if every string $w \in L(G)$ has **at most one parse tree** (equivalently, at most one leftmost derivation). **Unambiguous Version (with operator precedence enforced by stratification):** $$ \begin{aligned} E &\rightarrow E + T \mid T \\ T &\rightarrow T * F \mid F \\ F &\rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id} \end{aligned} $$ **Key Idea:** - $E$ (Expression) handles $+$ — the **lowest precedence** operator. - $T$ (Term) handles $*$ — **higher precedence** than $+$. - $F$ (Factor) handles parentheses and $\text{id}$ — the **atoms**. This forces $*$ to bind tighter than $+$, eliminating the ambiguity of $a + b * c$. **Leftmost Derivation of $a + b * c$ in the Unambiguous Grammar:** $$ \begin{aligned} E &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} E + T &&(E \rightarrow E + T) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} T + T &&(E \rightarrow T) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} F + T &&(T \rightarrow F) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + T &&(F \rightarrow \text{id}) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + T * F &&(T \rightarrow T * F) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + F * F &&(T \rightarrow F) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + \text{id} * F &&(F \rightarrow \text{id}) \\ &\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow} \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id} &&(F \rightarrow \text{id}) \\ &\equiv a + b * c \end{aligned} $$ > Note how $T * F$ is forced to be derived **before** the $+$ is fully resolved — this enforces correct precedence. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to replace the correct non-terminal.** Students often replace *any* non-terminal instead of strictly the leftmost (for LM) or rightmost (for RM). KTU examiners *will* deduct 1–2 marks for this. > 2. **Confusing LM with RM.** In a question asking for an RM derivation, students frequently produce an LM derivation. Always read the question **twice** and label each step with $\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow}$ or $\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow}$ as required. > 3. **Missing the start symbol clearly.** Always begin the derivation with $S$ (or $E$ for the expression grammar) on a line by itself. > 4. **Skipping intermediate steps.** KTU valuation requires **every** sentential form to be explicitly written. Showing only the start and the final string will fetch **zero** process marks. > 5. **Not identifying the production used.** Always cite the production rule (e.g., $p_1$, $E \rightarrow E + E$) beside each step. > 6. **Claiming LM and RM are always identical.** They are identical **only** for grammars with at most one non-terminal in every sentential form (e.g., $S \rightarrow aSb \mid ab$). For ambiguous grammars, they are typically different in step order. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Leftmost and Rightmost Derivations** > > - **Derivation** = a sequence of rewritings using productions, starting from the start symbol. > - **Sentential form** = any intermediate string in a derivation (may contain non-terminals). > - **Sentence** = a sentential form containing only terminal symbols. > - **Leftmost Derivation (LM):** always replace the **leftmost non-terminal**; denoted $\underset{lm}{\Rightarrow}$; used by **top-down LL parsers**. > - **Rightmost Derivation (RM):** always replace the **rightmost non-terminal**; denoted $\underset{rm}{\Rightarrow}$; used by **bottom-up LR parsers**. > - **Equivalence:** $S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow}_{lm} w \;\iff\; S \overset{*}{\Rightarrow}_{rm} w \;\iff\; \exists$ parse tree for $w$. > - **Uniqueness:** A grammar is **unambiguous** iff every $w \in L(G)$ has exactly one parse tree (equivalently, exactly one LM and one RM derivation). > - **Ambiguity** is detected by exhibiting **two distinct parse trees** (or two distinct LM derivations) for the same string. > - **Key tool for fixing ambiguity:** introduce precedence by stratifying non-terminals (e.g., $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$, $T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$, $F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$). > - **Notation summary:** $\Rightarrow$ (one step), $\overset{*}{\Rightarrow}$ (zero or more steps), $\overset{+}{\Rightarrow}$ (one or more steps). > - **Always** show the **production applied** alongside each derivation step in the KTU exam. > - **Left recursion** in a grammar prevents LL parsers from working — a major motivation for studying derivations carefully.

topics.title: Parse Trees
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Parse Trees # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Parse Tree (Derivation Tree):** A parse tree is an **ordered, rooted tree** that graphically represents the syntactic structure of a string derived from a **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)**. Each internal node is labeled by a **non-terminal** of the grammar, each leaf node is labeled by a **terminal** (or ε), and the root is labeled by the **start symbol** of the grammar. The children of an internal node, read from left to right, correspond to the right-hand side of a production rule used to replace that non-terminal during derivation. Formally, for a CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$, a parse tree satisfies: - Every internal node is labeled by a variable in $V$. - Every leaf is labeled by a symbol in $T \cup \{\varepsilon\}$. - If an internal node is labeled $A$ and its $n$ children (left to right) are labeled $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$, then $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n$ must be a production in $P$. - The **yield** of the parse tree is the string of terminal labels obtained by reading the leaves from left to right. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation > [!NOTE] > **The "Family Tree" Analogy:** Imagine a parse tree as a **family tree for a sentence** in a programming language. The **root** is the great-grandparent (the whole program/start symbol). The **branches** are grammatical categories (like ``, ``, ``), and the **leaves** at the very bottom are the actual words/tokens (like `x`, `+`, `5`, `;`). The tree reveals *how* a sentence was built, step by step, from a starting idea down to the concrete words—just like tracing your ancestry reveals how *you* came to be from your great-grandparent. ## 1.3 Key Terminology You Must Memorize | Term | Definition | KTU Significance | |------|------------|------------------| | **Root** | Topmost node, labeled $S$ (start symbol) | Anchors the entire derivation | | **Internal Node** | Node with one or more children, always a **non-terminal** | Represents intermediate derivation steps | | **Leaf (Vertex)** | Node with no children, labeled by a **terminal** or $\varepsilon$ | The yield is formed from these | | **Yield** | Concatenation of leaves (left-to-right) | The string the tree derives | | **Subtree** | A node + all its descendants | Represents a sub-derivation | | **Height** | Length of the longest path from root to a leaf | Related to derivation length | > [!TIP] > KTU examiners love testing the distinction between **derivation** (a sequence of string rewrites) and **parse tree** (a hierarchical structural diagram). Remember: a parse tree **ignores the ORDER of rule application** but **captures the structure**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Construction Logic of a Parse Tree — Step-by-Step The construction of a parse tree for an input string $w$ proceeds as follows: 1. **Initialize:** Mark the root of the tree with the start symbol $S$. 2. **Expand:** If a non-terminal $A$ appears at a leaf, and a production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n$ exists in $P$, then create $n$ children for that leaf, labeled $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ (left to right). 3. **Terminate:** When no leaf is labeled by a non-terminal (i.e., all leaves are terminals or $\varepsilon$), the tree is **complete**. 4. **Extract Yield:** Read all leaf labels left-to-right. The result is the derived string. 5. **Validation:** A string $w \in L(G)$ **if and only if** there exists a parse tree with yield $w$. ## 2.2 Why Parse Trees Matter — The "Why" Behind Them - **Compiler Design (Practical Engineering Utility):** The frontend of every compiler (LEX/YACC, ANTLR, Bison) constructs parse trees (or abstract syntax trees — ASTs) to perform semantic analysis. Without parse trees, type checking and code generation would be impossible. - **Theoretical Bridge:** They provide a **graphical, order-independent representation** of derivations, allowing us to prove that leftmost and rightmost derivations correspond to the *same* tree. - **Ambiguity Detection:** A grammar that generates two or more distinct parse trees for the same string is **ambiguous** — a critical concept in KTU Module 2. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Property / Formula | Statement | Exam Tip | |---|---------------------|-----------|----------| | 1 | $w \in L(G) \iff \exists$ parse tree with yield $w$ | Membership equivalence | Used in nearly every proof | | 2 | A parse tree is **ordered** | Left-to-right ordering of children is significant | Distinguishes from general trees | | 3 | **Innermost** leaves = terminals of yield | Read left to right | Never miss this in yield questions | | 4 | Number of leaves $= \mid w \mid + k$ | Where $k$ = number of $\varepsilon$-productions used | Useful in counting problems | | 5 | Height $\geq 1$ | Tree with $S$ directly yielding a terminal has height $1$ | For trivial grammars | | 6 | $\text{Yield}(T) = w$ | Yield is a string in $L(G)$ | Core definition | | 7 | Ambiguity condition | $\exists w \in L(G)$ with **two distinct** parse trees | Definition of ambiguity | > [!WARNING] > **Vertical Pipe Constraint (Rendering Safety):** In the KTU answer book, write absolute values as $\vert x \vert$ or use $\mid$ — never as `|x|` inside a markdown table row, as it breaks column alignment. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In **compiler construction**, the parse tree is the foundational data structure produced by the **syntax analysis phase** (the second phase after lexical analysis). It is then: - Pruned to form an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** for semantic analysis. - Traversed to generate **intermediate code** (three-address code, bytecode). - Used in **error recovery** to find the nearest valid continuation. In **natural language processing (NLP)**, parse trees (constituency trees) underpin modern transformer-based parsers used by search engines and chatbots. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Constructing a Parse Tree **Given Grammar:** $$G: S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$$ **Task:** Construct the parse tree for $w = aabb$. ### Step-by-Step Construction **Step 1:** Place start symbol at root. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Root:} \quad S \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2:** Apply $S \rightarrow aSb$ (leftmost non-terminal $S$ is the only one). $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Root expanded to three children: } a, S, b \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Apply $S \rightarrow aSb$ again to the middle child $S$. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Root's middle child } S \text{ now has children } a, S, b \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Apply $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ to terminate the middle $S$. $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Middle } S \text{ becomes leaf } \varepsilon \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5:** The yield is formed by reading all leaves left-to-right: $$w = a \cdot a \cdot \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot b = aabb$$ **Result:** Parse tree has height 3, contains **5 leaves** (4 terminals + 1 $\varepsilon$). ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Distinguishing Derivations from Parse Trees **Given Grammar:** $$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E \times E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$$ **Task:** Show that the string $\text{id} + \text{id} \times \text{id}$ admits **two different parse trees** (proving ambiguity). ### Tree 1 (Groups `+` first) Yields structure: $((\text{id}) + (\text{id})) \times (\text{id})$ - Root: $E$ - Children: $E$, $+$, $E$, $\times$, $E$ - Left $E \rightarrow (E) \rightarrow (\text{id})$ - Middle $E \rightarrow (E) \rightarrow (\text{id})$ - Right $E \rightarrow \text{id}$ ### Tree 2 (Groups `×` first) Yields structure: $(\text{id}) + ((\text{id}) \times (\text{id}))$ - Root: $E$ - Children: $E$, $+$, $E$ - Left $E \rightarrow \text{id}$ - Right $E \rightarrow E \times E \rightarrow \text{id} \times \text{id}$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Two distinct parse trees imply two distinct **leftmost derivations** (and rightmost derivations). This is the **syntactic signature of ambiguity**. KTU frequently asks to "prove that a grammar is ambiguous by exhibiting two parse trees." ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Counting & Structural Properties For a parse tree $T$ with yield $w$: | Property | Formula | Reasoning | |----------|---------|-----------| | Leaf count | $L = \vert w \vert + n_\varepsilon$ | Each $\varepsilon$-production adds one extra leaf | | Internal node count | $I = \sum_{A \in V} n_A$ | Sum of all non-terminal occurrences | | Branching factor | $b_i$ at node $i$ | Equals the RHS length of the rule applied | | Total nodes | $N = L + I$ | Leaves + Internal | | Height (recursive) | $h(T) = 1 + \max_i h(T_i)$ | $1$ for terminal/$\varepsilon$ leaves | | Yield uniqueness | $\text{Yield}(T)$ is unique | Order-independent of derivation sequence | ## 3.4 Theorem — Equivalence of Derivations and Parse Trees > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem (Hopcroft-Ullman):** Let $G = (V, T, P, S)$ be a CFG. A string $w \in T^*$ is in $L(G)$ **if and only if** there exists a parse tree with root $S$ and yield $w$. **Proof Sketch (Leftmost $\Rightarrow$ Tree):** Given a leftmost derivation $S = \alpha_0 \Rightarrow \alpha_1 \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow \alpha_n = w$, build the tree top-down. Each step that replaces a non-terminal $A$ by a production $A \rightarrow X_1 \cdots X_k$ corresponds to expanding the leaf labeled $A$ into $k$ children. By induction, the yield of the tree is exactly $w$. **Proof Sketch (Tree $\Rightarrow$ Leftmost):** Given a parse tree, perform a post-order traversal. At each internal node labeled $A$ with children $X_1 \cdots X_k$, apply the production $A \rightarrow X_1 \cdots X_k$ to the leftmost occurrence of $A$ in the current sentential form. The final sentential form equals the yield. ## 3.5 Inherent Ambiguity — A Subtle Theorem > [!WARNING] > **Definition (Inherently Ambiguous Language):** A context-free language $L$ is **inherently ambiguous** if **every** grammar $G$ such that $L(G) = L$ is ambiguous. Equivalently, $L$ is inherently ambiguous if there exists at least one string in $L$ that has **two distinct parse trees in every grammar** for $L$. **Classic Example:** $L = \{a^n b^n c^m \mid n, m \geq 1\} \cup \{a^n b^m c^m \mid n, m \geq 1\}$ is the textbook example of an inherently ambiguous CFL (Parikh's theorem applies). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Parse Tree for $w = aabb$ using $S \rightarrow aSb \mid \varepsilon$ ```mermaid graph TD nodeS["S"] nodeA1["a"] nodeS2["S"] nodeB1["b"] nodeA2["a"] nodeS3["S"] nodeB2["b"] nodeEps["eps"] nodeS --> nodeA1 nodeS --> nodeS2 nodeS --> nodeB1 nodeS2 --> nodeA2 nodeS2 --> nodeS3 nodeS2 --> nodeB2 nodeS3 --> nodeEps ``` **Yield (read leaves left-to-right):** $a \cdot a \cdot \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot b = aabb$ ✅ ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Parse Tree in a Compiler ```mermaid flowchart TD src["Source Code: x = a + b"] --> lex["Lexical Analyzer
Token Stream"] lex --> parser["Syntax Analyzer
CFG Rules + Parse Tree Builder"] cfg["Context Free Grammar G"] --> parser parser --> ptree["Parse Tree
Full Syntactic Structure"] ptree --> ast["Abstract Syntax Tree
Pruned Form"] ast --> sem["Semantic Analyzer
Type Checking"] sem --> ir["Intermediate Code
Three Address Code"] ir --> opt["Optimizer"] opt --> code["Target Code
Machine Code"] ``` ## 4.3 Parse Tree for $\text{id} + \text{id} \times \text{id}$ (Tree 1 — `+` first) ```mermaid graph TD nodeE1["E"] nodeE2["E"] nodePlus["+"] nodeE3["E"] nodeMul["x"] nodeE4["E"] nodeLP1["("] nodeE5["E"] nodeRP1[")"] nodeId1["id"] nodeE6["E"] nodeLP2["("] nodeE7["E"] nodeRP2[")"] nodeId2["id"] nodeE8["E"] nodeId3["id"] nodeE1 --> nodeE2 nodeE1 --> nodePlus nodeE1 --> nodeE3 nodeE1 --> nodeMul nodeE1 --> nodeE4 nodeE2 --> nodeLP1 nodeE2 --> nodeE5 nodeE2 --> nodeRP1 nodeE5 --> nodeId1 nodeE3 --> nodeLP2 nodeE3 --> nodeE7 nodeE3 --> nodeRP2 nodeE7 --> nodeId2 nodeE4 --> nodeId3 ``` ## 4.4 Parse Tree for $\text{id} + \text{id} \times \text{id}$ (Tree 2 — `×` first) ```mermaid graph TD nE1["E"] nE2["E"] nPlus["+"] nE3["E"] nId1["id"] nE4["E"] nMul["x"] nE5["E"] nId2["id"] nId3["id"] nE1 --> nE2 nE1 --> nPlus nE1 --> nE3 nE2 --> nId1 nE3 --> nE4 nE3 --> nMul nE3 --> nE5 nE4 --> nId2 nE5 --> nId3 ``` > [!NOTE] > Both trees derive the same string but have **different branching structures at the root** (Tree 1 has 5 children, Tree 2 has 3 children). This is the **defining hallmark of grammar ambiguity**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define a parse tree. What is the yield of a parse tree?** **Model Answer:** A parse tree is an ordered, rooted tree representing the syntactic structure of a string derived from a CFG. The root is labeled by the start symbol $S$, internal nodes are labeled by non-terminals, and leaves are labeled by terminals or $\varepsilon$. The **yield** is the string obtained by reading the leaf labels from left to right. If a parse tree has yield $w$, then $w \in L(G)$. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **What is meant by an ambiguous grammar? Give one example.** **Model Answer:** A CFG is **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string in its language that has **two or more distinct parse trees** (equivalently, two distinct leftmost derivations). **Example:** The grammar $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E \times E \mid \text{id}$ is ambiguous because the string $\text{id} + \text{id} \times \text{id}$ has two distinct parse trees differing in the grouping of `+` and `×`. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified 2024 Scheme]` **a)** Define a parse tree formally. For the grammar below, construct the parse tree for the string $00110$ and find its yield. **(7 marks)** $$S \rightarrow 0S1 \mid 0A \mid \varepsilon$$ $$A \rightarrow 1A \mid \varepsilon$$ **Model Solution:** **Formal Definition:** A parse tree for a CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is a rooted, ordered tree where: - The root is labeled $S$. - Each internal node is labeled by a non-terminal in $V$. - Each leaf is labeled by a terminal in $T$ or $\varepsilon$. - If internal node $A$ has children $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$ (left to right), then $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n \in P$. **Parse Tree Construction for $00110$:** Step 1: Apply $S \rightarrow 0S1$. Now we need $S \Rightarrow 0110$. Step 2: Apply $S \rightarrow 0A$. Then $A$ must produce $11$. Step 3: Apply $A \rightarrow 1A$. Then $A$ must produce $1$. Step 4: Apply $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$. So the second $A$ yields $\varepsilon$, and the previous $A$ yields $1$. Step 5: Apply $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ for the final $S$.
