- [ ] **PDA for $\{w c w^R\}$** is deterministic because the marker $c$ identifies the middle; uses 3 states and stack $\{Z_0, A, B\}$.
- [ ] **Tracing a string** is the *only* way to verify a PDA construction — KTU examiners award 2–3 marks for an explicit trace.
- [ ] **Non-determinism is essential** for CFLs not in DCFL; examples include $\{ww^R\}$, the language of palindromes, $\{a^n b^m c^n d^m\}$, and others.
- [ ] **PDAs are equivalent to CFGs** — every CFL has a PDA and vice versa (this is the topic of Module 4 conversion).
- [ ] **The initial stack symbol $Z_0$** is *always* present at the bottom — it cannot be popped until the very end (in empty-stack acceptance).
- [ ] **In empty-stack acceptance**, the set of final states $F$ may be empty (or even $\emptyset$) — acceptance is decided by stack emptiness alone.
- [ ] **Real-world use:** PDA theory is the foundation of **compiler syntax analysis** (parsing), used in tools like YACC, Bison, ANTLR, and LALR(1) parser generators.
- [ ] **Hierarchy to remember:** $\text{DFA} \subsetneq \text{NFA} \subsetneq \text{DPDA} \subsetneq \text{NPDA} \equiv \text{CFG/CFL} \subsetneq \text{LBA} \subsetneq \text{TM}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equivalence of NPDAs and CFGs - Conversions in Both Directions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equivalence of NPDAs and CFGs - Conversions in Both Directions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Equivalence Theorem (NPDAs ↔ CFGs):**
A language $L$ is a **Context-Free Language (CFL)** if and only if there exists a **Non-deterministic Pushdown Automaton (NPDA)** $M$ such that $L = L(M)$. Equivalently, the class of languages accepted by NPDAs is **exactly** the class of languages generated by Context-Free Grammars (CFGs).

$$\text{CFLs} \;\equiv\; \{L(M) \mid M \text{ is an NPDA}\} \;\equiv\; \{L(G) \mid G \text{ is a CFG}\}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Statement:** The equivalence is established by giving explicit, constructive proofs in both directions. Deterministic PDAs (DPDAs) are NOT equivalent to CFGs in general — only NPDAs enjoy this equivalence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Recipe and the Chef"

Imagine a **CFG as a recipe book** (rules for building sentences) and an **NPDA as a chef** with a single mental notepad (the stack) who can read the recipe in any non-deterministic order:

- **CFG → NPDA** is like giving a chef a recipe and watching him cook, using his notepad (stack) to track what ingredients (variables) still need to be added.
- **NPDA → CFG** is like observing the chef and writing down his *behavioral patterns* as a formal recipe.

Both produce the **same set of valid dishes (language)**, but one is generative (recipe) and the other is recognizing (chef with notepad).

## 1.3 Key Components of the Equivalence

| Direction | What is Given | What is Constructed | Acceptance Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| **CFG → NPDA** | CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | NPDA $M$ | Empty Stack (or Final State) |
| **NPDA → CFG** | NPDA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ | CFG $G$ | Generative (Start variable $S$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Stack Initialization Symbol Convention:** The initial stack symbol is denoted $Z_0$ and is **never erased** until acceptance — this guarantees the NPDA processes a finite input string correctly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Direction 1 — CFG to NPDA (Simulation of Leftmost Derivations)

### Construction Logic (Step-by-Step Intuition)

The NPDA simulates **leftmost derivations** of the CFG. The stack always holds a *suffix* of the current left-sentential form.

**Input Symbols Mapped to PDA Actions:**
- If the stack top is a **terminal** $a$ and the next input symbol is $a$ → **pop $a$** (matched).
- If the stack top is a **variable** $A$ → **non-deterministically** choose a production $A \to \alpha$ and **replace $A$ with $\alpha$** on the stack.
- If the stack is empty (only $Z_0$ left, then popped) → **accept**.

### Formal Construction

Given CFG $G = (V, T, P, S)$, construct NPDA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q, Z_0, \emptyset)$ with $Q = \{q\}$, $\Sigma = T$, $\Gamma = V \cup T$, and **transition function** $\delta$:

$$
\delta(q, a, a) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\} \quad \forall\, a \in \Sigma
$$

This is the **"pop-a-terminal" rule**: matches input terminal with stack top terminal.

$$
\delta(q, \varepsilon, A) = \{(q, \alpha) \mid A \to \alpha \in P\} \quad \forall\, A \in V
$$

This is the **"expand-a-variable" rule**: non-deterministically replaces variable $A$ with RHS $\alpha$.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** The transition function uses **$\varepsilon$-moves** when expanding variables, meaning the PDA does NOT consume input while expanding. This is the *non-determinism* that lets it try all productions in parallel.

### Acceptance Condition
The input $w$ is accepted if $M$ empties its stack after reading $w$, i.e.,

$$w \in L(M) \iff (q, w, Z_0) \vdash_M^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$$

## 2.2 Direction 2 — NPDA to CFG (Encoding PDA Computations as Grammar Rules)

### Core Idea
The CFG $G$ **generates strings that correspond to valid PDA computations**. Variables are encoded as triples $[qXp]$ representing the idea:

> *"Starting in state $q$ with $X$ on the stack, the sub-string of input consumed reaches state $p$ with $X$ removed (popped)."*

### Variable Set Construction
The variables of $G$ are:

$$V = \{[qXp] \mid q, p \in Q,\; X \in \Gamma\}$$

The **start variable** is $S$, with productions $S \to [q_0 Z_0 q]$ for every $q \in Q$ (allowing the PDA to end in any state).

### Two Types of Productions (Critical for KTU 14-Mark Answers)

**Type 1 Productions — Simulating a single PDA step (no intermediate stack changes):**

For every transition $\delta(q, a, X) \ni (r, Y_1 Y_2 \ldots Y_k)$ where $k \ge 1$:

$$
[qX p_k] \to a\,[rY_1 p_1]\,[rY_2 p_2] \cdots [rY_k p_k]
$$

where $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k$ are **fresh state variables** that allow the computation to "thread" through the stack.

**Type 2 Productions — "Stack-erosion" (intermediate pops yield empty stack):**

For $k = 2$ and transitions $\delta(q, a, X) \ni (r, YZ)$:

$$
[qXp_s] \to a\,[rYq_1]\,[q_1 Z p_s]
$$

The intermediate state $q_1$ is the "checkpoint" where $Y$ has been popped and $Z$ becomes the active stack symbol.

**Type 3 Productions — Bypassing state-symbol pairs (epsilon shortcuts):**

For all $q \in Q$ and $X \in \Gamma$:

$$
[qXq] \to \varepsilon
$$

This handles the case where $X$ is pushed and immediately popped, producing no net stack change.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Element | CFG → NPDA | NPDA → CFG |
|---|---|---|
| **Input** | $G = (V, T, P, S)$ | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ |
| **Output** | $M = (\{q\}, T, V \cup T, \delta, q, Z_0, \emptyset)$ | $G = (V', \Sigma, P', S)$ |
| **Terminals** | $T$ (preserved) | $\Sigma$ (preserved) |
| **Variables** | — | $V' = \{[qXp] \mid q, p \in Q, X \in \Gamma\}$ |
| **Start Symbol** | — | $S \to [q_0 Z_0 q]$ for $\forall q \in Q$ |
| **Terminal-Stack Rule** | $\delta(q, a, a) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$ | Encoded in Type 1 productions |
| **Variable-Stack Rule** | $\delta(q, \varepsilon, A) = \{(q, \alpha)\}$ | Encoded via Type 1 (with $a = \varepsilon$) |
| **Acceptance** | Empty stack $(q, w, Z_0) \vdash^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$ | $L(G) = \{w \mid S \Rightarrow^* w\}$ |
| **Non-determinism** | $\varepsilon$-moves on $A$ | Multiple RHS for one LHS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** This equivalence is the foundation of **parser generators** like YACC, ANTLR, and Bison. A CFG defines the language (program syntax), and an equivalent PDA is the *parsing engine* that recognizes valid programs. Compiler design depends directly on this theorem.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — CFG to NPDA Conversion

**Given CFG $G$ with productions:**
$$
S \to aSb \mid \varepsilon
$$

This generates the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \ge 0\}$.

**Step 1: Identify components**
- $V = \{S\}$, $T = \{a, b\}$, $P = \{S \to aSb, \; S \to \varepsilon\}$, $S$ is start.

**Step 2: Construct NPDA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q, Z_0, \emptyset)$**
- $Q = \{q\}$, $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$, $\Gamma = \{S, a, b\}$, $Z_0 = S$.

**Step 3: Build the transition function $\delta$**

**Rule (i)** — Match terminal with stack top:
$$
\delta(q, a, a) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}, \qquad \delta(q, b, b) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}
$$

**Rule (ii)** — Expand variable $S$ (non-deterministic choice of productions):
$$
\delta(q, \varepsilon, S) = \{(q, aSb), \; (q, \varepsilon)\}
$$

**Step 4: Trace the computation for input $w = aabb$**

We want to show $(q, aabb, S) \vdash_M^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$.

**Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q, aabb, S) &\vdash (q, aabb, aSb) && \text{[Use } S \to aSb \text{ via Rule (ii)]} \\
&\vdash (q, abb, Sb)   && \text{[Match } a \text{ with stack top } a \text{ via Rule (i)]} \\
&\vdash (q, abb, aSbb) && \text{[Use } S \to aSb \text{ via Rule (ii)]} \\
&\vdash (q, bb, Sbb)   && \text{[Match } a \text{ via Rule (i)]} \\
&\vdash (q, bb, bb)    && \text{[Use } S \to \varepsilon \text{ via Rule (ii)]} \\
&\vdash (q, b, b)      && \text{[Match } b \text{ via Rule (i)]} \\
&\vdash (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon) && \text{[Match } b \text{ via Rule (i) — ACCEPT]}
\end{aligned}
$$

The string $aabb$ is **accepted by empty stack**. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Explained:** The PDA pushes derivations onto the stack (Rule ii) and consumes terminals as they match the stack (Rule i). This precisely mirrors a **leftmost derivation** where the remaining sentential form is always on the stack.

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — NPDA to CFG Conversion

**Given NPDA $M$ with $Q = \{q_0, q_1\}$, $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$, $\Gamma = \{Z_0, X\}$ and transitions:**

$$
\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, XZ_0)\}
$$
$$
\delta(q_0, b, X) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}
$$
$$
\delta(q_1, b, Z_0) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}
$$

This PDA accepts $L = \{a b^n \mid n \ge 1\}$.

**Step 1: Identify variables of $G$**

$$
V' = \{[q_i X_j q_k] \mid q_i, q_k \in \{q_0, q_1\},\; X_j \in \{Z_0, X\}\}
$$

There are $2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8$ variables: $[q_0 Z_0 q_0], [q_0 Z_0 q_1], [q_0 X q_0], \ldots, [q_1 X q_1]$.

**Step 2: Start variable productions**

$$
S \to [q_0 Z_0 q_0] \mid [q_0 Z_0 q_1]
$$

(Both endings are allowed since the PDA may halt in either state.)

**Step 3: Build Type 1 productions from each transition**

**From $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, XZ_0)\}$:**

For each possible ending state $p \in \{q_0, q_1\}$, introduce an intermediate state variable $r$:

$$
[q_0 Z_0 p] \to a\,[q_0 X r]\,[r Z_0 p] \quad \text{for all } r, p \in \{q_0, q_1\}
$$

This yields $4$ productions. For example:

$$
[q_0 Z_0 q_0] \to a\,[q_0 X q_0]\,[q_0 Z_0 q_0]
$$
$$
[q_0 Z_0 q_1] \to a\,[q_0 X q_0]\,[q_0 Z_0 q_1]
$$
$$
[q_0 Z_0 q_0] \to a\,[q_0 X q_1]\,[q_1 Z_0 q_0]
$$
$$
[q_0 Z_0 q_1] \to a\,[q_0 X q_1]\,[q_1 Z_0 q_1]
$$

**From $\delta(q_0, b, X) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$:**

Here the stack-top $X$ is popped (replaced by $\varepsilon$), so we use the **bypass** form:

$$
[q_0 X p] \to b \quad \text{only if } p = q_1
$$

That is, $[q_0 X q_1] \to b$ (the only way to "consume $b$" while popping $X$ ending in state $q_1$).

**From $\delta(q_1, b, Z_0) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$:**

Similarly:

$$
[q_1 Z_0 q_1] \to b
$$

**Step 4: Derive the string $w = abb$ from $S$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &\Rightarrow [q_0 Z_0 q_1]   && \text{[Apply } S \to [q_0 Z_0 q_1]] \\
  &\Rightarrow a\,[q_0 X q_1]\,[q_1 Z_0 q_1] && \text{[Use first transition rule]} \\
  &\Rightarrow a\,b\,[q_1 Z_0 q_1]   && \text{[Apply } [q_0 X q_1] \to b] \\
  &\Rightarrow a\,b\,b   && \text{[Apply } [q_1 Z_0 q_1] \to b]
\end{aligned}
$$

The string $abb$ is **derived** from $S$. ✓

## 3.3 Proof Skeleton — Why Equivalence Holds

**Claim:** $L(G) = L(M)$ for the constructed $M$ from $G$.

**Proof Direction ($\supseteq$):** If $S \Rightarrow^* w$ via leftmost derivation $S = \alpha_0 \Rightarrow \alpha_1 \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow \alpha_n = w$, then the PDA $M$ mimics each step:
- Variable expansion $\to$ $\varepsilon$-transition using Rule (ii).
- Terminal match $\to$ input-consuming transition using Rule (i).
- Empty stack on RHS $\to$ stack becomes empty.

**Proof Direction ($\subseteq$):** If $(q, w, Z_0) \vdash_M^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$, then trace the sequence of moves *backwards*. Each $\varepsilon$-move on variable $A$ corresponds to a production $A \to \alpha$ of $G$, and each terminal pop corresponds to reading that terminal in $w$. The trace reconstructs a leftmost derivation of $w$ from $S$.

$$\therefore \;\; L(G) = L(M) \;\;\blacksquare$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — CFG to NPDA Conversion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CFG G with productions P] --> B[Construct NPDA M]
    B --> C[State Set Q = single state q]
    B --> D[Stack Alphabet Gamma = V union T]
    B --> E[Start Stack Symbol Z0 = S]
    B --> F[Transition Function delta]
    F --> F1[Rule 1: delta q a a = pop epsilon]
    F --> F2[Rule 2: delta q epsilon A = push alpha]
    F1 --> G[NPDA M accepts via empty stack]
    F2 --> G
    G --> H[Language L is preserved]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — NPDA to CFG Conversion Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[NPDA M with transition function delta] --> Q[Enumerate all state pairs q p]
    Q --> R[Create variable qX p for each pair and stack symbol X]
    R --> S[Define start productions S to q0 Z0 p]
    S --> T[Type 1: qX pk to a times rY1 p1 ... rYk pk]
    S --> U[Type 2: Bypass with intermediate states]
    S --> V[Type 3: qX q to epsilon for self-loop pops]
    T --> W[Final CFG G]
    U --> W
    V --> W
    W --> X[Same language L as original NPDA]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Flow of Equivalence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT[CFG DOMAIN]
        G1[Grammar G] --> G2[Production Rules P]
        G2 --> G3[Generates Language L]
    end
    subgraph RIGHT[NPDA DOMAIN]
        M1[PDA M] --> M2[Stack and States]
        M2 --> M3[Accepts Language L]
    end
    G3 <-->|Conversion 1| M2
    G2 <-->|Conversion 2| M1
    LEFT ~~~ RIGHT
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Simulation Cycle of CFG → NPDA

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ReadInput
    ReadInput --> MatchTerminal: Stack top is terminal a, input is a
    ReadInput --> ExpandVariable: Stack top is variable A
    MatchTerminal --> ReadInput: Pop a, advance input
    ExpandVariable --> ReadInput: Choose production A to alpha, push alpha
    ReadInput --> Accept: Stack empty and input consumed
    Accept --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Q:** State the equivalence theorem between NPDAs and CFGs.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> A language $L$ is a context-free language (i.e., generated by some CFG) if and only if $L$ is accepted by some Non-deterministic Pushdown Automaton (NPDA) by empty stack. In symbols: $\mathcal{L}(\text{CFG}) = \mathcal{L}(\text{NPDA})$. This is established by two constructive proofs: (1) given a CFG, we build an NPDA that simulates leftmost derivations by storing the sentential form on the stack; (2) given an NPDA, we build a CFG whose variables are triples $[qXp]$ encoding PDA stack-and-state configurations. **[3 marks]**

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q:** In the CFG-to-NPDA construction, what do the two rules of the transition function $\delta$ represent?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Rule 1:** $\delta(q, a, a) = \{(q, \varepsilon)\}$ — This rule handles **terminal matching**: when the input symbol equals the stack-top terminal, the terminal is popped and the input head advances. It corresponds to consuming a terminal in the derived string. **[1.5 marks]**
>
> **Rule 2:** $\delta(q, \varepsilon, A) = \{(q, \alpha) \mid A \to \alpha \in P\}$ — This rule handles **variable expansion**: when the stack top is a variable $A$, the PDA non-deterministically selects a production $A \to \alpha$ and pushes $\alpha$ onto the stack without consuming any input. **[1.5 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Question: Construct an NPDA equivalent to the CFG $G$ with the following productions:
$$
S \to aS \mid bA, \qquad A \to bA \mid aB \mid b, \qquad B \to aB \mid \varepsilon
$$

### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Construct the NPDA and write its transition function.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Components**
- $V = \{S, A, B\}$, $T = \{a, b\}$
- $\Sigma = T = \{a, b\}$, $\Gamma = V \cup T = \{S, A, B, a, b\}$
- $Q = \{q\}$, $Z_0 = S$, $F = \emptyset$ (acceptance by empty stack)

**Step 2: Terminal matching rules (Rule 1)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q, a, a) &= \{(q, \varepsilon)\} \\
\delta(q, b, b) &= \{(q, \varepsilon)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Variable expansion rules (Rule 2)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q, \varepsilon, S) &= \{(q, aS), \; (q, bA)\} \\
\delta(q, \varepsilon, A) &= \{(q, bA), \; (q, aB), \; (q, b)\} \\
\delta(q, \varepsilon, B) &= \{(q, aB), \; (q, \varepsilon)\}
\end{aligned}
$$

This NPDA $M$ is the answer for part (a). **[7 marks]**

### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Trace the computation of the NPDA on the string $w = abba$.

**Solution:**

We need to show $(q, abba, S) \vdash_M^* (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon)$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
(q, abba, S) &\vdash (q, abba, bA)   && S \to bA \\
&\vdash (q, bba, A)                  && \text{Match } b \\
&\vdash (q, bba, b)                  && A \to b \\
&\vdash (q, ba, b)                   && \text{Match } b \\
&\vdash (q, ba, aB)                  && A \to aB \\
&\vdash (q, a, B)                    && \text{Match } a \\
&\vdash (q, a, aB)                   && B \to aB \\
&\vdash (q, \varepsilon, B)          && \text{Match } a \\
&\vdash (q, \varepsilon, \varepsilon) && B \to \varepsilon \;\; \text{— ACCEPT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The string $abba$ is accepted. **[7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]`

### Question: Convert the following NPDA $M$ to an equivalent CFG.

NPDA $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, Z_0, F)$ where:
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{Z_0, X\}$
- Transitions:
  - $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, XZ_0)\}$
  - $\delta(q_0, a, X) = \{(q_0, XX)\}$
  - $\delta(q_0, b, X) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$
  - $\delta(q_1, b, X) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$
  - $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$

This PDA accepts strings of the form $a^n b^n$.

### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Identify the variables of the equivalent CFG.

**Solution:**

Variables are of the form $[q_i X_j q_k]$ where $q_i, q_k \in \{q_0, q_1\}$ and $X_j \in \{Z_0, X\}$.

**Enumerated variable set (8 variables):**
$$
[q_0 Z_0 q_0], \quad [q_0 Z_0 q_1], \quad [q_0 X q_0], \quad [q_0 X q_1]
$$
$$
[q_1 Z_0 q_0], \quad [q_1 Z_0 q_1], \quad [q_1 X q_0], \quad [q_1 X q_1]
$$

**Start variable productions:**
$$
S \to [q_0 Z_0 q_0] \mid [q_0 Z_0 q_1]
$$

**Reasoning:** The PDA starts in $q_0$ with $Z_0$ on stack and may finish in either $q_0$ or $q_1$. We must allow both endings. **[7 marks]**

### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Construct the Type 1, Type 2, and Type 3 productions.

**Solution:**

**Type 1 Productions — From $\delta(q_0, a, Z_0) = \{(q_0, XZ_0)\}$:**

Pushing two symbols requires intermediate states. For all $r, p \in \{q_0, q_1\}$:

$$
[q_0 Z_0 p] \to a\,[q_0 X r]\,[r Z_0 p]
$$

This gives $4$ productions. Example:

$$
[q_0 Z_0 q_1] \to a\,[q_0 X q_0]\,[q_0 Z_0 q_1]
$$
$$
[q_0 Z_0 q_1] \to a\,[q_0 X q_1]\,[q_1 Z_0 q_1]
$$

**Type 1 Productions — From $\delta(q_0, a, X) = \{(q_0, XX)\}$:**

Similarly:

$$
[q_0 X p] \to a\,[q_0 X r]\,[r X p] \quad \forall\, r, p \in \{q_0, q_1\}
$$

**Type 2 Productions — From $\delta(q_0, b, X) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$:**

Stack-top is popped, so:

$$
[q_0 X q_1] \to b
$$

(Only ending in $q_1$ is valid because that is the destination state.)

**Type 2 Productions — From $\delta(q_1, b, X) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$:**

$$
[q_1 X q_1] \to b
$$

**Type 2 Productions — From $\delta(q_1, \varepsilon, Z_0) = \{(q_1, \varepsilon)\}$:**

$$
[q_1 Z_0 q_1] \to \varepsilon
$$

**Type 3 Productions — Bypasses for self-loops:**

$$
[q_0 Z_0 q_0] \to \varepsilon, \qquad [q_1 Z_0 q_1] \to \varepsilon, \qquad [q_0 X q_0] \to \varepsilon, \qquad [q_1 X q_1] \to \varepsilon
$$

(The remaining four pairs are eliminated as they lead to dead ends.)

**Final verification:** The grammar $G$ derived above generates $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \ge 1\}$, matching the PDA. **[7 marks]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting intermediate states** in Type 1 productions: When a transition pushes $k \ge 2$ symbols, students often write $[qXp] \to a\,[rY_1][rY_2]$ — but the *second* variable's starting state must be a *fresh* state variable $p_1$, not $r$. This costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing up $a$ (input) with $\varepsilon$**: Variable expansion transitions use $\varepsilon$ as input (Rule 2), while terminal matching uses $a \in \Sigma$ (Rule 1). Writing $\delta(q, a, A)$ instead of $\delta(q, \varepsilon, A)$ is a **fatal error** worth **3 marks**.
> 3. **Omitting the start productions $S \to [q_0 Z_0 q]$ for all $q$**: Students often write only $S \to [q_0 Z_0 q_0]$. This restricts accepted strings unnecessarily. Deduct **1 mark**.
> 4. **Wrong choice of acceptance mode**: The CFG→NPDA construction uses **empty-stack acceptance** with $F = \emptyset$. Writing $F = \{q\}$ (final-state acceptance) and forgetting to add a transition to empty the stack loses **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-Save Before Exam):**

- **Equivalence Statement:** CFLs $\equiv$ languages accepted by NPDAs (NOT DPDAs in general). **[Must Memorize]**
- **CFG → NPDA:** Two transition rules only — terminal-match (Rule 1) and variable-expand (Rule 2). Use a **single state** $q$ and **empty-stack acceptance**.
- **NPDA → CFG:** Variables are triples $[qXp]$. Two production types — Type 1 (push, with intermediate states) and Type 2 (pop, bypass form).
- **Start Variable in NPDA → CFG:** $S \to [q_0 Z_0 q]$ for **every** $q \in Q$.
- **Type 1 push of $k$ symbols** requires $k$ fresh state variables $p_1, \ldots, p_k$ to thread the computation.
- **Type 2 pop** (one-symbol replacement by $\varepsilon$): $[qXp] \to a$ where $p$ must equal the destination state of the transition.
- **DFA vs. PDA Equivalence:** DFA $\equiv$ Regular Grammar (no stack). NPDA $\equiv$ CFG (with one stack).
- **Deterministic vs. Non-deterministic:** The conversion is **not symmetric** — DPDAs accept a *strict subset* of CFLs (the DCFLs), so DPDAs do NOT have a CFG equivalent in general.
- **Compiler Connection:** This theorem is the mathematical foundation of **LALR(1) and LL(k) parsers** used in compiler design.
- **Proof Technique:** Use induction on the length of the leftmost derivation (CFG→NPDA) or number of PDA moves (NPDA→CFG).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simplification of Context-Free Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Simplification of Context-Free Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Context-Free Grammar (CFG)** $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is said to be in a *simplified form* when it has been purged of all "redundant" or "problematic" constructs that do not contribute to the language generated, while still generating the **exact same language** $L(G)$. The KTU 2024 Scheme identifies four canonical simplification steps:

1. **Removal of Useless Symbols** (non-generating + non-reachable)
2. **Removal of Unit Productions** (renaming rules of the form $A \rightarrow B$)
3. **Removal of $\varepsilon$-productions** (null rules of the form $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$)
4. **Conversion to Normal Forms** (Chomsky Normal Form, Greibach Normal Form)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Useless Symbol**: A variable $X \in V$ is *useless* if either it cannot derive any terminal string (**non-generating**) or it cannot be reached from the start symbol $S$ (**non-reachable**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**: Every production is of the form $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$, where $A, B, C \in V$ and $a \in T$. The start symbol $S$ is not allowed on the RHS of any production.

> [!NOTE]
> **Greibach Normal Form (GNF)**: Every production is of the form $A \rightarrow a\alpha$, where $a \in T$ and $\alpha \in V^{\ast}$ (a string of variables, possibly empty).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spring Cleaning"
Imagine your study desk piled high with notes, old drafts, and a single working notebook. *Simplification* is the process of:
- **Throwing away drafts that lead nowhere** (useless symbols that generate no terminal string).
- **Removing sticky notes that just point to other notes** (unit productions $A \rightarrow B$).
- **Deleting empty pages that say "blank"** ($\varepsilon$-productions, if the language itself does not require the empty string).
- **Re-paginating** the remaining notes into a strict two-format standard (CNF/GNF) so a *parser-bot* (CYK algorithm) can read them efficiently.

The "language" of meaningful study material is **preserved**, but the rules governing the notes are now clean, minimal, and uniform.

### 1.3 Why Simplify? — Engineering Motivation
- **Compiler Construction**: Tools like **CYK parsing** and **LL/LR parsers** require CFGs in restricted forms (CNF, GNF) for guaranteed $O(n^3)$ or linear-time parsing.
- **Decidability**: Many CFL decision problems (emptiness, membership) become tractable on simplified grammars.
- **Equivalence Checking**: Reduced grammars enable comparison of two CFGs.
- **Pedagogical Clarity**: The **2 Normal Form Theorem** is a foundational pillar of the Chomsky hierarchy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Primitives of Simplification

#### 2.1.1 Removing $\varepsilon$-Productions
**Definition:** A production of the form $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is an *$\varepsilon$-production*.

**Algorithm — Finding Nullable Variables:**
1. Initialize $N = \emptyset$.
2. Add to $N$ every $A$ such that $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is in $P$.
3. Repeat until no change: if $A \rightarrow \alpha$ is in $P$ and every symbol in $\alpha$ is in $N$, then add $A$ to $N$.
4. For every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_k$, add all variants formed by deleting any subset of nullable $X_i$'s. Remove the original $\varepsilon$-productions. If $S$ was nullable, add the new start $S_0 \rightarrow S \mid \varepsilon$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The order is critical: **remove $\varepsilon$-productions FIRST**, then unit productions, then useless symbols. Reversing this order may leave useless symbols behind.

#### 2.1.2 Removing Unit Productions
**Definition:** A production of the form $A \rightarrow B$ where both $A, B \in V$ is a *unit production*.

**Algorithm:**
1. For each pair $(A, B)$ such that $A \Rightarrow^{\ast} B$ via unit productions (compute the *unit-pair relation*), find all non-unit productions $B \rightarrow \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \dots \mid \alpha_m$.
2. Add $A \rightarrow \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \dots \mid \alpha_m$ to $P$.
3. Delete all unit productions.

#### 2.1.3 Removing Useless Symbols
A variable is **useless** if it is *non-generating* OR *non-reachable*.

- **Generating Set**: $X$ is generating if $X \Rightarrow^{\ast} w$ for some $w \in T^{\ast}$.
- **Reachable Set**: $X$ is reachable if $S \Rightarrow^{\ast} \alpha X \beta$ for some $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^{\ast}$.

**Algorithm — Two-Pass:**
- *Pass A (Generating)*: Find all generating variables by fixed-point iteration.
- *Pass B (Reachable)*: Find all variables reachable from $S$ via the *surviving* generating productions.
- Discard anything not in both sets.

### 2.2 The Two Normal Form Theorems

**Theorem 1 (Chomsky Normal Form — CNF):** *Every CFL not containing $\varepsilon$ has a CFG in CNF.*

**Theorem 2 (Greibach Normal Form — GNF):** *Every CFL not containing $\varepsilon$ has a CFG in GNF.*

### 2.3 GNF Conversion Algorithm (from CNF)
Given a CNF grammar with variables ordered $A_1, A_2, \dots, A_n$:
1. For $i = 1$ to $n$:
2.    For $j = 1$ to $i-1$:
3.      Replace $A_i \rightarrow A_j \gamma$ with $A_i \rightarrow \delta_1 \gamma \mid \delta_2 \gamma \mid \dots \mid \delta_k \gamma$ where $A_j \rightarrow \delta_1 \mid \delta_2 \mid \dots \mid \delta_k$.
4.    Eliminate immediate left recursion among $A_i$-productions using the standard rule:
$$A \rightarrow A\alpha_1 \mid A\alpha_2 \mid \dots \mid A\alpha_m \mid \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \dots \mid \beta_n$$
becomes
$$A \rightarrow \beta_1 \mid \beta_2 \mid \dots \mid \beta_n \mid \beta_1 Z \mid \beta_2 Z \mid \dots \mid \beta_n Z$$
$$Z \rightarrow \alpha_1 \mid \alpha_2 \mid \dots \mid \alpha_m \mid \alpha_1 Z \mid \alpha_2 Z \mid \dots \mid \alpha_m Z$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Form | Constraint | Eliminated In |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\varepsilon$-production | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $A \in V$ | Step 1 |
| Unit production | $A \rightarrow B$ | $A, B \in V$ | Step 2 |
| Non-generating | $X \nRightarrow^{\ast} w$ | $w \in T^{\ast}$ | Step 3a |
| Non-reachable | $S \nRightarrow^{\ast} \alpha X \beta$ | — | Step 3b |
| CNF form | $A \rightarrow BC \,\vert\, a$ | $B, C \in V, a \in T$ | Step 4 |
| GNF form | $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ | $a \in T, \alpha \in V^{\ast}$ | Step 5 |
| Nullable | $A \Rightarrow^{\ast} \varepsilon$ | subset of $V$ | Step 1 |
| Left recursion | $A \rightarrow A\alpha$ | cycle $A \Rightarrow^{\ast} A$ | GNF prep |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** CYK membership testing, **GLR parsers** in compilers, **RNA secondary-structure prediction** in bioinformatics, and **software-model checking** all rely on these normal forms.

### 2.5 Engineering Application Spotlight
In the **GCC** and **Clang** compiler infrastructures, the front-end parser transforms source code using grammar normal forms. CNF guarantees the **CYK algorithm** runs in $O(n^3)$, where $n$ is the input length, enabling predictable performance. GNF enables **recursive-descent parsers** with single-token lookahead.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Canonical Worked Example — Full Simplification + CNF

**Problem:** Simplify the following CFG $G$ and convert to CNF.
$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB$$
$$A \rightarrow bSA$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid \varepsilon$$

This is the classical Hopcroft–Ullman example adapted to the KTU 2024 evaluation pattern.

---

#### Step 1 — Identify Nullable Variables

We must find all $X \in V$ such that $X \Rightarrow^{\ast} \varepsilon$.

**Iteration 0** (initial): $N_0 = \emptyset$.

**Iteration 1:** $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is in $P$. So $B$ is nullable.
$$N_1 = \{B\}$$

**Iteration 2:** Check each production:
- $S \rightarrow ASA$: requires $A$ and $S$ to be nullable. Neither is yet. ❌
- $A \rightarrow bSA$: requires $b \in T$, which is not nullable (terminals are not nullable). ❌
- $S \rightarrow aB$: requires $a \in T$ to be nullable. ❌

$$N_2 = \{B\} = N_1$$

**Fixed point reached.** Nullable set: $N = \{B\}$.

---

#### Step 2 — Remove $\varepsilon$-Productions

For every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_k$ in $P$, generate all strings obtained by deleting any subset of nullable $X_i$'s (the empty subset included). Then delete all original $\varepsilon$-productions.

**Production-by-production expansion:**

For $S \rightarrow ASA$ (no nullable symbols in RHS, but $A$ is not nullable either): no deletion possible, keep as is.

For $S \rightarrow aB$ (B is nullable): we get two variants:
- Keep $B$: $S \rightarrow aB$
- Delete $B$: $S \rightarrow a$

For $A \rightarrow bSA$ ($S$ and $A$ are not nullable, $b$ is terminal): no deletion possible.

For $B \rightarrow b$ (no nullable in RHS): keep as is.

**Delete $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$.**

**New grammar $G_1$:**
$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB \mid a$$
$$A \rightarrow bSA$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$

---

#### Step 3 — Remove Unit Productions

Inspect $G_1$ for any production of the form $A \rightarrow B$ with $A, B \in V$.

- $S \rightarrow ASA$: RHS has 3 variables. Not a unit production. ✓
- $S \rightarrow aB$: RHS has terminal + variable. Not a unit production. ✓
- $S \rightarrow a$: terminal. Not a unit production. ✓
- $A \rightarrow bSA$: terminal + 2 variables. Not a unit production. ✓
- $B \rightarrow b$: terminal. Not a unit production. ✓

**No unit productions found.** $G_1 = G_2$.

---

#### Step 4 — Remove Useless Symbols

**Step 4a — Generating Pass:**

Initialize $G_{\text{gen}} = \emptyset$.
- $B \rightarrow b$: $b \in T$. So $B$ is generating. $G_{\text{gen}} = \{B\}$.
- $S \rightarrow aB$: $a \in T, B \in G_{\text{gen}}$. So $S$ is generating. $G_{\text{gen}} = \{B, S\}$.
- $A \rightarrow bSA$: $b \in T, S \in G_{\text{gen}}$, but $A$ is the LHS, and we need all RHS symbols generating. $S$ is generating, $A$ is on RHS — but the production is for $A$, and we check $A$'s RHS. The RHS is $b, S, A$. $A$ is NOT generating yet. So $A$ is not yet generating. $G_{\text{gen}} = \{B, S\}$.
- $S \rightarrow ASA$: $A$ not generating. So this production doesn't add anything new.
- $S \rightarrow a$: terminal. Confirms $S$ generating.

Fixed point: $G_{\text{gen}} = \{B, S\}$.

**$A$ is non-generating!** We must remove all productions containing $A$ on the RHS or LHS.

**Pruning step:** Delete $A \rightarrow bSA$ and delete $A$ from RHS of $S \rightarrow ASA$ (so $S \rightarrow ASA$ becomes $S \rightarrow$ nothing... effectively deleted). Also delete $A$ from the variable set.

**Step 4b — Reachable Pass (on the surviving grammar):**

Surviving grammar:
$$S \rightarrow aB \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$

- $S$ is reachable (it's the start symbol).
- $B$ appears in $S \rightarrow aB$. So $B$ is reachable.
- No other variables.

$$R = \{S, B\}$$

All symbols are reachable.

**Final simplified grammar $G_2'$:**
$$S \rightarrow aB \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$

> [!NOTE]
> Although $A$ appeared in the original, it was **non-generating** and therefore useless. The simplification reduces the grammar to its essence.

---

#### Step 5 — Convert to CNF

CNF requires every production to be of the form:
- $A \rightarrow BC$ (two variables), OR
- $A \rightarrow a$ (one terminal).

**Check current grammar:**
- $S \rightarrow aB$: RHS has terminal + variable. **Not in CNF.**
- $S \rightarrow a$: terminal. ✓
- $B \rightarrow b$: terminal. ✓

**Fix $S \rightarrow aB$:** Introduce new variable $C_a$ with $C_a \rightarrow a$.

$$S \rightarrow C_a B$$
$$C_a \rightarrow a$$

**Final CNF Grammar $G_{CNF}$:**
$$S \rightarrow C_a B \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$
$$C_a \rightarrow a$$

**Verification:**
- $S \rightarrow C_a B$: RHS is two variables. ✓
- $S \rightarrow a$: terminal. ✓
- $B \rightarrow b$: terminal. ✓
- $C_a \rightarrow a$: terminal. ✓

All productions satisfy CNF. The language is preserved: $L(G_{CNF}) = L(G) = \{a^{n+1} b^{n+1} \cdot \text{mixed}\} \cup \{a\}$.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — GNF Conversion (Sneak Peek)

Take the simpler CNF grammar:
$$S \rightarrow a \mid aA$$
$$A \rightarrow b \mid bS$$

This is **already in GNF** (each production starts with a terminal followed by zero or one variable).

For a non-GNF example, say:
$$S \rightarrow AB$$
$$A \rightarrow a \mid aA$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid bB$$

**Order variables:** $A_1 = S, A_2 = A, A_3 = B$.

**For $i = 1$ (S):** No $j < 1$ to substitute. Left recursion: $S \rightarrow AB$ is fine. So $S$ productions are kept.

**For $i = 2$ (A):** No $j < 2$ to substitute. Keep $A \rightarrow a \mid aA$.

**For $i = 3$ (B):** Substitute $A$ in $S \rightarrow AB$? No, $A$ is not on LHS in the iteration sense. Check $B$'s productions: $B \rightarrow b \mid bB$. No left recursion. Keep.

**Resulting GNF:**
$$S \rightarrow AB$$
$$A \rightarrow a \mid aA$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid bB$$

But wait — $S \rightarrow AB$ starts with a *variable*, not a terminal! To fix, substitute $A$'s productions:
$$S \rightarrow aB \mid aAB$$
$$A \rightarrow a \mid aA$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid bB$$

Now $S$ starts with a terminal. ✓

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Apply What

| Original Grammar Contains | Apply Simplification | Resulting Benefit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\varepsilon$-productions | Step 1 (Nullable elimination) | Removes null ambiguity |
| Unit productions | Step 2 (Unit-pair substitution) | Eliminates trivial derivations |
| Non-generating variables | Step 3a (Generating pass) | Cleans dead ends |
| Unreachable variables | Step 3b (Reachability pass) | Removes orphans |
| Long RHS, mixed terminals | Step 4 (CNF conversion) | Enables CYK algorithm |
| Variable-leading RHS | Step 5 (GNF conversion) | Enables top-down parsing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **order is sacred**: $\varepsilon$-removal $\rightarrow$ Unit-removal $\rightarrow$ Useless-removal $\rightarrow$ CNF. Skipping or reordering can leave residual issues.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flowchart — The Simplification Pipeline

The following Mermaid diagram captures the canonical KTU 2024 simplification pipeline. Every block is a *transformation* that takes a CFG and produces an equivalent but cleaner CFG.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Raw CFG G]) --> s1[Step 1: Identify Nullable Variables N]
    s1 --> s1a[Compute Fixed Point of Nullable Set]
    s1a --> s1b[Generate Variants by Deleting Nullable RHS Symbols]
    s1b --> s1c[Drop Original Epsilon Productions]
    s1c --> s1d{Start Symbol S Nullable?}
    s1d -- Yes --> s1e[Add New Start S0 and S0 rightarrow S and S0 rightarrow epsilon]
    s1d -- No --> s2[Step 2: Compute Unit Pairs]
    s1e --> s2
    s2 --> s2a[For Each Unit Pair A B, Add Non-Unit Productions of B to A]
    s2a --> s2b[Delete All Unit Productions]
    s2b --> s3[Step 3: Remove Useless Symbols]
    s3 --> s3a[Pass A: Compute Generating Set Ggen]
    s3a --> s3b[Prune Productions with Non-Generating Symbols]
    s3b --> s3c[Pass B: Compute Reachable Set R from S]
    s3c --> s3d[Prune Non-Reachable Symbols]
    s3d --> s4[Step 4: Convert to Chomsky Normal Form]
    s4 --> s4a[Replace Mixed RHS aX with Ca X and Ca rightarrow a]
    s4a --> s4b[Break Long RHS X1 X2 Xk into Binary Chain]
    s4b --> s5{Convert to GNF Optional}
    s5 -- Yes --> s5a[Order Variables A1 to An]
    s5a --> s5b[Substitute Lower Indexed Variables in Higher Indexed RHS]
    s5b --> s5c[Eliminate Left Recursion via Z Variable]
    s5c --> endgnf([Grammar in GNF])
    s5 -- No --> endcnf([Grammar in CNF])
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Normal-Form Compiler Front-End

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputStage[Input Stage]
        srcG[Source CFG G]
    end
    subgraph pipeline[Simplification Pipeline]
        epsMod[Null Production Eliminator]
        unitMod[Unit Production Eliminator]
        uselessMod[Useless Symbol Filter]
        cnfMod[CNF Converter]
        gnfMod[GNF Converter]
    end
    subgraph outputStage[Output Stage]
        cnfOut[CNF Grammar]
        gnfOut[GNF Grammar]
    end
    srcG --> epsMod
    epsMod --> unitMod
    unitMod --> uselessMod
    uselessMod --> cnfMod
    cnfMod --> cnfOut
    cnfMod --> gnfMod
    gnfMod --> gnfOut
```

### 4.3 Decision Schematic — "Is the Grammar in CNF?"

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q1{All productions of form A rightarrow BC or A rightarrow a}
    q1 -- No --> fix1[Replace Mixed RHS with New Variable Ca]
    fix1 --> fix2[Break RHS longer than 2 using New Variables]
    fix2 --> check[Re-verify Each Production]
    q1 -- Yes --> ok([Grammar is in CNF])
    check --> q1
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above render as standard Markdown in any modern editor and can be imported into LaTeX via `mermaid2pgf` or rendered to PNG via `mmdc` (Mermaid CLI) for inclusion in KTU lab records.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define a useless symbol in a CFG. List the two conditions under which a variable is declared useless.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *useless symbol* is a variable that does not contribute to the derivation of any terminal string from the start symbol. A variable $X$ is useless if **either**:
1. **Non-generating:** $X \Rightarrow^{\ast} w$ has no solution for any $w \in T^{\ast}$ (the variable cannot derive a terminal string), **or**
2. **Non-reachable:** There is no derivation $S \Rightarrow^{\ast} \alpha X \beta$ for any $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup T)^{\ast}$ (the variable cannot be reached from the start symbol $S$).

Both conditions must be checked: a symbol that is generating but not reachable (or vice versa) is still useless and must be removed during simplification.

**(1 mark — definition; 1 mark — non-generating; 1 mark — non-reachable.)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)? Why is it useful in compiler design?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A CFG is in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** if every production is of the form $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$, where $A, B, C \in V$ (variables) and $a \in T$ (terminal). The start symbol $S$ does not appear on the right-hand side of any production, and the only allowed $\varepsilon$-production is $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (if the language includes the empty string).

**Usefulness in Compiler Design (any 2 of the following):**
- Enables the **CYK algorithm** for $O(n^3)$ membership testing of strings in the CFL.
- Simplifies the construction of **parse tables** in parsers.
- Provides a uniform structure that facilitates **grammar equivalence checking**.
- Foundation for **bottom-up parsers** like GLR and Earley parsers.

**(1 mark — definition; 2 marks — uses.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Full Simplification + CNF

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

Consider the CFG $G$ with productions:
$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB$$
$$A \rightarrow bSA$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid \varepsilon$$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Simplify the grammar by removing $\varepsilon$-productions, unit productions, and useless symbols.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the resulting grammar to **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

##### Part (a) — Simplification [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Identify Nullable Variables (2 marks)**

Nullable set $N$:
- $B \rightarrow \varepsilon \Rightarrow B \in N$.
- Check $S \rightarrow ASA$: $A, S \notin N$ yet. Not nullable.
- Check $A \rightarrow bSA$: contains terminal $b$. Not nullable.
- Fixed point: $N = \{B\}$.

**Step 2: Remove $\varepsilon$-Productions (2 marks)**

For each production, generate variants by deleting nullable RHS symbols.
- $S \rightarrow aB$: $B$ nullable $\Rightarrow$ add $S \rightarrow a$ (by deleting $B$).
- All other productions: no nullable symbols in RHS, keep unchanged.
- Delete $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

**Modified grammar $G_1$:**
$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB \mid a$$
$$A \rightarrow bSA$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$

**Step 3: Remove Unit Productions (1 mark)**

Inspect for $A \rightarrow B$ form. None found. $G_1 = G_2$.

**Step 4: Remove Useless Symbols (2 marks)**

- **Generating pass**: $B$ generates $b$. $S$ generates $a$ and $aB$. $A \rightarrow bSA$ requires $A$ to be generating — circular. So $A$ is non-generating.
- **Prune**: Delete $A \rightarrow bSA$ and remove $A$ from $S \rightarrow ASA$ (deleting that production entirely).
- **Reachable pass** on surviving grammar: $S$ is start, $B$ reachable from $S$. All symbols reachable.

**Final simplified grammar $G'$:**
$$S \rightarrow aB \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$

##### Part (b) — Convert to CNF [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Identify Non-CNF Productions (2 marks)**

- $S \rightarrow aB$ — RHS has terminal + variable. **Not in CNF.**
- $S \rightarrow a$ — terminal. ✓
- $B \rightarrow b$ — terminal. ✓

**Step 2: Introduce New Variable for Terminal (2 marks)**

Replace $a$ in $S \rightarrow aB$ with $C_a$ where $C_a \rightarrow a$:
$$S \rightarrow C_a B$$
$$C_a \rightarrow a$$

**Step 3: Verify CNF Compliance (3 marks)**

Final CNF grammar:
$$S \rightarrow C_a B \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$
$$C_a \rightarrow a$$

- $S \rightarrow C_a B$: two variables. ✓
- $S \rightarrow a$: one terminal. ✓
- $B \rightarrow b$: one terminal. ✓
- $C_a \rightarrow a$: one terminal. ✓

All productions satisfy CNF. ✓ **Language preserved.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Grammar + CNF

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

Consider the CFG $G$ with productions:
$$S \rightarrow aAb \mid bAa$$
$$A \rightarrow aSa \mid bSb \mid \varepsilon$$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Eliminate $\varepsilon$-productions and check for unit productions and useless symbols. State the simplified grammar.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Convert the simplified grammar to CNF.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

##### Part (a) — Simplification [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Nullable Variables (2 marks)**

- $A \rightarrow \varepsilon \Rightarrow A$ is nullable.
- $S \rightarrow aAb$: $A$ nullable $\Rightarrow$ add $S \rightarrow ab$ (delete $A$).
- $S \rightarrow bAa$: $A$ nullable $\Rightarrow$ add $S \rightarrow ba$ (delete $A$).
- $A \rightarrow aSa$: $S$ not nullable (yet). So $A$ not nullable via this.
- Fixed point: $N = \{A\}$.

**Step 2: Remove $\varepsilon$-Productions (3 marks)**

For each production:
- $S \rightarrow aAb$: keep $aAb$, add $ab$.
- $S \rightarrow bAa$: keep $bAa$, add $ba$.
- $A \rightarrow aSa$: no nullable RHS, keep.
- $A \rightarrow bSb$: no nullable RHS, keep.
- Delete $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

**Modified grammar $G_1$:**
$$S \rightarrow aAb \mid ab \mid bAa \mid ba$$
$$A \rightarrow aSa \mid bSb$$

**Step 3: Remove Unit Productions (1 mark)**

No productions of form $X \rightarrow Y$ where $X, Y \in V$. **No unit productions.**

**Step 4: Remove Useless Symbols (1 mark)**

- $A$ generates $aSa$ and $bSb$ (which produce non-empty terminal strings through $S$'s productions).
- $S$ is reachable from itself (start symbol).
- $A$ is reachable: $S \rightarrow aAb$ contains $A$.
- All symbols generating and reachable. **No useless symbols.**

**Final simplified grammar $G'$:**
$$S \rightarrow aAb \mid ab \mid bAa \mid ba$$
$$A \rightarrow aSa \mid bSb$$

##### Part (b) — Convert to CNF [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Identify Issues (2 marks)**

- $S \rightarrow aAb$: 2 terminals + 1 variable on RHS. ❌
- $S \rightarrow ab$: 2 terminals. ❌
- $S \rightarrow bAa$: 2 terminals + 1 variable. ❌
- $S \rightarrow ba$: 2 terminals. ❌
- $A \rightarrow aSa$: 2 terminals + 1 variable. ❌
- $A \rightarrow bSb$: 2 terminals + 1 variable. ❌

**Step 2: Introduce Variables for Terminals (2 marks)**

Let $C_a \rightarrow a$ and $C_b \rightarrow b$.

Replace each terminal with its variable:
- $S \rightarrow aAb \Rightarrow S \rightarrow C_a A C_b$
- $S \rightarrow ab \Rightarrow S \rightarrow C_a C_b$
- $S \rightarrow bAa \Rightarrow S \rightarrow C_b A C_a$
- $S \rightarrow ba \Rightarrow S \rightarrow C_b C_a$
- $A \rightarrow aSa \Rightarrow A \rightarrow C_a S C_a$
- $A \rightarrow bSb \Rightarrow A \rightarrow C_b S C_b$

**Step 3: Break Long RHS into Binary (3 marks)**

Each RHS now has 3 symbols. Introduce chain variables:
- Let $D_1 \rightarrow AC_b$ to replace $C_a A C_b$? Better: introduce fresh variables.
- $S \rightarrow C_a A C_b \Rightarrow S \rightarrow C_a D_1$ and $D_1 \rightarrow A C_b$.
- $S \rightarrow C_b A C_a \Rightarrow S \rightarrow C_b D_2$ and $D_2 \rightarrow A C_a$.
- $A \rightarrow C_a S C_a \Rightarrow A \rightarrow C_a D_3$ and $D_3 \rightarrow S C_a$.
- $A \rightarrow C_b S C_b \Rightarrow A \rightarrow C_b D_4$ and $D_4 \rightarrow S C_b$.

**Final CNF Grammar $G_{CNF}$:**
$$S \rightarrow C_a D_1 \mid C_a C_b \mid C_b D_2 \mid C_b C_a$$
$$A \rightarrow C_a D_3 \mid C_b D_4$$
$$C_a \rightarrow a$$
$$C_b \rightarrow b$$
$$D_1 \rightarrow A C_b$$
$$D_2 \rightarrow A C_a$$
$$D_3 \rightarrow S C_a$$
$$D_4 \rightarrow S C_b$$

All productions in CNF. ✓ Language preserved.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Wrong Order of Simplification**: Students often remove useless symbols *first*, which can incorrectly delete symbols needed in the $\varepsilon$-removal step. Always do **$\varepsilon$ → unit → useless**.
> 2. **Forgetting the New Start Symbol**: When $S$ is nullable, students forget to add $S_0 \rightarrow S \mid \varepsilon$ with $S_0$ as the new start. **Lose 1 mark.**
> 3. **Missing Variants in $\varepsilon$-Removal**: For $A \rightarrow BC$ where $B$ and $C$ are both nullable, the variants $A \rightarrow B$, $A \rightarrow C$, and $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ must all be added. **Lose 2 marks** per missed variant.
> 4. **CNF: Forgetting Terminal-to-Variable Substitution**: A production like $S \rightarrow aB$ is not in CNF. Students often write the final answer without introducing $C_a$. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 5. **CNF: Long RHS Not Broken**: A production $A \rightarrow BCD$ must be split into $A \rightarrow BE$ and $E \rightarrow CD$ with a fresh variable $E$. **Lose 2 marks.**
> 6. **GNF: Ignoring Left Recursion**: In GNF conversion, left recursion $A \rightarrow A\alpha \mid \beta$ must be replaced with $A \rightarrow \beta \mid \beta Z$ and $Z \rightarrow \alpha \mid \alpha Z$. **Lose 3 marks.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- **Simplification Order (Sacred Sequence):** $\varepsilon$-removal → Unit-removal → Useless-symbol-removal → CNF → GNF.
- **Nullable Set Algorithm:** Fixed-point iteration. Start with direct $\varepsilon$-rules, propagate via $A \rightarrow \alpha$ where all of $\alpha$ is nullable.
- **Variants in $\varepsilon$-Removal:** For $A \rightarrow X_1 \dots X_k$ with nullable symbols, include $2^m$ variants where $m$ is the count of nullable symbols in RHS — minus the all-deleted one (handled by the original $\varepsilon$-removal step).
- **Unit-Pair Relation:** Defined as $\{(A, B) : A \Rightarrow^{\ast} B \text{ via unit productions}\}$. Compute by fixed-point: include identity $(A, A)$, add $(A, C)$ if $(A, B)$ and $(B \rightarrow C)$ is unit.
- **Useless Symbols:** Always do **Generating first**, then **Reachability** on the pruned grammar. Reversing this is a common error.
- **CNF Constraints:** Every rule is $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$. No $\varepsilon$ (except possibly $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$). No terminals in RHS of length $\geq 2$ productions. No RHS of length $\geq 3$.
- **GNF Constraints:** Every rule is $A \rightarrow a\alpha$ where $a$ is a terminal. The first symbol of RHS is *always* a terminal. No left recursion in variable order.
- **GNF Algorithm:** Order variables, substitute lower-indexed variables into higher-indexed productions, eliminate left recursion with the standard $Z$-variable trick.
- **Equivalence Preservation:** Every simplification step must preserve $L(G)$. The language does *not* change.
- **Marks Distribution (KTU 2024):** Part A 3 marks (definitions/short answers). Part B 14 marks with internal choice; sub-parts usually 7 + 7.
- **Examiner's Pet Peeves:** Wrong simplification order, missing new start symbol, terminal leakage in CNF, unchained long RHS, ignored left recursion in GNF.
- **Time-Saver Tip:** When asked to *simplify and convert to CNF*, lay out your work in clearly numbered steps (Step 1, Step 2, ...). Examiners award partial marks per step.
- **Theorems to Memorize:** *Every CFL (not containing $\varepsilon$) has a CFG in CNF* and *has a CFG in GNF*. Both are existence theorems — construction given in the algorithms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elimination of Useless Symbols and Productions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Elimination of Useless Symbols and Productions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

Let the Context-Free Grammar be defined as the 4-tuple:

$$G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$$

where $V$ is the finite set of **variables (non-terminals)**, $\Sigma$ is the finite set of **terminals**, $R$ is the finite set of **productions**, and $S \in V$ is the designated **start symbol**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition 1 — Generating Symbol:**
> A symbol $X \in (V \cup \Sigma)$ is called **generating** if there exists at least one terminal string $w \in \Sigma^{*}$ such that $X \Rightarrow^{*} w$. In other words, a variable must eventually be reducible to a pure string of terminals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition 2 — Reachable Symbol:**
> A symbol $X \in (V \cup \Sigma)$ is called **reachable** if there exists a sentential form $\alpha X \beta$ derivable from the start symbol, i.e., $S \Rightarrow^{*} \alpha X \beta$. Equivalently, $X$ must appear in the "shadow" of the start symbol during some derivation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition 3 — Useful Symbol (The Central Definition):**
> A symbol $X$ is **useful** if and only if it is **both generating AND reachable**. Otherwise, $X$ is termed a **useless symbol**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition 4 — Useless Production:**
> A production $A \rightarrow \beta$ is called **useless** if either:
> 1. It contains a non-generating symbol anywhere in $\beta$, OR
> 2. The variable $A$ on the LHS is non-reachable.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Standard (Dec 2023/2024 Pattern):**
> A useless symbol is one that **fails the dual test**. Failing even one of the two criteria (generating or reachable) is sufficient to mark a symbol as useless. This is the single most tested definition in Module 3.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The City Map

Imagine a city road network where **$S$ is the City Hall** (start symbol).

| Mathematical Concept | Real-World Analogy | Why it Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Generating Symbol** | A road that *actually leads somewhere* (a terminal destination) | If a road is a dead-end, no driver can ever reach a destination using it. |
| **Reachable Symbol** | A road that *can be driven to* from City Hall | If a road exists in the city but is on an isolated island, no one can ever get there. |
| **Useful Symbol** | A road that is both reachable AND leads somewhere | A perfectly functional road — drivers can reach it AND use it to reach a destination. |
| **Useless Symbol** | A road that is either a dead-end OR on an unreachable island (or both) | Wastes city budget, confuses drivers — must be eliminated from the map. |

A road that is unreachable but does lead somewhere (a non-reachable generator) is **still useless** because nobody can use it. A road that is reachable but is a dead-end (a non-generating reachable symbol) is **still useless** because it stops the journey. **Both conditions must hold simultaneously** for a symbol to earn the "useful" label.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Eliminate Useless Symbols?**
> - **Compiler Design:** Reduces grammar size, simplifies parsing tables (LL/LR).
> - **Decidability:** Useful for proving decidability results in TOC (e.g., emptiness, finiteness).
> - **Minimality:** Forms one of the four sub-steps to convert any CFG into its **Greibach Normal Form (GNF)** and **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**.
> - **Correctness:** Ensures that language-preserving transformations do not leave dangling, meaningless variables that produce empty analysis errors downstream.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Step Algorithm — The Order is Sacred

> [!WARNING]
> **The order of elimination is CRITICAL and frequently tested in KTU.**
> The correct order is: **(1) Eliminate non-generating symbols FIRST, then (2) Eliminate non-reachable symbols.**
> Reversing this order can leave useless symbols in the grammar.

### Step 1 — Elimination of Non-Generating Symbols

**Objective:** Find the set of all generating variables and discard everything else.

**Iterative Procedure (Fixed-Point Computation):**
1. **Initialization (Base Set):** Every terminal $a \in \Sigma$ is trivially generating. Define the set:
$$\text{Gen}_0 = \Sigma$$
2. **Inductive Rule:** A variable $A$ is added to the generating set if there exists a production $A \rightarrow \beta$ such that **every symbol** in $\beta$ is already known to be generating (i.e., $\beta \in \text{Gen}_{i-1}^{*}$).
3. **Termination:** Repeat until no new variable can be added. The final set $\text{Gen}_{\text{final}}$ is the set of generating variables.
4. **Pruning:** Remove all non-generating variables and **all productions** that contain them.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this a fixed-point?** Each iteration can only add variables, never remove them. Since the set of variables $V$ is finite, the process is guaranteed to halt in at most $\vert V \vert + 1$ iterations.

### Step 2 — Elimination of Non-Reachable Symbols

**Objective:** From the resulting grammar, find all symbols reachable from $S$ and discard the rest.

**Iterative Procedure:**
1. **Initialization (Base Set):** The start symbol $S$ is trivially reachable. Define:
$$\text{Reach}_0 = \{S\}$$
2. **Inductive Rule:** A symbol $X$ is added to the reachable set if there exists a reachable variable $A \in \text{Reach}_{i-1}$ that has a production $A \rightarrow \alpha X \beta$ for some $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup \Sigma)^{*}$.
3. **Termination:** Repeat until no new symbol is added.
4. **Pruning:** Remove all non-reachable symbols and all productions containing them.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Order?**
> Consider a symbol that is non-generating but *would have been* removed by reachability. If we did reachability first, we would keep it (it might be "reachable" from $S$). Then in Step 1, we would remove it — fine. **But** there is a more subtle case: a symbol $X$ may be non-reachable in the *original* grammar, but become reachable *after* non-generating symbols are removed. Therefore, doing reachability first on the original grammar may incorrectly classify $X$ as non-reachable when it is actually non-generating. The reverse order is provably correct.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formal Definition | Key Property |
|---|---|---|
| Generating Set Iteration | $\text{Gen}_{i} = \text{Gen}_{i-1} \cup \{A \in V : \exists A \rightarrow \beta, \beta \in \text{Gen}_{i-1}^{*}\}$ | Monotonically non-decreasing |
| Reachable Set Iteration | $\text{Reach}_{i} = \text{Reach}_{i-1} \cup \{X \in V \cup \Sigma : \exists A \in \text{Reach}_{i-1}, A \rightarrow \alpha X \beta\}$ | Monotonically non-decreasing |
| Termination Bound | $\le \vert V \vert + 1$ iterations for Generation | Finite $V$ guarantees halt |
| Useful Symbol | $\text{Useful} = \text{Gen}_{\text{final}} \cap \text{Reach}_{\text{final}}$ | Conjunction (AND) |
| Useless Symbol | $V \cup \Sigma \setminus \text{Useful}$ | Complement set |
| Order of Operations | Gen $\rightarrow$ Reach | NOT interchangeable |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Used:** $\vert V \vert$ denotes the cardinality (count) of the variable set. The Kleene-star on a set means all finite concatenations of elements from that set.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Front-Ends (YACC, ANTLR, Bison):** Useless symbols inflate parse tables. The LALR(1) and SLR(1) table generators run faster and consume less memory on cleaned grammars.
- **Formal Verification:** In model checking, dead-code elimination (which is exactly the useless-symbol problem) reduces the state space exponentially.
- **Bioinformatics:** CFG-based RNA structure prediction tools (e.g., Infernal) apply similar reachability pruning to grammar rules.
- **Database Query Optimization:** Useless predicates in SQL query plans correspond to non-generating symbols — they can never produce output rows and should be eliminated from the query tree.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Worked Example

Consider the following Context-Free Grammar $G$ with start symbol $S$:

$$S \rightarrow aA \mid B$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow bB$$
$$C \rightarrow c$$

We will eliminate all useless symbols and productions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Initial Symbol Inventory:** Variables $V = \{S, A, B, C\}$; Terminals $\Sigma = \{a, b, c\}$. There are 7 productions in total.

### PHASE 1 — Elimination of Non-Generating Symbols

We must find the set of all generating variables.

**Iteration 0 (Base):**

$$\text{Gen}_0 = \Sigma = \{a, b, c\}$$

**Iteration 1:** We examine every production's RHS and add the LHS variable only if the entire RHS is in $\text{Gen}_0$.

- Production $A \rightarrow a$: RHS is $a \in \text{Gen}_0$. $\therefore$ **Add $A$**.
- Production $A \rightarrow aA$: RHS is $aA$. $A \notin \text{Gen}_0$ yet (in this iteration's check). $\therefore$ Reject.
- Production $B \rightarrow bB$: RHS is $bB$. $B \notin \text{Gen}_0$ yet. $\therefore$ Reject.
- Production $C \rightarrow c$: RHS is $c \in \text{Gen}_0$. $\therefore$ **Add $C$**.
- Production $S \rightarrow aA$: RHS is $aA$. $A$ not yet confirmed in $\text{Gen}_0$. $\therefore$ Reject.
- Production $S \rightarrow B$: RHS is $B$. $B \notin \text{Gen}_0$. $\therefore$ Reject.

$$\text{Gen}_1 = \{a, b, c, A, C\}$$

**Iteration 2:** Re-examine productions using updated $\text{Gen}_1$.

- Production $A \rightarrow aA$: RHS $aA \in \text{Gen}_1^{*}$ since $a, A \in \text{Gen}_1$. Already have $A$.
- Production $B \rightarrow bB$: RHS $bB \in \text{Gen}_1^{*}$ since $b, B \in \text{Gen}_1$? Wait — $B \notin \text{Gen}_1$. $\therefore$ Reject.
- Production $S \rightarrow aA$: RHS $aA \in \text{Gen}_1^{*}$. $\therefore$ **Add $S$**.

$$\text{Gen}_2 = \{a, b, c, A, C, S\}$$

**Iteration 3:** Check remaining productions.

- Production $B \rightarrow bB$: $B \notin \text{Gen}_2$. Reject.
- No new additions possible.

$$\text{Gen}_{\text{final}} = \{a, b, c, A, C, S\}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Identification of Non-Generating Symbol:** $B$ is the **only** non-generating variable because it has no production whose RHS reduces to a pure terminal string. $B$ is a "dead-end" — it can only produce $bB$ recursively.

**Pruning Phase 1:** Remove $B$, all productions containing $B$, and $C$ (wait — $C$ is generating because $C \rightarrow c$!).

Re-evaluation: $C$ is generating. Let us re-check why we would remove it.

Actually, in this example, **$C$ is generating** (since $C \rightarrow c$). So $C$ remains. But is $C$ reachable? That is a Phase 2 question. Let us NOT remove $C$ in Phase 1.

**Grammar after Phase 1 (only $B$ removed):**

$$S \rightarrow aA$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid a$$
$$C \rightarrow c$$

### PHASE 2 — Elimination of Non-Reachable Symbols

We must find the set of all symbols reachable from $S$ in the *new* grammar $G_1$.

**Iteration 0 (Base):**

$$\text{Reach}_0 = \{S\}$$

**Iteration 1:** Examine every production of $S$.

- Production $S \rightarrow aA$: New symbols on RHS are $a$ and $A$. $\therefore$ Add $a, A$.

$$\text{Reach}_1 = \{S, a, A\}$$

**Iteration 2:** Examine productions of newly added reachable variable $A$.

- Production $A \rightarrow aA$: $a$ and $A$ already in $\text{Reach}_1$. No additions.
- Production $A \rightarrow a$: $a$ already present. No additions.

$$\text{Reach}_2 = \{S, a, A\}$$

**Iteration 3:** No more reachable variables to process. The set is stable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** $C$ was **not reached** from $S$ in any derivation. $C$ exists in the grammar but is on an "isolated island" with no incoming edge from $S$.

$$\text{Reach}_{\text{final}} = \{S, a, A\}$$

**Pruning Phase 2:** Remove $C$ and its production $C \rightarrow c$.

**Final Grammar $G'$ with no useless symbols:**

$$S \rightarrow aA$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid a$$

## 3.2 Verification Matrix

| Symbol | Generating? | Reachable? | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| $S$ | Yes ($S \Rightarrow aA \Rightarrow aa$) | Yes (start symbol) | **Useful** |
| $A$ | Yes ($A \Rightarrow a$) | Yes ($S \rightarrow aA$) | **Useful** |
| $a$ | Yes (terminal) | Yes (in $S \rightarrow aA$) | **Useful** |
| $B$ | No (dead-end) | — (already removed) | **Useless** |
| $C$ | Yes ($C \Rightarrow c$) | No (unreachable) | **Useless** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note (Valuation Insight):** $C$ is a classic "trap" example. Students often forget to check $C$ in Phase 2 because it is generating. Generating alone is **not** sufficient — it must also be reachable. Many marks are lost when students incorrectly retain $C$ in the final grammar.

## 3.3 Language Preservation Proof Sketch

Let $G$ be the original grammar and $G'$ be the grammar after both phases. We claim $L(G) = L(G')$.

**Argument:**
- **$L(G') \subseteq L(G)$:** Every production of $G'$ is also a production of $G$ (we only removed productions).
- **$L(G) \subseteq L(G')$:** Take any $w \in L(G)$. Then $S \Rightarrow_{G}^{*} w$. Every variable used in this derivation must be both generating (it eventually produces terminals) and reachable (it appears under $S$). Since $G'$ retains all such symbols, the same derivation is valid in $G'$.

Therefore, $L(G') = L(G)$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — The Two-Step Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS([Start: Grammar G with start symbol S])
    node1[Phase 1: Identify Generating Variables]
    node1a["Initialize Gen0 = Sigma all terminals are trivially generating"]
    node1b["Iterate: Add variable A if a production A yields beta with all symbols in Gen"]
    node1c["Fixed point reached when no new variable can be added"]
    node1d["Remove all non-generating variables and their productions"]
    node2[Phase 2: Identify Reachable Symbols from S]
    node2a["Initialize Reach0 = left brace S right brace"]
    node2b["Iterate: For each reachable A, add all symbols appearing on the RHS of A productions"]
    node2c["Fixed point reached when no new symbol is added"]
    node2d["Remove all non-reachable symbols and their productions"]
    nodeE([End: Useless-Free Grammar G prime])

    nodeS --> node1
    node1 --> node1a
    node1a --> node1b
    node1b --> node1c
    node1c --> node1d
    node1d --> node2
    node2 --> node2a
    node2a --> node2b
    node2b --> node2c
    node2c --> node2d
    node2d --> nodeE
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Worked Example Production Graph

The following graph visualizes the original grammar's symbol reachability and generation status. Nodes with double-asterisk prefix are **useless** and will be removed.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS((S))
    nodeA((A))
    nodeB((B))
    nodeC((C))
    tA([a])
    tB([b])
    tC([c])

    nodeS -- "aA" --> nodeA
    nodeS -- "B" --> nodeB
    nodeA -- "aA" --> nodeA
    nodeA -- "a" --> tA
    nodeB -- "bB" --> nodeB
    nodeC -- "c" --> tC

    style nodeB fill:#fbb,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
    style nodeC fill:#fbb,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- $S$, $A$, and $a$ form one connected component — the **useful cluster**.
- $B$ and $b$ form a separate cycle — non-generating because no edge leads to a pure terminal.
- $C$ and $c$ form a tiny isolated cluster — generating but **unreachable from $S$**.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["INPUT LAYER"]
        blockG["Original Grammar G equals V Sigma R S"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE1["PHASE 1: GENERATION FILTER"]
        blockG1a["Terminal Identifier: Scan Sigma"]
        blockG1b["Variable Updater: Apply rule A yields beta iff beta is in Gen starred"]
        blockG1c["Non-Generating Pruner: Remove vars NOT in Gen final"]
    end

    subgraph PHASE2["PHASE 2: REACHABILITY FILTER"]
        blockG2a["BFS Initiator: Seed with start symbol S"]
        blockG2b["Edge Explorer: Traverse RHS of reachable productions"]
        blockG2c["Non-Reachable Pruner: Remove symbols NOT in Reach final"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        blockGp["Cleaned Grammar G prime with NO useless symbols and productions"]
    end

    blockG --> blockG1a
    blockG1a --> blockG1b
    blockG1b --> blockG1c
    blockG1c --> blockG2a
    blockG2a --> blockG2b
    blockG2b --> blockG2c
    blockG2c --> blockGp
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the terms: (i) Useless Symbol (ii) Generating Symbol (iii) Reachable Symbol, with respect to a context-free grammar.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Useless Symbol:** A symbol $X \in (V \cup \Sigma)$ of a CFG $G = (V, \Sigma, R, S)$ is said to be **useless** if there is no string $w \in L(G)$ such that $X$ is used in the derivation of $w$. Equivalently, $X$ fails to be generating or reachable (or both).

**(ii) Generating Symbol:** A symbol $X$ is **generating** if there exists some terminal string $w \in \Sigma^{*}$ such that $X \Rightarrow^{*} w$. Every terminal is trivially generating. A variable is generating only if it has a production whose entire right-hand side consists of symbols that are themselves known to be generating.

**(iii) Reachable Symbol:** A symbol $X$ is **reachable** if there exists a sentential form $\alpha X \beta$ (with $\alpha, \beta \in (V \cup \Sigma)^{*}$) such that $S \Rightarrow^{*} \alpha X \beta$. The start symbol $S$ is always reachable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark per definition with a precise condition. Diagrams or examples earn no extra marks here but show understanding.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Why is the order "first eliminate non-generating symbols, then non-reachable symbols" critical in simplifying a CFG? What happens if the order is reversed?**

**Model Answer:**

The order is critical because a non-reachable symbol in the original grammar may become reachable *only after* non-generating symbols are removed (since removing productions can change the reachability structure). The correct order — **Generation first, Reachability second** — is provably sufficient to remove all useless symbols.

**Counter-Example of Reversed Order:**

Consider:

$$S \rightarrow AC$$
$$A \rightarrow a \mid A$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$
$$C \rightarrow c$$

If we perform **reachability first** on the original grammar, we mark $\{S, A, C, a, c\}$ as reachable, and $B, b$ as non-reachable. We remove $B \rightarrow b$. The remaining grammar has $C \rightarrow c$, which is fine. But $A$ has no production terminating in terminals (only $A \rightarrow a \mid A$), so $A$ is non-generating. We would then remove $A$, but we missed the correct pruning.

**The correct order** first identifies $A$ as non-generating (since $A$ has no terminal-producing production, as $A \rightarrow A$ is recursive with no base case), removes it, and then in Phase 2, finds $C$ non-reachable.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "Reachability first, then Generation" because reachability is conceptually simpler. This is **wrong** and will cost 1 to 2 marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks. Internal choice is mandatory.

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 — Model]

Consider the following CFG $G$:

$$S \rightarrow aA \mid BC$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow bB \mid b$$
$$C \rightarrow cC \mid c$$
$$D \rightarrow dD \mid d$$

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Identify all generating and non-generating symbols in $G$. Construct the grammar $G_1$ obtained after removing all non-generating symbols and their productions.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** From $G_1$, identify all reachable and non-reachable symbols. Construct the final grammar $G'$ after removing all useless symbols and productions. Verify that $L(G) = L(G')$.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Step 1: Find generating symbols via fixed-point iteration.**

$$\text{Gen}_0 = \{a, b, c, d\}$$

**Iteration 1:**
- $A \rightarrow a$: $a \in \text{Gen}_0$. Add $A$. ✓
- $B \rightarrow b$: $b \in \text{Gen}_0$. Add $B$. ✓
- $C \rightarrow c$: $c \in \text{Gen}_0$. Add $C$. ✓
- $D \rightarrow d$: $d \in \text{Gen}_0$. Add $D$. ✓

$$\text{Gen}_1 = \{a, b, c, d, A, B, C, D\}$$

**Iteration 2:**
- $A \rightarrow aA$: $a, A \in \text{Gen}_1$. Already have $A$. ✓
- $B \rightarrow bB$: Already have $B$. ✓
- $C \rightarrow cC$: Already have $C$. ✓
- $D \rightarrow dD$: Already have $D$. ✓
- $S \rightarrow aA$: $a, A \in \text{Gen}_1$. Add $S$. ✓

$$\text{Gen}_2 = \{a, b, c, d, A, B, C, D, S\}$$

**Fixed-point reached.** All variables are generating.

**Wait — re-examine the problem:** Let us re-look at the original grammar carefully. $S \rightarrow BC$ requires both $B$ and $C$ to be generating. Both are. So $S$ is generating. The grammar $G_1$ is identical to $G$ in this case.

**Hmm**, to make this a richer example, let us modify the problem. The above grammar does not yield a non-generating symbol. The correct Part (a) answer for the given $G$:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction to Question A:** The grammar as stated has no non-generating variables. For a meaningful Part (a) demonstration, consider the variant below:
>
> $S \rightarrow aA \mid BC$, $A \rightarrow aA \mid a$, $B \rightarrow bB$, $C \rightarrow c$, $D \rightarrow d$.
>
> In this variant, $B$ is non-generating (only $B \rightarrow bB$, no base case). $D$ is non-generating only if it has no base case. With the original grammar, the Part (a) answer would state: **"All symbols are generating. $G_1 = G$."** (1 mark for the conclusion, 6 marks for the rigorous iteration).

**Final grammar $G_1$ (assuming the variant where $B$ and $D$ have no base cases):**

$$S \rightarrow aA$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid a$$
$$C \rightarrow c$$

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 2: Find reachable symbols from $S$ in $G_1$.**

$$\text{Reach}_0 = \{S\}$$

**Iteration 1:** $S \rightarrow aA$ adds $a$ and $A$.

$$\text{Reach}_1 = \{S, a, A\}$$

**Iteration 2:** $A \rightarrow aA$ — $a$ and $A$ already present. $A \rightarrow a$ — $a$ already present. No additions.

$$\text{Reach}_2 = \{S, a, A\}$$

**Fixed-point reached.** $C$ is **not reachable** from $S$.

**Final Grammar $G'$:**

$$S \rightarrow aA$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid a$$

**Language Verification:**

- $L(G')$: From $S \Rightarrow aA \Rightarrow aaA \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow a^{n}a = a^{n+1}$ for $n \geq 0$. So $L(G') = \{a^{n} : n \geq 1\}$.
- $L(G)$: The only derivations producing terminal strings from $S$ are $S \Rightarrow aA \Rightarrow \cdots \Rightarrow a^{n}$. The productions involving $B$, $C$, $D$ either produce no terminal strings (non-generating) or cannot be reached.
- Therefore $L(G) = L(G') = a^{+}$. ✓

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Model — Alternative Choice]

Consider the following CFG $G$:

$$S \rightarrow aS \mid bA$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid bB$$
$$B \rightarrow aB \mid b$$
$$C \rightarrow cC \mid c$$
$$D \rightarrow d$$

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Eliminate all useless symbols and useless productions from $G$ using the standard two-step algorithm. Show all iterations explicitly.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** List the final set of useful symbols. Briefly justify why each removed symbol is useless.

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

**Phase 1: Finding generating symbols.**

$$\text{Gen}_0 = \{a, b, c, d\}$$

**Iteration 1:**
- $A \rightarrow bB$: $b \in \text{Gen}_0$ but $B$ unknown. Reject.
- $B \rightarrow b$: $b \in \text{Gen}_0$. Add $B$. ✓
- $D \rightarrow d$: $d \in \text{Gen}_0$. Add $D$. ✓
- $C \rightarrow cC$: $C$ unknown. Reject.
- $S \rightarrow aS$, $S \rightarrow bA$: Reject (recursive or unknown).

$$\text{Gen}_1 = \{a, b, c, d, B, D\}$$

**Iteration 2:**
- $A \rightarrow bB$: $b, B \in \text{Gen}_1$. Add $A$. ✓
- $B \rightarrow aB$: $a, B \in \text{Gen}_1$. Already have $B$. ✓
- $C \rightarrow cC$: $C$ unknown. Reject.
- $S \rightarrow bA$: $b \in \text{Gen}_1$, $A$ not yet. Reject for this iteration.
- $S \rightarrow aS$: Reject.

$$\text{Gen}_2 = \{a, b, c, d, A, B, D\}$$

**Iteration 3:**
- $A \rightarrow aA$: $a \in \text{Gen}_2$, $A$ already present. ✓
- $S \rightarrow bA$: $b, A \in \text{Gen}_2$. Add $S$. ✓
- $C \rightarrow cC$: $C$ unknown. Reject.

$$\text{Gen}_3 = \{a, b, c, d, A, B, D, S\}$$

**Iteration 4:** No more productions can add $C$. $C$ is non-generating.

$$\text{Gen}_{\text{final}} = \{a, b, c, d, A, B, D, S\}$$

**Non-generating variable:** $C$.

**Pruning Phase 1:** Remove $C$ and $C \rightarrow cC$.

**Grammar $G_1$:**

$$S \rightarrow aS \mid bA$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid bB$$
$$B \rightarrow aB \mid b$$
$$D \rightarrow d$$

**Phase 2: Finding reachable symbols from $S$ in $G_1$.**

$$\text{Reach}_0 = \{S\}$$

**Iteration 1:** $S \rightarrow aS$ adds $a$. $S \rightarrow bA$ adds $b$ and $A$.

$$\text{Reach}_1 = \{S, a, b, A\}$$

**Iteration 2:** $A \rightarrow aA$ — $a, A$ present. $A \rightarrow bB$ — adds $B$.

$$\text{Reach}_2 = \{S, a, b, A, B\}$$

**Iteration 3:** $B \rightarrow aB$ — present. $B \rightarrow b$ — present. No new additions.

$$\text{Reach}_3 = \{S, a, b, A, B\}$$

**Non-reachable symbols:** $c$ (terminal) and $D$ (variable) and the production $D \rightarrow d$.

**Pruning Phase 2:** Remove $D$, $d$, and $D \rightarrow d$.

**Final Grammar $G'$:**

$$S \rightarrow aS \mid bA$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid bB$$
$$B \rightarrow aB \mid b$$

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Final Set of Useful Symbols:** $\{S, A, B, a, b\}$.

| Symbol | Status | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| $S$ | **Useful** | Reachable (start) AND generating ($S \Rightarrow^{*} a^{n}b a^{m} b a^{k} b$) |
| $A$ | **Useful** | Reachable ($S \rightarrow bA$) AND generating ($A \Rightarrow a^{*} b B$) |
| $B$ | **Useful** | Reachable ($A \rightarrow bB$) AND generating ($B \Rightarrow a^{*} b$) |
| $a, b$ | **Useful** | Reachable terminals AND trivially generating |
| $c$ | **Useless** | Non-generating (no derivation reaches a pure terminal) |
| $C$ | **Useless** | Non-generating (no production derives a terminal string) |
| $d$ | **Useless** | Non-reachable (no path from $S$ leads to $D$) |
| $D$ | **Useless** | Non-reachable (no path from $S$ involves $D$) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Reverse Order Trap:** Do **not** perform reachability elimination first. This is a guaranteed 1–2 mark deduction.
