> 2. **Forgetting $D$:** Many students remove $C$ in Phase 1 and stop. They forget that $D \rightarrow d$ also needs Phase 2 removal. $D$ is generating but unreachable — both conditions must be checked.
> 3. **Empty Production Confusion:** If a production has $\epsilon$ (epsilon) on the RHS, ensure the LHS variable is correctly classified. Epsilon is not a terminal, and the LHS is generating only if all other productions (or this one) lead to terminal strings.
> 4. **Skipping Iterations:** Always write the iteration number and the updated set. A single misnamed iteration costs 0.5 to 1 mark in KTU scripts.
> 5. **Verification Step Omission:** Always conclude with $L(G) = L(G')$. This 1-line verification distinguishes a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- **Useful Symbol = Generating ∩ Reachable** (logical AND, not OR).
- **Order of Elimination: Phase 1 (Generation) → Phase 2 (Reachability).** Never reverse.
- **Terminals are always generating** by default. Start $\text{Gen}_0 = \Sigma$.
- **Start symbol is always reachable** by default. Start $\text{Reach}_0 = \{S\}$.
- **Generating check:** A variable $A$ is generating iff there exists a production $A \rightarrow \beta$ where every symbol of $\beta$ is in the current generating set.
- **Reachable check:** A symbol $X$ is reachable iff there exists a reachable variable $A$ with a production $A \rightarrow \alpha X \beta$.
- **Traps to Avoid:**
  - A generating but unreachable symbol (e.g., $C$ in our example) is **still useless**.
  - A reachable but non-generating symbol is **still useless** (e.g., a dead-end $B$).
  - Removing a non-generating symbol can make previously unreachable symbols newly reachable — this is why order matters.
- **Termination:** Both algorithms halt in at most $\vert V \vert + 1$ iterations.
- **Language Equivalence:** $L(G) = L(G')$ is preserved by construction; always state this explicitly in your answer.
- **Examination Heuristic:** For every symbol, perform a **two-question mental test**:
  1. Can it eventually produce terminals? (Generation)
  2. Can $S$ reach it? (Reachability)
  - If either answer is **NO**, the symbol is useless and must be removed.
- **Pipeline Position:** This simplification is a pre-requisite step in the conversion of CFG → CNF → GNF, and is also used in the proof that $L(G) = \emptyset$ is decidable.
- **Examiner's Hot Spot:** A "tricky" question often provides a grammar where Phase 1 and Phase 2 both produce changes — practice identifying such grammars to score full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eliminating Epsilon Productions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Eliminating Epsilon Productions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of Context-Free Grammars (CFGs), an **ε-production (epsilon production)** is a production rule of the form:

$$A \rightarrow \varepsilon$$

where $A$ is a non-terminal symbol and $\varepsilon$ denotes the empty string. These productions are also commonly called **null productions** or **lambda productions**.

A non-terminal $A$ is called a **nullable variable (or nullable non-terminal)** if there exists a derivation:

$$A \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} \varepsilon$$

That is, $A$ can derive the empty string through one or more derivation steps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 3):**
> The process of eliminating ε-productions is a grammar simplification step. It transforms a given CFG $G$ into an equivalent CFG $G'$ such that $L(G) = L(G')$, but $G'$ contains no ε-productions (with the single exception of $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ if $\varepsilon \in L(G)$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Theorem:**
> If $G$ is a CFG with language $L$, then there exists a CFG $G'$ with no ε-productions such that $L(G') = L(G)$ if $\varepsilon \notin L$, and $L(G') = L(G) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$ if we strictly remove all ε-productions. To preserve $\varepsilon$ in the language, we keep only $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ when $S$ itself is nullable.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Skip Button" Analogy

Imagine you are writing a recipe (the grammar) that produces a string (a sentence). An **ε-production** is like a step in the recipe that says *"skip this ingredient entirely"* — it adds nothing to the final dish.

A **nullable variable** is like an ingredient that *can* be omitted. For example, in the recipe:

> "Bread + (Optional Cheese) + Patty + (Optional Sauce) + Bread"

Here, *Cheese* and *Sauce* are nullable — they may or may not appear. The recipe is valid regardless of whether they are included. Our goal is to **rewrite the recipe to explicitly list every possible combination** of optional ingredients, so that no step ever says "skip me."

### Why Do We Eliminate ε-Productions?

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Theoretical Normalisation** | Simplified grammars are easier to analyse (e.g., for CNF, GNF conversion). |
| **Parser Simplicity** | Many parsing algorithms (especially LL and LR parsers) cannot handle ε-productions cleanly. |
| **Closure Property Verification** | Helps in proving that CFLs are closed under union, concatenation, etc., without ε complications. |
| **Algorithm Compatibility** | Algorithms like CYK require grammars in CNF, which forbids ε-productions. |

## 1.3 Notational Conventions Used

- $V$ : Set of non-terminals (variables)
- $\Sigma$ : Set of terminal symbols
- $P$ : Set of production rules
- $S$ : Start symbol
- $NULL$ : Set of nullable variables
- $\varepsilon$ : Empty string (length = **0**)
- $\overset{*}{\Rightarrow}$ : "Derives in zero or more steps"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** A production of the form $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is permitted only for the start symbol $S$ in the *final* simplified grammar, and only if $\varepsilon \in L(G)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Nullable Set — Iterative Computation

The set of nullable variables is computed as the **least fixed point** of an iterative process. Let $N_i$ denote the set of nullable variables after the $i$-th iteration.

**Base Case:**
$$N_0 = \{ A \in V \mid A \rightarrow \varepsilon \in P \}$$

**Inductive Step:**
$$N_{i+1} = N_i \cup \{ A \in V \mid \exists A \rightarrow \alpha \in P, \text{ and every symbol in } \alpha \text{ is in } N_i \}$$

**Termination:** The process stops when $N_{i+1} = N_i$. The final set is:
$$NULL = N_i = N_{i+1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a fixed point?** Consider the grammar: $S \rightarrow ASA$, $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$. Here, $S$ is nullable only if we already know $S$ is nullable — a circular dependency. The fixed-point iteration resolves this elegantly by computing the *smallest* set closed under the production rules.

## 2.2 The Algorithm — Step-by-Step Procedure

Given a CFG $G = (V, \Sigma, P, S)$, we construct $G' = (V, \Sigma, P', S)$ with no ε-productions (except possibly $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$).

### Step 1: Compute NULL
Find the set of all nullable non-terminals using the fixed-point iteration above.

### Step 2: Generate Replacement Productions
For every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n$ in $P$ (where $n \geq 1$):

- For each subset $T \subseteq \{i \mid X_i \in NULL\}$, construct a new production:
$$A \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \cdots Y_n$$
where:
$$Y_i = \begin{cases} X_i & \text{if } i \notin T \\ \varepsilon \text{ (delete)} & \text{if } i \in T \end{cases}$$

- **Exclude** the case where $T = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ (i.e., the entire RHS is deleted, which would yield $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$).

### Step 3: Handle the Start Symbol
If $S \in NULL$ (i.e., $\varepsilon \in L(G)$), add the production $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ to $P'$.

### Step 4: Remove Original ε-Productions
Delete all productions of the form $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ from $P$ before processing (they contribute no new non-trivial variants).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Notation | Meaning | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| $NULL$ | Set of nullable variables | $NULL = \{A \in V : A \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} \varepsilon\}$ |
| $N_0$ | Initial nullable set | $N_0 = \{A : A \rightarrow \varepsilon \in P\}$ |
| $N_{i+1}$ | Next iteration set | $N_i \cup \{A : A \rightarrow \alpha, \alpha \subseteq N_i\}$ |
| $L(G)$ | Language of grammar $G$ | Set of all terminal strings derived from $S$ |
| $P'$ | New production set | All variants from Step 2 $\cup$ optional $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| Subset rule | Delete nullable symbols | $T \subseteq \{i : X_i \in NULL\}$, $T \neq \{1,\ldots,n\}$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The elimination of ε-productions is foundational in:

- **Compiler Design:** Converting grammars to forms suitable for parser generators (YACC, ANTLR, Bison).
- **Natural Language Processing:** Normalising probabilistic grammars for efficient parsing.
- **Program Verification:** Simplifying grammar-based specifications for model checking.
- **Bioinformatics:** RNA secondary structure grammars often need ε-removal to analyse loops.

## 2.5 Important Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Termination Guarantee:** The fixed-point iteration terminates in at most $\vert V \vert$ iterations, where $\vert V \vert$ is the number of non-terminals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Size Bound:** If the original grammar has $n$ productions and $\vert NULL \vert = k$, the new grammar can have up to $O(n \cdot 2^k)$ productions in the worst case. This is acceptable in practice because $k$ is typically small.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example

**Given CFG $G$:**
$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB$$
$$A \rightarrow B \mid S$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid \varepsilon$$

We will eliminate all ε-productions step by step.

---

### **Step 1: Compute the Set of Nullable Variables**

We apply the fixed-point iteration:

**Iteration $N_0$:** Identify variables that derive $\varepsilon$ directly.
$$N_0 = \{B\}$$
Reason: $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is a direct ε-production.

**Iteration $N_1$:** Find new variables whose RHS consists entirely of nullable symbols.
- Check $A \rightarrow B$: $B \in N_0$, so add $A$.
- Check $S \rightarrow ASA$: We need $A, S, A \in N_0$. But $S \notin N_0$ and $A \notin N_0$. So $S$ is not added.

$$N_1 = \{B, A\}$$

**Iteration $N_2$:** Check again.
- Check $S \rightarrow ASA$: We need $A, S, A \in N_1$. We have $A \in N_1$, but $S \notin N_1$. So $S$ is **not yet** added.

Hmm, $S$ is not added. Let me recheck.

Wait. $S \rightarrow ASA$ requires $A \in NULL$ AND $S \in NULL$ AND $A \in NULL$. Since $S$ is not in $N_1$, $S$ is not added. So:

$$N_2 = \{B, A\}$$

This means $S$ is **NOT nullable**. Let me re-examine the example.

Actually, let me reconsider. $S \rightarrow ASA$. If $A$ is nullable, can we have $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$? Yes. But the middle symbol is $S$ itself, which must be nullable. So this is circular.

So in this particular grammar, $S$ is NOT nullable. Let me confirm: Can $S$ derive $\varepsilon$?

$S \rightarrow ASA$. If $A$ derives $\varepsilon$, we have $S \rightarrow \varepsilon SA$. For this to yield $\varepsilon$, we need $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ — circular.

$S \rightarrow aB$. The $a$ cannot be erased, so $S$ cannot derive $\varepsilon$ through this production.

So $S$ is **not nullable** in this grammar.

$$NULL = \{A, B\}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Correction:** In this example, only $A$ and $B$ are nullable, not $S$. The circular case I initially considered doesn't apply because $S$ is in the middle of its own production.

---

### **Step 2: Generate Replacement Productions**

**Production $S \rightarrow ASA$:**
- Nullable symbols in RHS: $A$ (position 1), $A$ (position 3). $S$ is NOT nullable.
- Subsets of nullable positions to delete (excluding the full set):
  - Delete nothing: $ASA$
  - Delete first $A$: $SA$
  - Delete last $A$: $AS$
  - Delete both $A$'s: $S$
- New productions: $S \rightarrow ASA \mid SA \mid AS \mid S$

**Production $S \rightarrow aB$:**
- Nullable symbol: $B$ (position 2). $a$ is NOT nullable.
- Subsets to delete (excluding full):
  - Delete nothing: $aB$
  - Delete $B$: $a$
- New productions: $S \rightarrow aB \mid a$

**Production $A \rightarrow B$:**
- Nullable symbol: $B$.
- Subsets to delete (excluding full):
  - Delete nothing: $B$
  - Delete $B$: $\varepsilon$ — **excluded**
- New production: $A \rightarrow B$

**Production $A \rightarrow S$:**
- Nullable symbol: $S$. But $S$ is NOT nullable.
- Subsets to delete: only the empty subset.
- New production: $A \rightarrow S$

**Production $B \rightarrow b$:**
- Nullable symbol: none ($b$ is a terminal).
- New production: $B \rightarrow b$

**Production $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$:**
- This is the original ε-production. **Removed entirely.**

---

### **Step 3: Handle the Start Symbol**

Is $S$ nullable? **No**, $S \notin NULL$.

Therefore, we **do not** add $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

---

### **Step 4: Final Grammar $G'$**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow ASA \mid SA \mid AS \mid S \mid aB \mid a \\
A &\rightarrow B \mid S \\
B &\rightarrow b
\end{aligned}}$$

---

### **Step 5: Verification of Equivalence**

We can verify by tracing key derivations:

| Original Derivation | New Grammar Equivalent |
|---|---|
| $S \Rightarrow aB \Rightarrow ab$ | $S \Rightarrow aB \Rightarrow ab$ ✓ |
| $S \Rightarrow ASA \Rightarrow aBA \Rightarrow aba$ | $S \Rightarrow SA \Rightarrow aBA \Rightarrow aba$ ✓ |
| $S \Rightarrow ASA \Rightarrow aBS \Rightarrow aBb$ | $S \Rightarrow ASA \Rightarrow aBS$ (or $AS$) $\Rightarrow \cdots$ ✓ |

The language is preserved.

---

## 3.2 A Second Example (Start Symbol is Nullable)

**Given CFG $G_2$:**
$$S \rightarrow AB$$
$$A \rightarrow aA \mid \varepsilon$$
$$B \rightarrow bB \mid \varepsilon$$

**Nullable Set:**
- $N_0 = \{A, B\}$
- $N_1 = \{A, B, S\}$ (since $S \rightarrow AB$, and $A, B \in N_0$)
- $NULL = \{S, A, B\}$

**New Productions:**
- $S \rightarrow AB$: delete $A$, delete $B$, or both.
  - $AB$, $A$, $B$, $\varepsilon$ (excluded)
  - Add: $S \rightarrow AB \mid A \mid B$
- $A \rightarrow aA$: $A$ is nullable, $a$ is not.
  - $aA$, $a$
- $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$: removed.
- $B \rightarrow bB$: similar to above.
  - $bB$, $b$
- $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$: removed.

**Since $S$ is nullable, add $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$.**

**Final Grammar $G_2'$:**
$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow AB \mid A \mid B \mid \varepsilon \\
A &\rightarrow aA \mid a \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b
\end{aligned}}$$

**Verification:** $S \Rightarrow AB \Rightarrow aB \Rightarrow ab$, or $S \Rightarrow A \Rightarrow a$, or $S \Rightarrow B \Rightarrow b$, or $S \Rightarrow \varepsilon$. All correct.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Aspect | Example 1 ($S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB$) | Example 2 ($S \rightarrow AB$) |
|---|---|---|
| Nullable Set | $\{A, B\}$ | $\{S, A, B\}$ |
| Start Symbol Nullable? | No | Yes |
| $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ Added? | No | Yes |
| Number of New Productions | 8 | 6 |
| ε-Productions in Result | 0 | 1 (only $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following flowchart illustrates the complete procedure for eliminating ε-productions:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input: CFG with epsilon productions"] --> B["Step 1: Compute NULL set"]
    B --> B1["Initialize N0 with variables having direct epsilon production"]
    B1 --> B2["Iterate: Add A if all symbols in some production A to alpha are in current NULL"]
    B2 --> B3{"Does NULL change?"}
    B3 -->|"Yes"| B2
    B3 -->|"No - Fixed Point Reached"| C["Step 2: Generate Replacement Productions"]
    C --> C1["For each production A to X1 X2 to Xn"]
    C1 --> C2["Identify nullable Xi positions"]
    C2 --> C3["Generate all non-empty subsets of deletions"]
    C3 --> C4{"Full deletion yields epsilon?"}
    C4 -->|"Yes"| C5["Exclude this variant"]
    C4 -->|"No"| C6["Add A to new RHS to P-prime"]
    C5 --> D["Step 3: Handle Start Symbol"]
    C6 --> D
    D --> D1{"Is S in NULL?"}
    D1 -->|"Yes"| D2["Add S to epsilon to P-prime"]
    D1 -->|"No"| E["Step 4: Remove all original epsilon productions"]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F["Output: Equivalent CFG without epsilon productions"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture

This diagram shows the *architectural flow* of the algorithm, mapping how each component interacts with the others:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Stage"]
        I1["Original CFG G = V, Sigma, P, S"]
    end

    subgraph NULLCOMP["Nullability Analysis Engine"]
        N1["Direct Epsilon Detector"]
        N2["Fixed-Point Iterator"]
        N3["Nullable Set Repository"]
    end

    subgraph REWRITER["Production Rewriter Module"]
        R1["Subset Enumerator"]
        R2["Variant Constructor"]
        R3["Epsilon Filter"]
    end

    subgraph FINISH["Finalisation Module"]
        F1["Start Symbol Checker"]
        F2["Epsilon Eraser"]
        F3["Simplified Grammar Builder"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Stage"]
        O1["Equivalent CFG G-prime = V, Sigma, P-prime, S"]
    end

    I1 --> N1
    N1 --> N3
    N3 --> N2
    N2 -->|"Iterate until stable"| N3
    N3 --> R1
    I1 --> R1
    R1 --> R2
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> F1
    R3 --> F2
    F1 --> F3
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> O1
```

## 4.3 Nullable Set Computation — Visual Trace

For the grammar $S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB$, $A \rightarrow B \mid S$, $B \rightarrow b \mid \varepsilon$:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I0["Iteration N0: B has direct epsilon production"] --> N0["N0 = curly B"]
    N0 --> I1["Iteration N1: Check A to B since B in N0, add A"]
    I1 --> N1["N1 = curly A, B"]
    N1 --> I2["Iteration N2: Check S to ASA, need A, S, A all in N1"]
    I2 --> Q{"Is S in N1?"}
    Q -->|"No"| STOP["Fixed point reached"]
    Q -->|"Yes - hypothetical"| ADD["Add S to set"]
    STOP --> RESULT["NULL = curly A, B"]
    ADD --> RESULT
```

## 4.4 Transformation Mapping Diagram

This diagram shows how each original production maps to its new variants:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["S to ASA"] --> P1a["S to ASA"]
    P1 --> P1b["S to SA - delete first A"]
    P1 --> P1c["S to AS - delete last A"]
    P1 --> P1d["S to S - delete both A"]

    P2["S to aB"] --> P2a["S to aB"]
    P2 --> P2b["S to a - delete B"]

    P3["A to B"] --> P3a["A to B - keep"]

    P4["A to S"] --> P4a["A to S - keep"]

    P5["B to b"] --> P5a["B to b - keep"]

    P6["B to epsilon"] --> P6a["REMOVED entirely"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the following with respect to context-free grammars:**
**(a)** ε-production
**(b)** Nullable variable

**Model Answer:**

**(a) ε-production:** A production of the form $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$, where $A$ is a non-terminal and $\varepsilon$ is the empty string. It allows the non-terminal $A$ to be replaced by nothing during derivation.

**(b) Nullable variable:** A non-terminal $A$ is called nullable if $A \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} \varepsilon$, i.e., there exists a sequence of derivations that reduces $A$ to the empty string. Mathematically, $NULL = \{A \in V : A \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} \varepsilon\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Definition (2 marks) + Example (1 mark).

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State any two reasons why ε-productions need to be eliminated from a CFG.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Simplification of Grammar:** Eliminating ε-productions reduces the complexity of the grammar, making it easier to analyse and convert into normal forms like CNF (Chomsky Normal Form) or GNF (Greibach Normal Form).

2. **Parser Compatibility:** Many parsing algorithms (LL, LR, LALR) used in compiler construction cannot directly handle ε-productions. Their elimination is a prerequisite for automated parser generation tools like YACC and Bison.

3. **Closure Property Proofs:** The standard proofs that CFLs are closed under union, concatenation, and Kleene star assume grammars without ε-productions to avoid degenerate cases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution:** Each reason carries 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the procedure to eliminate ε-productions from a context-free grammar. (7 marks)

**(b)** Given the following CFG, eliminate all ε-productions. (7 marks)

$$S \rightarrow aSb \mid A$$
$$A \rightarrow bA \mid \varepsilon$$

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — The Procedure (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Discovery of Nullable Variables**

We compute the set $NULL$ of all nullable non-terminals using fixed-point iteration:
- Initialise $N_0 = \{A : A \rightarrow \varepsilon \in P\}$
- Iterate: $N_{i+1} = N_i \cup \{A : \exists A \rightarrow \alpha, \alpha \subseteq N_i\}$
- Stop when $N_i = N_{i+1}$. Then $NULL = N_i$.

**Step 2: Production Enumeration**

For each production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \cdots X_n$ in $P$ (where $n \geq 1$):
- Identify positions of nullable variables.
- For every non-empty proper subset of these positions, create a new production by deleting those symbols.
- Do not create $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ from this step.

**Step 3: Start Symbol Special Case**

If $S \in NULL$ (meaning $\varepsilon \in L(G)$), add the production $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ to the new grammar $G'$.

**Step 4: Cleanup**

Remove all original ε-productions from the grammar.

The resulting grammar $G'$ satisfies $L(G') = L(G)$ and contains no ε-productions (except possibly $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$).

#### **Part (b) — Worked Example (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute NULL**

**Iteration $N_0$:** Direct ε-productions.
- $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is direct.
$$N_0 = \{A\}$$

**Iteration $N_1$:** Check for indirect nullable variables.
- $S \rightarrow A$: $A \in N_0$, so add $S$.
- $S \rightarrow aSb$: $a$ and $b$ are terminals (not nullable).
$$N_1 = \{A, S\}$$

**Iteration $N_2$:** Check for further additions.
- No new additions possible.
$$N_2 = N_1$$

**Therefore:** $NULL = \{A, S\}$

**Step 2: Generate New Productions**

**Production $S \rightarrow aSb$:** No nullable variables in RHS.
$$S \rightarrow aSb \quad \text{(no change)}$$

**Production $S \rightarrow A$:** $A$ is nullable.
- Delete $A$: $\varepsilon$ — **excluded**
- Keep $A$
$$S \rightarrow A$$

**Production $A \rightarrow bA$:** $A$ is nullable, $b$ is not.
- Keep both: $bA$
- Delete $A$: $b$
$$A \rightarrow bA \mid b$$

**Production $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$:** **Removed entirely.**

**Step 3: Handle Start Symbol**

Is $S \in NULL$? **Yes** ($N_1 = \{A, S\}$).

Therefore, add $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

**Step 4: Final Grammar $G'$**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow aSb \mid A \mid \varepsilon \\
A &\rightarrow bA \mid b
\end{aligned}}$$

**Verification:** $L(G) = \{a^n b^n : n \geq 1\} \cup \{b^m : m \geq 1\} \cup \{\varepsilon\}$. The new grammar $G'$ produces the same set. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Define nullable non-terminal. Explain with an example how the set of nullable non-terminals is computed using fixed-point iteration. (7 marks)

**(b)** Eliminate ε-productions from the following grammar: (7 marks)

$$S \rightarrow AB$$
$$A \rightarrow aAb \mid \varepsilon$$
$$B \rightarrow bB \mid \varepsilon$$

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Definition and Fixed-Point Computation (7 marks)**

**Definition:** A non-terminal $A$ is **nullable** if $A \overset{*}{\Rightarrow} \varepsilon$.

**Fixed-Point Computation Example:**

Consider: $S \rightarrow AC$, $A \rightarrow aA \mid \varepsilon$, $C \rightarrow c$.

- $N_0 = \{A\}$
- $N_1 = N_0$ (no new additions since $S$ requires $A$ and $C$, but $C \notin N_0$)
- $NULL = \{A\}$

**Step-by-step procedure for general case:**

1. Start with $N_0 = \{A : A \rightarrow \varepsilon\}$.
2. Repeatedly add $A$ to $N_i$ if some production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ has all symbols in $N_i$.
3. Stop when $N_{i+1} = N_i$.

#### **Part (b) — Worked Example (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute NULL**

- $N_0 = \{A, B\}$ (both have direct ε-productions)
- $N_1 = \{A, B, S\}$ (since $S \rightarrow AB$, and $A, B \in N_0$)
- $NULL = \{A, B, S\}$

**Step 2: Generate New Productions**

**$S \rightarrow AB$:** $A$ and $B$ are nullable.
- Keep both: $AB$
- Delete $A$: $B$
- Delete $B$: $A$
- Delete both: $\varepsilon$ (excluded)
$$S \rightarrow AB \mid A \mid B$$

**$A \rightarrow aAb$:** No nullable variables.
$$A \rightarrow aAb$$

**$A \rightarrow \varepsilon$:** Removed.

**$B \rightarrow bB$:** $B$ nullable, $b$ not.
$$B \rightarrow bB \mid b$$

**$B \rightarrow \varepsilon$:** Removed.

**Step 3: Handle Start Symbol**

$S \in NULL$, so add $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$.

**Step 4: Final Grammar $G'$**

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned}
S &\rightarrow AB \mid A \mid B \mid \varepsilon \\
A &\rightarrow aAb \\
B &\rightarrow bB \mid b
\end{aligned}}$$

**Verification:** $L(G) = \{a^n b^n b^m : n, m \geq 0\} \cup \{a^n b^n : n \geq 0\} \cup \{b^m : m \geq 0\} \cup \{\varepsilon\}$. The new grammar produces the same set. ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Start Symbol:** Many students forget to add $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ when $S$ is nullable, losing 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Including ε in Variants:** Some students generate $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ as a "variant" from a production like $A \rightarrow B$ where $B$ is nullable. This is incorrect — the ε-productions are generated *only* for $S$ if it was nullable.
> 3. **Missing Subsets:** When there are multiple nullable symbols, students often miss certain combinations. Always enumerate all $2^k - 1$ non-empty subsets carefully.
> 4. **Not Removing Original ε-Productions:** Always explicitly state that the original $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ rules are removed.
> 5. **Confusing Nullable with Useless:** A nullable variable is not necessarily useless. It must still be processed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ An **ε-production** has the form $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$.
- ✅ A variable is **nullable** if it can derive $\varepsilon$ in zero or more steps.
- ✅ **Nullable set** is computed via **fixed-point iteration** (at most $|V|$ iterations).
- ✅ The **algorithm** has 4 steps: Compute NULL → Generate variants → Handle $S$ → Remove ε-productions.
- ✅ For each production with $k$ nullable symbols, generate up to $2^k - 1$ new productions.
- ✅ The variant $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is **never added** in Step 2 (it is only added for $S$ in Step 3 if needed).
- ✅ **$S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is added** only if $S$ was nullable in the *original* grammar.
- ✅ The result grammar $G'$ has **no ε-productions** except possibly $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$.
- ✅ $L(G') = L(G)$ is guaranteed by construction.
- ✅ The worst-case size of $G'$ is $O(n \cdot 2^k)$ where $n$ is the number of productions and $k = |NULL|$.
- ✅ This is a **prerequisite** for CNF and GNF conversions (Module 3/4 topics).
- ✅ **Fixed-point iterations always terminate** because the set $NULL$ can only grow, and $V$ is finite.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Circular dependencies (e.g., $S \rightarrow ASA$ where $S$ is in the middle) are resolved by fixed-point iteration, not by ad-hoc reasoning.
- ✅ **Verification step:** Always check a few derivations to confirm $L(G) = L(G')$.
- ✅ **Units/metrics:** No physical units — this is a discrete mathematics topic. Key parameter is $|NULL|$ (the cardinality of the nullable set).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Eliminating Unit Productions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Eliminating Unit Productions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

Let $G = (V, T, P, S)$ be a context-free grammar (CFG) where $V$ denotes the set of non-terminals (variables), $T$ the set of terminals, $P$ the set of productions, and $S$ the designated start symbol.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Unit Production (Definition)**
> A production of the form $\mathbf{A \rightarrow B}$ where both $A$ and $B$ are **non-terminals** ($A, B \in V$) and there are no terminal symbols on the right-hand side is called a **Unit Production** (also called a *single production* or *chain rule*).

If the grammar contains $\varepsilon$ (the empty string) on the right-hand side, the production $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is called a *null production* (or *epsilon production*). It is **not** classified as a unit production.

**Goal of Elimination:** Transform the given CFG into an equivalent grammar $G' = (V', T, P', S)$ such that:
1. $L(G') = L(G)$ (language equivalence).
2. $P'$ contains **no unit productions**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Real-World Analogy — The Office Hierarchy**
> Imagine a corporate office where Manager X forwards every document directly to Manager Y without adding any content (think: *"X says 'forward to Y'"*). This is essentially a *unit production* $X \rightarrow Y$ — no real work happens, it is just a chain of command.
>
> Now, if Manager Y actually does the work (e.g., $Y \rightarrow$ "write a report"), we can simply say that Manager X also writes the report directly (i.e., $X \rightarrow$ "write a report"). The *chain* is bypassed while preserving the final outcome.
>
> **Eliminating unit productions is exactly this**: we replace every chain-of-command shortcut ($A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow \dots \rightarrow \text{work}$) with a direct *work-producing* rule from the top.

### 1.3 Why Unit Productions Matter in KTU Examinations

Unit productions are problematic for several algorithmic and theoretical purposes. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to recognize this:

- **Parser Generators** (e.g., the CYK Algorithm) operate on **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)**, which strictly forbids unit productions.
- **Grammar simplification algorithms** used in *compiler design* often need unit-free grammars as an intermediate step.
- **Membership testing and parsing** become inefficient if unit productions are present, because the parser can spend many steps merely chasing non-terminal pointers without consuming input.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Standard KTU Position in the CNF Pipeline**
> When converting a CFG into CNF, the universally accepted KTU (and classical Hopcroft–Ullman) order is:
> 1. Eliminate start symbol from right-hand side.
> 2. Eliminate $\varepsilon$-productions.
> 3. **Eliminate unit productions** *(our current topic)*.
> 4. Eliminate useless symbols.
> 5. Convert to proper CNF form ($A \rightarrow BC$ and $A \rightarrow a$).
>
> Order 3 must strictly follow Order 2.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mathematical Foundation

Let us define the **unit derivation** relation precisely.

**Definition (Unit Derivation):** We say $A \Rightarrow^{*} B$ via unit productions if there exists a sequence of non-terminals $A = C_0, C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k = B$ such that for each $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$, the production $C_{i-1} \rightarrow C_i$ exists in $P$. The intermediate string during such derivation contains **only non-terminals** (no terminals).

The set of all such $B$ derivable from $A$ (including $A$ itself) is denoted:

$$U(A) = \{B \in V \mid A \Rightarrow^{*} B \text{ using only unit productions}\}$$

By definition, $A \in U(A)$ for every non-terminal $A$ (the trivial one-step derivation with $k=0$).

### 2.2 The Elimination Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic)

The classical Hopcroft–Ullman algorithm to eliminate unit productions proceeds as follows:

1. **Pre-condition:** The input CFG $G$ must have **no $\varepsilon$-productions**.
2. **Compute** $U(A)$ for every non-terminal $A \in V$. This can be done efficiently using the **Warshall-like transitive closure** algorithm on the unit-production graph.
3. **For every** non-terminal $A \in V$ and for every $B \in U(A)$:
   - For every **non-unit** production $B \rightarrow \alpha$ (where $\alpha$ is *not* a single non-terminal), add the production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $P'$.
4. **Delete** all unit productions from $P'$.
5. The resulting grammar $G'$ is equivalent to $G$ and contains no unit productions.

### 2.3 Theoretical Guarantees

> [!IMPORTANT] **Soundness and Completeness**
> - **Equivalence:** $L(G') = L(G)$ for every sentential form that derives a terminal string.
> - **Termination:** The new set of productions $P'$ is finite because the original grammar $G$ had finitely many productions.
> - **No new symbols:** $V' = V$; only the production set $P'$ changes. (Contrast this with $\varepsilon$-elimination, which may introduce new symbols.)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Notation | Meaning | Unit / Domain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $A \rightarrow B$ | Unit production | $A, B \in V$ |
| $U(A)$ | Set of non-terminals reachable from $A$ via unit productions | Subset of $V$ |
| $\vert U(A) \vert$ | Cardinality of $U(A)$ | Integer $\leq \vert V \vert$ |
| $\mathcal{U}$ | The set of all unit-production pairs $(A,B)$ with $A \Rightarrow^* B$ | Subset of $V \times V$ |
| $k$ | Length of unit derivation chain | Integer $\geq 0$ |
| $A \rightarrow \alpha$ | Non-unit production | $\vert \alpha \vert \geq 2$ or $\alpha \in T \cup T^{*}$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Construction:** Tools like YACC and Bison internally normalize grammars; removing unit productions is a critical pre-processing step before CNF conversion.
- **The CYK Membership Algorithm** (a KTU favourite) operates strictly on CNF, which is unit-production-free.
- **Program Analysis and Verification:** Decision procedures for formal verification use normalized CFGs to enable efficient reachability queries.
- **Bioinformatics:** RNA secondary structure prediction tools often use stochastic CFGs and benefit from unit-free normal forms.

### 2.6 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Reason |
| :--- | :--- |
| Eliminate $\varepsilon$-productions **first** | Otherwise $A \rightarrow B$ combined with $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ could introduce new null behaviour that messes up our unit chain analysis. |
| Compute $U(A)$ via transitive closure | Captures *all* chains of unit productions including indirect cycles (e.g., $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C \rightarrow A$). |
| Add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ for each non-unit $B \rightarrow \alpha$ | Preserves every derivation that previously went through the chain $A \Rightarrow^{*} B \Rightarrow \alpha$. |
| Remove all unit productions | Achieves the final unit-free form. |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Complete Walkthrough

**Given CFG $G$:**

$$
S \rightarrow A \mid BB
$$

$$
A \rightarrow a \mid B
$$

$$
B \rightarrow b
$$

We must eliminate all unit productions. First, observe there are **no $\varepsilon$-productions**, so the pre-condition is satisfied.

#### Step 1 — Identify the Unit Productions

Scanning $P$, we find the unit production:

$$
A \rightarrow B
$$

There is also the chain $S \rightarrow A$, which is **another unit production**. Let us list them:

| # | Unit Production |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $S \rightarrow A$ |
| 2 | $A \rightarrow B$ |

The non-unit productions are $S \rightarrow BB$, $A \rightarrow a$, $B \rightarrow b$.

#### Step 2 — Compute $U(A)$ for Every Non-Terminal

Using transitive closure on the unit-production graph $A \rightarrow B$, $S \rightarrow A$:

- **$U(S)$:** $S$ is trivially in $U(S)$. From $S$, we can directly go to $A$ (via $S \rightarrow A$), then from $A$ to $B$ (via $A \rightarrow B$). So:
$$U(S) = \{S,\ A,\ B\}$$

- **$U(A)$:** $A$ is trivially in $U(A)$. From $A$, we can go to $B$ (via $A \rightarrow B$).
$$U(A) = \{A,\ B\}$$

- **$U(B)$:** $B$ is trivially in $U(B)$. $B$ has no outgoing unit productions.
$$U(B) = \{B\}$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute Unit Chains with Their Non-Unit Consequences

For every $A$ and every $B \in U(A)$, we add the non-unit productions of $B$ to $A$.

**(a) For $S$, $B \in \{S, A, B\}$:**
- $B = S$: non-unit productions of $S$ are $\{S \rightarrow BB\}$. Add $S \rightarrow BB$ (already present).
- $B = A$: non-unit productions of $A$ are $\{A \rightarrow a\}$. Add $S \rightarrow a$.
- $B = B$: non-unit productions of $B$ are $\{B \rightarrow b\}$. Add $S \rightarrow b$.

**(b) For $A$, $B \in \{A, B\}$:**
- $B = A$: non-unit productions of $A$ are $\{A \rightarrow a\}$. Add $A \rightarrow a$ (already present).
- $B = B$: non-unit productions of $B$ are $\{B \rightarrow b\}$. Add $A \rightarrow b$.

**(c) For $B$, $B \in \{B\}$:**
- $B = B$: add $B \rightarrow b$ (already present).

#### Step 4 — Remove All Unit Productions

Delete $S \rightarrow A$ and $A \rightarrow B$.

#### Final Grammar $G'$

$$
S \rightarrow a \mid b \mid BB
$$

$$
A \rightarrow a \mid b
$$

$$
B \rightarrow b
$$

**Verification:** $L(G) = \{a, b, b b, b b b, \dots\} \cup \{a\}$ — let us verify by tracing each original derivation:
- $S \Rightarrow A \Rightarrow a$ ✓ (now $S \rightarrow a$)
- $S \Rightarrow A \Rightarrow B \Rightarrow b$ ✓ (now $S \rightarrow b$)
- $S \Rightarrow BB \Rightarrow bb$ ✓ (still $S \rightarrow BB$)

Language is preserved. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Cycle-Aware Example (Important!)

**Given CFG with a unit-production cycle:**

$$
S \rightarrow A
$$

$$
A \rightarrow B
$$

$$
B \rightarrow S \mid b
$$

Here $S, A, B$ form a cycle. Let us carefully apply the algorithm.

**Step 1:** Identify unit productions: $S \rightarrow A$, $A \rightarrow B$, $B \rightarrow S$.

**Step 2:** Compute $U(\cdot)$:

- $U(S) = \{S, A, B\}$ — since $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow S$ reaches all three.
- $U(A) = \{A, B, S\}$ — since $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow S \rightarrow A$ reaches all three.
- $U(B) = \{B, S, A\}$ — since $B \rightarrow S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B$ reaches all three.

**Step 3:** Non-unit productions are only $B \rightarrow b$. Therefore:

- $S$: $B \in U(S)$, add $S \rightarrow b$.
- $A$: $B \in U(A)$, add $A \rightarrow b$.
- $B$: add $B \rightarrow b$ (already present).

**Step 4:** Delete all unit productions.

**Final Grammar $G'$:**

$$
S \rightarrow b
$$

$$
A \rightarrow b
$$

$$
B \rightarrow b
$$

**Note:** Even though the original grammar had cycles and only $B$ did the real work, the algorithm correctly distributes $B \rightarrow b$ to every member of the unit cycle.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cost and Complexity

For a grammar with $|V|$ non-terminals and $|P|$ productions:

| Quantity | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Time to compute $U(A)$ via Warshall-style closure | $O(\vert V \vert^{3})$ |
| Worst-case number of new productions added | $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert P \vert)$ |
| New total productions $\vert P' \vert$ | $\leq \vert V \vert \cdot \vert P \vert$ |
| Number of non-terminals $\vert V' \vert$ | $\vert V \vert$ (unchanged) |
| Guarantee: no unit productions in $G'$ | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Algorithm Flow Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start: Input CFG G]) --> checkEps{"Any epsilon productions?"}
    checkEps -- Yes --> errElim["Step A: Eliminate epsilon productions first"]
    errElim --> checkEps
    checkEps -- No --> listUnit["Step 1: List all unit productions A to B"]
    listUnit --> buildGraph["Step 2: Build unit-derivation graph as directed edges"]
    buildGraph --> transitive["Step 3: Compute transitive closure using Warshall-like iteration"]
    transitive --> computeU["Step 4: Derive U set of A equals all B reachable via unit chains"]
    computeU --> scanProd["Step 5: For each A and each B in U of A, copy all non-unit B-productions to A"]
    scanProd --> delUnit["Step 6: Delete every unit production from P"]
    delUnit --> verify["Step 7: Verify L of G prime equals L of G"]
    verify --> endA([Output: Unit-production-free CFG G prime])
```

### 4.2 Unit-Derivation Graph for the Cycle Example

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS[S]
    nodeA[A]
    nodeB[B]
    nodeS -- unit S to A --> nodeA
    nodeA -- unit A to B --> nodeB
    nodeB -- unit B to S --> nodeS
    nodeB -- non-unit B to b --> nodeT((lowercase b terminal))
    nodeT -. distributes to S and A .- nodeS
    nodeT -. distributes to A .- nodeA
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Elimination Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"]
        gIn[Original CFG G with V T P S]
    end
    subgraph PreProcLayer["Pre-processing Layer"]
        epsCheck{Epsilon Free?}
        epsRem[Remove epsilon if needed]
    end
    subgraph ComputeLayer["Computation Layer"]
        graphBuild[Build Unit Derivation Graph]
        closure[Transitive Closure Warshall]
        uSets[Compute U of A for all A in V]
    end
    subgraph TransformLayer["Transformation Layer"]
        copyProd[Copy Non-Unit Productions across U Set]
        delUnit[Delete Unit Productions]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer["Output Layer"]
        gOut[Unit Free CFG G prime]
        verify[L of G prime equals L of G]
    end
    gIn --> epsCheck
    epsCheck -- No --> graphBuild
    epsCheck -- Yes --> epsRem
    epsRem --> graphBuild
    graphBuild --> closure
    closure --> uSets
    uSets --> copyProd
    copyProd --> delUnit
    delUnit --> gOut
    gOut --> verify
```

### 4.4 Visual Summary of the Substitution Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startNode[Production A to B unit] --> chainCheck{B in U of A?}
    chainCheck -- Yes --> fetchProd[Get all non-unit B productions: alpha 1 alpha 2 etc]
    fetchProd --> addProd[Add A to alpha 1, A to alpha 2, etc to grammar]
    addProd --> cleanup[Delete A to B unit production]
    cleanup --> nextUnit[Repeat for next unit production]
    chainCheck -- No --> nextUnit
    nextUnit --> moreUnit{More unit productions?}
    moreUnit -- Yes --> startNode
    moreUnit -- No --> finishNode([Final Grammar is Unit Free])
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]**
*Define a unit production in a context-free grammar. Give one example and explain why eliminating unit productions is an essential preprocessing step for converting a CFG into Chomsky Normal Form.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **unit production** is a production of the form $A \rightarrow B$ where both $A$ and $B$ are non-terminals of the grammar. The right-hand side contains exactly one symbol, and that symbol must be a non-terminal.

**Example:** $S \rightarrow A$ (where $S$ and $A$ are non-terminals).

**Why essential before CNF:**
Chomsky Normal Form (CNF) restricts every production to be of the form $A \rightarrow BC$ or $A \rightarrow a$, both of which are *not* unit productions. Therefore, eliminating unit productions is a mandatory step before the final CNF conversion. Furthermore, CNF is required as input to algorithms such as CYK parsing, and unit productions can otherwise cause ambiguity and infinite-derivation loops.

*(Examiner's note: Award full 3 marks if the student defines, illustrates, and justifies. Definition alone = 1 mark; example = 1 mark; CNF justification = 1 mark.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2023 Pattern]**
*State whether the following grammar contains unit productions. Justify briefly.*

$$
S \rightarrow aA \mid B
$$
$$
A \rightarrow b \mid ab
$$
$$
B \rightarrow a \mid bc
$$

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- $S \rightarrow aA$: **Not a unit production** (RHS contains terminals and a non-terminal).
- $S \rightarrow B$: **Unit production** ($S$ and $B$ are both non-terminals).
- $A \rightarrow b$: Not a unit production (terminal on RHS).
- $A \rightarrow ab$: Not a unit production (two terminal symbols).
- $B \rightarrow a$: Not a unit production (terminal on RHS).
- $B \rightarrow bc$: Not a unit production (two terminal symbols).

**Conclusion:** The grammar contains **exactly one unit production**, namely $S \rightarrow B$.

*(Examiner's note: Award 2 marks for correct identification and 1 mark for the conclusion.)*

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module-Level Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Pitfall 1:** Students often forget to compute $U(A)$ via *transitive closure* and only handle direct unit pairs. Indirect chains (e.g., $A \rightarrow B$ and $B \rightarrow C$ leading to $A \Rightarrow^{*} C$) are commonly missed. **Lose up to 4 marks** for this omission.
> - **Pitfall 2:** Students sometimes add the unit productions of $B$ (instead of *non-unit* productions) to $A$, creating an infinite regress. **Lose 2 marks** for this conceptual confusion.
> - **Pitfall 3:** Failing to verify that the resulting grammar is unit-production-free. **Lose 1 mark** if verification is absent.
> - **Pitfall 4:** Including $\varepsilon$-elimination steps in a "unit-production-only" question. This is **out of syllabus** for this topic and wastes time. Lose up to 2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3 Pattern]*

**(a)** Consider the following CFG $G$:

$$
S \rightarrow A \mid BC
$$
$$
A \rightarrow B \mid a
$$
$$
B \rightarrow C \mid b
$$
$$
C \rightarrow c
$$

Compute $U(S)$, $U(A)$, $U(B)$, and $U(C)$ for this grammar. Show all intermediate steps using transitive closure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the values of $U$ computed in part (a), construct a new equivalent grammar $G'$ that is **free of unit productions**. List every new production added and every unit production removed. Verify that $G'$ generates the same language as $G$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Computing $U(\cdot)$ via Transitive Closure:**

**Step 1 — Identify direct unit productions:**
Scanning the productions, the unit productions are:
- $S \rightarrow A$
- $A \rightarrow B$
- $B \rightarrow C$

**Step 2 — Build the unit-derivation graph:**
This is a linear chain $S \rightarrow A \rightarrow B \rightarrow C$.

**Step 3 — Apply transitive closure:**

- $U(C) = \{C\}$ (terminal node, no outgoing unit edges).
- $U(B) = \{B, C\}$ because $B \rightarrow C$.
- $U(A) = \{A, B, C\}$ because $A \rightarrow B$ and $B \rightarrow C$.
- $U(S) = \{S, A, B, C\}$ because $S \rightarrow A$, then via $A$ we reach $B$ and $C$.

**Tabulated Result:**

| Non-terminal $X$ | $U(X)$ |
| :--- | :--- |
| $S$ | $\{S, A, B, C\}$ |
| $A$ | $\{A, B, C\}$ |
| $B$ | $\{B, C\}$ |
| $C$ | $\{C\}$ |

*(7 marks: 2 for identifying unit productions, 3 for transitive closure reasoning, 2 for final table.)*

**(b) Construction of $G'$:**

**Step 1 — List non-unit productions of each non-terminal:**
- $S$: $S \rightarrow BC$ (non-unit, since RHS has 2 symbols).
- $A$: $A \rightarrow a$.
- $B$: $B \rightarrow b$.
- $C$: $C \rightarrow c$.

**Step 2 — Substitute across $U$ sets:**

- For $S$ and every $B \in \{S, A, B, C\}$:
  - $B = S$: add $S \rightarrow BC$ (already there).
  - $B = A$: add $S \rightarrow a$.
  - $B = B$: add $S \rightarrow b$.
  - $B = C$: add $S \rightarrow c$.

- For $A$ and every $B \in \{A, B, C\}$:
  - $B = A$: add $A \rightarrow a$ (already there).
  - $B = B$: add $A \rightarrow b$.
  - $B = C$: add $A \rightarrow c$.

- For $B$ and every $B \in \{B, C\}$:
  - $B = B$: add $B \rightarrow b$ (already there).
  - $B = C$: add $B \rightarrow c$.

- For $C$: $C \rightarrow c$ (already there).

**Step 3 — Remove all unit productions $S \rightarrow A$, $A \rightarrow B$, $B \rightarrow C$.**

**Final Grammar $G'$:**

$$
S \rightarrow BC \mid a \mid b \mid c
$$

$$
A \rightarrow a \mid b \mid c
$$

$$
B \rightarrow b \mid c
$$

$$
C \rightarrow c
$$

**Verification of Language Equivalence:**

The original language generated derivations of the form $S \Rightarrow A \Rightarrow B \Rightarrow C \Rightarrow c$, plus direct terminal productions $A \Rightarrow a$, $B \Rightarrow b$. In the new grammar:
- $S \rightarrow a$ captures the path $S \Rightarrow A \Rightarrow a$.
- $S \rightarrow b$ captures the path $S \Rightarrow A \Rightarrow B \Rightarrow b$.
- $S \rightarrow c$ captures the path $S \Rightarrow A \Rightarrow B \Rightarrow C \Rightarrow c$.
- $S \rightarrow BC$ captures the original non-unit direct derivation.
- All other productions preserve terminal outputs from $A$, $B$, $C$.

Hence $L(G') = L(G)$. $\blacksquare$

*(7 marks: 2 for substitution logic, 3 for the final grammar, 2 for verification.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Define a *unit production* and a *null production*. State the conditions under which a CFG is said to be free of unit productions. Explain with a diagram why null-productions must be eliminated *before* unit productions during grammar simplification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the CFG:

$$
E \rightarrow E + T \mid T
$$
$$
T \rightarrow T \ast F \mid F
$$
$$
F \rightarrow E \mid id
$$

Eliminate all unit productions from this grammar. Provide the set $U(\cdot)$ for each non-terminal and clearly show the resulting unit-free grammar. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Definitions and Ordering:**

- **Unit production:** A production of the form $A \rightarrow B$ where $A$ and $B$ are non-terminals.
- **Null production (epsilon production):** A production of the form $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ where $\varepsilon$ denotes the empty string.

**Condition for unit-freedom:** A CFG is unit-free if and only if **no production in its production set has a single non-terminal on its right-hand side**. Formally, there is no $A \in V$ and $B \in V$ such that $(A \rightarrow B) \in P$.

**Why Null-Productions Must Be Eliminated First:**

If a null production $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ exists alongside a unit production $A \rightarrow B$, then eliminating the unit production naively (without first removing null) could reintroduce nullable behaviour. Specifically:

```
A → B (unit)
B → ε (null)
```

becomes

```
A → ε (now also a null production!)
```

This breaks the property that the language is preserved. The correct pipeline is:
1. Compute NULLABLE set (non-terminals that derive $\varepsilon$).
2. Replace each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ by adding versions where nullable symbols are dropped.
3. Delete all $\varepsilon$-productions.
4. **Then** eliminate unit productions on a guaranteed $\varepsilon$-free grammar.

*(7 marks: 2 for definitions, 2 for unit-free condition, 3 for ordering justification.)*

**(b) Eliminating Unit Productions:**

The grammar is:

$$
E \rightarrow E + T \mid T
$$
$$
T \rightarrow T \ast F \mid F
$$
$$
F \rightarrow E \mid id
$$

**Unit productions identified:** $E \rightarrow T$, $T \rightarrow F$, $F \rightarrow E$.

**Compute $U(\cdot)$:**

- $U(E) = \{E, T, F\}$ because $E \rightarrow T \rightarrow F$.
- $U(T) = \{T, F, E\}$ because $T \rightarrow F \rightarrow E$.
- $U(F) = \{F, E, T\}$ because $F \rightarrow E \rightarrow T$.

The unit productions form a cycle, so every non-terminal can reach every other.

**Non-unit productions:**
- $E$: $E \rightarrow E + T$
- $T$: $T \rightarrow T \ast F$
- $F$: $F \rightarrow id$

**Substitution across $U$ sets:**

- For $E$: add $E \rightarrow id$ (from $F$), $E \rightarrow T \ast F$ (from $T$), $E \rightarrow E + T$ (from $E$, already there).
- For $T$: add $T \rightarrow id$ (from $F$), $T \rightarrow T \ast F$ (from $T$, already there), $T \rightarrow E + T$ (from $E$).
- For $F$: add $F \rightarrow id$ (from $F$, already there), $F \rightarrow E + T$ (from $E$), $F \rightarrow T \ast F$ (from $T$).

**Remove unit productions.**

**Final Unit-Free Grammar:**

$$
E \rightarrow E + T \mid T \ast F \mid id
$$

$$
T \rightarrow T \ast F \mid E + T \mid id
$$

$$
F \rightarrow id \mid E + T \mid T \ast F
$$

*(7 marks: 3 for $U(\cdot)$ computation, 3 for substitution and final grammar, 1 for correctness.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Unit production** = $A \rightarrow B$ where $A, B \in V$. **Not** the same as a null production $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$.
- **Pre-condition:** The grammar must already be free of $\varepsilon$-productions before applying the unit-production elimination algorithm.
- **Algorithm in 4 steps:**
  1. List all unit productions.
  2. Compute $U(A)$ for every non-terminal $A$ via transitive closure.
  3. For each $A$ and $B \in U(A)$, copy all *non-unit* productions of $B$ to $A$.
  4. Delete every unit production.
- **Cycles:** When unit productions form cycles (e.g., $A \rightarrow B \rightarrow A$), transitive closure correctly captures all reachable nodes, and the algorithm still terminates.
- **Preservation:** $L(G) = L(G')$; no terminals added or removed; only production set changes.
- **Position in CNF pipeline:** $\varepsilon$-elimination $\rightarrow$ **Unit-production elimination** $\rightarrow$ Useless-symbol elimination $\rightarrow$ Final CNF conversion.
- **Warshall-style closure** for $U(\cdot)$ runs in $O(|V|^3)$ time.
- **Cardinality bound:** The new production set has size at most $|V| \cdot |P|$.
- **Common student mistake:** Forgetting that $A \in U(A)$ trivially, leading to missing direct non-unit productions.
- **Another common mistake:** Adding unit productions of $B$ to $A$ instead of non-unit productions. Always copy *non-unit* productions only.
- **Verification step is mandatory** in KTU answer scripts; skipping it costs marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Chomsky Normal Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Chomsky Normal Form

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A Context-Free Grammar (CFG) $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is said to be in **Chomsky Normal Form (CNF)** if every production in $P$ is of one of the following two restricted forms:

$$A \rightarrow BC \quad \text{or} \quad A \rightarrow a$$

where $A, B, C \in V$ (non-terminals) with $B \neq C$ and $B, C$ must not be the start symbol (in some definitions), and $a \in T$ (terminals). The empty string production $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is permitted only if $\varepsilon \in L(G)$ and $S$ does not appear on the right-hand side of any production.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> A CFG is in **Chomsky Normal Form** if all its productions are of the form $A \rightarrow BC$ (two non-terminals) or $A \rightarrow a$ (a single terminal). No other forms are permitted.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Two-Brick Rule"

Imagine you are building a sentence using **two types of bricks**:
- 🧱 **Heavy bricks** (non-terminals) — must always be carried in **pairs** of two when they appear.
- 🧪 **Liquid droplets** (terminals) — can stand alone, but only one at a time.

> A CNF grammar is like a strict construction code: a non-terminal symbol can either "drop a single droplet" ($A \rightarrow a$) or "place exactly two heavy bricks on top of each other" ($A \rightarrow BC$). **No single heavy brick alone, no triplet, no mix!**

### 1.3 Why CNF Matters (Engineering Relevance)

- **Parsing Algorithms:** The **CYK (Cocke-Younger-Kasami) algorithm** — a cornerstone of compiler design and natural language processing — **requires** the input grammar to be in CNF. Without CNF, CYK cannot run in $O(n^3)$ time.
- **Decidability:** Membership testing ("is $w \in L(G)$?") becomes **decidable** and efficient only when the grammar is in CNF.
- **Theoretical Foundation:** CNF is a stepping stone to the **Pumping Lemma for CFLs** and closure properties.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU Module 3 expects students to (i) state the definition, (ii) convert any given CFG to CNF, and (iii) apply this to membership testing. Conversion is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question**.

### 1.4 Key Properties of CNF Grammars

| Property | Statement |
|---|---|
| **Derivation Tree Height** | If $w \in L(G)$ and $\vert w \vert = n \geq 1$, then any derivation tree has exactly $2n - 1$ nodes (excluding leaves) |
| **Tree Depth** | If $w \in L(G)$ and $w \neq \varepsilon$, the derivation tree has height $\leq 2n - 1$ |
| **String Length** | Every string $w \in L(G)$ with $\vert w \vert = n$ has a derivation of length **at most $2n - 1$** |

### 1.5 Theorem — Existence of CNF (Fundamental Result)

> **Theorem:** Every Context-Free Language (CFL) that does **not** contain the empty string $\varepsilon$ has a grammar in Chomsky Normal Form. If $\varepsilon \in L$, then a CNF grammar exists with the only exception being $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$, where $S$ does not appear on any RHS.

This theorem guarantees that **no CFG is "beyond" CNF** — we can always reach it through a deterministic algorithm.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 5-Stage Conversion Pipeline

Converting an arbitrary CFG to CNF is a **systematic 5-stage process**. Skipping any stage or reordering incorrectly will lead to a non-CNF grammar. KTU examiners frequently test the **order** itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Canonical Order (KTU Board Standard):**
> **Step 1 →** Remove $\varepsilon$-productions $\rightarrow$ **Step 2 →** Remove unit productions $\rightarrow$ **Step 3 →** Remove useless symbols $\rightarrow$ **Step 4 →** Replace terminals in mixed/long productions $\rightarrow$ **Step 5 →** Break down RHS of length $> 2$.

---

### 2.2 Stage-by-Stage Analysis

#### **Stage 1: Elimination of $\varepsilon$-Productions**
A production of the form $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ is called an $\varepsilon$-production or **null production**.

**Why?** CNF forbids RHS to be empty (except $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ in the special case).

**How?**
1. Find all **nullable non-terminals** — those that can derive $\varepsilon$.
2. For every production $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$, add new productions obtained by deleting any combination of nullable $X_i$'s (excluding the case where all are deleted, unless we add $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$).
3. Finally, **delete** all original $\varepsilon$-productions.

> An equivalent way: repeatedly scan for $\varepsilon$-productions and replace each occurrence of the nullable variable in RHS with the rest.

#### **Stage 2: Elimination of Unit Productions**
A unit production is of the form $A \rightarrow B$ where both $A$ and $B$ are non-terminals.

**Why?** CNF requires either two non-terminals or one terminal — never a single non-terminal.

**How?** Compute the **unit pairs** $(A, B)$ such that $A \Rightarrow^* B$ using only unit productions. For each pair, if $B \rightarrow \alpha$ is a non-unit production, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $P$. Then delete all unit productions.

#### **Stage 3: Elimination of Useless Symbols**
A symbol is **useless** if it is either non-generating or non-reachable.
- **Generating:** $X$ generates some terminal string.
- **Reachable:** $X$ appears in some derivation from $S$.

**Why?** Such symbols bloat the grammar and complicate subsequent steps.

**How?**
1. Find all generating symbols (start with terminals, repeatedly add variables that produce generating strings).
2. Keep only generating symbols and their productions.
3. Find reachable symbols (BFS/DFS from $S$).
4. Keep only reachable symbols and their productions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order matters here!** Eliminate *non-generating* symbols **first**, then *non-reachable* symbols. Reversing the order may leave symbols that appear reachable but are actually useless.

#### **Stage 4: Replace Terminals in Long/Mixed Productions**
A production with mixed terminal-non-terminal RHS or length $\geq 2$ containing terminals must be fixed.

**Why?** CNF allows only $A \rightarrow a$ (pure terminal) or $A \rightarrow BC$ (pure non-terminals).

**How?** For each terminal $a$ that appears in a production with RHS length $\geq 2$ or in mixed form, introduce a new variable $C_a$ with production $C_a \rightarrow a$, and replace $a$ by $C_a$ in the original production.

#### **Stage 5: Break Down RHS of Length $> 2$**
Any production $A \rightarrow B_1 B_2 \ldots B_k$ with $k \geq 3$ must be broken.

**How?** Introduce new variables $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_{k-2}$ and replace with:
$$A \rightarrow B_1 D_1, \quad D_1 \rightarrow B_2 D_2, \quad \ldots, \quad D_{k-2} \rightarrow B_{k-1} B_k$$

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Stage | Input Form | Output Form | New Symbols Introduced |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $A \rightarrow \alpha$ (without $\varepsilon$) | None |
| 2 | $A \rightarrow B$ | $A \rightarrow \alpha$ (non-unit) | None |
| 3 | $S \not\rightarrow^* w$ or $X$ unreachable | Removed entirely | None |
| 4 | $A \rightarrow aB$ (mixed) | $A \rightarrow C_a B$ | $C_a \rightarrow a$ |
| 5 | $A \rightarrow B_1 B_2 \ldots B_k$, $k \geq 3$ | $A \rightarrow B_1 D_1$ chain | $D_i$ new variables |

| Key Metric | Formula |
|---|---|
| Height of CNF derivation tree for $w$ with $\vert w \vert = n$ | $\leq 2n - 1$ |
| Total internal nodes | $2n - 1$ |
| Max length of any production RHS in CNF | $\leq 2$ |
| Number of new variables introduced in Stage 5 for one $k$-RHS production | $k - 2$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of CNF |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Design** | CYK parser for syntax analysis (semantic actions in YACC/Bison alternatives) |
| **Natural Language Processing** | Probabilistic CFGs (PCFGs) for parsing English, used in IBM Watson, Google Translate |
| **Bioinformatics** | RNA secondary structure prediction using stochastic CNF grammars |
| **Formal Verification** | Model checking of pushdown systems, software verification |
| **Programming Languages** | Foundation for grammar-based static analyzers |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Worked Example — Full 5-Stage Conversion

**Problem:** Convert the following CFG to Chomsky Normal Form.
$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB$$
$$A \rightarrow B \mid S$$
$$B \rightarrow b \mid \varepsilon$$

This single example is sufficient to demonstrate all five stages. We will track each stage meticulously.

---

#### **STAGE 1: Removal of $\varepsilon$-Productions**

**Step 1.1 — Identify Nullable Non-Terminals:**

A non-terminal $X$ is **nullable** if $X \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon$.

$$B \text{ is nullable (directly: } B \rightarrow \varepsilon\text{)}$$
$$A \text{ is nullable (via } A \rightarrow B \text{ and } B \rightarrow \varepsilon\text{)}$$
$$S \text{ is nullable (via } S \rightarrow ASA, A \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon, A \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon \Rightarrow S \Rightarrow^* \varepsilon\text{)}$$

**Step 1.2 — Generate all combinations by deleting nullable variables:**

For $S \rightarrow ASA$: Both $A$'s are nullable. Delete either, both, or none:
- Delete first $A$: $S \rightarrow SA$
- Delete second $A$: $S \rightarrow AS$
- Delete both $A$'s: $S \rightarrow S$ (useless, drop)

For $S \rightarrow aB$: $B$ is nullable. Delete $B$:
- $S \rightarrow a$

For $A \rightarrow B$: $B$ is nullable. Delete $B$:
- $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (will be removed in next step)

For $A \rightarrow S$: $S$ is nullable. Delete $S$:
- $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ (will be removed)

For $B \rightarrow b$: No nullable to delete. Stays as is.

**Step 1.3 — Drop $\varepsilon$-productions (except the start, if any):**

Final grammar after Stage 1:

$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid SA \mid AS \mid aB \mid a$$
$$A \rightarrow B \mid S \mid b$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logbook Entry:** The two $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$ productions are **dropped** (not added back). The $\varepsilon$-production for the start symbol $S$ is also dropped because $S$ still appears on the RHS of other productions.

---

#### **STAGE 2: Removal of Unit Productions**

**Step 2.1 — Identify unit productions** (form $X \rightarrow Y$ where $X, Y \in V$):
- $A \rightarrow B$ ✓
- $A \rightarrow S$ ✓

(No unit productions in $S$ or $B$.)

**Step 2.2 — Compute unit pairs and substitute non-unit productions:**

For the pair $(A, B)$: $B$'s non-unit productions are $B \rightarrow b$. Add $A \rightarrow b$.

For the pair $(A, S)$: $S$'s non-unit productions are:
$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid SA \mid AS \mid aB \mid a$$
Add all of these to $A$.

**Step 2.3 — Drop the unit productions:**

$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid SA \mid AS \mid aB \mid a$$
$$A \rightarrow b \mid ASA \mid SA \mid AS \mid aB \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$

---

#### **STAGE 3: Elimination of Useless Symbols**

**Step 3.1 — Identify generating symbols** (those that can derive a terminal string):

Starting with terminals $b, a$, we propagate:
- $B \rightarrow b$: $B$ is generating.
- $A \rightarrow b$: $A$ is generating.
- $S \rightarrow a$: $S$ is generating.

All non-terminals $\{S, A, B\}$ are **generating**.

**Step 3.2 — Identify reachable symbols** (BFS from $S$):

From $S$, we directly reach $A$ (via $S \rightarrow ASA, SA, AS$) and $B$ (via $S \rightarrow aB$).

All non-terminals $\{S, A, B\}$ are **reachable**.

**No symbols are removed in this stage.** The grammar is unchanged.

---

#### **STAGE 4: Replace Terminals in Mixed/Long Productions**

Identify productions with RHS length $\geq 2$ that contain terminals:

| Production | Issue | Action |
|---|---|---|
| $S \rightarrow ASA$ | Length 3, all non-terminals | Stage 5 only |
| $S \rightarrow SA$ | Length 2, all non-terminals | Already in CNF shape |
| $S \rightarrow AS$ | Length 2, all non-terminals | Already in CNF shape |
| $S \rightarrow aB$ | Length 2, mixed (terminal $a$) | Introduce $C$: $C \rightarrow a$, replace with $S \rightarrow CB$ |
| $S \rightarrow a$ | Length 1, pure terminal | **Already in CNF!** |
| $A \rightarrow b$ | Length 1, pure terminal | **Already in CNF!** |
| $A \rightarrow ASA$ | Length 3, all non-terminals | Stage 5 only |
| $A \rightarrow SA$ | Length 2, all non-terminals | Already in CNF shape |
| $A \rightarrow AS$ | Length 2, all non-terminals | Already in CNF shape |
| $A \rightarrow aB$ | Length 2, mixed | Replace with $A \rightarrow CB$ |
| $A \rightarrow a$ | Length 1, pure terminal | **Already in CNF!** |
| $B \rightarrow b$ | Length 1, pure terminal | **Already in CNF!** |

Introduce new variable $C$ with production $C \rightarrow a$.

Grammar after Stage 4:

$$S \rightarrow ASA \mid SA \mid AS \mid CB \mid a$$
$$A \rightarrow b \mid ASA \mid SA \mid AS \mid CB \mid a$$
$$B \rightarrow b$$
$$C \rightarrow a$$

---

#### **STAGE 5: Break Down RHS of Length $> 2$**

Identify productions with RHS length 3: $S \rightarrow ASA$ and $A \rightarrow ASA$.

Introduce a new variable $D$ with production $D \rightarrow AS$.

Replace $S \rightarrow ASA$ with $S \rightarrow DA$ and $A \rightarrow ASA$ with $A \rightarrow DA$.

---

### 3.2 ✅ Final Chomsky Normal Form

$$\boxed{\;S \rightarrow DA \mid SA \mid AS \mid CB \mid a\;}$$
$$\boxed{\;A \rightarrow b \mid DA \mid SA \mid AS \mid CB \mid a\;}$$
$$\boxed{\;B \rightarrow b\;}$$
$$\boxed{\;C \rightarrow a\;}$$
$$\boxed{\;D \rightarrow AS\;}$$

**Verification Checklist:**
- [x] All RHS are either a single terminal ($a$, $b$) or exactly two non-terminals.
- [x] No $\varepsilon$-productions (other than possibly $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$).
- [x] No unit productions.
- [x] No useless symbols.
- [x] Every original non-terminal remains generating and reachable.

---

### 3.3 Quick-Reference Conversion Table for the Master Example

| Stage | Productions Added | Productions Removed |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (Original) | $S \rightarrow ASA \mid aB$; $A \rightarrow B \mid S$; $B \rightarrow b \mid \varepsilon$ | — |
| 1 ($\varepsilon$-removal) | $S \rightarrow SA, AS, a$; $A \rightarrow b$ | $A \rightarrow \varepsilon$, $B \rightarrow \varepsilon$ |
| 2 (Unit removal) | $A \rightarrow ASA, SA, AS, aB, a$ | $A \rightarrow B, A \rightarrow S$ |
| 3 (Useless) | (none) | (none) |
| 4 (Terminal replace) | $C \rightarrow a$, $S \rightarrow CB$, $A \rightarrow CB$ | $S \rightarrow aB$, $A \rightarrow aB$ |
| 5 (Length reduce) | $D \rightarrow AS$, $S \rightarrow DA$, $A \rightarrow DA$ | $S \rightarrow ASA$, $A \rightarrow ASA$ |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Special Cases & Edge Conditions

| Case | Handling |
|---|---|
| Grammar already in CNF | Skip all 5 stages; just verify. |
| Start symbol $S$ appears on RHS | Introduce new start $S' \rightarrow S$ **before** Stage 1. |
| $\varepsilon \in L(G)$ | Add $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ to CNF (if $S$ is not on any RHS). |
| Unit pair $(A, A)$ | Trivially satisfied; ignore. |
| Nullable chain $A \Rightarrow B \Rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $A$ becomes nullable in one pass; $S$ may become nullable transitively. |
| Useless symbol conflict | Always eliminate **non-generating first**, then **non-reachable**. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The number of new variables introduced in Stage 5 for a single production of length $k$ is exactly $k - 2$. For $k = 3$, we add **1** variable. For $k = 4$, we add **2** variables, and so on. KTU examiners often ask: "How many new symbols were introduced?" — this is a 2-mark sub-question.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 CNF Conversion Pipeline (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input CFG G]:::startNode --> B{Contains epsilon productions?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Stage 1: Remove epsilon productions]:::processNode
    B -- No --> D{Contains unit productions?}
    C --> D
    D -- Yes --> E[Stage 2: Remove unit productions]:::processNode
    D -- No --> F{Has useless symbols?}
    E --> F
    F -- Yes --> G[Stage 3: Eliminate useless symbols]:::processNode
    F -- No --> H{Productions have terminals in mixed form?}
    G --> H
    H -- Yes --> I[Stage 4: Replace terminals with new variables]:::processNode
    H -- No --> J{Any production has RHS length greater than 2?}
    I --> J
    J -- Yes --> K[Stage 5: Break down long productions]:::processNode
    J -- No --> L[Final CNF Grammar G prime]:::endNode
    K --> L
    L --> M{Verify CNF properties}:::verifyNode
    M --> N[Output G in Chomsky Normal Form]:::endNode
    classDef startNode fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef verifyNode fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

This diagram maps how a CNF grammar plugs into the **CYK Parsing Engine**, which is the principal engineering application.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Input[Input Grammar G arbitrary CFG]:::srcNode --> Conv[CNF Conversion Engine 5 Stage Pipeline]:::procNode
    Conv --> CNFOut[Grammar G prime in CNF]:::dataNode
    CNFOut --> Parser[CYK Parsing Engine O of n cubed]:::procNode
    Input2[Input String w length n]:::srcNode --> Parser
    Parser --> Table[Triangular Parse Table n plus 1 rows]:::dataNode
    Table --> Decision{Membership Test}:::verifyNode
    Decision -- S in Table 1 n --> Accept[ACCEPT w belongs to L G]:::endNode
    Decision -- S not in Table --> Reject[REJECT w not in L G]:::endNode
```
