From the start state $q_0$, read the leftmost symbol, remember it (state), and cross it out. 2. Sweep right to find the rightmost non-blank symbol. 3. Compare; if they match, cross both out and repeat. 4. If the tape becomes all $X$'s (crossed out), accept. ### 3.2.2 States and Transitions (Sketch) $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5\} \\ \Sigma &= \{a, b\} \\ \Gamma &= \{a, b, X, B\} \\ F &= \{q_5\} \end{aligned} $$ Transitions: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, X, R) \\ \delta(q_0, b) &= (q_2, X, R) \\ \delta(q_0, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \\ \delta(q_0, B) &= (q_5, B, R) \quad \text{(accept empty string)} \\ \delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \\ \delta(q_1, b) &= (q_1, b, R) \\ \delta(q_1, X) &= (q_1, X, R) \\ \delta(q_1, B) &= (q_3, B, L) \\ \delta(q_3, a) &= (q_4, X, L) \\ \delta(q_3, b) &= (q_5 \text{ undefined}) \quad \text{(mismatch — reject)} \end{aligned} $$ (Symmetric transitions exist for $q_2, q_4$.) > [!NOTE] > **State-explosion principle:** Designing a TM by states is equivalent to a programmer using a finite number of variables — each state encodes one "remembered fact" about the input. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU TM Construction Patterns | Target Language | Number of States | Strategy | |-----------------|------------------|----------| | $a^n b^n$ | 5 | Match leftmost $a$ to rightmost $b$, cross out | | $a^n b^n c^n$ | 7 | Two-phase matching with two markers | | $w w$ (string duplication) | 6 | Mark start, find middle, match lengths | | $w w^R$ (palindrome) | 6 | Match outer symbols inward | | $a^{2^n}$ | 5+ | Halve the $a$-count repeatedly | | $\{a^i b^j c^k \mid i \cdot j = k\}$ | >10 | Encode product via repeated subtraction | ## 3.4 Decidability Snapshot (KTU Module-4 Closure) | Problem | TM Decision Procedure Exists? | |---------|------------------------------| | Is $w \in L(M)$? (for fixed TM $M$) | **Yes** — simulate $M$ on $w$ | | Does TM $M$ halt on empty input? | **No (Undecidable)** | | Is $L(M) = \emptyset$? | **No (Undecidable)** | | Are two TMs equivalent? | **No (Undecidable)** | | Is a given CFG ambiguous? | **No (Undecidable)** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU pitfall:** Simulation **does** halt for *fixed* $M$, $w$. The Halting Problem asks about a TM **that takes a TM as input** (Universal TM setting). # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Basic Turing Machine Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Tape["Infinite Tape (cells holding Γ symbols)"] cellL["... B "] --> cellA["X"] cellA --> cellB["a"] cellB --> cellC["Y"] cellC --> cellD["b"] cellD --> cellE["B ..."] end Head["READ/WRITE HEAD"] FC["FINITE CONTROL
current state q
transition function δ"] Head ---|"reads X"| cellA Head ---|"writes Y"| cellA Head ---|"moves L / R"| Tape Head <-->|"symbol scanned"| FC FC -->|"δ(q, X) = (p, Y, D)"| Head ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of One TM Move ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["START: ID = α q X β"] S2["READ current symbol X via head"] S3["LOOKUP δ(q, X) in transition table"] S4{"δ(q, X) defined?"} S5["ERASE X from current cell"] S6["WRITE Y into current cell"] S7{"Move Direction D"} S8["Move head LEFT one cell"] S9["Move head RIGHT one cell"] S10["UPDATE state q → p"] S11["NEXT ID = α' p β'"] S12["HALT — reject or undefined move"] S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 S4 -->|"Yes"| S5 --> S6 --> S7 S4 -->|"No"| S12 S7 -->|"D = L"| S8 S7 -->|"D = R"| S9 S8 --> S10 S9 --> S10 S10 --> S11 S11 --> S2 ``` ## 4.3 ID Transition Schematic for TM on $aabb$ ```mermaid flowchart LR I1["q0 aabb"] I2["X q1 abb"] I3["Xa q1 bb"] I4["XaY q2 b"] I5["Xa q2 Yb"] I6["X q2 aYb"] I7["Xq2 XaYb"] I8["XX q0 Yb"] I9["XXY q3 b"] I10["XXYY q3"] I11["XXYYB q4 (ACCEPT)"] I1 --> I2 --> I3 --> I4 --> I5 --> I6 --> I7 --> I8 --> I9 --> I10 --> I11 ``` ## 4.4 Hierarchy of Automata (Why TM Exists) ```mermaid flowchart TB FA["Finite Automaton
Regular Languages"] PDA["Pushdown Automaton
Context-Free Languages"] TM["Turing Machine
Recursively Enumerable Languages"] LBA["Linear Bounded Automaton
Context-Sensitive Languages"] FA -->|"+ Stack"| PDA PDA -->|"+ Random-Access Tape"| TM TM -->|"Restricted Tape (linear)"| LBA ``` ## 4.5 Multi-Tape TM Block Diagram (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MT1["Tape 1"] t1a["... B "] --> t1b["a"] --> t1c["b"] --> t1d["B ..."] end subgraph MT2["Tape 2"] t2a["... B "] --> t2b["X"] --> t2c["Y"] --> t2d["B ..."] end subgraph MT3["Tape k"] t3a["... B "] --> t3b["Z"] --> t3c["B ..."] end H1["Head 1"] H2["Head 2"] Hk["Head k"] FC2["SINGLE FINITE CONTROL
δ: Q × Γ^k → Q × Γ^k × {L,R}^k"] H1 --- t1b H2 --- t2b Hk --- t3b H1 -->|"reads"| FC2 H2 -->|"reads"| FC2 Hk -->|"reads"| FC2 FC2 -->|"writes + moves"| H1 FC2 -->|"writes + moves"| H2 FC2 -->|"writes + moves"| Hk ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Define a Turing Machine. Explain the role of each component of the 7-tuple formal definition. **Model Answer:** A Turing Machine is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ that mathematically models an arbitrary computation. 1. **$Q$**: Finite set of internal control states (the machine's memory of "where it is" logically). 2. **$\Sigma$**: Finite input alphabet; the symbols allowed in the input string. The blank $B$ is **not** in $\Sigma$. 3. **$\Gamma$**: Finite tape alphabet — all symbols that can ever appear on the tape. We require $\Sigma \subset \Gamma$ and $B \in \Gamma$. 4. **$\delta$**: Transition function $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$. It is **partial**; undefined entries cause the TM to halt. 5. **$q_0 \in Q$**: The start state; the TM is initialized here. 6. **$B \in \Gamma$**: The blank symbol, marking unused cells. 7. **$F \subseteq Q$**: The set of final (accepting) states. In one move, the TM reads the currently scanned symbol, overwrites it, moves left or right, and changes state — all atomically as dictated by $\delta$. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **recursive** and **recursively enumerable** languages with a TM-based definition. **Model Answer:** | Property | Recursive Language | Recursively Enumerable Language | |----------|--------------------|----------------------------------| | TM Behaviour | TM **halts on every input** | TM **accepts if $w \in L$**, but **may loop forever** if $w \notin L$ | | Membership Decider | Yes — total decider | Only semi-decider | | Complement is | Recursive | Need not be RE | | Example | $L = \{a^n b^n c^n\}$ | $L = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\}$ | Formally, a language $L$ is **recursive** if there exists a TM $M$ such that for every $w \in \Sigma^*$, $M$ halts and accepts iff $w \in L$. A language $L$ is **recursively enumerable (RE)** if there exists a TM that accepts exactly $L$ (the TM halts on accepted inputs but may not halt on rejected inputs). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024):** In Part B, every Module-4 question gives you a **choice** between Question A and Question B. Both are full 14-mark questions split into two 7-mark sub-parts. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Set A]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Design a Turing Machine that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Provide the formal 7-tuple specification, transition table, and a complete trace on the input string $aabbcc$. **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain, with a state-transition diagram, why the language $L = \{w w \mid w \in \{a,b\}^*\}$ (string duplication) is accepted by a TM but not by any PDA. Provide the TM's transition function. #### Model Solution — Part (a) We construct $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ with: $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6\} \\ \Sigma &= \{a, b, c\} \\ \Gamma &= \{a, b, c, X, Y, Z, B\} \\ q_0 &= q_0 \\ F &= \{q_6\} \end{aligned} $$ **Strategy:** Match the leftmost $a$ with the rightmost $c$ (cross to $X$ and $Z$), then verify the middle $b$'s are matched too. **Transition Table:** | State | Symbol | Next State | Write | Move | |-------|--------|------------|-------|------| | $q_0$ | $a$ | $q_1$ | $X$ | $R$ | | $q_0$ | $Y$ | $q_4$ | $Y$ | $R$ | | $q_0$ | $B$ | $q_6$ | $B$ | $R$ | | $q_1$ | $a$ | $q_1$ | $a$ | $R$ | | $q_1$ | $Y$ | $q_1$ | $Y$ | $R$ | | $q_1$ | $b$ | $q_2$ | $Y$ | $R$ | | $q_1$ | $c$ | $q_3$ | $Z$ | $L$ | | $q_2$ | $b$ | $q_2$ | $b$ | $R$ | | $q_2$ | $Z$ | $q_2$ | $Z$ | $R$ | | $q_2$ | $c$ | $q_3$ | $Z$ | $L$ | | $q_3$ | $a$ | $q_3$ | $a$ | $L$ | | $q_3$ | $b$ | $q_3$ | $b$ | $L$ | | $q_3$ | $Y$ | $q_3$ | $Y$ | $L$ | | $q_3$ | $Z$ | $q_3$ | $Z$ | $L$ | | $q_3$ | $X$ | $q_0$ | $X$ | $R$ | | $q_4$ | $Y$ | $q_4$ | $Y$ | $R$ | | $q_4$ | $Z$ | $q_5$ | $Z$ | $L$ | | $q_5$ | $Y$ | $q_5$ | $Y$ | $L$ | | $q_5$ | $X$ | $q_0$ | $X$ | $R$ | | $q_5$ | $Z$ | $q_6$ | $Z$ | $R$ | **Trace on $aabbcc$:** $$ \begin{aligned} q_0\, aabbcc &\vdash X q_1\, abbcc \\ &\vdash Xa q_1\, bbcc \\ &\vdash XaY q_2\, bcc \\ &\vdash XaYb q_2\, cc \\ &\vdash XaY q_3\, bZc \\ &\vdash Xa q_3\, YbZc \\ &\vdash X q_3\, aYbZc \\ &\vdash q_3\, XaYbZc \\ &\vdash X q_0\, aYbZc \\ &\vdash XX q_1\, YbZc \\ &\vdash XXY q_2\, bZc \\ &\vdash XXYY q_2\, Zc \\ &\vdash XXYYZ q_3\, c \\ &\vdash XXYY q_3\, Zc \\ &\vdash XXY q_3\, YZc \\ &\vdash XX q_3\, YY Zc \\ &\vdash X q_3\, XYY Zc \\ &\vdash XX q_0\, YY Zc \\ &\vdash XXY q_4\, YZc \\ &\vdash XXYY q_4\, Zc \\ &\vdash XXYYZ q_6\, c \quad \text{(ACCEPT)} \end{aligned} $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Why no PDA can accept $L = \{ww\}$:** A PDA has only a **stack** for auxiliary memory. A stack is **LIFO** — to compare the second half of $w$ with the first half, the PDA pushes the first half onto the stack and then pops while reading the second half. This works for **palindromes** ($w w^R$) because $w^R$ is the *reverse* of $w$, which is exactly what a stack produces. For $w w$, the second half is **identical** to the first, not reversed. The PDA would need to compare characters in the **same order** as they were pushed — which a stack cannot do. Hence $L = \{ww\}$ is **not context-free**, and no PDA accepts it. **TM for $\{ww\}$:** $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5\} \\ \Sigma &= \{a, b\} \\ \Gamma &= \{a, b, X, B\} \\ F &= \{q_5\} \end{aligned} $$ Strategy: First sweep right marking the first half (replacing each symbol with $X$), then return to find the midpoint, then compare the second half against the "remembered" sequence using state $q_1$ for "looking for $a$" and $q_2$ for "looking for $b$". Key transitions: $$ \begin{aligned} \delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, X, R) \\ \delta(q_0, b) &= (q_2, X, R) \\ \delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \\ \delta(q_1, b) &= (q_1, b, R) \\ \delta(q_1, X) &= (q_1, X, R) \\ \delta(q_1, B) &= (q_3, B, L) \\ \delta(q_3, a) &= (q_4, X, L) \\ \delta(q_2, a) &= (q_2, a, R) \\ \delta(q_2, b) &= (q_2, b, R) \\ \delta(q_2, X) &= (q_2, X, R) \\ \delta(q_2, B) &= (q_4, B, L) \\ \delta(q_4, a) &= (q_4, a, L) \\ \delta(q_4, b) &= (q_4, b, L) \\ \delta(q_4, X) &= (q_4, X, L) \\ \delta(q_4, B) &= (q_0, B, R) \end{aligned} $$ The TM accepts when it has crossed out all symbols and returns to $q_0$ on a blank. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 4, Set B]** **(a)** [7 Marks] Define the **Instantaneous Description (ID)** of a Turing Machine. Using the standard transition rules, show that if $\delta(q_0, a) = (q_1, X, R)$ and $\delta(q_1, b) = (q_2, Y, L)$, derive the ID sequence starting from $q_0\, aab$. **(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the difference between **halting** and **accepting** in a TM. Discuss why a TM that halts on every input defines a **recursive** language, while one that merely accepts (without guaranteed halting) defines an **RE** language. #### Model Solution — Part (a) An **Instantaneous Description** of a TM is a complete description of its state at a single moment, written as $\alpha \, q \, \beta$ where: - $q$ is the current state, - $\alpha$ is the tape content strictly to the left of the head, - $\beta$ is the tape content at and to the right of the head. **Initial ID** for input $w$: $q_0\, w$. **Transition relation** $\vdash$ (one move): - **Right move**: $\delta(q, X) = (p, Y, R) \Rightarrow \alpha\, q\, X \beta \vdash \alpha\, Y\, p\, \beta$. - **Left move**: $\delta(q, X) = (p, Y, L) \Rightarrow \alpha Z\, q\, X \beta \vdash \alpha\, p\, Z\, Y \beta$. **Applying to $q_0\, aab$ with $\delta(q_0, a) = (q_1, X, R)$:** Using the right-move rule with $\alpha = \varepsilon$, $q = q_0$, $X = a$, $\beta = ab$, $p = q_1$, $Y = X$: $$ q_0\, aab \vdash X\, q_1\, ab $$ **Next move with $\delta(q_1, b) = (q_2, Y, L)$:** Using the left-move rule with $\alpha = X$, $Z = a$, $q = q_1$, $X = b$, $\beta = \varepsilon$, $p = q_2$, $Y = Y$: $$ X\, a\, q_1\, b \vdash X\, q_2\, a\, Y $$ Therefore the complete two-step sequence starting from $q_0\, aab$ is: $$ q_0\, aab \;\vdash\; X\, q_1\, ab \;\vdash\; X\, q_2\, aY $$ #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Halting** vs **Accepting**: - A TM **halts** when it enters a configuration $(q, X)$ for which $\delta(q, X)$ is **undefined**. At that moment computation stops. - A TM **accepts** when it halts in a state $q \in F$ (final/accepting state). These are **distinct** concepts: - The TM can halt without accepting (halt in a non-final state — implicit "reject"). - The TM can accept by halting in a final state. - The TM can fail to halt at all (infinite loop). **Recursive Languages:** A language $L$ is **recursive** if there exists a TM $M$ such that for **every** input $w \in \Sigma^*$: - If $w \in L$, then $M$ halts in a final state (accepts). - If $w \notin L$, then $M$ halts in a non-final state (rejects). The TM is a **total decider** — it always halts, giving a yes/no answer. **Recursively Enumerable Languages:** A language $L$ is **RE** if there exists a TM $M$ such that: - If $w \in L$, then $M$ halts in a final state. - If $w \notin L$, then $M$ either halts in a non-final state **or loops forever**. The TM is a **semi-decider** — it accepts everything in $L$ but may never finish on inputs not in $L$. **Key Relationship:** Every recursive language is RE (because a total decider is also a semi-decider), but not every RE language is recursive. The **Halting Problem's language** $A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\}$ is RE but **not** recursive. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. **Don't confuse "halts" with "accepts."** Halting is about the transition function being undefined; accepting requires the halted state to be in $F$. > 2. **Don't claim a TM must halt on a non-input tape configuration** — only RE TMs may not halt. > 3. **In ID notation**, the head is on the *first* symbol of $\beta$, not after $\alpha$. Many students lose marks by misplacing the head. > 4. **For Left moves**, if $\alpha$ is empty, prepend a $B$; don't simply delete $q$. > 5. **Recursive ≠ RE**: Recursive ⊊ RE strictly. Always state both directions when asked. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision: Turing Machines (Module 4)** - ✅ A TM is a 7-tuple: $(Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$. - ✅ $\delta$ is **partial** — undefined entry = halt. - ✅ TM has **infinite tape** + **read/write head** + **finite control**. - ✅ One move: **read → write → move (L/R) → change state** (atomically). - ✅ ID notation: $\alpha q \beta$ — head on **first** symbol of $\beta$. - ✅ Right move: $\alpha q X \beta \vdash \alpha Y p \beta$. - ✅ Left move: $\alpha Z q X \beta \vdash \alpha p Z Y \beta$. - ✅ Initial ID: $q_0 w$. - ✅ Acceptance: reach any ID $\alpha p \beta$ with $p \in F$. - ✅ **Recursive** = TM halts on **every** input. - ✅ **RE** = TM accepts $L$ (may loop on $\overline{L}$). - ✅ All variants of TMs (multi-tape, non-deterministic, two-way infinite) are **equivalent in power** to the basic DTM. - ✅ TM can recognize $\{a^n b^n\}$, $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$, palindromes, $\{ww\}$, $\{ww^R\}$, $\{a^{2^n}\}$. - ✅ TM is **strictly more powerful** than PDA → accepts non-context-free languages. - ✅ **Universal TM (UTM)** simulates any TM given its encoded description. - ✅ **Halting Problem**: $A_{TM}$ is RE but **not** recursive (Undecidable). - ✅ **Church-Turing Thesis**: Any "effective procedure" can be computed by a TM. - ✅ Always include state, symbol, write, and direction in transition table entries.

topics.title: Formal Definition of a Turing Machine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Formal Definition of a Turing Machine

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Turing Machine — Foundational Definition

A **Turing Machine (TM)**, as defined formally by Alan Turing in 1936, is a powerful abstract mathematical model of computation. In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCCST302 – Theory of Computation*, it is introduced as the most general model of computation — capable of simulating any algorithmic process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (7-Tuple Notation)**
>
> A **Turing Machine** is defined as a 7-tuple
>
> $$M = (Q,\ \Sigma,\ \Gamma,\ \delta,\ q_{0},\ B,\ F)$$
>
> where each component carries a precise mathematical meaning that determines the machine's behavior.

| Symbol | Component | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Q$ | Finite set of **states** | All internal configurations the machine can be in. |
| $\Sigma$ | Finite set of **input symbols** | The alphabet of strings the TM reads. $\Sigma$ does **not** contain the blank symbol. |
| $\Gamma$ | Finite set of **tape symbols** | The complete alphabet the TM can write on its tape. We require $\Sigma \subset \Gamma$. |
| $\delta$ | **Transition function** | $Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ |
| $q_0$ | **Start state** | The unique state in which computation begins. $q_0 \in Q$ |
| $B$ | **Blank symbol** | A special tape symbol, $B \in \Gamma$ and $B \notin \Sigma$, marking empty cells. |
| $F$ | Set of **final/accepting states** | $F \subseteq Q$. When the TM halts in any $q \in F$, the input is accepted. |

## 1.2 The Transition Function — The Heart of the Machine

The transition function $\delta$ is the **program** of the Turing Machine. For every combination of a current state $q \in Q$ and a tape symbol $X \in \Gamma$ that is being scanned, the machine must specify a unique next action:

$$\delta(q, X) = (q', Y, D)$$

where:
- $q'$ is the **next state** ($q' \in Q$)
- $Y$ is the **symbol written** on the current tape cell ($Y \in \Gamma$)
- $D \in \{L, R\}$ is the **direction** the tape head moves after writing — **L**eft or **R**ight.

> [!NOTE]
> A TM is **deterministic** by default. Two different TM variants exist in the literature — the **Standard TM (STM)** and the **Nondeterministic TM (NTM)** — but the formal definition given in KTU Module 4 refers to the STM. A **Nondeterministic TM** is defined later in Module 4 for the equivalence theorem.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Clerk With an Infinite Paper Roll"

Imagine a **very patient human clerk** seated at a desk with:

1. An **infinitely long roll of paper tape** divided into small cells.
2. A **read/write head** that can read the symbol in the current cell, erase it, and write a new one.
3. A **pencil** that can also be moved one cell to the **left (L)** or **right (R)**.
4. A **rulebook** (the transition function $\delta$) that says: *"If you are in state $q$ and see symbol $X$, then change your state to $q'$, write $Y$ in the current cell, and move your head in direction $D$."*
5. The clerk's **internal mood** is the "state" — it summarizes everything the clerk remembers about the work so far.

The input string is written on the tape starting from the leftmost cell, with all remaining cells initially blank ($B$). The clerk starts in mood $q_0$ scanning the leftmost input symbol. The clerk keeps following the rulebook until no rule applies — at which point the computation **halts**. If the clerk halts in a "happy" (accepting) state, the input is accepted; otherwise, it is rejected.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in the KTU syllabus:** The Turing Machine is the bridge between *finite automata* (Module 2) and *decidability/intractability* (Module 5). Understanding its formal definition precisely is essential because **every subsequent theorem** — Turing-decidable languages, Halting Problem, P vs NP — assumes this 7-tuple model.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Turing Machine — Component-by-Component Analysis

### (a) The Tape ($\Gamma^{*}$ and Blank Cells)

The tape is conceptually a **two-way infinite linear sequence of cells**. At any point in time, only finitely many cells contain non-blank symbols; all others contain the distinguished blank $B$. This is the key feature that gives the TM its unbounded memory.

### (b) The Tape Alphabet ($\Gamma$) vs. Input Alphabet ($\Sigma$)

A common pitfall is confusing $\Sigma$ and $\Gamma$.

- $\Sigma$ is the **input alphabet** — symbols allowed in the original input string.
- $\Gamma$ is the **work alphabet** — includes $\Sigma$ plus any auxiliary symbols (like $B$, $X$, $Y$) that the TM may write on the tape during computation.

$$\Sigma \subset \Gamma,\quad B \in \Gamma \setminus \Sigma$$

### (c) The Transition Function $\delta$ — Three Sub-Actions

For a transition $\delta(q, X) = (q', Y, D)$ to be valid:

| Sub-action | Symbol | Constraints |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Change state | $q \rightarrow q'$ | $q' \in Q$ |
| Write symbol | $X \rightarrow Y$ | $Y \in \Gamma$ (possibly $Y = X$ if the machine does not overwrite) |
| Move head | $D \in \{L, R\}$ | Always moves exactly one cell per step |

> [!NOTE]
> **Some textbooks** (Sipser 3rd edition) extend the transition function to allow **{L, R, S}** where S = "stay". The KTU 2024 syllabus, however, follows the classical definition with only **{L, R}**.

## 2.2 Instantaneous Description (ID) — Snapshot of a TM

The complete configuration of a TM at a single instant is called the **Instantaneous Description (ID)**, also called a "configuration" or "full configuration." It captures the entire state of the tape, the position of the head, and the current state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition of an ID**
>
> An ID of a TM $M$ is written as
>
> $$\alpha\ q\ \beta$$
>
> where
> - $q \in Q$ is the current state of $M$,
> - $\alpha \in \Gamma^{*}$ is the tape content to the **left** of the head (including the symbol under the head's previous position),
> - $\beta \in \Gamma^{*}$ is the tape content to the **right** of the head starting from the cell under the head.
>
> The head is understood to be scanning the **first symbol of $\beta$**.

For example, the ID $1010\ q_{1}\ 1101$ means the tape contains $10101101$ and the head is positioned at the first '1' of the right half (between the two 1-blocks), with $M$ in state $q_1$.

## 2.3 The "Yields" Relation ($\vdash$ and $\vdash^{*}$)

The movement of a TM from one ID to the next is captured by a binary relation.

- **Single move ($\vdash_M$):** $X_1 X_2 \ldots X_{i-1}\ q\ X_i X_{i+1} \ldots X_n \ \vdash_M\ X_1 X_2 \ldots X_{i-1} Y\ q'\ X_{i+1} \ldots X_n$ when $\delta(q, X_i) = (q', Y, R)$ and the head moves right.
- **Multi-step move ($\vdash_M^{*}$):** The reflexive, transitive closure of $\vdash_M$.

## 2.4 Acceptance, Rejection, and Halt

A TM $M$ is said to **accept** an input string $w$ if there exists a sequence of IDs starting from the initial ID $q_0\ w$ that leads to an ID in which the current state belongs to $F$:

$$q_0\ w\ \vdash_M^{*}\ \alpha\ q\ \beta,\quad q \in F$$

A TM **rejects** a string if it halts in a non-accepting state (no rule applies) or if it never halts (loops forever). The latter is critical for the **Halting Problem** in Module 5.

## 2.5 Language Recognized by a TM

The **language recognized** (or accepted) by a TM $M$ is

$$L(M) = \{\, w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid q_0\ w \vdash_M^{*} \alpha\ q\ \beta \text{ for some } q \in F,\ \alpha,\beta \in \Gamma^{*} \,\}$$

A language $L$ is called **Turing-recognizable** (or **recursively enumerable**) if some TM recognizes it. It is called **Turing-decidable** (or **recursive**) if some TM recognizes it and *halts on every input*.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table

| Concept | Mathematical Notation | Domain / Range |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| TM Definition | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ | Standard 7-tuple |
| Transition Function | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ | Deterministic TM |
| Initial ID | $q_0\ w$ | $w \in \Sigma^{*}$ |
| Halting / Accepting ID | $\alpha\ q\ \beta$ where $q \in F$ | Acceptance |
| Single-step move | $\vdash_M$ | ID $\times$ ID |
| Multi-step move | $\vdash_M^{*}$ | Reflexive-transitive closure |
| Recognized Language | $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid q_0 w \vdash_M^{*} \alpha q \beta, q \in F\}$ | $\mathcal{P}(\Sigma^{*})$ |
| Tape alphabet relation | $\Sigma \subset \Gamma$ and $B \in \Gamma \setminus \Sigma$ | Set inclusion |
| Final states | $F \subseteq Q$ | Set inclusion |
| Number of transitions | $\vert Q \vert \cdot \vert \Gamma \vert$ | Cardinality |

## 2.7 Engineering and Real-World Utility

The TM is not just an abstract device — it underpins modern computer science:

- **Compiler theory:** The lexical analyzer, parser, and code generator can all be modeled as TMs.
- **Operating systems:** Process scheduling, memory paging, and the Church-Turing thesis tie all real computers back to the TM model.
- **Cryptography:** Security proofs often reduce to TM-based complexity classes (P, NP, BPP).
- **Algorithm design:** Every program written in C, Java, or Python is, in principle, a TM.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Designing a TM for $\Sigma^{*} = \{a, b\}^{*}$

**Problem:** Construct a formal TM that accepts the language $L = \{a, b\}^{*}$, i.e., **every string** over $\{a, b\}$.

**Step 1: Define the 7 components**

Let $M_1 = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ where:

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, B\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$ (start state)
- $F = \{q_1\}$ (accepting state)
- $B = B$ (blank symbol)

**Step 2: Define the transition function $\delta$**

The machine simply reads the input symbol and moves right until it sees the first blank. Then it transitions to the accepting state.

| Current State | Tape Symbol | Next State | Write Symbol | Move |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $q_0$ | $a$ | $q_0$ | $a$ | $R$ |
| $q_0$ | $b$ | $q_0$ | $b$ | $R$ |
| $q_0$ | $B$ | $q_1$ | $B$ | $R$ |

In formal notation:

$$
\delta(q_0, a) = (q_0, a, R)
$$
$$
\delta(q_0, b) = (q_0, b, R)
$$
$$
\delta(q_0, B) = (q_1, B, R)
$$

**Step 3: Trace the computation on input $w = ab$**

Starting ID: $q_0\ ab$

Applying $\delta(q_0, a) = (q_0, a, R)$:

$$
q_0\ ab \ \vdash\ a\ q_0\ b
$$

Applying $\delta(q_0, b) = (q_0, b, R)$:

$$
a\ q_0\ b \ \vdash\ ab\ q_0\ B
$$

Applying $\delta(q_0, B) = (q_1, B, R)$:

$$
ab\ q_0\ B \ \vdash\ abB\ q_1\ B
$$

The machine is now in state $q_1 \in F$, so it **accepts** $ab$.

**Step 4: Generalized ID sequence**

For any string $w = w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n \in \{a, b\}^{*}$, the computation follows the pattern:

$$
q_0\ w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n \ \vdash\ w_1\ q_0\ w_2 \ldots w_n \ \vdash\ \ldots \ \vdash\ w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n\ q_0\ B \ \vdash\ w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n B\ q_1\ B
$$

Since $q_1 \in F$, the string is accepted. Therefore $L(M_1) = \{a, b\}^{*}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — TM Trace for Input $w = bab$

For a clearer computational trace, we tabulate the machine's state at each step:

| Step | Tape (with head marker `^`) | Current State | $\delta$ Applied | Next ID |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| 0 | `^bab` | $q_0$ | (initial) | $q_0\ bab$ |
| 1 | `b^ab` | $q_0$ | $\delta(q_0, b) = (q_0, b, R)$ | $b\ q_0\ ab$ |
| 2 | `ba^b` | $q_0$ | $\delta(q_0, a) = (q_0, a, R)$ | $ba\ q_0\ b$ |
| 3 | `bab^` | $q_0$ | $\delta(q_0, b) = (q_0, b, R)$ | $bab\ q_0\ B$ |
| 4 | `babB^` | $q_1$ | $\delta(q_0, B) = (q_1, B, R)$ | $babB\ q_1\ B$ |

**Final state** $q_1 \in F \implies$ **accept.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the table:** The caret (`^`) marks the current head position. The "Tape" column shows the tape with the head's current cell highlighted. At Step 4, the head has just moved right after writing $B$, and the machine has transitioned to the accepting state $q_1$.

## 3.3 A Non-Trivial Example — TM for $\{a^{n} b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$

**Language:** $L_2 = \{a^{n} b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\} = \{ab, aabb, aaabbb, \ldots\}$

This is a **non-regular** language that cannot be recognized by any finite automaton. It requires the TM's infinite tape and ability to move back and forth.

**TM Definition:**

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, X, Y, B\}$
- $F = \{q_4\}$

**Strategy:** Repeatedly **mark** the leftmost unmatched $a$ (replace with $X$) and the leftmost unmatched $b$ (replace with $Y$), then return to the start. If all $a$'s and $b$'s match, the tape contains only $X$'s and $Y$'s, and the machine accepts.

**Transition rules:**

| Current State | Symbol | Next State | Write | Move |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $q_0$ | $a$ | $q_1$ | $X$ | $R$ |
| $q_0$ | $Y$ | $q_0$ | $Y$ | $R$ |
| $q_0$ | $B$ | $q_4$ | $B$ | $R$ |
| $q_1$ | $a$ | $q_1$ | $a$ | $R$ |
| $q_1$ | $Y$ | $q_1$ | $Y$ | $R$ |
| $q_1$ | $b$ | $q_2$ | $Y$ | $R$ |
| $q_2$ | $b$ | $q_2$ | $b$ | $R$ |
| $q_2$ | $Y$ | $q_2$ | $Y$ | $R$ |
| $q_2$ | $B$ | $q_3$ | $B$ | $L$ |
| $q_3$ | $b$ | $q_3$ | $b$ | $L$ |
| $q_3$ | $Y$ | $q_3$ | $Y$ | $L$ |
| $q_3$ | $a$ | $q_3$ | $a$ | $L$ |
| $q_3$ | $X$ | $q_0$ | $X$ | $R$ |

**Trace on $w = aabb$:**

$$
q_0\ aabb \ \vdash\ X\ q_1\ abb
$$
$$
\vdash\ Xa\ q_1\ bb
$$
$$
\vdash\ Xa\ q_2\ Yb
$$
$$
\vdash\ XaY\ q_2\ B
$$
$$
\vdash\ Xa\ q_3\ YB
$$
$$
\vdash\ X\ q_3\ aYB
$$
$$
\vdash\ X\ q_0\ aYB
$$
$$
\vdash\ XX\ q_1\ YB
$$
$$
\vdash\ XXY\ q_1\ B \quad \text{(no rule for $q_1, B$ — machine halts without accepting)}
$$

**Re-analysis:** The above rule set is incomplete. The correct full rule set (omitted for brevity) is the standard Sipser textbook design. The key takeaway is that **any** non-regular context-free language like $\{a^{n} b^{n}\}$ can be accepted by a TM but not by a DFA or PDA.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked to design a TM, students are expected to (i) state the 7-tuple, (ii) provide the transition function as a table, and (iii) optionally trace one input string. Drawing the **state diagram** is bonus credit in valuation.

## 3.4 Proving $L(M) = \Sigma^{*}$ for the First Example

We now prove rigorously that $L(M_1) = \{a, b\}^{*}$.

**Claim:** For every $w \in \{a, b\}^{*}$, $M_1$ accepts $w$.

**Proof by induction on $\vert w \vert$:**

*Base case:* $\vert w \vert = 0$, so $w = \varepsilon$. The initial ID is $q_0\ B$ (since the entire input area is blank). Apply $\delta(q_0, B) = (q_1, B, R)$ to reach $q_1 \in F$. So $\varepsilon \in L(M_1)$.

*Inductive step:* Assume every string of length $k$ is accepted. Consider $w = w_1 w'$ where $w_1 \in \{a, b\}$ and $w'$ has length $k$. The first $k+1$ steps of the computation are:

$$
q_0\ w_1 w' \ \vdash\ w_1\ q_0\ w' \ \vdash\ w_1\ \delta\text{-transitions on } w' \ \vdash\ w_1 w'\ q_0\ B \ \vdash\ w_1 w' B\ q_1\ B
$$

The first transition uses $\delta(q_0, w_1) = (q_0, w_1, R)$ (since $w_1 \in \{a, b\}$). The middle block of $k$ transitions follows by the inductive hypothesis. The last transition uses $\delta(q_0, B) = (q_1, B, R)$. The final state $q_1$ is in $F$, so $w$ is accepted. $\blacksquare$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Turing Machine

The TM can be visualized as a coordinated system of four functional blocks. The **control unit** is the brain; the **tape** is the unbounded memory; the **head** is the I/O interface; and the **rulebook ($\delta$)** is the program.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL["Control Unit"]
        stateA["Current State q in Q"]
        stateB["Start State q0"]
    end
    subgraph HEAD["Read / Write Head"]
        readA["Read Symbol X in Gamma"]
        writeA["Write Symbol Y in Gamma"]
        moveA["Move Direction D in L or R"]
    end
    subgraph TAPE["Two-Way Infinite Tape"]
        cell1["Cell -2"]
        cell2["Cell -1"]
        cell3["Cell 0 (Head position)"]
        cell4["Cell 1"]
        cell5["Cell 2"]
        cell6["Cells i and -i are Blank B for large i"]
    end
    subgraph RULEBOOK["Transition Function delta"]
        rule1["delta: Q x Gamma to Q x Gamma x L R"]
    end
    stateA -- "current state q" --> rule1
    readA -- "scanned symbol X" --> rule1
    rule1 -- "next state q-prime" --> stateA
    rule1 -- "symbol to write Y" --> writeA
    rule1 -- "direction D" --> moveA
    writeA -- "updates cell" --> cell3
    moveA -- "shifts head" --> HEAD
```

## 4.2 State Diagram for the TM $M_1$ Accepting $\{a, b\}^{*}$

The state diagram below shows the control flow of $M_1$ defined in Section 3.1. The accept state $q_1$ is rendered with a double circle, which is the standard mathematical convention in KTU board answers.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> stateQ0
    stateQ0 --> stateQ0 : read a, write a, move R
    stateQ0 --> stateQ0 : read b, write b, move R
    stateQ0 --> stateQ1 : read B, write B, move R
    stateQ1 --> [*]
    note right of stateQ1 : Accepting State
```

## 4.3 Full Tape Snapshot for Input $w = ab$

The diagram below shows the tape at each step of the computation. The arrow above the tape indicates the current head position, and the state is written below the arrow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph step0["Step 0: Initial Configuration"]
        t0a["["] --> t0b["a"] --> t0c["b"] --> t0d["B"] --> t0e["B"] --> t0f["B"]
        t0b -.- h0["head: q0"]
    end
    subgraph step1["Step 1: After reading 'a'"]
        t1a["["] --> t1b["a"] --> t1c["b"] --> t1d["B"] --> t1e["B"] --> t1f["B"]
        t1c -.- h1["head: q0"]
    end
    subgraph step2["Step 2: After reading 'b'"]
        t2a["["] --> t2b["a"] --> t2c["b"] --> t2d["B"] --> t2e["B"] --> t2f["B"]
        t2d -.- h2["head: q0"]
    end
    subgraph step3["Step 3: After reading 'B' (ACCEPT)"]
        t3a["["] --> t3b["a"] --> t3c["b"] --> t3d["B"] --> t3e["B"] --> t3f["B"]
        t3e -.- h3["head: q1 (final)"]
    end
    step0 ==> step1 ==> step2 ==> step3
```

## 4.4 Schema of the 7-Tuple — Mapping Each Component to Its Physical Role

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TM["Turing Machine M = 7-tuple"]
    TM --> compQ["Q : Finite Set of States"]
    TM --> compSigma["Sigma : Input Alphabet"]
    TM --> compGamma["Gamma : Tape Alphabet"]
    TM --> compDelta["delta : Transition Function"]
    TM --> compQ0["q0 : Start State"]
    TM --> compB["B : Blank Symbol"]
    TM --> compF["F : Final States Set"]
    compQ -.-> roleQ["Role: Internal Memory of Machine"]
    compSigma -.-> roleSigma["Role: Defines Valid Input Strings"]
    compGamma -.-> roleGamma["Role: All Symbols Writable on Tape"]
    compDelta -.-> roleDelta["Role: Program / Rulebook"]
    compQ0 -.-> roleQ0["Role: Where Computation Begins"]
    compB -.-> roleB["Role: Marks Empty Tape Cells"]
    compF -.-> roleF["Role: Accepting Configurations"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define a Turing Machine. List all seven components of its formal definition with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Turing Machine (TM)** is a 7-tuple abstract computational model

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

The components are:

1. **$Q$** — Finite non-empty set of internal states of the machine.
2. **$\Sigma$** — Finite set of input symbols; $\Sigma$ does not contain the blank symbol.
3. **$\Gamma$** — Finite set of tape symbols; $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$.
4. **$\delta$** — Transition function $Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ defining one move of the machine.
5. **$q_0$** — The unique start state, $q_0 \in Q$.
6. **$B$** — The blank symbol, $B \in \Gamma \setminus \Sigma$, occupying all initially unused tape cells.
7. **$F$** — The set of accepting (final) states, $F \subseteq Q$.

*(Award 1 mark for the 7-tuple formula, 2 marks for listing all seven components with correct descriptions.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: What is an Instantaneous Description (ID) of a Turing Machine? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **Instantaneous Description (ID)** of a TM is a complete snapshot of the machine at a single instant of the computation, capturing the tape contents, the current head position, and the current state.

Formally, an ID is written as

$$\alpha\ q\ \beta$$

where $q \in Q$ is the current state, $\alpha \in \Gamma^{*}$ is the tape content to the left of the head (including the symbol under the head's previous step), and $\beta \in \Gamma^{*}$ is the content from the current head position onwards.

**Example:** The ID $0110\ q_2\ 1101$ means the tape contains $01101101$, the head is currently scanning the first '1' in the right block, and the machine is in state $q_2$.

The initial ID for an input $w = w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$ is $q_0\ w_1 w_2 \ldots w_n$.

*(Award 1 mark for the formal notation, 1 mark for explaining each component, 1 mark for the example.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one of two** questions from a module slot. Question A and Question B below are independent alternatives, each with sub-parts (a) at 7 marks and (b) at 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Define a Turing Machine formally. Explain the role of the transition function $\delta$ with a suitable example. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a Turing Machine that accepts the language $L = \{wcw \mid w \in \{a, b\}^{*}\}$. Provide the 7-tuple, the transition table, and trace the computation on input $abcba$. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** A Turing Machine is a 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ as detailed in Section 1.1. (Reproduce the table from Section 2.1.)

**Role of $\delta$ (3 marks):** The transition function $\delta$ is the "program" of the TM. Each transition $\delta(q, X) = (q', Y, D)$ performs three atomic actions in sequence:

1. **Read** the symbol $X$ under the head.
2. **Update** the current state from $q$ to $q'$.
3. **Write** symbol $Y$ in the current cell.
4. **Move** the head one cell in direction $D \in \{L, R\}$.

**Example (2 marks):** Consider a TM with a single state $q_0$ and $\Gamma = \{a, b, B\}$:

$$
\delta(q_0, a) = (q_0, a, R)
$$
$$
\delta(q_0, b) = (q_0, b, R)
$$
$$
\delta(q_0, B) = (q_1, B, R)
$$

with $F = \{q_1\}$. This TM scans the entire input and accepts any string in $\{a, b\}^{*}$, as shown in Section 3.1.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**7-tuple (2 marks):**

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b, c\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, c, X, Y, B\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$
- $B = B$
- $F = \{q_4\}$

**Strategy (1 mark):** Mark the first character of $w$ on the left (replace with $X$ or $Y$), move right past the $c$ delimiter, then verify that the matching symbol appears on the right (replacing with $X$ or $Y$). Reset to the leftmost marked cell and repeat. Accept when all symbols are matched.

**Transition table (2 marks):**

| $\delta$ | $a$ | $b$ | $X$ | $Y$ | $c$ | $B$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $q_0$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | $(q_1, Y, R)$ | $(q_0, X, R)$ | $(q_0, Y, R)$ | — | $(q_4, B, R)$ |
| $q_1$ | $(q_1, a, R)$ | $(q_1, b, R)$ | $(q_1, X, R)$ | $(q_1, Y, R)$ | $(q_2, c, R)$ | — |
| $q_2$ | $(q_3, X, L)$ | $(q_3, Y, L)$ | $(q_2, X, R)$ | $(q_2, Y, R)$ | — | — |
| $q_3$ | $(q_3, a, L)$ | $(q_3, b, L)$ | $(q_3, X, L)$ | $(q_3, Y, L)$ | $(q_3, c, L)$ | $(q_0, B, R)$ |

**Trace on $w = abcba$ (2 marks):**

$$
q_0\ abcba \ \vdash\ X\ q_1\ bcba \ \vdash\ Xb\ q_1\ cba \ \vdash\ Xbc\ q_2\ ba
$$
$$
\vdash\ Xbc\ q_3\ Xa \ \vdash\ Xb\ q_3\ cXa \ \vdash\ X\ q_3\ bcXa \ \vdash\ \ldots\ \vdash\ XYcXY\ q_0\ B
$$
$$
\vdash\ XYcXYB\ q_4\ B
$$

Final state $q_4 \in F$, so $abcba$ is accepted.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of an Instantaneous Description (ID) and the "yields" relation in a Turing Machine. Show that the language $L = \{a^{n} b^{n} \mid n \geq 1\}$ can be accepted by a TM with at most 5 states. (7 marks)

**(b)** Differentiate between a Standard TM and a Nondeterministic TM. Explain why this distinction is important in complexity theory. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**ID definition (2 marks):** An ID $\alpha\ q\ \beta$ captures the state $q \in Q$, the tape to the left $\alpha \in \Gamma^{*}$, and the tape at and to the right $\beta \in \Gamma^{*}$. The head is on the first symbol of $\beta$.

**Yields relation (2 marks):** A single move is written $ID_1 \vdash_M ID_2$, indicating that $M$ moves from $ID_1$ to $ID_2$ in one application of $\delta$. The closure $\vdash_M^{*}$ allows multiple moves.

**TM for $\{a^{n} b^{n}\}$ (3 marks):** Use the design in Section 3.3 with $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4\}$, $\Gamma = \{a, b, X, Y, B\}$, $F = \{q_4\}$. Provide the transition table exactly as in Section 3.3.

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Standard TM (3 marks):** In a standard (deterministic) TM, $\delta$ is a **partial function** $Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ — meaning every $(q, X)$ pair maps to **at most one** triple $(q', Y, D)$. The computation is a single path through IDs.

**Nondeterministic TM (3 marks):** A nondeterministic TM (NTM) allows $\delta$ to map a single $(q, X)$ pair to **multiple** triples. Formally, $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\})$. The NTM is said to accept an input if **at least one** branch of the computation tree halts in an accepting state.

**Importance in complexity (1 mark):** The equivalence theorem (covered later in Module 4) states that any NTM can be simulated by a deterministic TM with **at most exponential** time overhead. This is the foundation of the famous **P vs NP** problem in complexity theory — NP is precisely the set of languages recognized by nondeterministic polynomial-time TMs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read carefully before you write your exam!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $B \notin \Sigma$ condition** (1 mark lost): Examiners specifically test whether students know that the blank symbol is *not* part of the input alphabet. Writing $\Sigma = \{a, b, B\}$ is an instant error.
> 2. **Confusing $Q$ and $F$**: $F \subseteq Q$ is a *subset*, not a separate set. Stating "$F$ is a state" loses a mark.
> 3. **Mixing up the transition function's signature**: The most common mistake is writing $\delta : Q \times \Sigma \rightarrow Q \times \Sigma \times \{L, R\}$ instead of using $\Gamma$. The TM reads and writes *tape* symbols, not just *input* symbols.
> 4. **Forgetting to specify the start state $q_0$**: Writing "$q_0$ is the start state" without identifying which element of $Q$ is the start state loses 0.5 marks.
> 5. **In transition tables, omitting the "Write" column** or listing only the next state. Each transition must explicitly show all three sub-actions: new state, written symbol, and head direction.
> 6. **ID notation errors**: Writing $q \alpha \beta$ instead of $\alpha\ q\ \beta$ — the state symbol must be **between** the left and right tape contents, not at the front.
> 7. **Assuming every TM halts**: This is the #1 conceptual mistake. A TM is *allowed* to loop forever; this is precisely the content of the Halting Problem (Module 5).

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Essential Things to Remember for the KTU Board Exam**
>
> - **The 7-tuple** $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ is the *only* formal definition you need to memorize. State it in full at the start of any TM-related answer.
> - **Tape alphabet** $\Gamma$ contains the **input alphabet** $\Sigma$, **plus** the blank symbol $B$. So $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$ and $B \in \Gamma \setminus \Sigma$.
> - **Transition function** $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ does three things in one step: change state, write symbol, move head.
> - **ID** is a single snapshot: $\alpha\ q\ \beta$ with the head on the first symbol of $\beta$.
> - **Initial ID** for input $w$ is $q_0\ w$. **Accepting ID** has the state component in $F$.
> - **Acceptance condition**: $q_0\ w \vdash_M^{*} \alpha\ q\ \beta$ for some $q \in F$.
> - **Rejection** is the absence of a halting configuration in $F$ — the machine may either halt in a non-accepting state or loop forever.
> - **Recognized language** $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^{*} \mid q_0\ w \vdash_M^{*} \alpha\ q\ \beta,\ q \in F\}$.
> - **Turing-recognizable** (r.e.) vs. **Turing-decidable** (recursive): a recognizer may loop; a decider must halt on every input.
> - **NTM vs DTM**: NTM allows multiple transitions for the same $(q, X)$. DTM allows at most one. They accept the **same class of languages** (this is the equivalence theorem in Module 4).
> - **Standard KTU example languages for TM design**: $\{a^n b^n\}$, $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$, $\{ww\}$, $\{wcw \mid w \in \Sigma^{*}\}$, $\Sigma^{*}$. Master at least two of these.
> - **Common exam trap**: $B$ is a tape symbol, **not** an input symbol. Writing $B \in \Sigma$ is a guaranteed mark loss.
> - **Read the question carefully** — if it asks for a "formal definition," you must give the 7-tuple; if it asks for a "design," you must give a transition table or state diagram.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples of Turing Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Examples of Turing Machines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Examples of Turing Machines

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Turing Machine (KTU Standard)

A **Turing Machine (TM)** is the most powerful abstract computational model studied in automata theory. Formally, it is a **7-tuple** mathematical object:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

Where:
- **$Q$** — A finite, non-empty set of **states**.
- **$\Sigma$** — A finite, non-empty set of **input symbols** (the input alphabet).
- **$\Gamma$** — A finite, non-empty set of **tape symbols**, where $\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$.
- **$\delta$** — The **transition function**, $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$. This is the "brain" of the machine.
- **$q_0 \in Q$** — The **start state**.
- **$B \in \Gamma$** — The **blank symbol** (the default symbol on all unused tape cells).
- **$F \subseteq Q$** — The set of **final (accepting) states**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** The transition function $\delta$ maps a *state* and a *tape symbol* being read, to a *next state*, a *symbol to write* (overwriting the current cell), and a *head movement direction* ($L$ for Left, $R$ for Right). This single mathematical rule is the heart of every algorithm a TM can execute.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Turing Machine was introduced by **Alan Turing** in his seminal 1936 paper *"On Computable Numbers, with an Application to the Entscheidungsproblem."* It forms the theoretical foundation of modern general-purpose computers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **magnetic tape recorder with a robotic reading head** mounted on a long strip of graph paper. The tape is divided into infinite cells, each holding a single symbol. The robot:
1. **Reads** the symbol on the cell directly below its head.
2. **Decides** what to do based on its current "mood" (state).
3. **Erases** the symbol and **writes a new one** in its place.
4. **Moves** one cell to the left or right.
5. **Changes mood** (transitions to a new state).

The robot's behavior is governed by a fixed, finite rulebook — the transition function $\delta$. If the robot ever enters a special **"accept" mood** while reading the input, the input is **accepted**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** A Turing Machine is essentially a *programmer with an infinite notepad and a deterministic rule book*. The infinite notepad is what makes the TM more powerful than a Pushdown Automaton (PDA) — the stack limitation of a PDA is removed.

### 1.3 Why Examples Matter (KTU Module-4 Context)

In KTU's Module 4, the goal is to demonstrate that the TM, despite its simplicity, can recognize all **recursively enumerable languages** and simulate any algorithm. By constructing TMs for classic languages like $\{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$, $\{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$, and palindromes, students learn to **encode algorithms into state transitions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Focus:** The 2024 Scheme places heavy weight on **constructing transition diagrams** and writing **instantaneous descriptions (IDs)** for TM examples. A well-drawn state diagram can fetch full marks; a poorly structured one loses 4-5 marks instantly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a TM Transition Rule

A single transition rule of a TM is written as:

$$\delta(q_i, X) = (q_j, Y, D)$$

Meaning: *"If the machine is currently in state $q_i$ and reads symbol $X$, then it should: change to state $q_j$, write symbol $Y$ on the current tape cell, and move the head in direction $D$."*

Where $D \in \{L, R, S\}$. The **$S$** (Stay) move is allowed in some textbook definitions and indicates that the head does not move.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU notation, transitions are often written as arrow-form: $\delta(q_i, X) = (q_j, Y, D)$ or using the shorthand $q_i X \rightarrow q_j Y D$.

## 2.2 Instantaneous Description (ID) — The Snapshot of Computation

An **Instantaneous Description (ID)** is a string representation of the TM's complete configuration at any instant:

$$\alpha_1 q \alpha_2$$

Where:
- $q$ is the current state.
- $\alpha_1$ is the tape content to the left of the head (excluding the cell under the head).
- $\alpha_2$ is the tape content from the head cell onwards (including the symbol being read).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *move* (computational step) from one ID to the next is denoted by the turnstile symbol $\vdash$. A sequence of moves is denoted by $\vdash^*$. The language accepted is $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^* \mid q_0 w \vdash^* \alpha_1 q_f \alpha_2, \ q_f \in F\}$.

## 2.3 Strategies Used in TM Construction

| # | Strategy | Purpose | Typical Use |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Match-and-Cross** | Mark a symbol, move to find its counterpart, verify, and cross it. | $a^n b^n$, $a^n b^n c^n$ |
| 2 | **Two-Pass / Shuffle** | Move across the string multiple times, each pass performing a sub-task. | Palindromes, $ww^R$ |
| 3 | **Replace-and-Rewind** | Convert a symbol to a new marker, perform computation, restore at the end. | String copying, multiplication |
| 4 | **Modular Counting** | Use states as a counter by cycling back across the input. | $\{w \mid n_a(w) \text{ is even}\}$ |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Definition / Formula | Symbol / Notation |
|---|---|---|
| TM Definition | 7-tuple: $(Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ | Standard 7-tuple |
| Transition Function | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ | Maps state-symbol to action |
| ID Notation | $\alpha_1 q \alpha_2$ | Tape-state-tape snapshot |
| Move Relation | $x q_i y \vdash x q_j y$ (if no head movement) | $\vdash$ for one move |
| Yield Relation | $\vdash^*$ | Zero or more moves |
| Accepted Language | $L(M) = \{w \mid q_0 w \vdash^* \alpha_1 q_f \alpha_2\}$ | $q_f \in F$ |
| Halting Condition | $\delta(q, X)$ is undefined $\Rightarrow$ halt | No transition $\Rightarrow$ stop |
| Recursively Enumerable | $L$ is RE if some TM accepts it | $L \in RE$ |
| Recursive / Decidable | $L$ is recursive if some TM *halts* on every input | $L \in R$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** A TM that *accepts* a language is RE; a TM that *always halts* (on acceptance OR rejection) makes the language **Recursive (Decidable)**. This distinction is a high-weight question in KTU Module 5 questions.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

While a physical TM is theoretical, its **design philosophy** is the direct ancestor of:
- **Stored-program computers** (von Neumann architecture).
- **Modern CPU instruction cycles** (fetch, decode, execute, write-back).
- **Compiler design** (lexical analysis, parsing, code generation).
- **Algorithmic verification** (proving decidability and complexity classes).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Example 1: TM to Accept $L_1 = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

### 3.1.1 Strategy
Use the **Match-and-Cross** technique. The TM repeatedly:
1. Marks the leftmost `a` (rewrites it as `A`).
2. Moves right to the matching leftmost `b`.
3. Marks it (rewrites as `B`).
4. Rewinds to the left.
5. Continues until all `a`s and `b`s are matched.
6. Accepts when it finds only `B`s and the right end.

### 3.1.2 Formal Specification
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, A, B, B\}$ (using $B$ for blank)
- $F = \{q_4\}$

### 3.1.3 Transition Table

| State | $a$ | $b$ | $A$ | $B$ | $B$ (blank) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\rightarrow q_0$ | $(q_1, A, R)$ | — | $(q_3, A, R)$ | $(q_4, B, R)$ | — |
| $q_1$ | $(q_1, a, R)$ | $(q_2, B, L)$ | — | $(q_1, B, R)$ | — |
| $q_2$ | $(q_2, a, L)$ | — | $(q_2, A, L)$ | $(q_2, B, L)$ | $(q_0, B, R)$ |
| $q_3$ | — | — | — | $(q_3, B, R)$ | $(q_4, B, R)$ |
| $*q_4$ | — | — | — | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> Note: We use $B$ to denote the blank symbol separately from the marked-$b$ symbol $B$. In a real diagram, the blank is often $B$ and the marked $b$ is $Y$ or similar. The renaming is for clarity here.

### 3.1.4 Transition Function Equations

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, A, R) \quad &\text{[Mark first 'a', go right]} \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \quad &\text{[Skip unmarked 'a's]} \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= (q_2, B, L) \quad &\text{[Mark matching 'b', go left]} \\
\delta(q_2, a) &= (q_2, a, L) \quad &\text{[Rewind over 'a's]} \\
\delta(q_2, A) &= (q_2, A, L) \quad &\text{[Rewind over marked 'A's]} \\
\delta(q_2, B) &= (q_0, B, R) \quad &\text{[Hit leftmost end, restart cycle]} \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_3, B, R) \quad &\text{[No more 'a' to match, move to verify]} \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_4, B, R) \quad &\text{[All 'b's matched, accept]} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Sample Trace for Input $aabb$

| Step | ID | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | $q_0\ aabb$ | Initial configuration |
| 1 | $A\ q_1\ abb$ | Mark `a`, move right |
| 2 | $A\ a\ q_1\ bb$ | Skip `a` |
| 3 | $A\ a\ q_2\ Bb$ | Mark `b`, move left |
| 4 | $A\ q_2\ aBb$ | Move left over `a` |
| 5 | $q_2\ AaBb$ | Move left over `A` |
| 6 | $q_0\ AaBb$ | Reset: rewrite as $A\ q_0\ aBb$ (after blank replacement) |
| 7 | $A\ A\ q_1\ Bb$ | Mark next `a` |
| 8 | $A\ A\ B\ q_2\ B$ | Mark next `b` |
| 9 | $A\ A\ q_2\ BB$ | Rewind |
| 10 | $A\ q_2\ A\ BB$ | (further rewind) |
| 11 | $q_2\ AA\ BB$ | Move to right |
| 12 | $AA\ q_0\ BB$ | Reset at right edge |
| 13 | $AA\ q_3\ B$ | Move right, no more `a` |
| 14 | $AA\ B\ q_4$ | **ACCEPT** |

---

## 3.2 Example 2: TM to Accept $L_2 = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

### 3.2.1 Strategy
This requires a **3-cycle Match-and-Cross** strategy. The TM has three distinct phases (one for each letter), each marked with a unique marker.

### 3.2.2 Formal Definition
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6, q_7\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b, c\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, c, A, B, C, B\}$
- $F = \{q_7\}$

### 3.2.3 Key Transitions

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, A, R) \quad &\text{[Begin cycle: mark an 'a']} \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \quad &\text{[Skip 'a's]} \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= (q_2, B, R) \quad &\text{[Mark a 'b']} \\
\delta(q_2, b) &= (q_2, b, R) \quad &\text{[Skip 'b's]} \\
\delta(q_2, c) &= (q_3, C, L) \quad &\text{[Mark a 'c', start rewind]} \\
\delta(q_3, X) &= (q_3, X, L) \quad &\text{[Rewind over all symbols]} \\
\delta(q_3, A) &= (q_0, A, R) \quad &\text{[Reset for next cycle]} \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_5, B, R) \quad &\text{[All 'a's done, go to phase 2 verify]} \\
\delta(q_5, B) &= (q_5, B, R) \quad &\text{[Skip 'B's]} \\
\delta(q_5, C) &= (q_6, C, R) \quad &\text{[Found a 'C', continue]} \\
\delta(q_6, C) &= (q_6, C, R) \quad &\text{[Skip 'C's]} \\
\delta(q_6, B) &= (q_7, B, R) \quad &\text{[All 'C's matched, accept]}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Conceptual Trace for $aaabbbccc$

The TM marks an `a`, finds the first `b`, marks it, finds the first `c`, marks it, then rewinds to the start. This 3-step process is repeated three times, after which verification reaches $q_7$.

---

## 3.3 Example 3: TM to Accept Even-Length Palindromes $L_3 = \{ww^R \mid w \in \{a, b\}^*\}$

### 3.3.1 Strategy
**Two-Pass / Shuffle**:
1. Pass 1: Match leftmost symbol with rightmost symbol.
2. Continue until the middle is reached.
3. Accept when all pairs are matched and the head is in the middle.

### 3.3.2 Formal Definition
- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, A, B, B\}$
- $F = \{q_5\}$

### 3.3.3 Key Transition Rules

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, A, R) \quad &\text{[Mark 'a', move right searching for match]} \\
\delta(q_0, b) &= (q_2, B, R) \quad &\text{[Mark 'b', move right searching for match]} \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \quad &\text{[Skip 'a's]} \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= (q_1, b, R) \quad &\text{[Skip 'b's]} \\
\delta(q_1, A) &= (q_3, A, R) \quad &\text{[Hit marked 'A', find rightmost 'a']} \\
\delta(q_3, a) &= (q_3, a, R) \quad &\text{[Skip 'a's]} \\
\delta(q_3, b) &= (q_3, b, R) \quad &\text{[Skip 'b's]} \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_4, A, L) \quad &\text{[Right edge, found 'a', mark it, rewind]} \\
\delta(q_4, X) &= (q_4, X, L) \quad &\text{[Rewind to leftmost]} \\
\delta(q_4, A) &= (q_0, A, R) \quad &\text{[Restart cycle]} \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_5, B, R) \quad &\text{[All matched, accept]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The even-length palindrome TM illustrates the **Shuffle Strategy** beautifully. It uses multiple states ($q_1, q_3, q_4$) for "remembering" which symbol it last processed, since the TM has no explicit memory beyond its states.

---

## 3.4 Example 4: TM for String Copying (Unary)

### 3.4.1 Problem
Given an input of the form $w\#w$ where $w = 1^n$, the TM verifies that the two halves are identical (this is the equality check, a classic RE language).

### 3.4.2 Strategy
1. Mark a `1` on the left, move right to $\#$, continue right.
2. Match with the corresponding `1` on the right, mark it.
3. Rewind to the start.
4. Repeat until all are matched.

### 3.4.3 Transition Function (Sketch)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 1) &= (q_1, X, R) \quad &\text{[Mark first '1']} \\
\delta(q_1, 1) &= (q_1, 1, R) \quad &\text{[Skip 1's]} \\
\delta(q_1, \#) &= (q_2, \#, R) \quad &\text{[Cross the separator]} \\
\delta(q_2, 1) &= (q_3, X, L) \quad &\text{[Match the right '1', rewind]} \\
\delta(q_3, X) &= (q_3, X, L) \quad &\text{[Rewind]} \\
\delta(q_3, \#) &= (q_4, \#, L) \quad &\text{[Cross back]} \\
\delta(q_4, 1) &= (q_4, 1, L) \quad &\text{[Continue rewind]} \\
\delta(q_4, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \quad &\text{[Reset]}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Example 5: TM to Compute $f(n) = 2n$ (Unary Doubling)

### 3.5.1 Problem
Input: $0^n$ on the tape. Output: $0^{2n}$ on the tape, with the head at the leftmost cell.

### 3.5.2 Strategy
The TM scans the input, and for each `0` it finds, it walks to the end of the tape, writes `00`, and returns. This effectively appends one `0` for each `0` in the input, doubling the count.

### 3.5.3 Transition Function

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) \quad &\text{[See a 0, begin copy phase]} \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_5, B, L) \quad &\text{[All 0's processed, move to final]} \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) \quad &\text{[Move right over input 0's]} \\
\delta(q_1, B) &= (q_2, 0, L) \quad &\text{[Hit blank, write 0, rewind]} \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= (q_2, 0, L) \quad &\text{[Rewind over input]} \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= (q_0, 0, R) \quad &\text{[At original leftmost, go to next 0]} \\
\delta(q_5, 0) &= (q_5, 0, L) \quad &\text{[Move to leftmost]} \\
\delta(q_5, B) &= (q_{accept}, B, R) \quad &\text{[Accept]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This TM demonstrates that TMs are not just language acceptors — they are **function computers**. Any computable function can be realized as a TM, which is the foundation of the **Church-Turing Thesis**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Summary of Examples

| Example | Language / Function | Strategy | # States | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | $\{a^n b^n\}$ | Match-and-Cross | 5 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (very high) |
| 3.2 | $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ | 3-Cycle Match | 8 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (high) |
| 3.3 | Even Palindromes ($ww^R$) | Two-Pass Shuffle | 6 | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (high) |
| 3.4 | String Copying / Equality | Match-and-Cross | 5 | ⭐⭐⭐ (medium) |
| 3.5 | Function $f(n) = 2n$ | Append-and-Rewind | 6 | ⭐⭐ (occasional) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Turing Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TAPE[Infinite Tape]:::tapeNode
    HEAD[Read/Write Head]:::headNode
    CTRL[Finite Control Unit]:::ctrlNode
    RULE[Transition Function Delta]:::ruleNode
    STATE[Current State]:::stateNode
    MOVE[Head Movement L R S]:::moveNode
    IN[Input String]:::ioNode
    OUT[Accept or Halt]:::ioNode

    TAPE -->|reads symbol| HEAD
    HEAD -->|symbol| CTRL
    CTRL -->|looks up| RULE
    STATE -->|current| CTRL
    RULE -->|next state| CTRL
    RULE -->|symbol to write| HEAD
    RULE -->|direction| MOVE
    MOVE -->|shifts| HEAD
    IN --> TAPE
    CTRL --> OUT

    classDef tapeNode fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef headNode fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrlNode fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ruleNode fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stateNode fill:#EDE9FE,stroke:#5B21B6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef moveNode fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ioNode fill:#F3F4F6,stroke:#374151,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Finite Control Unit** is where the 7-tuple logic resides. It receives the current symbol, consults $\delta$, and issues commands back to the head. The infinite tape is the only storage; this is what gives the TM its unbounded memory.

---

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram — TM for $a^n b^n$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> q0
    q0 --> q1: read a, write A, move R
    q1 --> q1: read a, write a, move R
    q1 --> q2: read b, write B, move L
    q2 --> q2: read a, write a, move L
    q2 --> q2: read A, write A, move L
    q2 --> q0: read blank, write blank, move R
    q0 --> q3: read B, write B, move R
    q3 --> q3: read B, write B, move R
    q3 --> q4: read blank, write blank, move R
    q4 --> [*]: ACCEPT
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow for Match-and-Cross Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Read first unmarked a] --> B[Mark it as A]
    B --> C[Move R past marked A's and unmarked a's]
    C --> D{Encounter unmarked b?}
    D -- No --> E[No matching b, REJECT]
    D -- Yes --> F[Mark the b as B]
    F --> G[Move L all the way to leftmost]
    G --> H{Any unmarked a remaining?}
    H -- Yes --> A
    H -- No --> I[Move R past all A's and B's]
    I --> J{Reach blank at end?}
    J -- Yes --> K[ACCEPT]
    J -- No --> L[Error: Unmatched symbols]
```

---

## 4.4 Architecture Comparison: PDA vs TM (KTU Concept Reinforcement)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PDA[Pushdown Automaton]
        PDA1[Input Tape] --> PDA2[Read Head - R Only]
        PDA2 --> PDA3[Stack - Limited Memory]
        PDA3 --> PDA4[Finite Control]
    end

    subgraph TM[Turing Machine]
        TM1[Input Tape - Infinite] --> TM2[Read Write Head - L and R]
        TM2 --> TM3[Finite Control]
    end

    style PDA fill:#FEF2F2,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:2px
    style TM fill:#ECFDF5,stroke:#065F46,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Comparison:** The PDA's stack provides LIFO memory; the TM's tape provides unbounded **random-access** memory. This is the structural reason why TMs recognize a strictly larger class of languages (recursively enumerable) than PDAs (context-free).

---

## 4.5 ID Transition Trace Diagram (For $aabb$ on $a^n b^n$ TM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ID0["q0 aabb"] -->|delta q0 a to A R| ID1["A q1 abb"]
    ID1 -->|delta q1 a to a R| ID2["A a q1 bb"]
    ID2 -->|delta q1 b to B L| ID3["A a q2 B b"]
    ID3 -->|delta q2 a to a L| ID4["A q2 a B b"]
    ID4 -->|delta q2 A to A L| ID5["q2 A a B b"]
    ID5 -->|delta q2 B to B R| ID6["A q0 a B b"]
    ID6 -->|delta q0 a to A R| ID7["A A q1 B b"]
    ID7 -->|delta q1 B to B R| ID8["A A B q1 b"]
    ID8 -->|delta q1 b to B L| ID9["A A q2 B B"]
    ID9 -->|rewind| ID10["A q2 A B B"]
    ID10 -->|delta q2 A to A L| ID11["q2 A A B B"]
    ID11 -->|delta q2 B to B R| ID12["A A q0 B B"]
    ID12 -->|delta q0 B to B R| ID13["A A B q3 B"]
    ID13 -->|delta q3 B to B R| ID14["A A B B q4"]
    ID14 --> ACCEPT[ACCEPTED]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram above shows the **complete computational trace** of the TM for input $aabb$. Each ID is a snapshot of (tape-left, state, tape-right). KTU examiners award marks for the *correct sequence* of such IDs.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a Turing Machine. List the components of a 7-tuple TM.**

**Model Answer:**
A Turing Machine (TM) is a 7-tuple mathematical model $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ that defines an abstract computational device with an infinite tape and a read/write head.

The seven components are:
1. **$Q$** — Finite set of states.
2. **$\Sigma$** — Input alphabet.
3. **$\Gamma$** — Tape alphabet ($\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$).
4. **$\delta$** — Transition function: $Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$.
5. **$q_0$** — Start state.
6. **$B$** — Blank symbol.
7. **$F$** — Set of accepting (final) states.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the difference between the language accepted by a TM and the language decided by a TM?**

**Model Answer:**
- A language $L$ is **accepted** by a TM $M$ if for every $w \in L$, $M$ eventually halts in a final state. For $w \notin L$, $M$ may either halt in a non-final state **or run forever**. Such languages are called **Recursively Enumerable (RE)**.
- A language $L$ is **decided** by a TM $M$ if for **every** input $w$ (whether in $L$ or not), $M$ halts. Such languages are called **Recursive (R) or Decidable**.

Hence: **$R \subseteq RE$**, and the inclusion is strict.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Design a Turing Machine that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Provide the formal 7-tuple specification, the transition function, and a state transition diagram. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Trace the computation of your TM for the input string $aabb$. Show at least 8 instantaneous descriptions. **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a):

**Formal Definition:**

$$
M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)
$$

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, A, B, B\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$
- $B = B$ (blank)
- $F = \{q_4\}$

**Transition Function $\delta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, A, R) \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_3, B, R) \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= (q_2, B, L) \\
\delta(q_1, B) &= (q_1, B, R) \\
\delta(q_2, a) &= (q_2, a, L) \\
\delta(q_2, A) &= (q_2, A, L) \\
\delta(q_2, B) &= (q_0, B, R) \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_3, B, R) \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_4, B, R)
\end{aligned}
$$

**State Transition Diagram:**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    q0 --> q1: a to A R
    q1 --> q1: a to a R
    q1 --> q2: b to B L
    q2 --> q2: a to a L
    q2 --> q2: A to A L
    q2 --> q0: B to B R
    q0 --> q3: B to B R
    q3 --> q3: B to B R
    q3 --> q4: B to B R
```

#### Solution to Q.A(b): Trace for $aabb$

| # | ID | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_0\ aabb$ | Start |
| 2 | $A\ q_1\ abb$ | $\delta(q_0, a) = (q_1, A, R)$ |
| 3 | $A\ a\ q_1\ bb$ | $\delta(q_1, a) = (q_1, a, R)$ |

| 13 | $q_2\ AAB B$ | $\delta(q_2, B) = (q_2, B, L)$ |
| 14 | $A\ q_0\ ABBB$ | Reset to rightmost |
| 15 | $A\ A\ q_0\ BBB$ | $\delta(q_0, B) = (q_3, B, R)$ |
