| 17 | $A\ A\ B\ B\ q_4$ | **ACCEPT** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Allocation Tip:** The 7-tuple carries 2 marks, the transition function/diagram carries 3 marks, and the trace carries 2 marks for Q.A(a). For Q.A(b), 1 mark per correct ID with up to 7 marks for full trace.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Construct a Turing Machine to accept the language $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Define the 7-tuple and write the transition rules. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Briefly explain the **Match-and-Cross** strategy used in TM construction. Why is it more powerful than the corresponding PDA construction? **\[7 Marks\]**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a):

**7-Tuple Definition:**

$$
M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)
$$

- $Q = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4, q_5, q_6, q_7\}$
- $\Sigma = \{a, b, c\}$
- $\Gamma = \{a, b, c, A, B, C, B\}$
- $q_0 = q_0$, Blank $= B$, $F = \{q_7\}$

**Transition Rules:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_1, A, R) \\
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_1, a, R) \\
\delta(q_1, b) &= (q_2, B, R) \\
\delta(q_2, b) &= (q_2, b, R) \\
\delta(q_2, c) &= (q_3, C, L) \\
\delta(q_3, a) &= (q_3, a, L) \\
\delta(q_3, b) &= (q_3, b, L) \\
\delta(q_3, A) &= (q_0, A, R) \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_5, B, R) \\
\delta(q_5, B) &= (q_5, B, R) \\
\delta(q_5, C) &= (q_6, C, R) \\
\delta(q_6, C) &= (q_6, C, R) \\
\delta(q_6, B) &= (q_7, B, R)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Strategy:** The TM enters 3 sub-cycles — for each `a` in the input, it marks one `b` and one `c`. After all are marked, it verifies and accepts.

---

#### Solution to Q.B(b):

**Match-and-Cross Strategy:**

The **Match-and-Cross** strategy is a systematic TM design technique with the following steps:

1. **Match Phase:** Identify the leftmost unmarked symbol of one type (e.g., `a`).
2. **Mark Phase:** Overwrite it with a unique marker (e.g., `A`).
3. **Search Phase:** Move the head right to find the corresponding unmatched symbol (e.g., `b`).
4. **Cross Phase:** Mark the matched symbol (e.g., `B`).
5. **Rewind Phase:** Return to the leftmost end.
6. **Repeat:** Continue until all symbols of the first type are exhausted.
7. **Verify:** Traverse the tape to check that all symbols of the second type are also marked.

**Why more powerful than PDA:**

| Feature | PDA | TM |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | Single LIFO Stack | Infinite Tape |
| Access | Top of stack only | Any cell (L and R) |
| Memory | Bounded by stack height | Unbounded |
| Languages | Context-Free | Recursively Enumerable |
| Re-wind | Not possible | Possible (head moves L) |

The PDA's stack limitation prevents it from remembering the **count** of `a`s once it has matched them with `b`s. The TM's tape allows the head to revisit and verify, enabling the recognition of **non-context-free** languages like $a^n b^n c^n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Takeaway:** A language is **Context-Free** if and only if it is accepted by some PDA. A language is **Recursively Enumerable** if and only if it is accepted by some TM. The strict containment $CFL \subset RE$ is proven by exhibiting a non-CFL (like $a^n b^n c^n$) that a TM can recognize but no PDA can.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in TM Construction Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Rewind Step:** Students often omit the "move head back to leftmost" rule, leaving the TM stranded in the middle. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **No Final Verification:** After marking all `a`s, students forget to scan for unmarked `b`s, accepting strings like $a^n b^{n+1}$. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Missing Transitions:** A single undefined $\delta$ entry causes the TM to crash. Examiners check at least 3-4 critical transitions. **Loss: 1-3 marks.**
> 4. **Confusing Blank $B$ with Marker $B$:** Using the same symbol for the blank and a marked `b` leads to ambiguity. **Always use distinct markers** (e.g., $Y$, $X$, or capitalized forms).
> 5. **No Start/Accept Arrow:** State diagrams must clearly mark the start state (with a "→") and the accept state (with a double circle). **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 6. **Direction Errors:** Writing $L$ instead of $R$ or vice versa in transitions. **Loss: 1-2 marks per error.**
> 7. **ID Notation Mistakes:** Writing the ID as $q a b b$ instead of $q_0 a a b b$ or $a a b b$ with the state misplaced. **Loss: 0.5-1 mark per ID.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Examples of Turing Machines (KTU Module 4):**

- **TM Formal Definition:** Always quote the 7-tuple $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$. Missing even one component loses marks.
- **Transition Format:** Standard form is $\delta(q_i, X) = (q_j, Y, D)$ where $D \in \{L, R\}$. Some texts allow $S$ (Stay) as a third direction.
- **ID Notation:** An instantaneous description has the form $\alpha_1 q \alpha_2$ — tape-left, state, tape-right.
- **Move Symbol:** $\vdash$ for one move; $\vdash^*$ for zero or more moves.
- **Three Primary Strategies to Memorize:**
  1. **Match-and-Cross** — for $a^n b^n$, $a^n b^n c^n$.
  2. **Shuffle / Two-Pass** — for $ww^R$, $w w^R$ palindromes.
  3. **Append-and-Rewind** — for unary functions like $f(n) = 2n$.
- **The Blank Symbol $B$** is *always* present on the tape beyond the input. It is the TM's terminator.
- **The Accepting Configuration** is $\alpha q_f \beta$ for any $\alpha, \beta$ and any $q_f \in F$. The TM halts as soon as it enters an accepting state.
- **Halting ≠ Accepting:** A TM can halt in a non-accepting state (reject) OR it may never halt (loop forever on inputs not in $L$).
- **Recursive vs RE:**
  - $L$ is **Recursive (R)** if some TM *halts on every input* and accepts exactly $L$.
  - $L$ is **Recursively Enumerable (RE)** if some TM *halts and accepts on inputs in $L$* but may loop on inputs not in $L$.
  - **Strict Containment:** $R \subset RE$. Example of RE-but-not-R language: the Halting Problem.
- **$a^n b^n$ TM uses 5 states** ($q_0, q_1, q_2, q_3, q_4$) — know this as a *benchmark*.
- **$a^n b^n c^n$ TM uses 8 states** — 3 for marking, 3 for rewinding, 2 for verification.
- **State Diagrams must include:**
  - Clear start state marker ($\rightarrow$).
  - Double circle for accept states.
  - Labels in the form `read / write, direction` (e.g., `a / A, R`).
- **Real-world link:** The TM is the ancestor of stored-program computers. Modern CPUs cycle through fetch-decode-execute, which is essentially TM's read-decide-write-move cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Design a TM means: design the states, design the tape symbols, design the moves, then verify with a trace."* Practice at least 5 TM constructions to internalize the workflow.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turing Machines as Language Acceptors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Turing Machines as Language Acceptors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Turing Machine

A **Turing Machine (TM)** is a 7-tuple formal computational model denoted as:

$$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$

Where each component carries a precise mathematical meaning:

| Component | Symbol | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Finite set of states | $Q$ | Control unit states (the "brain") |
| Input alphabet | $\Sigma$ | Symbols allowed in the input string |
| Tape alphabet | $\Gamma$ | All symbols writable on the tape ($\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$) |
| Transition function | $\delta$ | $Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ |
| Start state | $q_0 \in Q$ | Initial configuration state |
| Blank symbol | $B \in \Gamma \setminus \Sigma$ | Default empty cell marker |
| Final states | $F \subseteq Q$ | Accepting states (halting subset) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The Turing Machine is introduced here as the *most powerful* recognizer in the Chomsky hierarchy. It supersedes Pushdown Automata because it has **unlimited, random-access memory** via an infinite tape, making it capable of recognizing both **Recursive** and **Recursively Enumerable** languages.

### 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Endless Tape" Analogy

Imagine a tiny, obsessive librarian seated at a desk:

- The desk has an **infinite strip of paper** (the tape) running left and right, divided into tiny cells.
- The librarian can only read **one cell at a time** through a magnifying glass (the read/write head).
- After inspecting a cell, the librarian can: **(1)** erase what is written, **(2)** scribble a new symbol, and **(3)** shift the magnifying glass one cell **left** or **right**.
- The librarian's behavior is dictated by a strict **rulebook** (the transition function $\delta$) keyed to "what state I'm in" and "what I see on the current cell."
- If the librarian ever reaches a rule marked **"FINAL — STOP"**, the input string is **accepted**.

This librarian is the **Turing Machine**, and the infinite tape is precisely what makes it more powerful than any finite-state machine or pushdown automaton.

> [!NOTE]
> **Acceptance Semantics:** A string $w$ is *accepted* by a TM $M$ if, starting from the initial configuration $q_0 w$, the machine eventually enters a final state $q_f \in F$. The language $L(M)$ is the set of all such accepted strings.

### 1.3 Language Acceptance — Two Fundamental Notions

A TM can **accept** languages in two mathematically distinct ways:

1. **Acceptance by Final State (Standard):** $w \in L(M)$ iff $M$ enters some $q_f \in F$. Halting is *not* required — the machine may loop forever on rejected inputs.
2. **Acceptance by Halting (Total Computation):** $w \in L(M)$ iff $M$ halts (enters a configuration with no defined move). This is the stricter definition, used to define **Recursive (Decidable)** languages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decidability Distinction:**
> - **Recursively Enumerable (RE):** Accepted by *some* TM that halts on accepted inputs (may loop on rejected inputs).
> - **Recursive (Decidable):** Accepted by a TM that **always halts** on every input — accepted or rejected.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Transition Function — Operational Anatomy

The transition function $\delta$ is the heart of a TM. For a deterministic TM (DTM):

$$\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$$

This means: *given the current state and the tape symbol scanned, the machine deterministically (a) moves to a new state, (b) writes a new symbol, and (c) shifts the head left or right.*

For a **non-deterministic TM (NTM)**:

$$\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{(Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** By the *Church-Turing thesis* and the equivalence theorem, every NTM has an equivalent DTM. The NTM-DTM equivalence is a *favorite 7-mark derivation* in KTU boards.

### 2.2 Instantaneous Description (ID) — Snapshots of Computation

The **configuration** of a TM at any instant is captured by an **Instantaneous Description (ID)**. If the tape contains $X_1 X_2 \ldots X_{i-1} q X_{i+1} \ldots X_n$, this means:

- $X_1 X_2 \ldots X_n$ is the non-blank portion of the tape.
- $q$ is the current state.
- The head is scanning $X_i$ (the symbol immediately to the right of $q$).
- All cells beyond $X_n$ (and before the leftmost non-blank symbol) hold $B$.

A **move** of the TM is denoted by the *turnstile* operator $\vdash$ (or $\vdash_M$):

$$X_1 X_2 \ldots X_{i-1} \, q \, X_i X_{i+1} \ldots X_n \;\vdash\; X_1 X_2 \ldots X_{i-1} Y \, p \, X_{i+1} \ldots X_n \quad \text{(head moves right)}$$

$$X_1 X_2 \ldots X_{i-1} \, q \, X_i X_{i+1} \ldots X_n \;\vdash\; X_1 \ldots X_{i-2} \, p \, X_{i-1} Y X_{i+1} \ldots X_n \quad \text{(head moves left)}$$

These are valid only when $\delta(q, X_i) = (p, Y, R)$ or $\delta(q, X_i) = (p, Y, L)$ respectively.

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Definition Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formal Expression | Notes |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | TM Definition | $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ | Standard 7-tuple |
| 2 | DTM Transition | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ | Deterministic |
| 3 | NTM Transition | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{(Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\})}$ | Non-deterministic |
| 4 | Move Right (ID) | $u \, q \, a \, v \;\vdash\; u \, a \, p \, v$ if $\delta(q,a)=(p,b,R)$, then write $b$, not $a$ | Head shifts right |
| 5 | Move Left (ID) | $u \, q \, a \, v \;\vdash\; u \, p \, b \, v$ if $\delta(q,a)=(p,b,L)$ | Head shifts left |
| 6 | Language Accepted | $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^* \mid q_0 w \vdash^* \alpha q_f \beta,\; q_f \in F\}$ | Final-state acceptance |
| 7 | Recursive Language | $L$ is recursive iff $\exists$ TM that halts on **every** input | Always terminates |
| 8 | RE Language | $L$ is RE iff $\exists$ TM that halts on every $w \in L$ | May loop on $\overline{L}$ |
| 9 | Closure under Complement (Recursive) | If $L$ recursive, then $\overline{L}$ recursive | TM exists for both |
| 10 | Closure under Complement (RE) | RE languages are **NOT** closed under complement | Famous asymmetry |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Although the Turing Machine is a *theoretical* device, its **acceptance semantics** are foundational to:

- **Compiler Design:** Determining whether a source program is *syntactically* (and partially *semantically*) valid is precisely a language-acceptance problem.
- **Software Verification:** Model checking and formal verification tools use TM-equivalent decision procedures.
- **Decidability Analysis of Algorithms:** When we ask "Is there an algorithm to solve this problem?", we ask "Is the language of YES-instances Recursive?"
- **Operating Systems & Computability:** The famous *Halting Problem* (proven via diagonalization using TM acceptance) explains why no antivirus can perfectly detect all infinite loops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — TM that Accepts $L = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$

This is a **classic KTU 14-mark problem** testing a student's ability to design a Turing Machine for a non-context-free language. PDAs cannot recognize $a^n b^n c^n$, but a TM can.

#### 3.1.1 Informal Algorithm (Strategy)

The high-level strategy uses a **4-phase** cycle. We repeatedly scan the tape and mark one $a$, one $b$, and one $c$ in each pass, until all are marked.

| Phase | Action |
|-------|--------|
| 1 | Find the leftmost unmarked $a$, replace it with $A$, then traverse right to find an unmarked $b$, replace it with $B$, then continue right to find an unmarked $c$, replace it with $C$. Return to the start. |
| 2 | Repeat Phase 1 until no $a$ remains on the tape. |
| 3 | Scan right; if no $b$ or $c$ remains unmarked, accept. Otherwise, reject. |
| 4 | If at any point we expect a symbol and find the wrong one (e.g., $b$ before any $a$), halt without accepting. |

#### 3.1.2 Formal State Design (State Matrix)

The complete transition function $\delta$ is given in the matrix below. States are explained in the legend.

**State Legend:**
- $q_0$: start / restart after marking a triple.
- $q_1$: scanning right to find the first $a$.
- $q_2$: replacing leftmost $a$ with $A$.
- $q_3$: scanning right past $A$'s and $B$'s to find the first $b$.
- $q_4$: replacing $b$ with $B$.
- $q_5$: scanning right past $A$'s, $B$'s, and previously marked $C$'s to find the first $c$.
- $q_6$: replacing $c$ with $C$, then moving left to return to start.
- $q_7$: verifying no unmarked $b$ or $c$ remains — accept state.
- $q_r$: reject sink state (implicit trap for invalid inputs).

**Transition Function $\delta$ (Selected Critical Entries):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_1, a) &= (q_2, a, R) \\
\delta(q_2, a) &= (q_3, A, R) \\
\delta(q_3, b) &= (q_4, b, R) \\
\delta(q_4, b) &= (q_5, B, R) \\
\delta(q_5, c) &= (q_6, c, R) \\
\delta(q_6, C) &= (q_6, C, L) \\
\delta(q_6, B) &= (q_6, B, L) \\
\delta(q_6, A) &= (q_0, A, R) \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_7, B, R) \\
\delta(q_7, B) &= (q_7, B, R) \quad \text{(implicit — halts in final state)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> All other undefined transitions (e.g., $\delta(q_3, c)$ or $\delta(q_4, a)$) implicitly lead to the reject state $q_r$, ensuring that malformed inputs such as $aabbc$ or $aabbb$ are rejected.

#### 3.1.3 Trace on Input $w = aabbcc$

| Step | ID (Tape Snapshot) | Active State | Action |
|------|---------------------|--------------|--------|
| 0 | $q_0 \, aabbcc$ | $q_0$ | Start |
| 1 | $a \, q_1 \, abbcc$ | $q_1$ | Read $a$, move right |
| 2 | $a \, q_2 \, abbcc$ | $q_2$ | Read $a$, overwrite with $A$ |
| 3 | $aA \, q_3 \, bbcc$ | $q_3$ | Read $b$ |
| 4 | $aAb \, q_4 \, bcc$ | $q_4$ | Read $b$, overwrite with $B$ |
| 5 | $aAbB \, q_5 \, cc$ | $q_5$ | Read $c$ |
| 6 | $aAbBc \, q_6 \, c$ | $q_6$ | Read $c$, overwrite with $C$ |
| 7 | $aAbBcC \, q_6 \, B$ | $q_6$ | Move left over $C$ |
| 8 | $aAbBc \, q_6 \, CB$ | $q_6$ | Move left over $B$ |
| 9 | $aAbB \, q_6 \, CCB$ | $q_6$ | Continue left |
| 10 | $aAb \, q_6 \, BCC$ | $q_6$ | Continue left |
| 11 | $aA \, q_6 \, BBCC$ | $q_6$ | See $A$, switch to $q_0$ |
| 12 | $aAB \, q_0 \, BCC$ | $q_0$ | Restart — no more $a$'s found |
| 13 | $aABBCC \, q_0 \, B$ | $q_0$ | Reach $B$ — go to $q_7$ |
| 14 | $aABBCC \, B \, q_7$ | $q_7$ | **ACCEPT** |

The trace demonstrates that $M$ halts in the final state $q_7$ for $w = aabbcc$, confirming acceptance.

#### 3.1.4 Proof of Correctness (Logical Argument)

We argue that $L(M) = \{a^n b^n c^n \mid n \geq 1\}$.

**Part 1 — $L(M) \subseteq L$ (Soundness):**
Suppose $M$ accepts $w$. Each successful iteration of the cycle replaces exactly one $a$ with $A$, one $b$ with $B$, and one $c$ with $C$. For the machine to enter $q_7$, no $a$, $b$, or $c$ may remain unmarked. Hence the number of $a$'s, $b$'s, and $c$'s in $w$ are all equal to some $n \geq 1$, and the symbols must appear in the strict order $a^n b^n c^n$ (else the search for $b$ or $c$ would fail). Thus $w \in L$.

**Part 2 — $L \subseteq L(M)$ (Completeness):**
Given $w = a^n b^n c^n$, the TM repeatedly finds and marks the leftmost unmarked $a$, then a matching $b$, then a matching $c$, by sweeping rightward. After $n$ iterations, all symbols are converted to uppercase. The final sweep from $q_0$ reaches the blank $B$ with no unmarked symbols in between, transitioning to $q_7$ and accepting.

Hence $L(M) = L$. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common TM Design Patterns

| Language Class | Pattern | Sample Language | TM Complexity |
|----------------|---------|-----------------|---------------|
| $a^n b^n$ | Mark-leftmost-$a$, match-with-$b$, return | $\{a^n b^n\}$ | Linear in $n$ |
| $a^n b^n c^n$ | Nested triple marking (see above) | $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ | Quadratic |
| $ww$ (palindrome) | Match first/Last, then second/second-last | $\{w w^R\}$ | Linear |
| $w w$ (copy) | Mark first symbol, find matching symbol | $\{w \# w\}$ | Quadratic |
| $\{a^{2^n}\}$ | Repeatedly halve the gap between $a$'s | $\{a, aa, aaaa, \ldots\}$ | Non-trivial |
| $L = \Sigma^*$ | Two-state TM: $q_0$ reads anything → $q_f$ | All strings | Trivial |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Strategy:** When asked to "design a TM for $L$", always provide: **(1)** Informal algorithm in 3–5 lines, **(2)** State transition table, **(3)** A trace on at least one accepted input, and **(4)** A brief correctness sketch. This four-part structure aligns with the KTU valuation key's 14-mark breakdown.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture of a Turing Machine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CTRL["Finite Control (States Q)"]
        stateA["State q0: Start"]
        stateB["State q1: Scan Right"]
        stateC["State q2: Write/Shift"]
        stateF["State qf: Accept"]
    end
    subgraph TAPE["Infinite Tape (Cells indexed -inf ... +inf)"]
        cell0["Cell 0: a"]
        cell1["Cell 1: b"]
        cell2["Cell 2: c"]
        cell3["Cell 3: B"]
        cell4["Cell 4: B"]
        cell5["..."]
    end
    subgraph HEAD["Read/Write Head"]
        head1["Current Cell Indicator"]
    end
    CTRL -- "delta q, symbol" --> HEAD
    HEAD -- "reads symbol" --> CTRL
    HEAD -- "writes new symbol" --> TAPE
    TAPE -- "shifts L or R" --> HEAD
```

**Interpretation of the schematic:**
- The **Finite Control** is a finite set of states — the only "memory" of bounded size.
- The **Tape** is conceptually infinite in both directions, with blank symbol $B$ filling all unused cells.
- The **Read/Write Head** is the bridge: it reads from the tape, sends the symbol up to the control, and writes back based on the transition function $\delta$.

### 4.2 Configuration Transition Diagram (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    startState(["Initial ID: q0 w"])
    midState1["ID: x q y z (mid-computation)"]
    midState2["ID: x p y z (after one move right)"]
    midState3["ID: u q y z (after one move left)"]
    acceptState(["Final ID: alpha qf beta  (q_f in F)"])
    rejectState(["Non-Halting / Reject Path: infinite loop"])
    startState -- "delta q0, a = q, a, R" --> midState1
    midState1 -- "delta q, b = p, c, R" --> midState2
    midState1 -- "delta q, b = p, c, L" --> midState3
    midState2 -- "repeat moves" --> midState1
    midState3 -- "repeat moves" --> midState1
    midState1 -- "reaches qf in F" --> acceptState
    midState1 -- "no defined move OR trap" --> rejectState
```

### 4.3 State Diagram for the TM Accepting $a^n b^n c^n$

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    q0(("q0"))
    q1(("q1"))
    q2(("q2"))
    q3(("q3"))
    q4(("q4"))
    q5(("q5"))
    q6(("q6"))
    q7(("q7 - ACCEPT"))
    qr(("qr - REJECT"))
    q0 -- "a / a, R" --> q1
    q1 -- "a / a, R" --> q2
    q2 -- "a / A, R" --> q3
    q3 -- "b / b, R" --> q4
    q4 -- "b / B, R" --> q5
    q5 -- "c / c, R" --> q6
    q6 -- "A / A, R" --> q0
    q6 -- "B / B, L" --> q6
    q6 -- "C / C, L" --> q6
    q0 -- "B / B, R" --> q7
    q1 -- "B / B, R" --> qr
    q2 -- "B / B, R" --> qr
    q3 -- "a / a, R" --> qr
    q3 -- "c / c, R" --> qr
    q4 -- "a / a, R" --> qr
    q4 -- "c / c, R" --> qr
    q5 -- "a / a, R" --> qr
    q5 -- "b / b, R" --> qr
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to Read the Edge Labels:** Each label is in the format `read / write, direction`. For example, `a / a, R` means: "If the head reads an $a$, write $a$ back (no change) and move the head one cell to the Right." The reject transitions to $q_r$ are left implicit in the matrix above but shown explicitly in the diagram for completeness.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Define a Turing Machine. List all seven components of its formal definition.

**Model Answer:**
A Turing Machine (TM) is a 7-tuple formal model of computation, $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$, where:
1. $Q$ — finite set of states.
2. $\Sigma$ — finite input alphabet.
3. $\Gamma$ — finite tape alphabet ($\Sigma \subseteq \Gamma$).
4. $\delta$ — transition function.
5. $q_0 \in Q$ — start state.
6. $B \in \Gamma \setminus \Sigma$ — blank symbol.
7. $F \subseteq Q$ — set of final (accepting) states.

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** Distinguish between a Recursive language and a Recursively Enumerable language.

**Model Answer:**
A language $L$ is **Recursive** iff there exists a TM that *halts on every input*, accepting strings in $L$ and rejecting strings not in $L$. A language is **Recursively Enumerable (RE)** iff there exists a TM that halts and accepts every string in $L$, but may *loop forever* on strings not in $L$. Every Recursive language is RE, but not vice versa.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

#### **Question A** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

> Design a Turing Machine that accepts the language $L = \{a^n b^n \mid n \geq 1\}$. Provide the formal state transition table and a trace for the input $w = aaabbb$.

**(a)** Give the informal algorithm and state transition table. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Informal Algorithm:*
1. Start at the leftmost cell.
2. Find the leftmost unmarked $a$; if none exists, check that no $b$ remains; if so, accept.
3. Replace the leftmost $a$ with $A$.
4. Move right, skipping all $A$'s, to find the leftmost unmarked $b$.
5. Replace it with $B$.
6. Move left back to the leftmost cell and repeat from Step 2.
7. If at any point an unexpected symbol is encountered, reject.

*State Transition Table:*

| State | Read $a$ | Read $b$ | Read $A$ | Read $B$ | Read $B$ (blank context) |
|-------|----------|----------|----------|----------|---------------------------|
| $\to q_0$ | $(q_1, a, R)$ | — | — | — | — |
| $q_1$ | $(q_1, a, R)$ | — | — | — | — |
| $q_2$ | $(q_2, a, R)$ | — | — | $(q_2, A, R)$ | — |
| $q_2$ (after marking) | — | $(q_3, B, L)$ | — | — | — |
| $q_3$ | $(q_3, a, L)$ | — | $(q_3, A, L)$ | — | — |
| $q_3$ (left end) | — | — | — | — | $(q_0, A, R)$ |
| $q_0$ (verify) | — | — | — | — | $(q_f, B, R)$ |
| $q_f$ | — | — | — | — | $(q_f, B, R)$ |

**(b)** Trace the execution on $w = aaabbb$. **(7 marks)**

| Step | ID | State | Action |
|------|----|----|--------|
| 0 | $q_0 \, aaabbb$ | $q_0$ | Start |
| 1 | $a \, q_1 \, aabbb$ | $q_1$ | Skip $a$ |
| 2 | $aa \, q_1 \, abbb$ | $q_1$ | Skip $a$ |
| 3 | $aaa \, q_1 \, bbb$ | $q_1$ | See $b$ → enter $q_2$ |
| 4 | $aaa \, q_2 \, bbb$ | $q_2$ | Replace $b$ with $B$ |
| 5 | $aaaB \, q_3 \, bb$ | $q_3$ | Move left |
| 6 | $aaa \, q_3 \, Bbb$ | $q_3$ | Continue left |
| 7 | $aa \, q_3 \, aBbb$ | $q_3$ | Continue left |
| 8 | $a \, q_3 \, aaBbb$ | $q_3$ | Continue left |
| 9 | $q_3 \, aaaBbb$ | $q_3$ | Reach left end |
| 10 | $A \, q_0 \, aaBbb$ | $q_0$ | Mark leftmost $a$ as $A$ |
| 11 | $Aa \, q_1 \, aBbb$ | $q_1$ | Continue right |
| 12 | $Aaa \, q_1 \, Bbb$ | $q_1$ | See $B$ → enter $q_2$ |
| 13 | $AaaB \, q_2 \, bb$ | $q_2$ | Replace $b$ with $B$ |
| ... (similar pattern repeats for remaining $a$'s and $b$'s) | | | |
| Final | $AAABBB \, q_f$ | $q_f$ | **ACCEPT** |

---

#### **Question B** (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

> **(a)** State and explain the formal definition of the language accepted by a Turing Machine. Distinguish between acceptance by final state and acceptance by halting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **language accepted** by a TM $M$ is the set of all strings over $\Sigma$ for which there exists a sequence of moves from the initial ID to an ID containing a final state:

$$L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^* \mid q_0 w \vdash^*_M \alpha q_f \beta, \text{ for some } q_f \in F, \alpha, \beta \in \Gamma^*\}$$

The $\vdash^*$ notation means "zero or more moves of $M$."

**Acceptance by Final State** requires only that $M$ reaches some $q_f \in F$ at some point in its computation. The machine may continue computing indefinitely afterward (but typically the convention is that the machine halts in $q_f$).

**Acceptance by Halting** requires that $M$ reaches a configuration with *no defined move* (a "stuck" or "halted" state) that is also accepting. This is a stricter condition and corresponds to **Recursive** (decidable) languages.

| Criterion | Final State | Halting |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| Halts on accepted input? | Usually yes | Yes |
| Halts on rejected input? | Not required | **Required** |
| Defines language class | Recursively Enumerable | Recursive |
| RE complement closed? | **No** | Yes |

> **(b)** Construct a TM that accepts the language $L = \{wcw \mid w \in \{a, b\}^*\}$. Describe your design and provide a partial trace. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Algorithm:*
1. Start at the leftmost cell. The first symbol must be $a$ or $b$ (not $c$); otherwise reject.
2. Read the first symbol, remember it in the state (e.g., $q_a$ for $a$, $q_b$ for $b$), and replace it with $A$ or $B$ respectively.
3. Move right until the symbol $c$ is found; skip over it.
4. Continue right to find the rightmost unmarked symbol.
5. Verify it matches the remembered symbol. If so, replace it with the uppercase version; if not, reject.
6. Move left back to the cell just after the leftmost $A$ or $B$, and repeat.
7. When no $a$ or $b$ remains on the left of $c$, verify that no $a$ or $b$ remains unmarked on the right. If so, accept.

*Selected Transitions:*
$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, a) &= (q_a, A, R) \\
\delta(q_0, b) &= (q_b, B, R) \\
\delta(q_a, c) &= (q_{a,1}, c, R) \\
\delta(q_b, c) &= (q_{b,1}, c, R) \\
\delta(q_{a,1}, a) &= (q_{a,2}, A, L) \\
\delta(q_{a,1}, b) &= (\text{reject}) \\
\delta(q_{a,2}, c) &= (q_{a,2}, c, L) \\
\delta(q_{a,2}, A) &= (q_0, A, R) \\
\delta(q_0, c) &= (q_{\text{check}}, c, R)
\end{aligned}
$$

*Trace for $w = abcb$:*

| Step | ID | State | Action |
|------|----|----|--------|
| 0 | $q_0 \, abcb$ | $q_0$ | Start |
| 1 | $A \, q_a \, bcb$ | $q_a$ | Mark $a \to A$, remember $a$ |
| 2 | $Ab \, q_a \, cb$ | $q_a$ | Move right to $c$ |
| 3 | $Abc \, q_{a,1} \, b$ | $q_{a,1}$ | Skip $c$, look for matching $a$ |
| 4 | $Abcb \, q_{a,1}$ | $q_{a,1}$ | See $b$, but expected $a$ → **REJECT** |

This rejects because the input $abcb$ does **not** match the pattern $wcw$ (since $b \neq a$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the blank symbol handling:** Many students forget to define $\delta(q, B)$ transitions. The TM must explicitly deal with reaching the end of input.
> 2. **Skipping the "return to start" transitions:** A common error is marking one $a$ and one $b$ but not adding the transition that moves the head back to the leftmost cell for the next pass.
> 3. **Omitting the rejection of malformed inputs:** Students often design TMs that accept correctly but fail to define what happens for strings like $abb$, $baab$, or empty input. Always specify implicit reject transitions.
> 4. **Confusing TM acceptance with PDA acceptance:** TMs accept by *entering a final state*, not by *empty stack*. Mixing up these two acceptance modes is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 5. **Not providing a trace:** In KTU boards, a complete trace on at least one input string is mandatory for full 14 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Turing Machine** is the most powerful standard automaton in the Chomsky hierarchy, defined as a 7-tuple $(Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$.
- The **transition function** $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R\}$ specifies: *new state, new symbol, head movement*.
- An **Instantaneous Description (ID)** is a snapshot of the TM's tape, state, and head position; the move relation is denoted by $\vdash$ (single move) and $\vdash^*$ (zero or more moves).
- The **language accepted** by a TM $M$ is $L(M) = \{w \in \Sigma^* \mid q_0 w \vdash^* \alpha q_f \beta,\ q_f \in F\}$.
- **Recursive** languages are accepted by TMs that halt on **every** input.
- **Recursively Enumerable (RE)** languages are accepted by TMs that halt on accepted inputs but may **loop** on rejected inputs.
- Every **Recursive** language is also **RE**, but not vice versa — a foundational asymmetry of computability theory.
- **Closure facts:** Recursive languages are closed under complement; RE languages are **not** closed under complement.
- **Standard TM design strategy** for $\{a^n b^n c^n\}$ uses nested marking: replace one $a$, then one $b$, then one $c$ per cycle, returning to the start each time.
- **PDA cannot, but TM can:** Languages like $a^n b^n c^n$, $\{w w\}$, and $\{w w^R\}$ are non-context-free, yet are recognized by Turing Machines.
- **Church-Turing Thesis:** Any function computable by any algorithmic process is computable by some TM.
- **Halting Problem** is undecidable: no TM can, for arbitrary $M$ and $w$, correctly determine whether $M$ halts on $w$ — proved via diagonalization over the enumeration of TMs.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always pair a TM design question with: (1) informal strategy, (2) formal $\delta$ table, (3) a trace on sample input, and (4) a brief correctness argument. This four-part structure is the gold standard for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Turing Machines as Computers of Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Turing Machines as Computers of Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Turing Machine (TM)** is said to *compute a function* $f : \mathbb{N} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ (or $f : \mathbb{N} \times \mathbb{N} \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$) if, when started with a tape containing the input $n$ (or pair $(n, m)$) encoded in a standard format, it eventually **halts** with the output $f(n)$ (or $f(n, m)$) written on the tape in the same standard format.

Formally, a TM $M$ computes a partial function $f: \Sigma^{*} \rightarrow \Sigma^{*}$ if:

$$q_0 w \vdash^{*}_{M} q_f \; f(w)$$

where $q_0$ is the start state, $q_f$ is a unique **halting state**, and $\vdash^{*}$ denotes zero or more moves. If $M$ does not halt on $w$, then $f(w)$ is **undefined**.

### 1.1.1 Standard Input Number Representations (Encoding Schemes)

A natural number $n$ must be represented as a string on the tape. KTU 2024 mandates familiarity with **two standard encodings**:

| Encoding Scheme | Representation of $n$ | Symbol Used | Example: $n=3$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Unary Notation** | $0^{n}$ (or $1^n$) — $n$ repeated symbols | $0$ | $000$ |
| **Binary Notation** | Base-2 representation padded with $0$ | $0, 1$ | $011$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** In all KTU exam problems on function computation, the input is conventionally assumed to be in **unary form** $0^{n}$ unless the question explicitly states binary. For two arguments $(n, m)$, inputs are written as $0^{n} 1 \, 0^{m}$.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> **The "Mathematical Black Box" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine a **vending machine** that accepts tokens (your inputs $n, m$). You insert tokens in a specific arrangement — say, 5 blue chips followed by 1 red separator followed by 3 blue chips ($00000\,1\,000$). The machine has internal gears, tracks, and buttons (the **states** and **transitions**). When you press "Start" (begin computation), it whirrs, rearranges chips, removes some, adds some, and finally ejects an output tray containing, say, 8 blue chips ($00000000 = 8$).
>
> - The **input tray** = initial tape contents.
> - The **internal gears** = finite control (states + transition function).
> - The **moving arm** = read/write head.
> - The **output tray** = tape contents when the machine halts.
> - The **result it computes** = the function $f(n, m) = n + m = 8$.
>
> If the machine never stops spinning (an infinite loop), then the function is **partial** — defined only on inputs where it halts.

## 1.3 Why TMs as Function Computers Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility of TM-as-Function Model:**
> Every algorithm ever written — sorting, searching, encryption, even the operating system — is, in principle, a function from inputs to outputs. Showing that a TM can compute a function is **the strongest possible proof that the function is computable** (Church-Turing Thesis). This is the foundation of modern computability theory and compiler design.

**Key Characteristics Highlighted:**
- The TM is **deterministic** unless otherwise stated.
- Every move must be precisely defined (no undefined transitions on the valid input).
- A TM is a **computer of functions** $\iff$ it halts on every valid input with the correct output.
- A TM is a **computer of partial functions** $\iff$ it may or may not halt (e.g., Halting Problem is undecidable).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic

The process of designing a TM to compute a function $f(n, m)$ follows a structured four-phase pipeline:

### Phase 1 — Input Encoding (Pre-Processing Phase)
- Input is laid out on the tape as $0^{n} 1 \, 0^{m}$ (unary) or $\langle n \rangle \, 1 \, \langle m \rangle$ (binary).
- The **read/write head** is positioned at the leftmost cell containing the first input symbol.
- The TM enters its initial state $q_0$.

### Phase 2 — State-Based Transformation (Main Loop)
- The TM uses states as **"checkpoints"** in the algorithm.
- Each transition $\delta(q_i, X) = (q_j, Y, D)$ performs:
  - **Read** symbol $X$ under the head.
  - **Change state** from $q_i$ to $q_j$ (mark progress).
  - **Write** symbol $Y$ on the current cell.
  - **Move** the head one cell in direction $D \in \{L, R\}$ or stay ($S$).

### Phase 3 — Result Accumulation (Computation Phase)
- For arithmetic functions, the TM typically **erases one input** while **copying/appending** to a result section.
- Example for $f(n, m) = n + m$: erase each $0$ in $0^m$ and append it to the output segment.

### Phase 4 — Output Preparation & Halt (Post-Processing Phase)
- The TM repositions the head to the **leftmost cell of the output**.
- It enters the unique halting state $q_f$.
- The tape now reads $0^{f(n,m)}$ (or its binary equivalent).

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

| Step | **Why** it is needed | **How** it is implemented |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Erase input markers** | To free up tape cells for output | Write a blank symbol $B$ where input $0$ used to be |
| **Use $1$ as delimiter** | To separate the two operands $n$ and $m$ | The state changes when $1$ is read |
| **Return head to output start** | KTU requires output head at leftmost | Move head Left until $B$ is found, then Right one cell |
| **Halt in unique $q_f$** | To signal computation completion unambiguously | Reached only after all operands consumed |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Reference Table — TM Function Computation**

| Function Name | Mathematical Definition | Input Format (Unary) | Output Format | Key TM Strategy |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Successor** | $f(n) = n + 1$ | $0^n$ | $0^{n+1}$ | Append one $0$ at right end |
| **Constant Function** | $f(n) = c$ | $0^n$ | $0^c$ | Erase all input, write $c$ zeros |
| **Proper Subtraction** | $n \ominus m = \max(n-m, 0)$ | $0^n 1 0^m$ | $0^{\max(n-m,0)}$ | Pair-erasure with backtracking |
| **Addition** | $f(n,m) = n + m$ | $0^n 1 0^m$ | $0^{n+m}$ | Move $0$s past $1$ to end |
| **Multiplication** | $f(n,m) = n \times m$ | $0^n 1 0^m$ | $0^{nm}$ | Nested loop: copy block $m$ times |
| **Copy String** | $f(w) = ww$ | $w$ | $ww$ | Mark-and-copy with delimiter |
| **String Compare** | $f(u,v) = 1$ if $u=v$ | $u 1 v$ | $1$ or $0$ | Pairwise match test |

### 2.3.1 Boundary Conditions & Halt Conditions

- **Total Function:** $M$ halts on **every** input in the domain.
- **Partial Function:** $M$ halts on **some** inputs (others loop forever).
- **Halting Condition:** The TM enters state $q_f$ with output correctly placed.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Connection:** The principle that *every algorithm is a computable function* underlies the design of:
> - **CPU instruction sets** — each instruction computes a function on registers.
> - **Cryptographic hash functions** — implemented as TMs in theory, optimized as circuits in practice.
> - **Database query engines** — every SQL query computes a function from tables to result sets.
> - **Compiler optimization passes** — each pass transforms (computes a function on) intermediate code.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandatory Work-Out:** We will exhaustively construct TMs for the **four most frequently examined functions** in descending order of difficulty. Every transition, every state, every move is shown in full.

---

## 3.1 TM for Successor Function $f(n) = n + 1$

**Goal:** Convert $0^n$ on the tape to $0^{n+1}$.

**Strategy:**
1. Move right until the first blank $B$.
2. Write a $0$ there.
3. Return to leftmost cell and halt.

**Transition Function $\delta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) \quad &\text{[Skip leading zeros, move right]} \\
\delta(q_0, B) &= (q_1, 0, R) \quad &\text{[If input empty, write single 0]} \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) \quad &\text{[Keep moving right through zeros]} \\
\delta(q_1, B) &= (q_f, 0, R) \quad &\text{[Append new 0 at the end, then halt]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace for $n = 3$:** Input tape $= 000$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_0 000 &\vdash q_1 000 \quad \text{(read 0, move R)} \\
&\vdash q_1 000 \quad \text{(read 0, move R)} \\
&\vdash q_1 000 \quad \text{(read 0, move R)} \\
&\vdash q_f 0000 \quad \text{(read B, write 0, halt)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output:** $0000 = 0^{3+1}$ ✅

---

## 3.2 TM for Addition $f(n, m) = n + m$

**Goal:** Convert $0^n 1 0^m$ on the tape to $0^{n+m}$.

**Strategy:**
1. Move right past the separator $1$.
2. Replace each $0$ in the second operand with $B$ (erase).
3. At the rightmost end (first $B$ encountered after the last $0$), re-write $B$ as $0$.
4. This effectively **transfers** each $0$ from $m$-region to beyond $n$-region.

**Transition Function $\delta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_0, 0, R) \quad &\text{[Skip zeros in } n \text{ region]} \\
\delta(q_0, 1) &= (q_1, 1, R) \quad &\text{[Cross separator, enter } m \text{ region]} \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) \quad &\text{[Continue past } m \text{ region]} \\
\delta(q_1, B) &= (q_2, B, L) \quad &\text{[Hit end of tape, turn around]} \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= (q_2, 0, L) \quad &\text{[Move left through } m \text{ region]} \\
\delta(q_2, 1) &= (q_3, 0, R) \quad &\text{[Replace separator 1 with 0, move right]} \\
\delta(q_3, 0) &= (q_3, 0, R) \quad &\text{[Skip over already-transferred zeros]} \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_4, 0, R) \quad &\text{[Append final 0, halt preparation]} \\
\delta(q_4, 0) &= (q_4, 0, R) &\text{(cleanup)} \\
\delta(q_4, B) &= (q_f, B, R) \quad &\text{[Halt]}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace for $n = 2, m = 3$:** Input $= 00\,1\,000$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_0\,001000 &\vdash q_0\,001000 \vdash \cdots \vdash q_1\,001000 \\
&\vdash q_1\,001000 \vdash q_2\,00100B \\
&\vdash q_3\,00000B \\
&\vdash q_4\,00000B \\
&\vdash q_f\,000000B
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** State $q_2$ travels leftward through $0$'s until it hits $1$, replaces $1$ with $0$, and the loop repeats. Each pass **adds one $0$** to the output segment, ultimately producing $0^{n+m}$.

**Output:** $000000 = 0^5 = 0^{2+3}$ ✅

---

## 3.3 TM for Proper Subtraction $n \ominus m = \max(n - m, 0)$

**Goal:** Convert $0^n 1 0^m$ to $0^{\max(n-m, 0)}$.

**Strategy:**
1. For each $0$ in $m$-region, erase **one** $0$ from $n$-region and one $0$ from $m$-region.
2. If $m > n$, the result is empty (proper subtraction result is 0 zeros).
3. Replace the separator $1$ with $0$ to unify output.

**Transition Function $\delta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_1, X, R) \quad &\text{[Erase leftmost } n \text{-zero, mark as } X]} \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) \quad &\text{[Skip remaining } n \text{-zeros]} \\
\delta(q_1, 1) &= (q_2, 1, R) \quad &\text{[Cross separator to } m \text{-region]} \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= (q_3, Y, L) \quad &\text{[Erase one } m \text{-zero, mark as } Y]} \\
\delta(q_3, 1) &= (q_3, 1, L) \quad &\text{[Move left past separator]} \\
\delta(q_3, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \quad &\text{[Return to start of } n \text{-region, repeat]} \\
\delta(q_2, B) &= (q_4, B, L) \quad &\text{[m exhausted first → result empty handling]} \\
\delta(q_4, Y) &= (q_4, Y, L) \quad &\text{[Cleanup pass]} \\
\delta(q_4, 1) &= (q_4, 1, L) \quad &\text{[Continue cleanup]} \\
\delta(q_4, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \quad &\text{[Loop back]} \\
\delta(q_0, Y) &= (q_5, B, R) \quad &\text{[n exhausted first → clean Y's]} \\
\delta(q_5, Y) &= (q_5, B, R) \quad &\text{[Erase remaining Y's]} \\
\delta(q_5, 1) &= (q_5, B, R) \quad &\text{[Erase separator]} \\
\delta(q_5, B) &= (q_6, B, L) \quad &\text{[Begin unmarking X's]} \\
\delta(q_6, X) &= (q_6, 0, L) \quad &\text{[Convert X back to 0]} \\
\delta(q_6, B) &= (q_f, B, R) \quad &\text{[Halt at start of result]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic — Pair Erasure Principle:** Each iteration of the outer loop consumes **one** zero from each operand simultaneously. This is the canonical KTU pattern for subtraction. State $q_0$ initiates a new "round"; $q_4$ handles the case $n < m$ (result is empty); $q_5$ handles cleanup.

**Trace for $n = 3, m = 5$ (i.e., $3 \ominus 5 = 0$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_0\,000\,1\,00000 &\vdash q_1\,X00\,1\,00000 \\
&\vdash q_2\,X00\,1\,Y0000 \\
&\vdash q_3\,X00\,1\,Y0000 \\
&\vdash q_0\,X00\,1\,Y0000 \quad \text{(loop)} \\
&\vdots \\
&\vdash q_0\,XX\,1\,YYYY\quad \text{(after 3 rounds, n exhausted)} \\
&\vdash q_5\,BBB\,1\,BBBB \\
&\vdash q_6\,BBB\,1\,BBBB \\
&\vdash q_f\,BBB\,1\,BBBB
\end{aligned}
$$

**Output:** Empty tape (representing 0) ✅

---

## 3.4 TM for Multiplication $f(n, m) = n \times m$

**Goal:** Convert $0^n 1 0^m$ to $0^{n \cdot m}$.

**Strategy — Nested Loop:**
1. **Outer loop:** Repeatedly erase one $0$ from $m$-region.
2. **Inner loop:** For each erased $m$-zero, copy the entire $n$-region to the output.

**Simplified Transition Sketch:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_1, X, R) \quad &\text{[Mark } n \text{-zero]} \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) \quad &\text{[Skip to separator]} \\
\delta(q_1, 1) &= (q_2, 1, R) \quad &\text{[Enter } m \text{-region]} \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= (q_3, Y, R) \quad &\text{[Erase } m \text{-zero, mark Y]} \\
&\quad \vdots \quad &\text{[Copy all } n \text{-zeros (converted to 0') to output]} \\
\delta(q_3, \text{end}) &= (q_0, \text{end}, L) \quad &\text{[Return for next outer iteration]} \\
\delta(q_0, 1) &= (q_f, \ldots) \quad &\text{[When } m \text{ exhausted, clean up and halt]}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiplication requires a *nested* state pattern**, often using marked symbols (like $0'$ or $Z$) to distinguish "already-copied" zeros. This is the most complex construction in KTU Module 4.

---

## 3.5 Summary Comparison Matrix

| Function | Input | Output | # States (Approx.) | Complexity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Successor $n+1$ | $0^n$ | $0^{n+1}$ | $3$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ |
| Constant $c$ | $0^n$ | $0^c$ | $4$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| Addition $n+m$ | $0^n 1 0^m$ | $0^{n+m}$ | $5$ | $\mathcal{O}(n+m)$ |
| Proper Sub $n \ominus m$ | $0^n 1 0^m$ | $0^{\max(n-m,0)}$ | $7$ | $\mathcal{O}(nm)$ |
| Multiplication $nm$ | $0^n 1 0^m$ | $0^{nm}$ | $10+$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^2 m)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level TM-as-Function-Computer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputBox[/"Input Tape 0 to the power of n 1 0 to the power of m"/] --> stateInit[State q0]
    stateInit --> phase1[/"Phase 1 Read and Skip Zeros"/]
    phase1 --> phase2[/"Phase 2 Cross Separator 1"/]
    phase2 --> phase3[/"Phase 3 Modify or Erase Operands"/]
    phase3 --> loopCheck{All Operands Consumed?}
    loopCheck -- No --> phase3
    loopCheck -- Yes --> phase4[/"Phase 4 Unify Output and Clean Up"/]
    phase4 --> haltState[State qf Halting State]
    haltState --> outputBox[/"Output Tape 0 to the power of f n comma m"/]
```

---

## 4.2 TM State Transition Diagram — Successor Function $f(n) = n+1$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateQ0
    stateQ0 --> stateQ1 : Read 0, write 0, move R
    stateQ0 --> stateQ1 : Read B, write 0, move R
    stateQ1 --> stateQ1 : Read 0, write 0, move R
    stateQ1 --> stateQf : Read B, write 0, move R
    stateQf --> [*] : HALT
```

**Edge Legend (KTU Notation):**

| Transition | Notation | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $(q_i, X) \to (q_j, Y, R)$ | $\delta(q_i, X) = (q_j, Y, R)$ | Read $X$, write $Y$, move right |
| $(q_i, X) \to (q_j, Y, L)$ | $\delta(q_i, X) = (q_j, Y, L)$ | Read $X$, write $Y$, move left |
| $(q_i, X) \to (q_j, Y, S)$ | $\delta(q_i, X) = (q_j, Y, S)$ | Read $X$, write $Y$, stay |

---

## 4.3 TM State Transition Diagram — Proper Subtraction $n \ominus m$

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateSubQ0
    stateSubQ0 --> stateSubQ1 : Read 0, write X, move R
    stateSubQ1 --> stateSubQ1 : Read 0, write 0, move R
    stateSubQ1 --> stateSubQ2 : Read 1, write 1, move R
    stateSubQ2 --> stateSubQ3 : Read 0, write Y, move L
    stateSubQ3 --> stateSubQ3 : Read 1, write 1, move L
    stateSubQ3 --> stateSubQ0 : Read X, write X, move R
    stateSubQ0 --> stateSubQ5 : Read Y, write B, move R
    stateSubQ5 --> stateSubQ5 : Read Y, write B, move R
    stateSubQ5 --> stateSubQ5 : Read 1, write B, move R
    stateSubQ5 --> stateSubQ6 : Read B, write B, move L
    stateSubQ6 --> stateSubQ6 : Read X, write 0, move L
    stateSubQ6 --> stateSubSubF : Read B, write B, move R
    stateSubSubF --> [*] : HALT after proper subtraction
```

---

## 4.4 Function Family Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph arithmeticFamily["Arithmetic Functions Computable by TMs"]
        direction TB
        successorBlock["Successor Function f of n equals n plus 1"]
        constantBlock["Constant Function f of n equals c"]
        additionBlock["Addition Function f of n comma m equals n plus m"]
        subtractionBlock["Proper Subtraction n minus m equals max of n minus m comma 0"]
        multiplicationBlock["Multiplication Function f of n comma m equals n times m"]
    end

    subgraph stringFamily["String Functions Computable by TMs"]
        direction TB
        copyBlock["Copy Function f of w equals w w"]
        compareBlock["Equality Test f of u comma v equals 1 if u equals v else 0"]

    end

    stringFamily --> tmCore
    tmCore --> outputTape[Output on Tape]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Note (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Each full question in University Exam carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Part (a) typically for **7 marks** and Part (b) for **7 marks**. Short answer (Part A) questions carry **3 marks** each.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Turing Machine as a computer of functions.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A Turing Machine $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ is said to compute a (total) function $f: \Sigma^{*} \to \Sigma^{*}$ if for every input $w \in \Sigma^{*}$:

$$q_0 w \vdash^{*}_M \alpha \, q_f \, \beta \quad \text{with} \quad \alpha \beta = f(w)$$

where $q_f \in F$ is a halting state. If $M$ fails to halt on some $w$, then $f$ is a **partial** function computed by $M$.

> **Memorize the formal definition** — it carries 3 marks as a direct recall question.

---

### **Q2. Differentiate between total and partial functions computed by a TM.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Total Function | Partial Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Defined for all inputs in domain | Defined only on a subset of domain |
| **TM Behavior** | Halts on **every** input | Halts on **some** inputs, may loop on others |
| **Example** | Successor $f(n) = n+1$ | Halting predicate (uncomputable) |
| **Computability** | Recursive function | Recursively enumerable function |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (With Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Construction of Addition TM** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Design a Turing Machine that computes the **addition function** $f(n, m) = n + m$ where inputs are in unary form $0^n 1 0^m$. Provide the complete transition function $\delta$ and explain your strategy. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Trace the execution of your TM for the input $n = 2, m = 3$. Show at least 8 moves. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Input format:** $0^n 1 0^m$ on tape, head at leftmost cell.

**Strategy:**
- Skip over leading zeros of $n$.
- Cross the separator $1$.
- Move to the end of the $m$-region (first blank $B$).
- Erase one $0$ from $m$-region per loop and convert the separator $1$ to $0$.
- Repeat until all of $m$ is consumed.

**Transition Function $\delta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_0, 0, R) \\
\delta(q_0, 1) &= (q_1, 1, R) \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) \\
\delta(q_1, B) &= (q_2, B, L) \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= (q_2, 0, L) \\
\delta(q_2, 1) &= (q_3, 0, R) \\
\delta(q_3, 0) &= (q_3, 0, R) \\
\delta(q_3, B) &= (q_f, B, R)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Strategy explained:** State $q_0$ skips $n$'s zeros, $q_1$ skips $m$'s zeros to reach the blank, $q_2$ returns leftward to the separator, $q_3$ converts separator $1 \to 0$ and re-positions for next iteration.

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Initial tape:** $0\,0\,1\,0\,0\,0\quad (\text{i.e., } n=2, m=3)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_0\,001000 &\vdash q_0\,001000 \quad [\delta(q_0,0)=(q_0,0,R)] \\
&\vdash q_1\,001000 \quad [\delta(q_0,1)=(q_1,1,R)] \\
&\vdash q_1\,001000 \quad [\delta(q_1,0)=(q_1,0,R)] \\
&\vdash q_1\,001000 \quad [\delta(q_1,0)=(q_1,0,R)] \\
&\vdash q_2\,00100B \quad [\delta(q_1,B)=(q_2,B,L)] \\
&\vdash q_2\,00100B \quad [\delta(q_2,0)=(q_2,0,L)] \\
&\vdash q_3\,00000B \quad [\delta(q_2,1)=(q_3,0,R)] \\
&\vdash q_f\,00000B \quad [\delta(q_3,B)=(q_f,B,R)]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Output:** $00000 = 0^5 = 0^{2+3}$ ✅

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Construction of Proper Subtraction TM** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Design a Turing Machine to compute the **proper subtraction function** $n \ominus m = \max(n-m, 0)$. Clearly state the transition function $\delta$ for all required states. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Trace the execution of the TM for $n = 4, m = 2$. Show at least 10 moves. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Strategy (Pair Erasure):** For each $0$ in $m$-region, simultaneously erase one $0$ from $n$-region. The separator $1$ is replaced by $0$ upon completion. If $n$ exhausts before $m$, the result is empty (representing 0).

**Marking Symbols:** Use $X$ to mark erased $n$-zeros, $Y$ to mark erased $m$-zeros.

**Transition Function $\delta$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(q_0, 0) &= (q_1, X, R) \\
\delta(q_1, 0) &= (q_1, 0, R) \\
\delta(q_1, 1) &= (q_2, 1, R) \\
\delta(q_2, 0) &= (q_3, Y, L) \\
\delta(q_3, 1) &= (q_3, 1, L) \\
\delta(q_3, X) &= (q_0, X, R) \\
\delta(q_0, Y) &= (q_4, B, R) \\
\delta(q_4, Y) &= (q_4, B, R) \\
\delta(q_4, 1) &= (q_4, B, R) \\
\delta(q_4, X) &= (q_5, 0, R) \\
\delta(q_5, X) &= (q_5, 0, R) \\
\delta(q_5, B) &= (q_f, B, R)
\end{aligned}
$$

**State Purpose Table:**

| State | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| $q_0$ | Initiate a new round of pair-erasure |
| $q_1$ | Traverse $n$-region to separator |
| $q_2$ | Position on first $m$-zero |
| $q_3$ | Return to $n$-region via separator |
| $q_4$ | Cleanup: erase $Y$'s and separator (when $n < m$) |
| $q_5$ | Unmark $X$'s back to $0$ |

---

#### **Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Initial tape:** $0\,0\,0\,0\,1\,0\,0$ (i.e., $n=4, m=2$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_0\,0000100 &\vdash q_1\,X000100 \quad [\delta(q_0,0)=(q_1,X,R)] \\
&\vdash q_1\,X000100 \quad [\delta(q_1,0)=(q_1,0,R)] \\
&\vdash q_1\,X000100 \quad [\delta(q_1,0)=(q_1,0,R)] \\
&\vdash q_2\,X000100 \quad [\delta(q_1,1)=(q_2,1,R)] \\
&\vdash q_3\,X000Y00 \quad [\delta(q_2,0)=(q_3,Y,L)] \\
&\vdash q_3\,X000Y00 \quad [\delta(q_3,1)=(q_3,1,L)] \\
&\vdash q_0\,X000Y00 \quad [\delta(q_3,X)=(q_0,X,R)] \\
&\vdash q_1\,XX00Y00 \quad [\delta(q_0,0)=(q_1,X,R)] \\
&\vdash q_2\,XX00Y00 \quad [\delta(q_1,1)=(q_2,1,R)] \\
&\vdash q_3\,XX00YY0 \quad [\delta(q_2,0)=(q_3,Y,L)] \\
&\vdash q_3\,XX00YY0 \quad [\delta(q_3,1)=(q_3,1,L)] \\
&\vdash q_0\,XX00YY0 \quad [\delta(q_3,X)=(q_0,X,R)] \\
&\vdash q_4\,XX00BB0 \quad [\delta(q_0,Y)=(q_4,B,R)] \\
&\vdash q_4\,XX00BB0 \quad [\delta(q_4,Y)=(q_4,B,R)] \\
&\vdash q_4\,XX00BB0 \quad [\delta(q_4,1)=(q_4,B,R)] \\
&\vdash q_5\,XX00BB0 \quad [\delta(q_4,X)=(q_5,0,R)] \\
&\vdash q_5\,XX00BB0 \quad [\delta(q_5,X)=(q_5,0,R)] \\
&\vdash q_f\,XX00BB0 \quad [\delta(q_5,B)=(q_f,B,R)]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Output:** $XX00$ → after unmarking $X \to 0$ in a separate final pass (or included in $q_5$ logic), result is $0000 = 0^2 = 0^{4-2}$ ✅

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Missing Halting State Transition** — Students often forget to define $\delta$ for the **last state before $q_f$**. Always ensure the transition into $q_f$ is explicit.
> 2. **Wrong Input Encoding** — Mixing unary and binary conventions. **Default to unary** unless problem says otherwise.
> 3. **Head Position on Halt** — Examiners check whether head is at the **leftmost cell of output**. A correctly computed result with head at the wrong position loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Incomplete Trace** — Traces must show **every move** explicitly. Skipping intermediate states loses partial marks.
> 5. **Forgetting Edge Cases** — $n = 0$, $m = 0$, and $n = m$ cases must be addressed separately for full credit.
> 6. **Undefined Transitions** — Every valid $(q, X)$ pair must have a defined transition. Leaving gaps loses 1 mark per gap.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: TMs as Computers of Functions**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- [ ] TM computes **total function** $\iff$ halts on every input with correct output.
- [ ] TM computes **partial function** $\iff$ halts on subset; loops on rest.
- [ ] **Unary encoding** of $n$ is $0^n$; binary uses $\langle n \rangle$.
- [ ] Two-argument input format: $0^n 1 0^m$ (unary, KTU default).

### 🔑 Standard Function Patterns
- [ ] **Successor** $f(n) = n+1$: Append one $0$ at rightmost blank.
- [ ] **Addition** $n+m$: Convert separator $1 \to 0$ per iteration.
- [ ] **Proper Subtraction** $n \ominus m$: Pair-erasure with $X$, $Y$ markers.
- [ ] **Multiplication** $nm$: Nested loop using marked symbols.
- [ ] **Copy** $f(w) = ww$: Mark-and-recopy with delimiter.
- [ ] **String Compare**: Pairwise match with reject on mismatch.

### 🔑 Construction Principles
- [ ] Always define transitions for **every** $(q, X)$ pair used.
- [ ] Use **marking symbols** ($X$, $Y$, etc.) to remember processed regions.
- [ ] Final state $q_f$ must have **head positioned correctly** on output.
- [ ] Verify edge cases: $n = 0$, $m = 0$, $n = m$, $n < m$, $n > m$.

### 🔑 KTU Exam Strategy
- [ ] For 7-mark questions: provide $\delta$ table + 8-move trace + brief strategy note.
- [ ] For 14-mark questions: full TM design + complete trace + verification.
- [ ] Always **state the input encoding explicitly** in your solution.
- [ ] Memorize the transition tables for successor, addition, and proper subtraction — these appear every KTU cycle.

### 🔑 Pitfall Avoidance
- [ ] Never use $B$ (blank) as part of meaningful output — replace with $0$ before halting.
- [ ] Never leave the head past the output region — always return to leftmost.
- [ ] Never confuse **partial** with **total** functions in definitions.
- [ ] Never omit the cleanup/unmarking phase for proper subtraction.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Turing Machines as Computers of Functions**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme PCCST302 — Theory of Computation*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variants of Turing Machines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Variants of Turing Machines # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Turing Machine Variant A **Variant of a Turing Machine (TM)** is a modified computational model that extends the capabilities of the basic (standard) deterministic single-tape Turing Machine by adding extra tapes, heads, tracks, or non-determinism, while **retaining the same computational power** — i.e., recognizing exactly the same class of languages: the **recursively enumerable (RE) languages** $\mathcal{L}(\text{TM})$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** > The 2024 KTU syllabus lists these primary variants: > 1. **Multitrack Turing Machine** > 2. **Multitape Turing Machine** > 3. **Nondeterministic Turing Machine** > 4. **Universal Turing Machine** > 5. **Turing Machine as a transducer / Enumerator** Formally, a standard TM is the 7-tuple: $$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$ where each symbol retains its standard meaning as per Hopcroft, Ullman & Motwani's classical textbook presentation. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Carpenter's Workshop** Think of a **standard single-tape Turing Machine** as a **carpenter with one workbench, one ruler, and one pencil** — he can only measure and mark one piece of wood at a time. - A **Multitrack TM** = the same carpenter using a ruler with **multiple parallel scales** engraved on it (multiple independent tracks on one tape). - A **Multitape TM** = the carpenter now has **multiple workbenches**, each with its own measuring tool, working simultaneously. - A **Nondeterministic TM** = the carpenter has **multiple apprentices** exploring all possible cuts in parallel and reporting back the first successful one. - A **Universal TM** = a **master foreman** who reads written instructions (encoded programs) and tells other carpenters what to build — essentially a programmable general-purpose computer. - An **Enumerator** = a **printing press** that outputs strings belonging to a language one by one, without ever being given an input. Despite these seemingly different "toolkits," every variant computes **exactly the same class of languages** as the original single-tape deterministic TM — this is the **Church–Turing Thesis** in action. --- ## 1.3 Why Study Variants? — KTU Perspective > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Motivation:** > Studying TM variants serves two critical purposes in your B.Tech curriculum: > 1. **Theoretical:** Proves the **robustness of the TM model** — no matter what "hardware upgrade" you give the machine, the set of solvable problems does not grow. > 2. **Practical:** Bridges the gap between abstract automata and **real-world computation** (modern CPUs are essentially Universal TM implementations with finite memory). The fundamental result: **All these variants are equivalent in computational power to the standard TM**, although they may differ in **time/space complexity** (we do not measure that here in TOC). --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Variant 1 — Multitrack Turing Machine ### Definition A **k-track TM** has a single tape divided into **k parallel tracks**. Each cell of the tape now holds a **k-tuple of symbols** — one symbol from each track. The tape alphabet $\Gamma$ is structured as the Cartesian product of $k$ individual track alphabets: $$\Gamma = \Gamma_1 \times \Gamma_2 \times \dots \times \Gamma_k$$ A transition reads/writes a k-tuple atomically. ### Operational Logic 1. The read/write head reads a k-tuple at the current cell. 2. Based on the current state and the k-tuple, the transition function $\delta$ decides: - A new state, - A new k-tuple to write, - Head movement (L / R / S). 3. One move touches **all k tracks simultaneously**. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition:** A multitrack TM is just a regular TM with a richer alphabet. Since any finite alphabet can be re-encoded, **a k-track TM is equivalent to a standard TM**. --- ## 2.2 Variant 2 — Multitape Turing Machine ### Definition A **k-tape TM** possesses: - **k independent tapes**, each with its own read/write head. - A single finite control (state register). Formally: $$M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$$ where the transition function is: $$\delta : Q \times \Gamma^k \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma^k \times \{L, R, S\}^k$$ In a single move, $M$ reads k symbols (one per tape), writes k symbols, and moves k heads independently. ### Key Equivalence Theorem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem (Multitape TM ≡ Standard TM):** > Every language accepted by a multitape TM is also accepted by some standard single-tape TM, and vice versa. Hence **$\mathcal{L}(\text{Multitape-TM}) = \mathcal{L}(\text{TM})$**. The standard proof converts any $k$-tape TM into an equivalent single-tape TM by using **$2k$ tracks** to simulate the $k$ tapes using a "virtual tape with marked head positions." This is elaborated in Section 3. --- ## 2.3 Variant 3 — Nondeterministic Turing Machine (NTM) ### Definition An NTM has the transition function: $$\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow \mathcal{P}(Q \times \Gamma \times \{L, R, S\})$$ i.e., for a given state and tape symbol, **multiple possible moves** exist (a finite set). ### Acceptance Criterion A string $w$ is accepted if **there exists at least one computation path** (branch) that leads to an accepting state. Rejection means **all paths reject or run forever**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem (Determinism vs. Nondeterminism for TMs):** > Every NTM $N$ has an equivalent deterministic TM $D$ such that $L(N) = L(D)$. > > **Proof Sketch:** $D$ simulates all branches of $N$'s computation tree using a **BFS (breadth-first search)** strategy on a 3-tape simulation TM. This is the most heavily tested topic in KTU Module 4. --- ## 2.4 Variant 4 — Universal Turing Machine (UTM) ### Definition A **Universal TM** $U$ takes two inputs: 1. An **encoding** $\langle M \rangle$ of any TM $M$ (using a fixed binary or string encoding scheme). 2. An **input string** $w$. It then **simulates** $M$ on $w$. Formally: $$L(U) = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w \}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Historical Note:** Proposed by Alan Turing (1936). The UTM is the **theoretical blueprint of the modern stored-program computer (von Neumann architecture).** Every program running on your laptop is being interpreted by a UTM-style abstract machine. ### Encoding Convention States, tape symbols, and transitions of $M$ are encoded as binary strings and concatenated with delimiters like `111`. The transition function $\delta(q_i, X_j) = (q_k, X_l, D_m)$ becomes a fixed codeword. --- ## 2.5 Variant 5 — TM as a Transducer & Enumerator ### TM as a Transducer A TM used as a transducer halts with output on its tape after processing input. The output is a function $f : \Sigma^* \rightarrow \Sigma^*$. > Example: A TM that computes $f(w) = ww$ (doubles the input) using a 2-tape construction. ### Enumerator An **Enumerator** $E$ is a TM with **no input tape** — instead it has an output-only "printer." It generates (enumerates) strings of a language one by one, separated by a delimiter (typically `#$). The language of $E$ is the set of all strings it ever prints: $$L(E) = \{ w \in \Sigma^* \mid E \text{ eventually prints } w \}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Theorem (Enumerator Equivalence):** > A language $L$ is recursively enumerable **iff** there exists an enumerator that prints exactly the strings of $L$ (possibly with duplicates and in any order). > > Equivalently: **$L$ is r.e. $\iff$ $L$ is accepted by some TM.** --- ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Variant | Key Equation / Definition | Acceptance Condition | Equivalent to Std. TM? | |---|---------|---------------------------|----------------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Standard TM** | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma \times \{L,R\}$ | $w \in L$ iff some path reaches $q_f$ | — (Baseline) | | 2 | **Multitrack TM** | $\Gamma = \Gamma_1 \times \dots \times \Gamma_k$ | Same as standard TM | **Yes** | | 3 | **Multitape TM** | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma^k \rightarrow Q \times \Gamma^k \times \{L,R,S\}^k$ | $\exists$ computation leading to accept | **Yes** | | 4 | **Nondeterministic TM** | $\delta : Q \times \Gamma \rightarrow 2^{Q \times \Gamma \times \{L,R,S\}}$ | $\exists$ accepting branch | **Yes** | | 5 | **Universal TM** | $L(U) = \{\langle M, w\rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w\}$ | Simulation halts in $q_f$ | **Yes (decides $A_{TM}$)** | | 6 | **Enumerator** | No input; prints $w_i$ on output tape | $L(E) = \{ w \mid E \text{ prints } w\}$ | **Yes (r.e. languages)** | | Key Constants & Symbols | Meaning | |--------------------------|---------| | $\mathcal{L}(\text{TM})$ | Class of recursively enumerable languages | | $\langle M \rangle$ | String encoding of TM $M$ | | $2^{Q}$ | Power set of $Q$ (set of subsets of states) | | $B$ | Blank symbol | | $\{L, R, S\}$ | Left, Right, Stay moves | --- ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter beyond exams?** > - **Compiler Design:** A real CPU is a Universal TM with finite memory (a *Real RAM machine*). > - **Decidability:** Variants prove that decidability/undecidability are **invariant under model changes** — this is why the **Halting Problem** remains undecidable on *any* of these machines. > - **Complexity Theory:** Although computationally equivalent, multitape TMs give **different time bounds** — leading to the polynomial-time equivalence that defines the class $\mathbf{P}$ (single-tape) vs. $\mathbf{P}$ (multitape) — both same class but different constants! > - **VLSI / Hardware Verification:** TM encodings underpin model-checking tools (SPIN, NuSMV). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Major Proof: Multitape TM → Single-Tape TM Equivalence This is the **most important KTU derivation** for Module 4. Let us prove: > **Theorem:** If $L$ is accepted by a $k$-tape TM $M$, then there exists a single-tape TM $M'$ that also accepts $L$. ### Construction Strategy We build $M'$ using a **single tape with $2k$ tracks**: - For each of the $k$ virtual tapes of $M$, we use **two tracks**: - **Upper track** of pair $i$: stores the actual tape contents of tape $i$ of $M$. - **Lower track** of pair $i$: stores a single **head marker** (a special symbol, say $X_i$ for head $i$) placed in the cell currently scanned by head $i$ of tape $i$ of $M$. > [!NOTE] > Conceptually: imagine writing all $k$ tapes stacked vertically, then dotting the head position with a marker below each line. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Initial Tape Setup** Initially, $M'$ receives input $w = a_1 a_2 \dots a_n$ on its single tape. $M'$: 1. Places $w$ on track 1 (upper track of pair 1) of its tape. 2. Writes the head marker $X_1$ under the **leftmost** cell of $w$. 3. Writes blanks everywhere else. 4. Records $k-1$ new blank virtual tapes by writing head markers $X_2, X_3, \dots, X_k$ just to the right of $X_1$. **Step 2 — Simulating One Move of $M$** To simulate one move of the multitape TM, $M'$ must: (a) **Sweep Right** — Visit all $k$ head markers in order (left to right). As $M'$ crosses each marker, it reads the tape symbol (upper track) at that head and the **state** is updated to remember this tuple of $k$ symbols in its finite control. Since $M'$ has finite control, it can remember a tuple of $k$ symbols in its state — this works because $k$ is finite and the tape alphabet is finite. (b) **Second Sweep Right** — Now $M'$ knows exactly what move $\delta$ of $M$ dictates (because it has read all $k$ symbols at the $k$ head positions). It sweeps right again, and at each head marker: - Rewrites the upper-track symbol with the new symbol dictated by $\delta$. - Moves the head marker one cell left, right, or keeps it (deleting the old marker and placing a new one in the new cell). - Records its updated internal state to remember whether it is on the $i$-th marker. (c) **Final Check** — If during the first sweep $M'$ ever discovers that two virtual head markers occupy the **same cell**, it must interleave them — this requires a 4-track modification (store the symbol above and below to distinguish the two heads). This complication is handled by using $2k$ tracks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why does this work?** > Each move of $M$ takes $O(n)$ time on $M'$ (where $n$ is the length of the tape visited). For a TM running in $T(n)$ steps, $M'$ runs in $O(T(n)^2)$ — polynomial slowdown. Crucially, **accept/reject is preserved**. **Step 3 — Acceptance** If $M$ ever enters an accepting state $q_f$, then during simulation $M'$ also enters $q_f$ and halts, accepting the input. Similarly, if $M$ halts without accepting, so does $M'$. $\blacksquare$ ### Explicit Mathematical Form of the Simulation Let $M = (Q, \Sigma, \Gamma, \delta, q_0, B, F)$ be a 2-tape TM for simplicity. Define $M' = (Q', \Sigma, \Gamma', \delta', q'_0, B, F')$ where: $$\Gamma' = \Gamma \times \Gamma \times \{B, X_1, X_2\}$$ The state set $Q'$ encodes the state of $M$ plus a marker of progress: $$Q' = Q \times \{1, 2, \dots, k\} \cup \{q'_0\}$$ The transition $\delta'$ is defined piecewise for the three phases: **Phase 1 — Right sweep (Phase 1 collects tape symbols):** $$\delta'(q, [a_1, a_2, m]) = (q', [a_1, a_2, m'], R)$$ where $m, m' \in \{B, X_1, X_2\}$ are head markers; $q'$ remembers the symbols read so far. **Phase 2 — Right sweep rewriting:** $$\delta'(q, [b_1, b_2, X_i]) = (q', [c_1, c_2, X_i'], R)$$ where $(q', c_i, D_i)$ is determined by the original $\delta(q, b_1, b_2)$, and $X_i'$ reflects whether the head moved (i.e., we shift the marker one cell). **Phase 3 — Return to leftmost head:** The TM sweeps back to the leftmost marker to begin the next simulation step. This construction is fully mechanical and forms the basis of the KTU 14-mark expected question. --- ## 3.2 Equivalence Analytical Matrix | From Variant | To Variant | Construction | Time Slowdown | |--------------|------------|--------------|---------------| | Multitrack TM → Std TM | Rename $\Gamma_1 \times \dots \times \Gamma_k$ as a single alphabet | None (constant factor) | | Multitape TM → Std TM | $2k$-track single tape with head markers | $O(T(n)^2)$ | | NTM → DTM (Multitape) | 3-tape DTM with BFS over computation tree | Exponential ($2^{O(T(n))}$) | | Std TM → NTM | Make $\delta$ a singleton-valued function | $O(T(n))$ | | Std TM → UTM | Encode $M$, simulate on input tape | Polynomial overhead | | TM → Enumerator | Run TM on all $w \in \Sigma^*$ in dovetailing | May never halt | | Enumerator → TM | Interleave simulation with input check | Deterministic r.e. simulation | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — Acceptance of $L = \{w\#w \mid w \in \{0,1\}^*\}$ on 2-Tape TM > [!NOTE] > **Classic KTU-Favorite Problem:** **Problem:** Design a 2-tape TM for $L = \{w\#w \mid w \in \{0,1\}^*\}$ and argue its equivalence to a 1-tape TM. **2-Tape Algorithm:** 1. **Copy phase:** Copy $w$ from tape 1 to tape 2. 2. **Match phase:** Move tape 1 head to the cell after `#`. Move tape 2 head back to the start. 3. **Compare phase:** Simultaneously move both heads right. At each step, check that the symbols match. If mismatch → reject. If both reach blank together → accept. **Reduction to 1-tape TM:** Apply the $2k$-track construction with $k=2$, giving a 4-track single-tape TM. The simulation runs in $O(n^2)$ for an $n$-cell string — quadratic slowdown, still polynomial. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Multitape TM Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CTRL["FINITE CONTROL Q"] ST[("State q in Q")] end subgraph T1["TAPE 1"] D1["... B B [a1] a2 a3 ... B B ..."] H1["Head 1"] end subgraph T2["TAPE k-1"] D2["... B B [b1] b2 b3 ... B B ..."] H2["Head 2"] end subgraph TK["TAPE k"] DK["... B B [c1] c2 c3 ... B B ..."] HK["Head k"] end CTRL <-->|delta| T1 CTRL <-->|delta| T2 CTRL <-->|delta| TK style CTRL fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5 style T1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00 style T2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00 style TK fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00 ``` **Caption:** A k-tape TM with shared finite control. Each tape has its own independent head. The transition function $\delta$ reads k symbols simultaneously and writes k symbols + moves k heads per step. --- ## 4.2 Reduction Flow — Multitape TM to Single-Tape TM (2k-Track) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["START: k-tape TM M
on input w"] --> B["Construct M'
with 2k tracks"] B --> C["Phase A:
Place w on Track 1
Set head markers X1...Xk"] C --> D["Phase B:
Sweep RIGHT
Read all k symbols"] D --> E["Phase C:
Apply delta of M
Decide new symbols and moves"] E --> F["Phase D:
Sweep RIGHT again
Update symbols
Shift head markers"] F --> G{"Did M reach
accept state?"} G -->|Yes| H["M' ACCEPTS"] G -->|No| I{"More moves
to simulate?"} I -->|Yes| D I -->|No| J["M' REJECTS / LOOPS"] style A fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828 style B fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style C fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style D fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825 style E fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825 style F fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825 style G fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style H fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 style I fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style J fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C ``` **Caption:** Equivalence reduction algorithm. The 2k-track single-tape TM $M'$ faithfully simulates every move of $M$ with $O(n)$ overhead per simulated step. --- ## 4.3 NTM → DTM Equivalence (BFS over Computation Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["NTM N
on input w"] --> B["Construct DTM D
3-tape design"] B --> C["Tape 1: Input w (read-only)"] B --> D["Tape 2: Simulation tape
stores current branch"] B --> E["Tape 3: Address tape
stores next branch to try"] C --> F["Initialize address 1"] F --> G["Simulate branch i
of N on Tape 2"] G --> H{"Branch accepts?"} H -->|Yes| I["D ACCEPTS w"] H -->|No| J["Increment address
on Tape 3"] J --> K{"More addresses
to explore?"} K -->|Yes| G K -->|No| L["D REJECTS w"] style A fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828 style B fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style C fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00838F style D fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00 style E fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F style F fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A style G fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825 style H fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style I fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20 style J fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#F9A825 style K fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#2E7D32 style L fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C ``` **Caption:** Deterministic simulation of an NTM via breadth-first exploration of the computation tree. This 3-tape DTM is then reducible to a single-tape TM by the multitape-to-single-tape theorem. --- ## 4.4 Universal TM (UTM) Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph IN["INPUT TAPE of U"] E1["Encoding of TM M
e.g., 111 0 11 ... "] E2["Separator: 111111"] E3["Input string w"] end subgraph CTRL["UTM Finite Control"] P1["Parse phase:
Decode M from input"] P2["Execute phase:
Simulate M on w"] P3["Output phase:
Accept or Reject"] end subgraph WT["WORK TAPE of U"] W1["Current state of M"] W2["Current tape of M (simulated)"] W3["Current head position"] end IN --> CTRL CTRL <--> WT style IN fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0 style CTRL fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#EF6C00 style WT fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A ``` **Caption:** The Universal TM architecture — a stored-program interpreter. This is the theoretical ancestor of every modern CPU + firmware + operating system combination. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a Universal Turing Machine? How does it differ from a standard TM?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Universal Turing Machine (UTM)** is a TM $U$ that takes an encoding $\langle M \rangle$ of any arbitrary TM $M$ together with an input string $w$, and simulates $M$'s execution on $w$.
