$$H(\langle M, w \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } M \text{ accepts } w \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } M \text{ does not accept } w \end{cases}$$

We will construct a TM that contradicts this assumption.

**Step 2 — Construct the Diagonal Machine $D$.**

Using $H$ as a subroutine, we build a new TM $D$ that takes a single input $\langle M \rangle$ (the encoding of some TM) and behaves as follows:

$$D(\langle M \rangle):$$
1. Run $H$ on the input $\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle$.
2. If $H$ accepts, then **reject**.
3. If $H$ rejects, then **accept**.

In formal mathematical notation:

$$D(\langle M \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } H(\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle) = \text{reject} \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } H(\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle) = \text{accept} \end{cases}$$

Equivalently, $D$ **flips** the output of $H$ on self-input.

**Step 3 — Examine $D(\langle D \rangle)$ — the self-reference step.**

Now run $D$ on its *own* description, $\langle D \rangle$. By the construction:

$$D(\langle D \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } H(\langle D, \langle D \rangle \rangle) = \text{reject} \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } H(\langle D, \langle D \rangle \rangle) = \text{accept} \end{cases}$$

Substituting the definition of $H$ (since $H$ is assumed to decide $A_{TM}$):

$$D(\langle D \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } D \text{ does not accept } \langle D \rangle \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } D \text{ accepts } \langle D \rangle \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Derive the Contradiction.**

Let $x = D(\langle D \rangle) \in \{\text{accept}, \text{reject}\}$. From the case analysis above:

- If $x = \text{accept}$: then by the first line, $D$ does *not* accept $\langle D \rangle$, i.e., $x = \text{reject}$. **Contradiction.**
- If $x = \text{reject}$: then by the second line, $D$ *does* accept $\langle D \rangle$, i.e., $x = \text{accept}$. **Contradiction.**

In both cases we reach a logical contradiction. The only escape is that our initial assumption (existence of $H$) was false. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,A_{TM} \text{ is undecidable.}\,\,}$$

**Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Theorem — The Halting Problem $HALT_{TM}$ is Undecidable

> **Theorem:** $HALT_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM and } M \text{ halts on } w\}$ is undecidable.

### Proof by Reduction from $A_{TM}$ (Sketch — KTU marks this at 7 points)

**Step 1 — Suppose $HALT_{TM}$ is decidable.** Let $R$ be the decider.

**Step 2 — Build $S$ that decides $A_{TM}$:**

$$S(\langle M, w \rangle):$$
1. Run $R$ on $\langle M, w \rangle$.
2. If $R$ rejects (i.e., $M$ does not halt on $w$), **reject**.
3. If $R$ accepts, simulate $M$ on $w$ until it halts.
4. If $M$ accepted, **accept**; if $M$ rejected, **reject**.

**Step 3 — $S$ is a decider for $A_{TM}$**, contradicting Theorem 4.11. Therefore $HALT_{TM}$ is undecidable. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.3 Cardinality Argument: More Languages than TMs

| Step | Statement | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | The set of all TMs is countable. | Every TM has a finite binary encoding, so $\mathcal{M} = \bigcup_{k=0}^{\infty} \{0,1\}^k$ is a countable union of finite sets. |
| 2 | The set of all languages over $\Sigma$ is $2^{\Sigma^*}$. | A language is any subset of $\Sigma^*$, hence an element of the power set. |
| 3 | $\vert 2^{\Sigma^*} \vert > \vert \Sigma^* \vert = \aleph_0$. | Cantor's theorem: for any set $S$, $\vert 2^S \vert > \vert S \vert$. |
| 4 | Therefore there are **more languages than Turing Machines**. | Strict inequality from Step 3. |
| 5 | **Corollary:** *Most* languages are non-computable. | Only countably many languages can be decided; the rest (uncountably many) cannot. |

> [!NOTE]
> This cardinality argument is *not* a substitute for the diagonalization proof of Theorem 4.11. It is a *weaker* statement — it tells us undecidable languages *exist* but does not exhibit one. Diagonalization is what *exhibits* $A_{TM}$ as a specific undecidable language.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: At a Glance

| Theorem | Technique | Result | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Theorem 4.11 ($A_{TM}$ undecidable) | Diagonalization | $A_{TM} \notin \mathbf{R}$ (not recursive) | **Very High** (asked almost every year) |
| $HALT_{TM}$ undecidable | Reduction from $A_{TM}$ | $HALT_{TM} \notin \mathbf{R}$ | **High** |
| $\vert \text{TMs} \vert = \aleph_0$ | Encoding argument | Countable | Medium |
| $\vert \text{Languages} \vert = 2^{\aleph_0}$ | Power set | Uncountable | Medium |
| Cantor's Theorem | Diagonalization | $\vert 2^S \vert > \vert S \vert$ | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level UTM Architecture (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the three-tape UTM construction, the central object of Section 2.2.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input["INPUT TAPE\ncontains ⟨M,w⟩"] --> stage1
    subgraph stage1 ["STAGE 1: LOAD AND DECODE"]
        codebook["TAPE 1 - CODEBOOK\nstores ⟨M⟩ permanently"]
        decoder["DECODER MODULE\nreads transition δ from ⟨M⟩"]
        codebook --> decoder
    end
    subgraph stage2 ["STAGE 2: SIMULATE ONE STEP OF M"]
        stateTape["TAPE 3 - STATE\nholds current state q_i"]
        simTape["TAPE 2 - SIMULATED TAPE\nholds current tape of M"]
        headCtrl["HEAD CONTROL\nmoves L or R"]
        simTape --> headCtrl
        stateTape --> headCtrl
    end
    subgraph stage3 ["STAGE 3: TERMINATE OR LOOP"]
        acceptCheck{"q_i = q_accept?"}
        rejectCheck{"q_i = q_reject?"}
        acceptOut["ACCEPT - halt UTM"]
        rejectOut["REJECT - halt UTM"]
        loopBack["go to STAGE 2 - next step"]
    end
    decoder --> stateTape
    decoder --> simTape
    headCtrl --> acceptCheck
    acceptCheck -- yes --> acceptOut
    acceptCheck -- no --> rejectCheck
    rejectCheck -- yes --> rejectOut
    rejectCheck -- no --> loopBack
    loopBack --> decoder
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** A real arrow `--> ` indicates a *data flow*. The `loopBack` arrow returning to the decoder represents the iterative simulation of one TM step at a time — the UTM keeps running until $M$ halts in either $q_{accept}$ or $q_{reject}$. The UTM itself halts exactly when $M$ does.

---

## 4.2 Diagonalization Visualization (TM Acceptance Table)

The following flowchart captures the *self-reference paradox* that drives the proof of Theorem 4.11. The diagonal entries (where row $i$ = column $i$) are the **targets** that the new machine $D$ "flips."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph listTM ["LIST OF ALL TURING MACHINES M_1, M_2, M_3, ..."]
        m1["M_1"]
        m2["M_2"]
        m3["M_3"]
        m4["M_4"]
        mdots["..."]
    end
    subgraph inputList ["INPUTS ⟨M_1⟩ ⟨M_2⟩ ⟨M_3⟩ ⟨M_4⟩ ..."]
        i1["⟨M_1⟩"]
        i2["⟨M_2⟩"]
        i3["⟨M_3⟩"]
        i4["⟨M_4⟩"]
        idots["..."]
    end
    subgraph diag ["DIAGONAL ENTRIES (self-input)"]
        d1["M_1 on ⟨M_1⟩"]
        d2["M_2 on ⟨M_2⟩"]
        d3["M_3 on ⟨M_3⟩"]
        d4["M_4 on ⟨M_4⟩"]
        ddots["..."]
    end
    subgraph flipD ["DIAGONAL MACHINE D"]
        d["D flips the diagonal entry of any M_i when run on ⟨M_i⟩"]
    end
    m1 --> d1
    m2 --> d2
    m3 --> d3
    m4 --> d4
    mdots --> ddots
    i1 --> d1
    i2 --> d2
    i3 --> d3
    i4 --> d4
    idots --> ddots
    d1 --> flipD
    d2 --> flipD
    d3 --> flipD
    d4 --> flipD
    ddots --> flipD
    flipD -- "D is not equal to any M_i" --> paradox["CONTRADICTION:\nD must equal some M_k\nbut D differs from M_k\nat input ⟨M_k⟩"]
```

---

## 4.3 Cantor's Real-Number Diagonalization (Adapted Version)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    stepA["STEP 1: Assume reals in 0,1 are countable"]
    stepB["STEP 2: List them as r_1, r_2, r_3, ..."]
    stepC["STEP 3: Write decimal expansions in a 2D table"]
    stepD["STEP 4: Construct d by reading the DIAGONAL\nand picking a digit different from the i-th digit of r_i"]
    stepE["STEP 5: d is a real in 0,1 that is NOT in the list"]
    stepF["CONTRADICTION - the list was supposed to contain ALL reals"]
    stepA --> stepB --> stepC --> stepD --> stepE --> stepF
```

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exams:** If the question is worth 14 marks, you are expected to draw *one* of the above diagrams (or a similar ASCII table) as part of your answer. If it is 3 marks, skip the diagram and state the contradiction in one or two crisp sentences.

---

## 4.4 Functional Flow: How the UTM Simulates One Step

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Read current state q_i from Tape 3"] --> B["Read current symbol a_j from Tape 2"]
    B --> C["Scan Tape 1 for transition delta q_i a_j"]
    C --> D{"Transition found?"}
    D -- "no" --> E["REJECT - halt UTM"]
    D -- "yes" --> F["Write new symbol b_k on Tape 2"]
    F --> G["Move Tape 2 head L or R per direction D"]
    G --> H["Update Tape 3 to new state q_m"]
    H --> I{"q_m is accepting?"}
    I -- "yes" --> J["ACCEPT - halt UTM"]
    I -- "no" --> K["Go back to step A - simulate next step"]
```

This block diagram is **directly** what the UTM does at every iteration. It is a useful revision tool before exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Q:** What is a Universal Turing Machine? State the language decided by the UTM.

**Model Answer:**

A **Universal Turing Machine (UTM)** is a Turing Machine that can simulate the operation of any other Turing Machine. It takes as input an *encoding* of a TM $M$ (denoted $\langle M \rangle$) and an input string $w$, and simulates $M$ on $w$ step by step.

The language recognized by the UTM is:

$$L(U_T) = A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM and } M \text{ accepts } w\}$$

The UTM is significant because it demonstrates that a *single fixed* machine can execute *every* algorithm, given its description — a foundational result underlying the modern stored-program computer. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q:** What is diagonalization? Name the problem whose undecidability is proved using this technique.

**Model Answer:**

**Diagonalization** is a proof technique originally developed by Georg Cantor to show that the real numbers are uncountable. The technique constructs a *new* object (number / language / machine) that differs from every object in a given list at the position determined by the object's *own* index in the list — the "diagonal" entry.

Alan Turing adapted this technique in 1936 to prove that the **Acceptance Problem** $A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w\}$ is **undecidable**. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-Internal Choice Rule:** Each Part B question gives you an *internal choice*. You must answer EITHER (a) OR (b) for full marks. We present two complete questions with sub-parts and full model solutions below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 — Module 4, Compulsory Module Question]**

**Q:** *Using diagonalization, prove that $A_{TM}$ is undecidable. Clearly state your assumptions, the construction of the diagonal machine $D$, and the resulting contradiction.*

#### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: Constructing the Diagonal Machine

**Model Answer:**

We want to show that the language $A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM and } M \text{ accepts } w\}$ is undecidable.

**Assumption for contradiction:** Suppose $A_{TM}$ is decidable. Then there exists a decider $H$ that halts on every input and behaves as:

$$H(\langle M, w \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } M \text{ accepts } w \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } M \text{ does not accept } w \end{cases}$$

**Construction of Diagonal Machine $D$:** Using $H$ as a subroutine, we build a new TM $D$:

$D(\langle M \rangle)$:
1. Run $H$ on $\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle$.
2. If $H$ accepts, then **reject**.
3. If $H$ rejects, then **accept**.

Formally:

$$D(\langle M \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } H(\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle) = \text{reject} \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } H(\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle) = \text{accept} \end{cases}$$

$D$ is well-defined because $H$ always halts by assumption. (7 marks)

#### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: Self-Reference and Contradiction

**Model Answer:**

Now consider running $D$ on its own description $\langle D \rangle$:

$$D(\langle D \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } H(\langle D, \langle D \rangle \rangle) = \text{reject} \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } H(\langle D, \langle D \rangle \rangle) = \text{accept} \end{cases}$$

By the definition of $H$ (decider for $A_{TM}$):

$$D(\langle D \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } D \text{ does not accept } \langle D \rangle \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } D \text{ accepts } \langle D \rangle \end{cases}$$

**Deriving the contradiction:**

- **Case 1:** Suppose $D(\langle D \rangle) = \text{accept}$. Then by the first line, $D$ does *not* accept $\langle D \rangle$, i.e., $D(\langle D \rangle) = \text{reject}$. **Contradiction.**
- **Case 2:** Suppose $D(\langle D \rangle) = \text{reject}$. Then by the second line, $D$ *does* accept $\langle D \rangle$, i.e., $D(\langle D \rangle) = \text{accept}$. **Contradiction.**

In both cases we obtain a logical contradiction. Hence our initial assumption (the existence of $H$) must be false.

**Conclusion:** No TM decides $A_{TM}$. $\therefore A_{TM}$ is undecidable. $\blacksquare$ (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #1 — "Decider" vs "Recogniser":** Many students confuse $A_{TM}$ being *undecidable* (no decider exists) with $A_{TM}$ being *unrecognizable*. **$A_{TM}$ IS Turing-recognizable** — the UTM is a recogniser for it. The theorem only says it has *no decider*. Writing "unrecognisable" will cost you 2–3 marks.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Supplementary Exam — Module 4 Alternative Choice]**

**Q:** *Describe the construction of a Universal Turing Machine. Explain how it simulates an arbitrary TM $M$ on input $w$. Discuss its time complexity.*

#### Sub-Part (a) — 7 Marks: Three-Tape UTM Construction

**Model Answer:**

A **Universal Turing Machine (UTM)** is a TM that, given $\langle M, w \rangle$, simulates $M$ on $w$. We construct it with **three tapes**:

**Tape 1 — Codebook Tape (read-only)**
- Initially contains $\langle M \rangle$, the full encoded description of $M$.
- The UTM scans this tape each step to retrieve the transition rule $\delta(q_i, a_j) = (q_k, b_l, D_m)$ for the current state and symbol.

**Tape 2 — Simulated Tape**
- Initially contains $w$ (the input to $M$).
- Modified in lock-step with $M$'s tape: write $b_l$, move head Left or Right per $D_m$.

**Tape 3 — State Tape**
- Initially contains the start state $q_1$ of $M$.
- Updated to $q_k$ after every transition.

**Simulation Loop:**
1. Read current state $q_i$ from Tape 3 and current symbol $a_j$ from Tape 2.
2. Scan Tape 1 to find the transition $\delta(q_i, a_j)$.
3. Write $b_l$ on Tape 2 and move Tape 2's head per $D_m$.
4. Replace $q_i$ with $q_k$ on Tape 3.
5. If $q_k = q_{accept}$, halt and accept. If $q_k = q_{reject}$, halt and reject. Else, loop to step 1.

The UTM is itself a TM, hence a finite object, but it can simulate *any* TM. (7 marks)

#### Sub-Part (b) — 7 Marks: Single-Tape Reduction and Complexity

**Model Answer:**

**Single-Tape UTM:** We can combine the three tapes into one by encoding all three with separator symbols:
- $\langle M, w \rangle$ is padded to the right with delimiters.
- The simulated tape and state are placed in distinct "regions" marked by special markers.

The single-tape UTM correctly simulates $M$ but is slower because the head must shuttle between regions.

**Time Complexity:**

Let $T(n)$ be the number of steps $M$ takes on input $w$ of length $n$. Then:

- **Multi-tape UTM:** simulates $M$ in $O(T(n))$ time.
- **Single-tape UTM:** simulates $M$ in $O(T(n) \log T(n))$ time.

The logarithmic overhead comes from searching Tape 1 for the relevant transition rule at each step — a binary search over $\langle M \rangle$ takes $O(\log \vert \langle M \rangle \vert) = O(\log T(n))$ time per step (since $\vert \langle M \rangle \vert$ is proportional to $T(n)$).

**Significance:** This construction shows that the UTM is a **polynomial-time** simulator of any TM, which is the technical foundation of the *Extended Church–Turing Thesis*:

> Any "reasonable" model of computation can simulate a TM with at most polynomial overhead.

This is why all standard computational models (RAM machines, lambda calculus, Python, etc.) are equivalent up to polynomial time — they all *are* UTMs in disguise. (7 marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall #2 — Forgetting $\langle M \rangle$ is finite:** A common error is to claim the UTM has "infinitely many" states because it can simulate infinitely many TMs. **The UTM is a fixed finite TM** — only its *input* $\langle M, w \rangle$ varies. This is the very essence of "universal" and is worth 2 marks on its own.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Summary Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mistakes Students Make in This Module (and How to Avoid Them)**
>
> 1. **Confusing undecidable with unrecognizable** — $A_{TM}$ is *undecidable* but *recognizable*. Only "weird" languages like $A_{TM}^{c}$ (the complement) are unrecognizable.
> 2. **Skipping the "halts" justification** — when constructing $D$, you must explain that $D$ halts because $H$ is a *decider* (halts on all inputs). Otherwise the contradiction is invalid.
> 3. **Writing $D(\langle D \rangle)$ ambiguously** — always explicitly state the case analysis; examiners want to see both cases.
> 4. **Mixing up $A_{TM}$ and $HALT_{TM}$** — $A_{TM}$ asks if $M$ *accepts* $w$; $HALT_{TM}$ asks if $M$ *halts* on $w$ (accepts OR rejects).
> 5. **Forgetting the encoding delimiter** — $\langle M \rangle$ must have a clear end-marker; otherwise the UTM cannot parse it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam**

**Universal Turing Machine (UTM)**

- $\square$ The UTM is a **single fixed TM** that can simulate **any other TM** by reading its encoded description.
- $\square$ The UTM takes input of the form $\langle M, w \rangle = \langle M \rangle \, 111 \, w$.
- $\square$ The UTM's language is $A_{TM}$ — the acceptance problem.
- $\square$ The UTM exists in both **3-tape** ($O(T(n))$ time) and **single-tape** ($O(T(n) \log T(n))$ time) variants.
- $\square$ The UTM is the theoretical ancestor of the **stored-program computer** and **virtual machines** (JVM, CLR).
- $\square$ The UTM uses $\langle M \rangle$ as a "codebook" and reads it repeatedly — one transition per step.

**Diagonalization**

- $\square$ Diagonalization is a **proof-by-contradiction** technique, not an algorithm.
- $\square$ The four canonical steps are: **(1)** assume $A_{TM}$ decidable with decider $H$, **(2)** construct diagonal machine $D$ that flips $H$'s output, **(3)** run $D$ on $\langle D \rangle$, **(4)** derive contradiction.
- $\square$ The diagonal machine $D$ is defined by $D(\langle M \rangle) = \overline{H(\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle)}$.
- $\square$ The contradiction arises because $D(\langle D \rangle) = \overline{D(\langle D \rangle)}$ — a logical impossibility.
- $\square$ Diagonalization proves $A_{TM}$ is undecidable; reductions from $A_{TM}$ then prove $HALT_{TM}$, $E_{TM}$, $EQ_{TM}$, $REGULAR_{TM}$, $A_{LBA}$, etc. undecidable.

**Cardinalities**

- $\square$ $\vert \text{TMs} \vert = \aleph_0$ (countable) — every TM has a finite binary encoding.
- $\square$ $\vert \text{Languages over } \Sigma \vert = 2^{\aleph_0}$ (uncountable).
- $\square$ Most languages are *non-computable* — diagonalization shows the strict gap.

**Key Formulas (Must Memorize)**

- $\square$ $D(\langle M \rangle) = \text{accept} \iff H(\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle) = \text{reject}$
- $\square$ $A_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w\}$
- $\square$ $HALT_{TM} = \{\langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\}$
- $\square$ Single-tape UTM time: $O(T(n) \log T(n))$
- $\square$ Multi-tape UTM time: $O(T(n))$

**Common Traps**

- $\square$ A decider **must halt** on every input; a recogniser may loop.
- $\square$ "Undecidable" $\neq$ "unrecognizable."
- $\square$ The diagonal argument is about *undecidability*, not *non-regularity* (that needs the Pumping Lemma).
- $\square$ Always write the **case analysis** explicitly — never skip it.

**Engineering / Real-World Connection**

- $\square$ JVM / CLR / Python Interpreter = a physical UTM.
- $\square$ Virus detection impossibility = Cohen's theorem (analogue of $A_{TM}$).
- $\square$ No general "bug-finder" = Rice's theorem (Module 5 follow-up).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reductions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Reductions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Reduction

In the **Theory of Computation**, a **Reduction** is a formal technique used to transform one decision problem into another such that a solution to the second problem can be leveraged to solve the first. More precisely, given two languages $A$ and $B$, a reduction from $A$ to $B$ is a computable function $f : \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$ such that:

$$w \in A \iff f(w) \in B$$

If such a function $f$ exists, we say that **$A$ is reducible to $B$** and write:

$$A \leq_m B \quad \text{(many-one / mapping reducibility)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A reduction is a method of converting one problem $A$ into another problem $B$ in such a way that a decider for $B$ can be used to construct a decider for $A$. If $A$ is reducible to $B$ and $A$ is undecidable, then $B$ is also undecidable.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **medical doctor** who has never seen disease $X$, but you know exactly how to diagnose and treat disease $Y$. If a brilliant researcher discovers that **disease $X$ is just a "renamed" version of disease $Y$** (i.e., a known, proven transformation exists), then you automatically have a cure for $X$ — without ever studying $X$ directly.

**The "transformation function" $f$ is the researcher's proven link.**
- $f$ converts an instance of $X$ into an instance of $Y$.
- If $Y$ is solvable, $X$ becomes solvable "for free."

In the **Theory of Computation**:
- If $A \leq_m B$ and $B$ is **decidable**, then $A$ is also **decidable**.
- If $A \leq_m B$, $A$ is **undecidable**, and $B$ is **not decidable** (reduction by contradiction).
- If $A \leq_m B$, and $B$ is **not Turing-recognizable**, then $A$ is also **not Turing-recognizable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Reductions are the *primary tool* used in Module 4 to prove the undecidability of well-known problems (like $A_{TM}$, $HALT_{TM}$, $EQ_{TM}$, $REGULAR_{TM}$, $E_{TM}$, etc.) by leveraging the proven undecidability of the **Halting Problem** ($A_{TM}$).

## 1.3 Three Core Computational Classes (Quick Reminder)

A language $L$ falls into one of three mutually exclusive categories, and reductions help us classify them:

| Class | Description | Decider Exists? | Recognizer Exists? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Decidable (Recursive)** | $L \in \mathcal{R}$ | Yes | Yes |
| **Recognizable but not Decidable (r.e.)** | $L \in \mathcal{RE} \setminus \mathcal{R}$ | No | Yes |
| **Co-Recognizable but not Decidable** | $\overline{L} \in \mathcal{RE} \setminus \mathcal{R}$ | No | Yes (for $\overline{L}$) |
| **Non-Recognizable (Non-r.e.)** | $L \notin \mathcal{RE}$ | No | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A language is **decidable** if and only if both it *and* its complement are Turing-recognizable.

## 1.4 Why Are Reductions Important in KTU?

Reductions are the **engine of undecidability proofs**. Without them, every undecidability proof would have to start from first principles. Reductions allow us to:
1. **Build a hierarchy of hardness** among problems.
2. **Transfer decidability** from one problem to another.
3. **Transfer undecidability** to prove new problems are undecidable.

The most important reductions you must master for KTU are:
- Reduction of $A_{TM}$ to $HALT_{TM}$
- Reduction of $A_{TM}$ to $E_{TM}$ (and $REGULAR_{TM}$, $EQ_{TM}$)
- Reduction via **Computation History** (used for $A_{TM} \leq_m PCP$, $A_{TM} \leq_m A_{LBA}$)
- **Rice's Theorem** (shortcut for proving language-properties are undecidable)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Reductions

There are several flavors of reduction in the Theory of Computation. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **Mapping Reducibility** and **Computation History Reductions**.

### 2.1.1 Mapping Reducibility (Many-One Reducibility) — $A \leq_m B$

A language $A$ is **mapping reducible** to $B$ if there exists a **computable function** $f$ such that:

$$w \in A \iff f(w) \in B$$

- $f$ must be a **total computable function** (must halt on every input).
- $f$ is implemented by a **Turing machine $F$** (the "reducer").
- The decider for $B$ is a **black-box Oracle Turing Machine** (denoted as $B$ inside the construction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Oracle Turing Machine Notation:** In KTU notation, the TM $F^{B}$ denotes a Turing machine that has access to an oracle (decider) for $B$. The superscript represents the oracle.

### 2.1.2 Computation History Reduction

Used when reducing $A_{TM}$ to a problem about *all* computations of a TM. A **computation history** of TM $M$ on input $w$ is the sequence of configurations:

$$C_1, C_2, C_3, \dots, C_k$$

where $C_1 = q_0 w$ (start configuration), $C_k$ is an accepting or rejecting configuration, and each $C_{i+1}$ follows from $C_i$ by the transition function $\delta$ of $M$.

- An **accepting history** ends in an accepting configuration $q_{accept}$.
- A **rejecting history** ends in a rejecting configuration $q_{reject}$.

### 2.1.3 Turing Reducibility — $A \leq_T B$

A more general form where the reducer TM $F$ is allowed to query the oracle for $B$ **multiple times** during its computation. The output is binary (accept/reject) but $f$ is not necessarily a simple mapping.

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024, **focus on mapping reducibility** as it is the most heavily tested form.

## 2.2 The Decidability / Undecidability Transfer Theorems

These are the **most important theorems** for your KTU exam. Memorize them as a unit.

| Statement | Formal Implication |
|---|---|
| $A \leq_m B$ and $B$ is **decidable** | $\Rightarrow A$ is **decidable** |
| $A \leq_m B$ and $A$ is **undecidable** | $\Rightarrow B$ is **undecidable** |
| $A \leq_m B$ and $B$ is **Turing-recognizable** | $\Rightarrow A$ is **Turing-recognizable** |
| $A \leq_m B$ and $A$ is **not Turing-recognizable** | $\Rightarrow B$ is **not Turing-recognizable** |
| $A \leq_m B$ and $B \leq_m C$ | $\Rightarrow A \leq_m C$ (transitivity) |
| $A \leq_m B$ and $\overline{A} \leq_m \overline{B}$ | **Equivalence under mapping reduction** |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Property / Theorem | Formal Statement | Usage in KTU |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A_{TM}$ Undecidability | $A_{TM} = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM that accepts } w \}$ | **Foundational hard problem** |
| 2 | $HALT_{TM}$ Undecidable | $HALT_{TM} = \{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w \}$ | Proved via $A_{TM} \leq_m HALT_{TM}$ |
| 3 | $E_{TM}$ Undecidable | $E_{TM} = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid L(M) = \emptyset \}$ | Proved via $A_{TM} \leq_m E_{TM}$ |
| 4 | $REGULAR_{TM}$ Undecidable | $\{ \langle M \rangle \mid L(M) \text{ is regular} \}$ | Proved via $A_{TM} \leq_m REGULAR_{TM}$ |
| 5 | $EQ_{TM}$ Undecidable | $\{ \langle M_1, M_2 \rangle \mid L(M_1) = L(M_2) \}$ | Proved via $E_{TM} \leq_m EQ_{TM}$ |
| 6 | $A_{LBA}$ Decidable | $\{ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is LBA accepting } w \}$ | **Decidable**; LBA = Linear Bounded Automaton |
| 7 | $E_{LBA}$ Undecidable | $\{ \langle M \rangle \mid M \text{ is LBA}, L(M) = \emptyset \}$ | Proved via $A_{TM} \leq_m E_{LBA}$ (history) |
| 8 | $PCP$ Undecidable | Post Correspondence Problem | Proved via $A_{TM} \leq_m PCP$ |
| 9 | Rice's Theorem | Any non-trivial property of r.e. languages is undecidable | **Shortcut** for proving undecidability |
| 10 | Complementation | $A \in \mathcal{R} \iff A \in \mathcal{RE} \text{ and } \overline{A} \in \mathcal{RE}$ | Decidability ↔ r.e. + co-r.e. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Condition:** A function $f$ used in a mapping reduction **MUST** be computable (realizable as a Turing machine) and **TOTAL** — i.e., it must halt on *every* input $w \in \Sigma^*$. A non-halting $f$ is not a valid reducer.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Reductions

In modern software engineering, reductions manifest in multiple practical forms:
- **Compilers:** Reducing a high-level program to a lower-level instruction set (each language $A$ is reduced to machine code $B$).
- **SAT Solvers:** NP-hard problems in circuit design, scheduling, and cryptography are reduced to Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) problems.
- **Model Checking:** Hardware/software verification problems are reduced to state-exploration problems.
- **Cybersecurity:** Halting problem analyses directly enable detection of infinite loops and denial-of-service vulnerabilities.

## 2.5 The Master Diagram of Reductions

The following hierarchy (informally) shows the known reduction paths in the KTU syllabus:

$$A_{TM} \longrightarrow HALT_{TM} \longrightarrow EQ_{TM}$$

$$A_{TM} \longrightarrow E_{TM} \longrightarrow EQ_{TM}$$

$$A_{TM} \longrightarrow REGULAR_{TM} \longrightarrow EQ_{TM}$$

$$A_{TM} \longrightarrow PCP$$

$$A_{TM} \longrightarrow E_{LBA} \text{ (via computation history)}$$

$$A_{TM} \longrightarrow A_{CFG} \text{ ambiguity}$$

> Each arrow represents a known mapping reduction that has been formally proven.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains the **canonical reduction proofs** required for the KTU Board Examination. Every transition is shown explicitly. A common student error is to skip the construction of the oracle machine $F^{B}$ — we will not skip any step.

## 3.1 Proof: $A_{TM} \leq_m HALT_{TM}$ (HALT is Undecidable)

**Goal:** Show that the Halting Problem $HALT_{TM}$ is undecidable.

**Assumption for contradiction:** Suppose $R$ is a decider for $HALT_{TM}$. We will build a decider $S$ for $A_{TM}$, contradicting the known undecidability of $A_{TM}$.

### Step 1 — Define the Reduction Function $f$

We define $f$ as a Turing machine that transforms an input pair $\langle M, w \rangle$ into a new pair $\langle M', w \rangle$, where $M'$ behaves as follows:

**Construction of $M'$ (on input $x$):**
1. **Simulate** $M$ on input $w$.
2. If $M$ accepts $w$, then **accept $x$**.
3. If $M$ rejects $w$ (or loops), then **enter an infinite loop on $x$**.

In simpler terms: $M'$ ignores its own input $x$ and behaves exactly like $M$ on $w$.

### Step 2 — Verify the Equivalence $w \in A_{TM} \iff f(w) \in HALT_{TM}$

We must show the biconditional holds in **both directions**.

**Case 1: $w \in A_{TM}$ (i.e., $M$ accepts $w$)**
- Then $M'$ halts on *any* input $x$ (it accepts $x$).
- Therefore $\langle M', x \rangle \in HALT_{TM}$ for all $x$, in particular $\langle M', w \rangle \in HALT_{TM}$.
- So $f(w) = \langle M', w \rangle \in HALT_{TM}$. ✓

**Case 2: $w \notin A_{TM}$ (i.e., $M$ does not accept $w$, either rejects or loops)**
- If $M$ loops on $w$, then $M'$ loops on every input $x$.
- So $\langle M', w \rangle \notin HALT_{TM}$.
- If $M$ rejects $w$, then $M'$ enters an infinite loop on every input $x$.
- So $\langle M', w \rangle \notin HALT_{TM}$.
- Therefore $f(w) \notin HALT_{TM}$. ✓

**Both cases verified, so the biconditional holds.**

### Step 3 — Build the Oracle Machine $S$ for $A_{TM}$

$$S = \text{"On input } \langle M, w \rangle:$$

1. **Construct** the TM $M'$ as described in Step 1. (This is a finite, mechanical procedure — $f$ is computable.)
2. **Run** the assumed decider $R$ on input $\langle M', w \rangle$.
3. If $R$ accepts, **accept** $\langle M, w \rangle$. (Reason: $M$ halts on $w$, so $M$ either accepts or rejects. But to confirm $w \in A_{TM}$ we must verify $M$ accepts. *However*, $S$ cannot differentiate accept from reject via the halting oracle — see Pitfall 1 below.)
4. If $R$ rejects, **reject** $\langle M, w \rangle$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall 1:** Many students incorrectly conclude from $R$ accepting that $M$ *accepts* $w$. This is wrong! $R$ only tells us that $M$ *halts* on $w$. To fix this, the standard construction of $M'$ must be modified: $M'$ should **accept $x$ if $M$ accepts $w$** and **reject $x$ if $M$ does not accept $w$** (using a non-halting helper). However, the simplest version concludes **$HALT_{TM}$ is undecidable, and we derive that $A_{TM} \leq_m HALT_{TM}$**. The mapping is correct; the construction of $S$ is the more subtle step. Consult your textbook's full version of this proof.

### Step 4 — Derive Contradiction

Since $S$ is a decider for $A_{TM}$ but we know (by diagonalization) that $A_{TM}$ is undecidable, our assumption that $R$ exists must be **false**.

$$\boxed{\therefore HALT_{TM} \text{ is undecidable.}}$$

---

## 3.2 Proof: $A_{TM} \leq_m E_{TM}$ ($E_{TM}$ is Undecidable)

**Goal:** Show that $E_{TM} = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid L(M) = \emptyset \}$ is undecidable.

### Step 1 — Define the Reduction Function $f$

$f$ takes input $\langle M, w \rangle$ and produces $\langle M_1 \rangle$, where $M_1$ is constructed as follows:

**Construction of $M_1$ (on input $x$):**
1. Simulate $M$ on $w$.
2. If $M$ accepts $w$, then **accept $x$**.
3. (If $M$ rejects or loops, $M_1$ does not accept $x$ — it just halts without accepting.)

### Step 2 — Verify $w \in A_{TM} \iff f(w) \in E_{TM}$

**Case 1: $w \in A_{TM}$ (M accepts w)**
- $M_1$ accepts every input $x$ (it ignores $x$ and accepts once $M$ accepts $w$).
- $L(M_1) = \Sigma^*$, which is **non-empty**.
- So $\langle M_1 \rangle \notin E_{TM}$. ✓

**Case 2: $w \notin A_{TM}$ (M does not accept w)**
- $M_1$ never accepts any input.
- $L(M_1) = \emptyset$, which is **empty**.
- So $\langle M_1 \rangle \in E_{TM}$. ✓

The biconditional holds (with one being complement — see the technical note below).

> [!NOTE]
> **Technical Note:** The mapping is actually $A_{TM} \leq_m \overline{E_{TM}}$, not $A_{TM} \leq_m E_{TM}$ directly. To fix this, the textbook uses $M_1$ that accepts on $x$ if $M$ accepts $w$ and rejects on $x$ if $M$ doesn't accept $w$. Then $L(M_1) \neq \emptyset \iff M$ accepts $w$. The mapping $A_{TM} \leq_m \overline{E_{TM}}$ combined with $\overline{E_{TM}} \leq_m E_{TM}$ (via $M_2$ that swaps accept and reject) gives $A_{TM} \leq_m E_{TM}$. For the KTU exam, the *first* form is usually acceptable but **state both cases explicitly**.

### Step 3 — Build the Oracle Machine $S$ for $A_{TM}$

$$S = \text{"On input } \langle M, w \rangle:$$

1. Construct $M_1$ as above.
2. Run the assumed decider $T$ for $E_{TM}$ on $\langle M_1 \rangle$.
3. If $T$ accepts (i.e., $L(M_1) = \emptyset$), then **reject** $\langle M, w \rangle$.
4. If $T$ rejects (i.e., $L(M_1) \neq \emptyset$), then **accept** $\langle M, w \rangle$.

### Step 4 — Derive Contradiction

$$\boxed{\therefore E_{TM} \text{ is undecidable.}}$$

---

## 3.3 Proof: $A_{TM} \leq_m EQ_{TM}$ ($EQ_{TM}$ is Undecidable)

**Goal:** Show that $EQ_{TM} = \{ \langle M_1, M_2 \rangle \mid L(M_1) = L(M_2) \}$ is undecidable.

**Strategy:** Reduce $E_{TM}$ to $EQ_{TM}$ (since we already know $E_{TM}$ is undecidable).

### Step 1 — Define the Reduction Function $f$

$f$ takes input $\langle M \rangle$ and produces $\langle M, M_{\emptyset} \rangle$, where $M_{\emptyset}$ is a fixed TM that **rejects every input** (so $L(M_{\emptyset}) = \emptyset$).

### Step 2 — Verify $\langle M \rangle \in E_{TM} \iff f(\langle M \rangle) \in EQ_{TM}$

**Case 1: $\langle M \rangle \in E_{TM}$ (L(M) = ∅)**
- $L(M) = L(M_{\emptyset}) = \emptyset$.
- So $\langle M, M_{\emptyset} \rangle \in EQ_{TM}$. ✓

**Case 2: $\langle M \rangle \notin E_{TM}$ (L(M) ≠ ∅)**
- $L(M) \neq L(M_{\emptyset})$.
- So $\langle M, M_{\emptyset} \rangle \notin EQ_{TM}$. ✓

The biconditional holds. ✓

### Step 3 — Derive Contradiction

If $EQ_{TM}$ had a decider $R$, then we could decide $E_{TM}$ by feeding $R$ the input $\langle M, M_{\emptyset} \rangle$. This contradicts the undecidability of $E_{TM}$.

$$\boxed{\therefore EQ_{TM} \text{ is undecidable.}}$$

---

## 3.4 Proof: $A_{TM} \leq_m REGULAR_{TM}$ (REGULAR is Undecidable)

**Goal:** Show that $REGULAR_{TM} = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid L(M) \text{ is regular} \}$ is undecidable.

### Step 1 — Define the Reduction Function $f$

$f$ takes input $\langle M, w \rangle$ and produces $\langle M_2 \rangle$, where $M_2$ behaves as follows:

**Construction of $M_2$ (on input $x$):**
1. If $x = 0^n 1^n$ for some $n \geq 0$, then **accept**.
2. Otherwise, simulate $M$ on $w$. If $M$ accepts $w$, **accept**.

### Step 2 — Verify $w \in A_{TM} \iff f(w) \in REGULAR_{TM}$

**Case 1: $w \in A_{TM}$**
- $M_2$ accepts **all** strings (both $\{0^n 1^n\}$ and anything else).
- $L(M_2) = \Sigma^*$, which is **regular**.
- So $\langle M_2 \rangle \in REGULAR_{TM}$. ✓

**Case 2: $w \notin A_{TM}$**
- $M_2$ accepts only the non-regular language $\{0^n 1^n \mid n \geq 0\}$.
- $L(M_2) = \{0^n 1^n \mid n \geq 0\}$, which is **NOT regular** (by pumping lemma).
- So $\langle M_2 \rangle \notin REGULAR_{TM}$. ✓

The biconditional holds. ✓

$$\boxed{\therefore REGULAR_{TM} \text{ is undecidable.}}$$

---

## 3.5 Rice's Theorem (Shortcut for KTU)

**Statement:** Let $P$ be a **non-trivial** property of Turing-recognizable languages. Then the language

$$L_P = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid L(M) \text{ satisfies } P \}$$

is **undecidable**.

**Conditions for Rice's Theorem to apply:**
1. $P$ is a property of **languages** (not of TMs themselves).
2. $P$ is **non-trivial**: there exist at least one r.e. language satisfying $P$ and at least one r.e. language not satisfying $P$.

**Examples where Rice's Theorem applies (all undecidable):**
- $L(M) = \emptyset$ → $E_{TM}$ ✓
- $L(M)$ is regular → $REGULAR_{TM}$ ✓
- $L(M)$ is finite ✓
- $L(M)$ is infinite ✓
- $L(M)$ contains $\varepsilon$ ✓
- $L(M) = \Sigma^*$ ✓

**Examples where Rice's Theorem does NOT apply (decidable):**
- $M$ has at least 5 states (this is a property of the **TM**, not the **language**).
- $M$ halts in fewer than 100 steps (about runtime, not language).
- $M$ has 2 states (decidable — just inspect $\langle M \rangle$).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often apply Rice's Theorem to properties of the TM *description* (e.g., "M has 2 states"), but these are **decidable** by simply inspecting the input. Rice's Theorem is for **language properties only**.

---

## 3.6 Computation History Reduction Framework

**Used for:** $A_{TM} \leq_m A_{LBA}$, $A_{TM} \leq_m E_{LBA}$, $A_{TM} \leq_m PCP$.

**Definition:** A **computation history** of TM $M$ on input $w$ is the sequence of configurations $C_1, C_2, \dots, C_k$ where:
- $C_1 = q_0 w$ is the start configuration.
- Each $C_{i+1}$ is obtained from $C_i$ by applying one step of $M$'s transition function.
- $C_k$ is either an accepting or rejecting configuration.

**Canonical use — proving $A_{TM} \leq_m E_{LBA}$:**

Given $\langle M, w \rangle$, construct LBA $B$ that on input $x$:
1. Check if $x = C_1 \# C_2 \# \dots \# C_k$, a valid computation history of $M$ on $w$.
2. Verify $C_1 = q_0 w$ and $C_k$ is an accepting configuration.
3. Accept iff all checks pass.

**Key insight:** $L(B) \neq \emptyset$ iff $M$ accepts $w$. Since checking an LBA's language emptiness is undecidable ($E_{LBA}$), $A_{TM}$ is undecidable. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Reduction Hierarchy (Mermaid)

This diagram maps the canonical reduction relationships in the KTU Module 4 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    ATM[ATM Undecidable Foundational Problem]
    HALT[HALT TM Undecidable]
    EMPTY[ETM Undecidable]
    EQ[EQ TM Undecidable]
    REG[REGULAR TM Undecidable]
    CFG[AMBIG CFG Undecidable]
    PCP[PCP Undecidable]
    ELBA[ELBA Undecidable]
    ALBA[ALBA Decidable]
    RICE[Rice Theorem Shortcut]
    
    ATM -->|reduce| HALT
    ATM -->|reduce| EMPTY
    ATM -->|reduce| REG
    ATM -->|reduce| PCP
    ATM -->|history reduce| ELBA
    ATM -->|history reduce| ALBA
    
    EMPTY -->|reduce| EQ
    REG -->|reduce| EQ
    ATM -->|reduce| CFG
    
    EMPTY -. uses .-> RICE
    REG -. uses .-> RICE
    
    style ATM fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff
    style HALT fill:#ffa500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EMPTY fill:#ffa500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style EQ fill:#ffa500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style REG fill:#ffa500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CFG fill:#ffa500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PCP fill:#ffa500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ELBA fill:#ffa500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ALBA fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RICE fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> **Legend:** 🔴 Red = Foundational hard problem. 🟠 Orange = Undecidable problems. 🟢 Green = Decidable problems. 🔵 Blue = Theorem/Technique.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Mapping Reduction

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT[Input w in A] -->|w| REDUCER[Reduction Function f Computable TM F]
    REDUCER -->|f of w| ORACLE[Decider for B Black Box]
    ORACLE -->|accept or reject| OUTPUT[Output in B]
    
    style INPUT fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style REDUCER fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ORACLE fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style OUTPUT fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **input** is a string $w$ that may or may not belong to language $A$.
- The **reducer** is a Turing machine $F$ that mechanically transforms $w$ into $f(w)$.
- The **oracle** decides membership of $f(w)$ in $B$.
- The **output** is the result, which is also the result of deciding $w \in A$.

## 4.3 Computation History Sequence Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1[C1 Start Config q0 w] -->|step 1| C2[C2]
    C2 -->|step 2| C3[C3]
    C3 -->|step k-1| CK[Ck End Config]
    CK -->|type| ACC[qaccept]
    CK -->|type| REJ[qreject]
    
    style C1 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CK fill:#ffcccb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ACC fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style REJ fill:#808080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Note:** A computation history is the *full* sequence of every configuration from start to halting — it captures the entire run of the TM as a single string.

## 4.4 Rice's Theorem Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[Property P of Languages] --> Q1{Is P a property of LANGUAGES not TMs?}
    Q1 -->|No| DEC[Decidable Inspect input]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is P non-trivial? Exists L1 in P, L2 not in P both r.e.}
    Q2 -->|No| DEC2[Decidable All or None]
    Q2 -->|Yes| UNDEC[Undecidable Apply Rice Theorem]
    
    style START fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DEC fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DEC2 fill:#90ee90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style UNDEC fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.5 Reduction Strategy Roadmap for KTU Problems

```mermaid
graph TD
    PROB[New Problem: Is L Undecidable?] --> A{Does Rice Apply?}
    A -->|Yes| RICE[Apply Rice Theorem]
    A -->|No| B{Known Undecidable Problem to reduce from?}
    B -->|A TM| ATMRED[Build f from A TM]
    B -->|E TM| ETMRED[Build f from E TM]
    B -->|HALT TM| HALTRED[Build f from HALT TM]
    B -->|None fit| HISTORY[Use Computation History]
    
    ATMRED --> VERIFY[Verify Biconditional]
    ETMRED --> VERIFY
    HALTRED --> VERIFY
    RICE --> DONE[Conclusion: Undecidable]
    HISTORY --> VERIFY
    VERIFY --> DONE
    
    style PROB fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RICE fill:#87ceeb,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style DONE fill:#ff6b6b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2023]
**Define mapping reducibility. Show that if $A \leq_m B$ and $B$ is decidable, then $A$ is decidable.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** A language $A$ is **mapping reducible** to language $B$ (written $A \leq_m B$) if there exists a computable function $f : \Sigma^* \to \Sigma^*$ such that for every $w$,
> $$w \in A \iff f(w) \in B$$

> **Proof:** Let $R$ be a decider for $B$. Construct a decider $S$ for $A$ as follows:
> $$S = \text{"On input } w:$$
> 1. Compute $f(w)$ using the computable function $f$.
> 2. Run $R$ on $f(w)$.
> 3. If $R$ accepts, accept; if $R$ rejects, reject."

> Since $f$ is computable (always halts) and $R$ is a decider, $S$ always halts with the correct answer. Hence $A$ is decidable. ✓ (3 marks)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**State and explain Rice's Theorem with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement:** Let $P$ be a non-trivial property of the language recognized by a Turing machine. Then the problem of determining whether a given TM $M$ has language satisfying property $P$ is **undecidable**.

> **Example:** The language $E_{TM} = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid L(M) = \emptyset \}$ is undecidable by Rice's Theorem because:
> - "Having an empty language" is a property of the **language** $L(M)$, not the machine.
> - It is non-trivial: $L(M_1) = \emptyset$ (satisfies) and $L(M_2) = \{0\}^*$ (does not satisfy). ✓ (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Prove that the **Halting Problem** ($HALT_{TM}$) is undecidable by reducing $A_{TM}$ to $HALT_{TM}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Prove that the language $REGULAR_{TM} = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid L(M) \text{ is regular} \}$ is undecidable.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — $A_{TM} \leq_m HALT_{TM}$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — State the reduction goal (1 mark):**
We construct a computable function $f$ such that $\langle M, w \rangle \in A_{TM} \iff f(\langle M, w \rangle) \in HALT_{TM}$.

**Step 2 — Construct the reduction function (2 marks):**
Define $f(\langle M, w \rangle) = \langle M', w \rangle$, where $M'$ is a TM that on input $x$:
1. Simulates $M$ on $w$.
2. If $M$ accepts $w$, then $M'$ **accepts** $x$.
3. If $M$ rejects $w$ (or loops), then $M'$ **enters an infinite loop** on $x$.

**Step 3 — Verify the biconditional (2 marks):**
- **Forward:** If $\langle M, w \rangle \in A_{TM}$, then $M$ accepts $w$, so $M'$ accepts $x$, so $M'$ halts on $w$, so $\langle M', w \rangle \in HALT_{TM}$. ✓
- **Backward:** If $\langle M, w \rangle \notin A_{TM}$, then $M$ does not accept $w$, so $M'$ loops on $x$, so $\langle M', w \rangle \notin HALT_{TM}$. ✓

**Step 4 — Derive contradiction (2 marks):**
If $HALT_{TM}$ had a decider $R$, then we could build a decider for $A_{TM}$:
$$S = \text{"On input } \langle M, w \rangle:$$
1. Construct $M'$ as above.
2. Run $R$ on $\langle M', w \rangle$.
3. Output $R$'s answer."

This contradicts the undecidability of $A_{TM}$. Hence $HALT_{TM}$ is undecidable. ✓

---

**Model Solution to (b) — $A_{TM} \leq_m REGULAR_{TM}$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define the reduction (1 mark):**
Construct $f(\langle M, w \rangle) = \langle M_2 \rangle$, where $M_2$ on input $x$:
1. If $x$ has the form $0^n 1^n$ for some $n \geq 0$, **accept**.
2. Otherwise, simulate $M$ on $w$. If $M$ accepts $w$, **accept**.

**Step 2 — Construct $M_2$ explicitly (2 marks):**
The TM $M_2$ has two branches: (a) pattern-match $x$ against the non-regular language $\{0^n 1^n\}$; (b) run $M$ on $w$. The construction is clearly computable (it is a finite procedure on input $\langle M, w \rangle$).

**Step 3 — Verify the biconditional (2 marks):**
- **Case 1: $w \in A_{TM}$.** $M$ accepts $w$, so $M_2$ accepts **all** strings $x$. $L(M_2) = \Sigma^*$ (regular). So $\langle M_2 \rangle \in REGULAR_{TM}$. ✓
- **Case 2: $w \notin A_{TM}$.** $M$ never accepts $w$, so $M_2$ accepts only the strings $0^n 1^n$. $L(M_2) = \{0^n 1^n\}$, which is **non-regular** (pumping lemma). So $\langle M_2 \rangle \notin REGULAR_{TM}$. ✓

**Step 4 — Conclude (2 marks):**
If $REGULAR_{TM}$ were decidable, we could decide $A_{TM}$, a contradiction. Therefore $REGULAR_{TM}$ is undecidable. ✓

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Using Rice's Theorem, prove that the language $L = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid L(M) \text{ is finite} \}$ is undecidable.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Show that $E_{TM} \leq_m EQ_{TM}$, and hence prove $EQ_{TM}$ is undecidable.

---

**Model Solution to (a) — Rice's Theorem for Finiteness (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Recall Rice's Theorem (1 mark):**
> Any **non-trivial** property of the language recognized by a Turing machine is undecidable.

**Step 2 — Identify the property (1 mark):**
Property $P$: "$L(M)$ is finite." This is a property of the **language** $L(M)$, not of the TM $M$ itself. So Rice's Theorem applies.

**Step 3 — Show $P$ is non-trivial (2 marks):**
- **Witness 1 (P holds):** Let $M_1$ be a TM that accepts no strings. Then $L(M_1) = \emptyset$, which is finite. ✓
- **Witness 2 (P fails):** Let $M_2$ be a TM that accepts all strings (e.g., $M_2$ immediately moves to $q_{accept}$). Then $L(M_2) = \Sigma^*$, which is infinite. ✓
- Both are r.e. languages, so $P$ is non-trivial over r.e. languages.

**Step 4 — Apply Rice's Theorem (1 mark):**
By Rice's Theorem, $L = \{ \langle M \rangle \mid L(M) \text{ is finite} \}$ is undecidable.

**Step 5 — Conclude with a remark (2 marks):**
The result is a direct consequence of the general theorem. Notice we did **not** need to construct a reduction — Rice's Theorem is a *shortcut* for proving undecidability of language properties. The result generalizes to all non-trivial language properties (regular, context-free, infinite, contains a particular string, etc.).

---

**Model Solution to (b) — $E_{TM} \leq_m EQ_{TM}$ (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define the reduction function (1 mark):**
Define a computable function $f(\langle M \rangle) = \langle M, M_{\emptyset} \rangle$, where $M_{\emptyset}$ is a fixed TM that **rejects every input** (i.e., $L(M_{\emptyset}) = \emptyset$).

**Step 2 — Construct $M_{\emptyset}$ explicitly (1 mark):**
$M_{\emptyset}$ on input $x$: immediately transition to $q_{reject}$. This is a simple, fixed TM (independent of $M$).

**Step 3 — Verify the biconditional (2 marks):**
- **Case 1:** $\langle M \rangle \in E_{TM}$ means $L(M) = \emptyset$. Then $L(M) = L(M_{\emptyset}) = \emptyset$, so $\langle M, M_{\emptyset} \rangle \in EQ_{TM}$. ✓
- **Case 2:** $\langle M \rangle \notin E_{TM}$ means $L(M) \neq \emptyset$. Then $L(M) \neq L(M_{\emptyset})$, so $\langle M, M_{\emptyset} \rangle \notin EQ_{TM}$. ✓

**Step 4 — Show $f$ is computable (1 mark):**
$f$ takes $\langle M \rangle$ and outputs the pair $\langle M, M_{\emptyset} \rangle$. Since $M_{\emptyset}$ is hard-coded, this is a trivial finite transformation. $f$ is total computable. ✓

**Step 5 — Derive undecidability of $EQ_{TM}$ (2 marks):**
Suppose $EQ_{TM}$ had a decider $R$. We could decide $E_{TM}$ as follows:
$$T = \text{"On input } \langle M \rangle:$$
1. Compute $\langle M, M_{\emptyset} \rangle$.
2. Run $R$ on this pair.
3. Output $R$'s answer."

This contradicts the undecidability of $E_{TM}$. Hence $EQ_{TM}$ is undecidable. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE marks on Reduction problems (Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify BOTH directions of the biconditional** (1-2 marks lost). A reduction $A \leq_m B$ requires $w \in A \iff f(w) \in B$. Showing only one direction is incomplete.
>
> 2. **Constructing a non-computable $f$** (2-3 marks lost). The function $f$ must be a **total, computable** TM. If your construction invokes an oracle or assumes undecidable operations, $f$ is invalid.
>
> 3. **Confusing the Halting Problem with the Acceptance Problem** (1-2 marks lost). $A_{TM}$ is "does $M$ accept $w$?" not "does $M$ halt on $w$?". They are *related* but *not identical* — the reduction must be careful about the distinction.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to derive the contradiction** (2 marks lost). The final step must explicitly say: "This contradicts the undecidability of $A_{TM}$ / $E_{TM}$ / $HALT_{TM}$, hence the assumed decider does not exist."
>
> 5. **Applying Rice's Theorem to non-language properties** (2 marks lost). "M has 2 states" is decidable. Rice's Theorem applies only to **language properties**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the witness languages for non-triviality** (1 mark lost). When applying Rice's Theorem, you must provide BOTH: (a) an r.e. language satisfying $P$, and (b) an r.e. language not satisfying $P$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Reductions**

- ✅ A **reduction** transforms one decision problem into another such that a solution to the second solves the first.
- ✅ **Mapping reduction** $A \leq_m B$ requires a **total computable function** $f$ with $w \in A \iff f(w) \in B$.
- ✅ **Decidability transfers UP** ($A \leq_m B$ and $B$ decidable $\Rightarrow A$ decidable).
- ✅ **Undecidability transfers DOWN** ($A \leq_m B$ and $A$ undecidable $\Rightarrow B$ undecidable).
- ✅ The **Halting Problem** $HALT_{TM}$ is the canonical hard problem for reductions of type "halts on input."
- ✅ $E_{TM}$, $REGULAR_{TM}$, $EQ_{TM}$ are reduced from $A_{TM}$ (or $E_{TM}$).
- ✅ **Computation histories** are used for reductions involving LBAs, PCPs, and other structural problems.
- ✅ **Rice's Theorem** is a SHORTCUT for proving language properties are undecidable. The property must be **non-trivial** and a property of **languages**, not machines.
- ✅ The **decision procedure** to remember: $w \in A$? $\to$ compute $f(w)$ $\to$ query oracle for $B$ $\to$ output oracle's answer.
- ✅ $A_{TM}$, $HALT_{TM}$, $E_{TM}$, $EQ_{TM}$, $REGULAR_{TM}$, $PCP$, $E_{LBA}$ are all **undecidable**.
- ✅ $A_{LBA}$, $A_{CFG}$, $E_{DFA}$ are **decidable** (last one is from Module 2).
- ✅ $EQ_{DFA}$, $EQ_{CFG}$ are **decidable**; $EQ_{REX}$ is **decidable** (regex equivalence).
- ✅ **Transitivity:** $A \leq_m B$ and $B \leq_m C$ implies $A \leq_m C$.
- ✅ A language is **decidable** $\iff$ both it AND its complement are Turing-recognizable.
- ✅ For the KTU exam, ALWAYS explicitly state: (1) construction of $f$, (2) verification of biconditional, (3) contradiction with known undecidable problem.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decidable and Undecidable Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCST302 - THEORY OF COMPUTATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Decidable and Undecidable Problems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition — Decidable Problem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decidable Language (Recursive Language):** > A language $L \subseteq \Sigma^{*}$ is called **decidable** (or **recursive**) if there exists a **Turing Machine $M$** such that for every string $w \in \Sigma^{*}$, $M$ halts and: > - $M$ **accepts** $w$ if $w \in L$ > - $M$ **rejects** $w$ if $w \notin L$ > > Equivalently, a problem is **decidable** if a corresponding Turing Machine is guaranteed to **halt on every input** and give a definitive YES/NO answer in **finite time**. The corresponding decision problem is called a **decidable problem**. If such a TM exists, the problem is said to be **solvable**. ### 1.2 Formal Definition — Undecidable Problem > [!IMPORTANT] > **Undecidable Language (Non-Recursive Language):** > A language $L$ is called **undecidable** (or **non-recursive**) if there is **no Turing Machine** that always halts and decides membership in $L$. That is, for every candidate TM $M$: > - Either $M$ loops forever on some input, **or** > - $M$ gives the wrong answer on at least one input. > > The decision problem corresponding to $L$ is called an **undecidable problem**. > [!NOTE] > **Semi-Decidable (Recursively Enumerable) Bridge:** > A language may not be decidable, but may be **Turing-recognizable** (recursively enumerable). For such languages, a TM exists that accepts every $w \in L$, but may **run forever** on $w \notin L$. > > **Hierarchical Picture (KTU Gold):** > All Decidable $\subset$ Turing-Recognizable $\subset$ All Languages ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Courtroom Imagine a courtroom judge: | Scenario | Real-World Analogy | TOC Equivalent | |---|---|---| | A clear case with written evidence | Judge reads papers, gives verdict, goes home | **Decidable** problem — TM halts | | A case with no clear evidence, but the judge spots the liar | Judge declares guilt but cannot always prove innocence | **Turing-Recognizable** (r.e.) language | | A case where no matter what evidence, the judge can never decide | The Halting Problem | **Undecidable** problem | > [!TIP] > **Why is the Halting Problem undecidable?** Because asking *"Will this program ever stop?"* requires simulating the program — but the simulation itself may run forever. There is no general finite procedure that can look at *every* program and *every* input and predict termination. ### 1.4 The Central Decision Problems of TOC (KTU Syllabus) > [!IMPORTANT] > The decision problem corresponding to a language $L$ is the algorithmic question: > > $$\text{Given } w \in \Sigma^{*},\ \text{is } w \in L\ ?$$ > > The encoding of a TM $M$ is denoted $\langle M \rangle$, and an input $w$ is encoded as $\langle M, w \rangle$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Taxonomy of Languages (Chomsky Hierarchy ↔ Decidability) | Language Class | Decision Problem | Decidable? | Recognizable? | |---|---|---|---| | Regular ($REG$) | $A_{DFA}, A_{NFA}, A_{REX}, E_{DFA}, EQ_{DFA}$ | **Yes** | Yes | | Context-Free ($CFL$) | $A_{CFG}, E_{CFG}$ | **Yes** | Yes | | Context-Free ($CFL$) | $EQ_{CFG}, ALL_{CFG}, REG_{CFG}$ | **No** | No (some are undecidable but co-r.e.) | | Recursive (Decidable) | $A_{TM}$ complement variants | **Mixed** | Yes | | Recursively Enumerable | $A_{TM}$ (Halting) | **No** | Yes | | Non-r.e. | $\overline{A_{TM}}$ | **No** | **No** | ### 2.2 The Seven Canonical Decision Problems (KTU High-Yield) > [!NOTE] > Memorize the formal statement of each — KTU exams routinely test this verbatim. | Problem | Statement | Status | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | $A_{DFA}$ | Is $w \in L(B)$ for DFA $B$? | **Decidable** | Simulate $B$ on $w$ | | $A_{NFA}$ | Is $w \in L(B)$ for NFA $B$? | **Decidable** | Convert NFA→DFA, then run | | $A_{REX}$ | Does $R$ generate $w$? | **Decidable** | Convert $R \to$ NFA | | $E_{DFA}$ | Is $L(B) = \emptyset$? | **Decidable** | Mark reachable states | | $EQ_{DFA}$ | Is $L(A) = L(B)$? | **Decidable** | Symmetric difference + emptiness | | $A_{CFG}$ | Is $w \in L(G)$? | **Decidable** | CYK / Chomsky Normal Form | | $E_{CFG}$ | Is $L(G) = \emptyset$? | **Decidable** | Find productive non-terminals | | $A_{TM}$ | Does $M$ accept $w$? | **Undecidable** | Self-reference diagonalization | | $HALT_{TM}$ | Does $M$ halt on $w$? | **Undecidable** | Reduces from $A_{TM}$ | | $E_{TM}$ | Is $L(M) = \emptyset$? | **Undecidable** | Reduces from $A_{TM}$ | | $EQ_{TM}$ | Is $L(M_1) = L(M_2)$? | **Undecidable** | Reduces from $E_{TM}$ | | $REGULAR_{TM}$ | Is $L(M)$ regular? | **Undecidable** | Reduction using modified TMs | | $PCP$ | Does a Post Correspondence System have a match? | **Undecidable** | Reduces from $A_{TM}$ | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Statement | Meaning | |---|---|---| | 1 | $L \text{ is decidable} \iff L \in R$ | $R$ = class of recursive (decidable) languages | | 2 | $L \text{ is r.e.} \iff L \in RE$ | $RE$ = class of recursively enumerable | | 3 | $R \subsetneq RE \subsetneq \Sigma^{*}$ | Strict containment hierarchy | | 4 | $L \in R \iff L \in RE \text{ and } \overline{L} \in RE$ | Complement test for decidability | | 5 | $A_{TM} \in RE$ but $A_{TM} \notin R$ | Halting problem is r.e. but not decidable | | 6 | $\overline{A_{TM}} \notin RE$ | Complement of halting is not even r.e. | | 7 | $A \leq_{m} B$ | $A$ is **mapping reducible** to $B$ | | 8 | If $A \leq_{m} B$ and $B \in R \Rightarrow A \in R$ | Reducibility preserves decidability upward | | 9 | If $A \leq_{m} B$ and $A \notin R \Rightarrow B \notin R$ | Reducibility transfers undecidability downward | | 10 | $A \in R \iff A \leq_{m} \emptyset$ (trivially) | Sanity check (rare KTU trick) | ### 2.4 Rice's Theorem — The "Generalized Undecidability" Hammer > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rice's Theorem (1953):** > Let $\mathcal{P}$ be any **non-trivial property** of the language recognized by a Turing Machine. Then the language > > $$\mathcal{L}_{\mathcal{P}} = \{\ \langle M \rangle \mid \mathcal{P}(L(M)) \text{ is true}\ \}$$ > > is **undecidable**. > > **"Non-trivial"** means $\mathcal{P}$ holds for some but not all TMs. **Examples caught by Rice's Theorem:** - *"Is $L(M)$ empty?"* → Non-trivial → Undecidable ($E_{TM}$) - *"Is $L(M)$ finite?"* → Non-trivial → Undecidable ($FINITE_{TM}$) - *"Is $L(M)$ regular?"* → Non-trivial → Undecidable ($REGULAR_{TM}$) - *"Does $L(M)$ contain a specific string $w$?"* → Non-trivial → Undecidable > [!NOTE] > **Rice's Theorem does NOT apply** to: > - Properties of the **machine description itself** (e.g., "Does $M$ have exactly 7 states?") — these are decidable. > - The **trivial** properties (always true / always false). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 EXHAUSTIVE PROOF: $A_{TM}$ is Undecidable (Diagonalization) > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important proof** in TOC — KTU almost always tests it. Memorize the structure. **Theorem:** $A_{TM} = \{\ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM that accepts input } w\ \}$ is **undecidable**. **Proof by contradiction (assume $H$ decides $A_{TM}$):** **Step 1 — Assume for contradiction** that TM $H$ decides $A_{TM}$. $$H(\langle M, w \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } M \text{ accepts } w \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } M \text{ does not accept } w \end{cases}$$ **Step 2 — Construct a new TM $D$** that uses $H$ as a subroutine: $$D(\langle M \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } M \text{ does NOT accept } \langle M \rangle \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } M \text{ accepts } \langle M \rangle \end{cases}$$ **Step 3 — Implement $D$ formally:** On input $\langle M \rangle$ (the encoding of some TM $M$): 1. Run $H$ on input $\langle M, \langle M \rangle \rangle$. 2. If $H$ accepts, then **reject**. 3. If $H$ rejects, then **accept**. **Step 4 — Derive the contradiction** by feeding $\langle D \rangle$ to $D$: $$D(\langle D \rangle) = \begin{cases} \text{accept} & \text{if } D \text{ does NOT accept } \langle D \rangle \\ \text{reject} & \text{if } D \text{ accepts } \langle D \rangle \end{cases}$$ This is a **logical contradiction** — $D$ accepts its own description if and only if $D$ does not accept its own description. **Step 5 — Conclude.** TM $H$ cannot exist, so $A_{TM}$ is **undecidable**. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 EXHAUSTIVE PROOF: $\overline{A_{TM}}$ is Not Turing-Recognizable **Theorem:** $\overline{A_{TM}} = \{\ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ is a TM that does NOT accept } w\ \}$ is **not r.e.** **Proof:** **Step 1 — Assume** $B$ is a TM that recognizes $\overline{A_{TM}}$. **Step 2 — Use $B$ to construct $H$ that supposedly decides $A_{TM}$**: On input $\langle M, w \rangle$: 1. Simulate $B$ on $\langle M, w \rangle$. 2. If $B$ accepts (meaning $M$ does not accept $w$), then **reject**. 3. If $B$ rejects, then **accept**. **Step 3 — Validate the cases:** | Case | Behavior of $B$ | What $H$ outputs | Correct? | |---|---|---|---| | $M$ accepts $w$ | $B$ rejects (input not in $\overline{A_{TM}}$) | Accept | ✓ | | $M$ doesn't accept $w$ | $B$ accepts | Reject | ✓ | **Step 4 — But** $H$ decides $A_{TM}$, contradicting Theorem 3.1. **Step 5 — Conclusion:** $B$ cannot exist; $\overline{A_{TM}} \notin RE$. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.3 EXHAUSTIVE PROOF: $HALT_{TM}$ is Undecidable (via Reduction) **Theorem:** $HALT_{TM} = \{\ \langle M, w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\ \}$ is **undecidable**. **Proof by reduction from $A_{TM}$:** **Step 1 — Assume** for contradiction that $R$ decides $HALT_{TM}$. **Step 2 — Construct** a TM $S$ that decides $A_{TM}$ using $R$ as a subroutine. On input $\langle M, w \rangle$: 1. Run $R$ on $\langle M, w \rangle$. 2. If $R$ rejects, then **reject** (and halt). 3. If $R$ accepts, simulate $M$ on $w$ until it halts. 4. If $M$ accepts, **accept**; if $M$ rejects, **reject**. **Step 3 — Verification:** | Case | $R$ outcome | $S$ outcome | Correct decision? | |---|---|---|---| | $M$ halts and accepts $w$ | Accept | Simulates, accepts | ✓ | | $M$ halts and rejects $w$ | Accept | Simulates, rejects | ✓ | | $M$ loops on $w$ | Reject | Rejects without simulation | ✓ | **Step 4 — But** $S$ decides $A_{TM}$, which contradicts Theorem 3.1. **Step 5 — Conclusion:** $R$ cannot exist; $HALT_{TM}$ is **undecidable**. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.4 EXHAUSTIVE PROOF: $E_{TM}$ is Undecidable (via Reduction) **Theorem:** $E_{TM} = \{\ \langle M \rangle \mid L(M) = \emptyset\ \}$ is undecidable. **Proof by reduction from $A_{TM}$:** **Step 1 — Assume** $T$ decides $E_{TM}$. **Step 2 — Construction.** Given $\langle M, w \rangle$, build a TM $M_1$: - $M_1$ on input $x$: ignores $x$, runs $M$ on $w$. If $M$ accepts $w$, $M_1$ accepts $x$; else $M_1$ loops. **Step 3 — Analysis:** $$L(M_1) = \begin{cases} \Sigma^{*} & \text{if } M \text{ accepts } w \\ \emptyset & \text{if } M \text{ does not accept } w \end{cases}$$ **Step 4 — Decision procedure for $A_{TM}$:** On input $\langle M, w \rangle$: 1. Construct $\langle M_1 \rangle$. 2. Run $T$ on $\langle M_1 \rangle$. 3. If $T$ accepts (i.e., $L(M_1) = \emptyset$), **reject** $A_{TM}$. 4. If $T$ rejects (i.e., $L(M_1) \neq \emptyset$), **accept** $A_{TM}$. **Step 5 — But** this decides $A_{TM}$, contradiction. So $E_{TM}$ is **undecidable**. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.5 EXHAUSTIVE PROOF: $REGULAR_{TM}$ is Undecidable **Theorem:** $REGULAR_{TM} = \{\ \langle M \rangle \mid L(M) \text{ is regular}\ \}$ is undecidable. **Proof by reduction from $A_{TM}$:** **Step 1 — Assume** $R$ decides $REGULAR_{TM}$. **Step 2 — Build** $M_2$ from $\langle M, w \rangle$: On input $x$: - If $x = 0^n 1^n$ for some $n$, then **accept**. - Else, run $M$ on $w$. If $M$ accepts, **accept**; else **reject**. **Step 3 — Analysis:** $$L(M_2) = \begin{cases} \Sigma^{*} & \text{if } M \text{ accepts } w \\ \{0^n 1^n \mid n \geq 0\} & \text{if } M \text{ does not accept } w \end{cases}$$ Since $\{0^n 1^n \mid n \geq 0\}$ is **non-regular**, and $\Sigma^{*}$ is regular: $$REGULAR_{TM}(\langle M_2 \rangle) \iff M \text{ does NOT accept } w$$ **Step 4 — Decision for $A_{TM}$:** 1. Build $M_2$. 2. Run $R$ on $\langle M_2 \rangle$. 3. If $R$ accepts → $L(M_2)$ regular → $M$ did not accept $w$ → **reject**. 4. If $R$ rejects → $L(M_2)$ non-regular → $M$ accepted $w$ → **accept**. **Step 5 — Contradiction** with Theorem 3.1. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.6 EXHAUSTIVE PROOF: Post Correspondence Problem (PCP) is Undecidable > [!IMPORTANT] > **PCP Definition (KTU syllabus):** A *Post Correspondence System* is a finite collection of dominoes: > > $$\left[\ \frac{t_1}{b_1}\ \right],\ \left[\ \frac{t_2}{b_2}\ \right],\ \ldots,\ \left[\ \frac{t_k}{b_k}\ \right]$$ > > where $t_i, b_i \in \Sigma^{+}$. A *match* is a sequence $i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_n$ of indices (repetition allowed) such that > > $$t_{i_1} t_{i_2} \cdots t_{i_n} = b_{i_1} b_{i_2} \cdots b_{i_n}$$ > > $PCP = \{\ \langle P \rangle \mid P \text{ is a PCP that has a match}\ \}$. **Theorem:** $PCP$ is **undecidable**. **Proof Sketch (Reduction from $A_{TM}$):** **Step 1 — Given** $\langle M, w \rangle$, construct a PCP instance $P$ such that $M$ accepts $w$ **if and only if** $P$ has a match. **Step 2 — Encoding.** Each computation history of $M$ on $w$ is encoded as a string. $P$ is designed so that any match corresponds to a valid accepting computation history. **Step 3 — Key dominoes** (one of several constructions): | Top | Bottom | Purpose | |---|---|---| | $\#$ | $\# q_0 w_1 w_2 \cdots w_n \#$ | Start configuration | | $X$ | $X$ | Copy tape symbols | | $qa$ | $b q' c$ | Simulate transition $\delta(q,a) = (q', b, R)$ | | $cq$ | $q$ | Move tape head left | | $\# q_{acc}$ | $\#$ | Terminate at accept state | **Step 4 — A match exists** $\iff$ $M$ reaches $q_{accept}$ on input $w$ $\iff$ $M$ accepts $w$. **Step 5 — Therefore** if $PCP$ were decidable, $A_{TM}$ would be decidable, contradiction. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.7 EXHAUSTIVE PROOF: $\overline{A_{CFG}}$ is Undecidable (Undecidable CFL Problems) **Theorem:** $\overline{A_{CFG}} = \{\ \langle G, w \rangle \mid w \notin L(G)\ \}$ is undecidable. **Proof by reduction from $A_{TM}$:** **Step 1 — Assume** $R$ decides $\overline{A_{CFG}}$. **Step 2 — From** $\langle M, w \rangle$, build CFG $G$ that generates **all strings** *except* those that are valid accepting computation histories of $M$ on $w$. **Step 3 — Then:** $$w' \notin L(G) \iff w' \text{ is an accepting history} \iff M \text{ accepts } w$$ **Step 4 — Decision for $A_{TM}$:** run $R$ on $\langle G, w' \rangle$ for some dummy string. (Detailed construction is in Sipser; KTU exams accept the high-level reduction.) **Step 5 — Contradiction.** $\blacksquare$ > [!NOTE] > **Undecidable CFL problems** (proven via similar reductions): > - $EQ_{CFG}$ — Are two CFGs equivalent? > - $ALL_{CFG}$ — Does $G$ generate $\Sigma^{*}$? > - $REGULAR_{CFG}$ — Is $L(G)$ regular? > - $A_{CFG} \cap A_{CFG}$ — Intersection of two CFLs (membership) --- ### 3.8 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Reducibility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (Many-One / Mapping Reducibility):** > Language $A$ is **mapping reducible** to language $B$, written $A \leq_{m} B$, if there exists a **computable function** $f: \Sigma^{*} \to \Sigma^{*}$ such that: > > $$w \in A \iff f(w) \in B$$ > > Properties: > - If $A \leq_{m} B$ and $B$ is decidable, then $A$ is decidable. > - If $A \leq_{m} B$ and $A$ is undecidable, then $B$ is undecidable. | Source $A$ | Target $B$ | Reducer $f(\langle M, w \rangle)$ | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | $A_{TM}$ | $HALT_{TM}$ | $(\langle M \rangle, w)$ where $M'$ simulates $M$ then accepts | Halting iff $M$ accepts | | $A_{TM}$ | $E_{TM}$ | Construct $M_1$ that accepts $\Sigma^*$ iff $M$ accepts $w$ | $L(M_1) = \emptyset$ iff $M$ rejects $w$ | | $A_{TM}$ | $EQ_{TM}$ | $(\langle M_1 \rangle, \langle M_{empty} \rangle)$ | $L(M_1) = L(M_{empty})$ iff $L(M_1) = \emptyset$ | | $A_{TM}$ | $REGULAR_{TM}$ | Build $M_2$ | $L(M_2)$ regular iff $M$ rejects $w$ | | $A_{TM}$ | $PCP$ | Construct PCP from $M, w$ | Match exists iff $M$ accepts $w$ | --- ### 3.9 Quick Verification: $E_{DFA}$ is Decidable **Theorem:** $E_{DFA} = \{\ \langle B \rangle \mid L(B) = \emptyset,\ B \text{ is a DFA}\ \}$ is decidable. **Proof.** Let $T$ be the decider: $$T(\langle B \rangle):$$ 1. Mark the start state of $B$. 2. Repeat: mark any state reachable from a marked state via a transition. 3. If no accept state is marked → **accept**; else → **reject**. $T$ always halts because the number of states is finite and BFS terminates. $\blacksquare$ ## 4. w ∈ L(G)"] Check -- No --> Reject["REJECT
